| Male and female | **NEVER** in the same holding pen |
| Adult and juvenile | **NEVER** in the same holding pen |
| Juveniles | Must be placed in a **designated juvenile area** |

> **Exam Alert:** The separation rules are absolute — there are no exceptions. Male/female separation and adult/juvenile separation are mandatory. Juveniles must be placed in a designated juvenile area, not simply a separate cell in the adult holding area.

### Suicide and Assault Prevention — Item Removal

The following items must be removed from prisoners upon placement in a holding pen:

| Item | Reason |
|------|--------|
| Belts | Suicide/assault prevention |
| Neckties | Suicide/assault prevention |
| Shoelaces | Suicide/assault prevention |
| Drawstrings | Suicide/assault prevention |
| Jackets | Suicide/assault prevention |
| Overcoats | Suicide/assault prevention |

> **Exam Alert:** Know all 6 items that must be removed: **belts, neckties, shoelaces, drawstrings, jackets, and overcoats**. A common exam question lists items and asks which must be removed. Remember the mnemonic: **B-N-S-D-J-O** (Belts, Neckties, Shoelaces, Drawstrings, Jackets, Overcoats).

### Menstrual Hygiene

| Rule | Detail |
|------|--------|
| Products | Issue menstrual hygiene products **on request** |
| Restroom access | Assign a **female MOS** to escort prisoner to restroom |

> **Sergeant Focus:** Menstrual hygiene products must be provided upon request — do not wait for the prisoner to ask repeatedly. A female MOS must be assigned to escort the prisoner to the restroom. Failure to provide these basic accommodations is a supervisory failure.

---

## Common Exam Angles

1. **30-minute inspections** — Designated MOS checks every 30 minutes; supervisor also inspects personally
2. **3-hour maximum** — Standard max detention in holding pen; extension requires desk officer/detective squad supervisor + Command Log
3. **Separation rules** — Male/female NEVER together; adult/juvenile NEVER together; juveniles in designated area
4. **6 items removed** — Belts, neckties, shoelaces, drawstrings, jackets, overcoats
5. **No ill/intoxicated/EDP** — These prisoners cannot be placed in holding pens
6. **Supervisor documentation** — Record condition (sleeping, alert) AND position (sitting, lying down)
7. **Menstrual hygiene** — Products on request; female MOS escort to restroom
8. **PD244-1410** — Prisoner Holding Pen Roster must be maintained
9. **Unusual occurrence definition** — Unarraigned adult or juvenile under 18: assaults MOS, sexual assault/harassment, escape/attempt, other unusual
10. **Typed letterhead (49) copies** — 3 for adult prisoner, 2 for child DOA, 1 for child in custody
11. **Escape/attempt** — Notify IAB Command Center in addition to standard notifications
12. **Prisoner DOA or seriously injured by police action** — UMOS: request PS response, safeguard scene, obtain medical attention; PS comply with PG 221-05

### Prisoner DOA or Seriously Injured by Police Action / Police Firearms Discharge

| Role | Duty |
|------|------|
| UMOS | Immediately request response of Patrol Supervisor; safeguard crime scene; obtain medical attention |
| Patrol Supervisor | Respond and comply with **PG 221-05** |

## Sergeant Study Notes

- The sergeant must **personally inspect** prisoners in holding pens — this is not delegable to a police officer
- Document prisoner **condition** (sleeping, alert) and **position** (sitting, lying down) during each inspection
- The **3-hour maximum** is the standard; extensions are the exception and require both supervisory approval AND Command Log documentation
- **Ill, intoxicated, and emotionally disturbed** prisoners are categorically excluded from holding pens — immediate hospital removal is required
- The **6 items** removed for suicide/assault prevention are frequently the subject of exam questions — know the complete list
- **Juveniles** must be placed in a designated juvenile area — mere separation from adults in the same holding area is insufficient
- Prisoner holding pen management is a high-liability area — thorough documentation of inspections protects both the prisoner and the Department
- Any unusual occurrence (injury, illness, escape, death, altercation) requires immediate notification of the desk officer and patrol supervisor

---

# Section 210 — Medical/Psychiatric Treatment, Bedside Arraignment, Prisoner Meals, and Unusual Occurrences

Covers P.G. 210-04, 210-05, 210-06, 210-07

---

## P.G. 210-04: Prisoners Requiring Medical/Psychiatric Treatment

> **Updated:** 01/14/26 | R.O. 8 | 10 pages

### When to Remove a Prisoner to the Hospital

A prisoner must be removed to the hospital when any of the following conditions exist:

| Condition | Action |
|-----------|--------|
| Prisoner is ill | Remove to hospital |
| Prisoner is injured | Remove to hospital |
| Prisoner is intoxicated | Remove to hospital |
| Prisoner is emotionally disturbed | Remove to hospital |
| Prisoner requests medical attention | Remove to hospital |

> **Exam Alert:** Ill, intoxicated, and emotionally disturbed prisoners are NOT to be placed in holding pens (see P.G. 210-08). They must be removed to a hospital for evaluation and treatment.

### Which Hospital?

| Situation | Hospital |
|-----------|----------|
| Non-emergency | **Local** hospital |
| Life-threatening | **Nearest** hospital |
| Narcotics/dangerous substance ingestion | Go **DIRECTLY** to **nearest** hospital (NEVER to stationhouse) |

> **Exam Alert:** A perp who ingests narcotics or a dangerous substance goes directly to the **nearest** hospital — NEVER to the stationhouse first. The exam may present a scenario asking whether to process the arrest first; the answer is always go directly to the hospital.

### Narcotics Ingestion — Triple Notification

When a prisoner ingests narcotics or a dangerous substance, notify **Patrol Supervisor AND Communications Section** three times:

| Notification | When |
|-------------|------|
| 1st | Upon observing or suspecting ingestion |
| 2nd | When transport to hospital is initiated |
| 3rd | When 84 (arrived) at hospital |

Additionally, inform **EMS and hospital personnel** of the type and quantity of drugs ingested.

### UMOS — Initial Medical Treatment Duties

| Duty | Details |
|------|---------|
| Request ambulance | Accompany prisoner to hospital from place of arrest if necessary |
| Notify | Desk Officer |
| Medical Treatment of Prisoner Form | Prepare when prisoner: receives treatment, refuses treatment after claiming injury/illness, has apparent need for treatment, has previously treated injuries, has prescribed medication, has communicable disease status |

> **Exam Alert:** A prisoner who **volunteers** their positive communicable disease status triggers MTP preparation **only** if not already on the Prisoner Movement Slip.

### Spit Hood Procedures (NEW in R.O. 8)

| Role | Duty |
|------|------|
| **ESU Member** | Apply spit hood if prisoner is spitting at or threatening to spit at any person |
| ESU Member | Monitor and remain with subject wearing spit hood until relieved by a medical professional with minimum EMT certification, unless hood is removed |
| ESU Member | If disagreement between EMS and ESU about application/removal, request **ESU Supervisor** to respond |
| **ESU Supervisor** | Confer with EMS supervisor to resolve disagreement about spit hood application or removal |

> **Exam Alert:** Only **ESU personnel** may apply a spit hood — NOT regular MOS. If there is a disagreement between EMS and ESU about applying or removing the spit hood, the **ESU Supervisor** resolves it by conferring with the EMS supervisor.

### MTP Triggers — When to Prepare

Prepare **MEDICAL TREATMENT OF PRISONER (PD244-150)** for each prisoner who:

| Trigger |
|---------|
| a. Receives medical/psychiatric treatment |
| b. Refuses treatment after claiming injury or illness |
| c. Is in apparent need of treatment |
| d. May require prescribed medication |
| e. Volunteers communicable disease status (if not on Prisoner Movement Slip) |
| f. Already has previously treated injuries |
| g. **Has a spit hood placed on them** (NEW) |

### Medical Treatment of Prisoner Form (MTP) — Key Details

| Detail | Rule |
|--------|------|
| Reserve function | Enter info from Section I of MTP into MTP app in FORMS by selecting the "Reserve" function; number generated by FORMS entered on the MTP |
| Copy of MTP | **MUST** accompany prisoner until no longer in NYPD custody |
| HHC entitled to copy | Allow hospital personnel to make a **copy** of the MTP upon completion of treatment |
| Signing | If perp, doctor, or hospital staff refuses to sign, note on MTP and in Digital Activity Log |
| MTP copies to DOC | Forward **three copies** (white, blue, pink) with prisoner to DOC |
| Buff copy retained | BCS retains **fourth (buff) copy** at Borough Court Section facility |

### Section I of MTP — Transport Numbers

| Transport Method | Number Recorded |
|-----------------|----------------|
| Taken by **EMS** (ambulance) | **ACR** # (Ambulance Call Report) |
| Taken by **other means** (RMP, etc.) | **PCR** # (Patient Care Report) |

> **Memory Aid — ACR vs PCR:** **A** = Ambulance (ACR), **P** = Police/other (PCR). The ACR number comes from the ambulance attendant. The PCR number also comes from the ambulance attendant (received from them).

### Remarks Section of MTP

| Content | Notes |
|---------|-------|
| Specific treatment instructions from EMS | — |
| Whether EMS took perp to hospital room for further treatment | — |
| **RMA** | Check-box for RMA; does **NOT** go in remarks |

### Supervisor at Scene

| Duty | Details |
|------|---------|
| If perp removed from scene direct to hospital | Ensure MTP is prepared per steps above and ADDITIONAL DATA (Preparation of MTP) |
| Review and sign | **Section I** of MTP |

### Desk Officer — Prisoner Needs Medical Treatment During Arrest Process

| Duty | Details |
|------|---------|
| Medical/drug/psych treatment needed | Call ambulance and have prisoner taken to hospital |
| Command Log entry | ACR or PCR # AND if Medic Alert Emblem worn, name of whom notified |
| 2nd Command Log entry | When prisoner leaves the stationhouse |
| MTP | Ensure prepared; review and sign Section I |
| Escort | Assign UMOS to escort prisoner; direct A/O to continue arrest process when possible |
| Relief | Ensure appropriate relief for guarding officer |

> **See Also:** PG 210-01 — Release of confidential status information (communicable disease): Only **DCLM** (Deputy Commissioner, Legal Matters) can authorize release. Isolate prisoner if isolated cell is available.

### Arresting/Escorting Officer — Transport to Hospital

| Step | Duty |
|------|------|
| 15 | **Rear cuff** AND place **leg restraints** before transporting to hospital |
| 15a | Handcuffs and leg restraints will be **DOUBLE LOCKED** |
| 16 | **Remain with prisoner at all times** in hospital |
| 17 | Notify D.O. of prisoner status **prior to transport** from local hospital to Bellevue/Elmhurst |
| 18 | Ride **inside the ambulance** when accompanying prisoner to Bellevue or Elmhurst |
| 19 | Make digital **Activity Log** entry: (a) Medic Alert Emblem if worn, (b) **PIN** (Patient Identification Number) — in place of ACR/PCR |
| 20 | Notify D.O. precinct of arrest/BCS re: Medical Alert Emblem and PIN |

### Prisoners Requiring Psychiatric Admission

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| Notify D.O. of psych status | Before leaving local hospital for Bellevue/Elmhurst |
| Transport | Ride in **(private) ambulance** with prisoner to Bellevue/Elmhurst |
| Digital Activity Log | Enter **PIN** # (Patient Identification Number) obtained from hospital intake |

> **Exam Alert:** The **PIN** (Patient Identification Number) is different from the ACR or PCR number. The PIN is obtained from hospital intake people — NOT from EMS. The exam tests: ACR = initial EMS transport, PCR = initial RMP/other transport, PIN = secondary transport to Bellevue/Elmhurst.

### Psych Admission — Where Transported?

| Gender | Hospital |
|--------|----------|
| **Men** | **Bellevue** |
| **Women** | **Elmhurst General** |

> **Exam Alert:** Generally, men go to **Bellevue** and women go to **Elmhurst** for psychiatric admission. Transport is done by hospital via **private contract** — NOT FDNY EMS.

### Psych Admission — D.O. Pct of Arrest/Boro Court Section

| Duty | Details |
|------|---------|
| Confer with medical staff | Medical staff determines appropriate transfer method and hospital |
| Determine additional resources | D.O. determines if additional resources needed for the transfer |
| Additional resources decision | **Patrol Supervisor** decides if extra personnel or equipment (like ESU) are needed |

### Desk Officer — Psych Treatment Documentation

| Duty | Details |
|------|---------|
| Command Log entry | **PIN** # (NOT ACR or PCR), Medic Alert information, new location of prisoner |

> **Exam Alert:** For psych transfers, the Desk Officer logs the **PIN number** in the Command Log — NOT the ACR or PCR. The exam may present choices mixing up these numbers.

### Escorting Officer at Hospital

| Step | Duty |
|------|------|
| 23 | Request **room change** if security is inadequate (Escorting officer does this — NOT Patrol Supervisor!) |
| 24 | Do NOT remove handcuffs or leg restraints unless requested by attending physician |
| 24a | If physician requests removal, **inform physician of circumstances of arrest** |
| 24b | If physician **still** requests removal, request response of **patrol supervisor** to evaluate need for additional personnel and equipment (including ESU) to ensure safety during treatment prior to removal |
| 25 | If asked to leave exam room: remain **immediately outside room**, attempt to maintain **visual contact** |
| 26 | Make digital Activity Log entries; request attending physician to sign entries |
| 26a | If physician refuses to sign, indicate refusal on Activity Log |
| 27 | Complete entries on **MTP** form |
| 28 | Notify D.O. precinct of arrest (who notifies BCS immediately) if prisoner admitted to any hospital |

> **Prior Test:** Doctor asks the guarding officer to remove cuffs — what is the correct response? Explain circumstances of arrest. If doctor still wants them off, request **Patrol Supervisor** to respond. Do NOT remove without PS present and authorizing. BUT if doctor asks you to **leave the room**, you do NOT need the PS — just step outside and maintain visual contact.

> **Exam Alert:** There is a critical distinction between a doctor asking to **remove restraints** (requires PS response and authorization) versus asking the officer to **leave the examination room** (officer complies, steps outside, maintains visual contact — no PS needed). The exam frequently tests this difference.

### DOC Taking Custody — Psych Admission

| Step | Role | Duty |
|------|------|------|
| 29 | D.O. Command of Arrest | Inquire with DOC prison ward whether prisoner can be lodged; request DOC take custody |
| 29a | D.O. | Make entry in Telephone Record |
| 29b | D.O. | Notify escorting officer of results of inquiry with DOC |
| 30 | Escorting Officer | Search prisoner per P.G. 208-05 for weapons, evidence, contraband **regardless of prior search** |
| 30a | Escorting Officer | Make digital Activity Log entry indicating results of search |
| 31 | Escorting Officer | Forward copy of **OLBS ARREST WORKSHEET (PD244-159)**, **MTP**, Prisoner Movement Slip, and physical copy of active warrant(s) to DOC Supervisor |
| 31 (warrant only) | Escorting Officer | If prisoner has active warrant only and no arrest number, forward MTP and physical copy of active warrant(s) only |
| 31a | Escorting Officer | If DOC **refuses** custody, notify D.O. command of arrest |

> **Exam Alert:** If DOC refuses to take custody of prisoner, the **D.O. command of arrest** should consult with a supervisor from the appropriate **Borough Court Section**. The BCS supervisor will resolve custody issues with the **DOC Custody Management Unit**.

### When Prisoner Released Back from DOC

| Step | Role | Duty |
|------|------|------|
| 32 | BCS | Immediately notify D.O. precinct of arrest when DOC releases prisoner to police custody |
| 33 | D.O. Command of Arrest | Make Telephone Record entry (identity, location, arrest/warrant info); arrange EO to respond within **4 hours** |
| 34 | D.O. Command of Arrest | Arrange for EO to return to DOC prison ward within **4 hours** of receiving notification from BCS |

> **Exam Alert:** The command **MUST** take custody within **4 hours** from notification by DOC. If unable, a notification must be made to the **Borough Court Section supervisor**, who will coordinate with DOC Custody Management Unit.

### Escorting Officer — Prisoner Released from DOC

| Step | Duty |
|------|------|
| 35 | Transport prisoner: (a) Return to command of arrest if processing not completed; (b) Remove to BCS if processing completed |
| 35b(1) | **Confer with BCS Supervisor** before transporting wheelchair/mobility-impaired prisoner |
| 36 | Deliver completed **MTP** to D.O., Borough Court Section facility |

### D.O. Borough Court Section — MTP Distribution

| Step | Duty |
|------|------|
| 37 | Forward **three copies** of MTP (white, blue, pink) with prisoner to DOC |
| 37a | Retain **fourth (buff) copy** at BCS facility |
| 38 | Direct A/O-EO to obtain receipt for prisoner on **pink copy** and return to BCS |
| 38a-d | Complete entries on buff copy; return buff to A/O-EO; file pink at BCS; forward duplicate MTP to A/O command of arrest if different |

### D.O. Command of Arrest — After DOC Release

| Step | Duty |
|------|------|
| 39 | Escorting Officer delivers buff copy MTP to D.O. command of arrest |
| 40 | Ensure all data from buff MTP entered into **FORMS** "Medical Treatment of Prisoner" application |
| 41 | **Finalize ALL pending submissions** within FORMS |

### Operations Coordinator, Command of Arrest

| Step | Duty |
|------|------|
| 42 | Ensure all **MTPs finalized** within FORMS |
| 43 | **FILE** the buff copy Medical Treatment of Prisoner Form |

### Prescription Drugs — Medication at Time of Arrest

| Duty | Details |
|------|---------|
| Invoice | Medication invoiced per P.G. 208-03, "Arrests - General Processing" |
| MTP | Ensure all appropriate captions on MTP are filled out; pharmacy label info (name, address, phone of pharmacy/physician) recorded in Remarks section |

### Pre-Arraignment Prisoners Requiring Prescription Medications

| Step | Duty |
|------|------|
| Hospital physician needs info | Provide **PROPERTY CLERK INVOICE (PD521-141)** number for invoiced medication |
| Doctor requests medication delivery | D.O. shall: (a) Check medication name against Property Clerk Invoice, (b) Complete "R.T.O." function in FORMS Property, sign rank and name, include pertinent details in Remarks, (c) Release medication to UMOS for delivery, (d) Make Command Log entry |
| UMOS delivering medication | Request physician to **sign** digital Activity Log; if refuses, note refusal with full title and name |
| Medication returned to command | Will be "**INVOICED AGAIN**" per procedure |

### Doctor Gives Prisoner Medicine To Go

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| Take custody of meds | **No voucher** needed |
| Give meds to | **Nurse Practitioner (NP)** at BCS until prisoner needs to self-administer |
| NP not authorized to administer | If NP cannot administer that form of medication, prisoner must be brought to hospital |

> **Exam Alert:** When a doctor provides medicine for a prisoner to take, the medication goes to the **Nurse Practitioner at BCS** — no voucher is needed. But if the NP is not authorized to administer that specific form of medication, the prisoner must go back to the hospital.

### Medication Brought to Precinct by Family/Household Member

| Rule | Details |
|------|---------|
| Acceptance | UMOS are **NOT required to accept** medication from relatives/household members |
| Invoicing | UMOS are **NOT required to invoice** prisoner medications brought to command by relatives/household members |
| Conditions for MTP | Prepare MTP when properly identified relative appears at Dept facility where prisoner is located AND: (a) indicates prisoner has medical condition/needs medication, AND/OR (b) brings medication in pharmacy container with prisoner's name |

> **Exam Alert:** UMOS are **NOT required to accept** AND are **NOT required to invoice** medication from family. Both points are testable — the updated procedure now explicitly states both.

### Prisoner Not at Facility Where Family Brought Medication

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| UMOS | Determine if prisoner still in Dept custody; ascertain location; inform supervisor |
| Supervisor notified | Contact D.O./supervisor at facility where prisoner located — **DUAL Command Log entry** (both sides make entries) |
| Command Log entries include | Time of notification, prisoner's name, name of desk officer/supervisor receiving/making notification |
| D.O./Supervisor where prisoner located | Prepare Medical Treatment of Prisoner Form |

### Prisoner Security — Additional Personnel for Hospital Transport

Patrol supervisors and desk officers shall assign additional personnel to accompany arresting officers based on:

| Factor |
|--------|
| a. Type of offense (violent crime, resisting arrest, etc.) |
| b. Physical **size** of prisoner |
| c. Prisoner's **past history** (history of violence, escape attempts) |
| d. Suspected **escape opportunity** |
| e. **Lack of physical signs of injury** (may be faking injury to attempt escape) |

> **Exam Alert:** One of the factors for assigning additional personnel is **lack of physical signs of injury** — meaning the prisoner might be faking to create an escape opportunity. This is a counterintuitive factor that the exam may test.

### Prisoner on Hospital Bed or Gurney

| Rule | Detail |
|------|--------|
| Handcuff to bed/gurney | **Double locked** to the bed or gurney |
| Second pair of cuffs | Use second pair to handcuff prisoner's free wrist to bed/gurney **before** removing first pair for treatment |
| Constant observation | Keep prisoner under constant observation even when handcuffed to bed/gurney |

### Long Chain Handcuffs and Leg Restraints

Patrol supervisors and desk officers shall direct that **long chain handcuffs and leg restraints** be used if available and appropriate to secure prisoners.

### ESU Consideration

| Rule | Detail |
|------|--------|
| ESU | Supervisors may consider the assignment of ESU for **extraordinary circumstances** |

### Leg Restraints — Mandatory Use

| Situation | Leg Restraints Required? |
|-----------|------------------------|
| ALL prisoners transported to hospital for medical treatment | **YES — MUST** be used |
| Prisoner refused to be fingerprinted | **YES — MUST** be used |
| Potential escape risk | YES |
| Punitive purposes | **NO — PROHIBITED** |

> **Exam Alert:** Leg restraints **MUST** be used for ALL prisoners transported to a hospital for medical treatment AND for prisoners who refused fingerprinting. If D.O. determines extenuating circumstances preclude placing leg restraints, a **Command Log entry** must be made detailing the reasons.

> **Sergeant Focus:** When a prisoner requires medical treatment, the patrol supervisor must be notified. The sergeant should verify that the desk officer has properly documented the condition, arranged transport, and assigned a guard. Prisoners with medical conditions cannot simply be held in the cellblock. The supervisor must also evaluate the need for additional personnel, ESU, and ensure leg restraints are applied for hospital transport.

---

## P.G. 210-05: Bedside Arraignment

### Purpose

Reduce manpower required to guard hospitalized prisoners by expediting the arraignment process at the hospital.

### Mobile Livescan Device (MLD)

| Rule | Detail |
|------|--------|
| What it is | Portable fingerprint scanner that transmits via cellular to DCJS |
| Staffing requirement | At least **2 MOS present** during MLD fingerprinting |
| Prohibited use | MLD may NOT be used for any purpose other than hospitalized prisoner fingerprints |

> **Exam Alert:** The MLD requires a minimum of **2 MOS present** during fingerprinting. It is strictly limited to hospitalized prisoner fingerprints — using it for any other purpose is prohibited.

### Bedside Arraignment Procedures

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| Legal assistance | Guarding member asks prisoner about need for legal assistance and/or interpreter |
| Fingerprinting | Use MLD with 2 MOS present |
| Delay notification | If bedside arraignment delayed **more than 6 days**, notify borough court section |
| Bail posting | Bail is posted at **borough criminal court**, NOT at the stationhouse |

> **Exam Alert:** Bail for a hospitalized prisoner is posted at the **borough criminal court** — never at the stationhouse. The 6-day notification threshold is also frequently tested.

### Prisoner Released from Hospital Before Arraignment

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| Medical release | Guarding member obtains **signed medical release** from hospital |
| Restraint | Escort prisoner **rear cuffed** to court |
| Transport | Standard prisoner transport procedures apply |

> **Sergeant Focus:** If a hospitalized prisoner is discharged before arraignment, the guarding member must obtain a signed medical release and escort the prisoner to court in rear cuffs. The sergeant should verify this process is followed — prisoners should not be released from the hospital without proper documentation and escort.

---

## P.G. 210-06: Prisoner Meals

### Meal Schedule and Cost

| Detail | Rule |
|--------|------|
| **Meal Period 1** | 0500-0600 |
| **Meal Period 2** | 1200-1300 |
| **Meal Period 3** | 1800-1900 |
| **Maximum cost** | **$1.80 per meal per prisoner** |
| **Prohibited items** | NO hot liquids (coffee, tea, hot chocolate) |

> **Exam Alert:** The $1.80 maximum meal cost and the prohibition on hot liquids are high-frequency exam items. Know all three meal periods and the exact dollar amount.

### Additional Meal Authorization

| Situation | Authority |
|-----------|-----------|
| Medical condition requiring additional meals | **Desk officer** authorizes |
| Unusual conditions (extended processing, etc.) | **Desk officer** authorizes |
| Prisoner missed meal period due to processing | **Desk officer** authorizes meal on request |

> **Sergeant Focus:** The desk officer has authority to authorize additional meals beyond the standard three periods. This includes situations where a prisoner missed a scheduled meal due to processing activities. Sergeants should ensure meal requests are handled promptly — denying food to a prisoner who missed a meal period is not acceptable.

### Expense Reporting

| Detail | Rule |
|--------|------|
| Form | Expense Report |
| Submission deadline | By the **15th of each month** |

---

## P.G. 210-07: Prisoners — Unusual Occurrence

> **Updated:** 10/27/25 | R.O. 112 | 2 pages

### Purpose

To make notifications and investigate certain unusual occurrences related to prisoners.

### Definition — Unusual Occurrence

A prisoner in NYPD custody, or an unarraigned prisoner in NYC DOC custody, who:

| Type |
|------|
| a. **Assaults** a member of the service |
| b. **Escapes** or attempts to escape |
| c. Is **sexually assaulted/harassed** or alleges they were sexually assaulted/harassed by another prisoner or any other person |
| d. Is involved in any other occurrence of an **unusual nature** |

### Desk Officer

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 1 | Notify **commanding officer/duty captain** AND **borough court section supervisor** concerned |
| 2 | Notify **Operations Unit**, including: (a) Name and sex of prisoner/child in custody, (b) Charge for which prisoner/child is held |
| 3 | Notify **Internal Affairs Bureau Command Center** if prisoner **escapes or attempts to escape** |

> **Exam Alert:** IAB is notified ONLY for escape/attempted escape — NOT for all unusual occurrences. The D.O. notifies CO/Duty Captain + BCS Supervisor + Operations Unit for ALL unusual occurrences, but IAB only for escapes.

### Commanding Officer / Duty Captain

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 4 | Conduct **investigation** of incident |
| 5 | Prepare report on **Typed Letterhead** |
| 6 | Forward **three copies** of report to **Chief of Department, DIRECT** (adult prisoners); **two copies** if child in custody dies |
| 7 | Forward **one additional copy** to EACH of the following: |

| Report Distribution |
|---------------------|
| a. Chief of Patrol (DIRECT) |
| b. Chief of Internal Affairs |
| c. Chief of Training |
| d. Commanding Officer, Criminal Justice Bureau |
| e. Commanding Officer, Performance Monitoring Unit |
| f. Each intermediate command |
| g. CO of member's command (if member not assigned to reporting command) |

> **Exam Alert:** The report goes to **Chief of Department DIRECT** (3 copies) — not through channels. One additional copy goes to each of 7 recipients listed above.

### Prisoner Escape

In the event of a prisoner escape, arresting/escorting officers will **immediately**:

| Action |
|--------|
| Notify **radio dispatcher** |
| Notify **immediate supervisor** (e.g., desk officer of member's command) |
| Conduct **search** to apprehend the prisoner |

> **Sergeant Focus:** For unusual occurrences, the CO/Duty Captain must personally investigate and prepare typed letterhead reports. The distribution is extensive — 3 copies DIRECT to Chief of Dept, plus 7 additional individual copies. For escapes, IAB must be notified in addition to Operations Unit.

### Notes on Related Procedures

| If... | Then comply with... |
|-------|-------------------|
| Prisoner sustains physical injury or attempts suicide | P.G. 221-03, "Reporting and Investigation of Force Incident or Injury to Persons During Police Action" |
| Prisoner dies or is likely to die | P.G. 221-05, "Person Dies or Sustains a Serious Injury and Is Likely to Die in Police Custody or in Connection With Police Action" |

---

## Common Exam Angles

1. **$1.80 per meal** — Know the exact dollar figure
2. **No hot liquids** — Coffee, tea, and hot chocolate are all prohibited
3. **Three meal periods** — 0500-0600, 1200-1300, 1800-1900
4. **MLD requirements** — 2 MOS present, hospitalized prisoners ONLY
5. **6-day delay notification** — Notify borough court section if bedside arraignment delayed more than 6 days
6. **Bail location** — Borough criminal court, NOT the stationhouse
7. **Desk officer meal authority** — Can authorize additional meals for medical/unusual conditions or missed meal periods
8. **Expense report** — Due by the 15th of each month
9. **ACR vs PCR vs PIN** — ACR = ambulance transport, PCR = RMP/other transport, PIN = secondary transport to psych facility
10. **Which hospital** — Non-emergency = local; life-threatening = nearest; narcotics ingestion = DIRECTLY to nearest (NEVER stationhouse)
11. **Psych admission destination** — Men to Bellevue, Women to Elmhurst; transport by private ambulance (not FDNY EMS)
12. **Cuffs off vs. leave room** — Doctor asks to remove restraints = request PS response (evaluate additional personnel/ESU); doctor asks to leave room = comply, step outside, maintain visual contact (no PS needed)
13. **UMOS not required to accept OR invoice family medication** — Both acceptance and invoicing are discretionary (R.O. 8 explicitly states both)
14. **Medicine to go from doctor** — Goes to Nurse Practitioner at BCS; no voucher; if NP cannot administer, prisoner goes to hospital
15. **DOC custody** — D.O. Command of Arrest requests DOC take custody; if refused, BCS Supervisor resolves with DOC Custody Management Unit
16. **4-hour deadline** — MUST take custody within 4 hours from DOC notification; if unable, notify BCS Supervisor
17. **Lack of physical signs of injury** — Factor for additional personnel (prisoner may be faking)
18. **Triple notification for narcotics ingestion** — PS and Communications Section notified 3 times (observation, transport initiated, arrived at hospital)
19. **Spit hood — ESU only** — Only ESU personnel may apply a spit hood; ESU Supervisor resolves disagreements with EMS
20. **Leg restraints mandatory for hospital transport** — ALL prisoners transported to hospital MUST have leg restraints; also required for refusal to be fingerprinted
21. **MTP copies** — 3 copies (white, blue, pink) to DOC; buff copy retained at BCS; MTP MUST accompany prisoner at all times
22. **Operations Coordinator** — Responsible for ensuring all MTPs are finalized in FORMS and filing buff copy
23. **Unusual Occurrence — IAB notification** — IAB notified ONLY for escape/attempted escape; Operations Unit notified for ALL unusual occurrences
24. **Unusual Occurrence report distribution** — 3 copies DIRECT to Chief of Dept, plus 1 copy each to 7 additional recipients

## Sergeant Study Notes

- The **desk officer** controls meal authorization — both standard meals and additional meals for medical or unusual conditions
- Expense reports for prisoner meals must be submitted by the **15th of each month** — track this deadline
- The **MLD** is a restricted-use device: hospitalized prisoner fingerprints only, with 2 MOS present
- When bedside arraignment is delayed more than **6 days**, the borough court section must be notified — the sergeant should monitor this timeline
- Prisoners released from the hospital before arraignment must be escorted **rear cuffed** to court with a **signed medical release**
- Ill, intoxicated, and emotionally disturbed prisoners must be removed to hospital — they cannot be held in standard cellblock or holding pen conditions
- The patrol supervisor must be notified whenever a prisoner requires medical treatment
- Know the **three transport numbers**: ACR (ambulance), PCR (RMP/other), PIN (secondary psych transport) — each has a different source and context
- The **escorting officer** requests room changes when hospital security is inadequate — NOT the Patrol Supervisor
- Removing restraints at a doctor's request: PS evaluates need for **additional personnel and equipment (including ESU)** to ensure safety prior to removal — but leaving the exam room does NOT require a PS
- For narcotics ingestion, the **triple notification** to PS and Communications is a high-frequency exam item
- **Medication returned to command** after delivery will be "invoiced again" — know this procedural detail
- When DOC refuses custody, the BCS Supervisor resolves the issue with DOC's Custody Management Unit
- **Spit hoods** are applied ONLY by ESU; ESU Supervisor resolves any EMS disagreement — this is a new testable procedure
- **Leg restraints are mandatory** for ALL hospital transport and for prisoners who refused fingerprinting — extenuating circumstances require Command Log entry
- The **Operations Coordinator** is responsible for ensuring all MTPs are finalized within FORMS — a sergeant-level responsibility
- For **unusual occurrences**, IAB is notified only for escape/attempt — the report goes DIRECT to Chief of Dept (3 copies)
- **Prisoner on hospital bed/gurney**: use second pair of cuffs to secure free wrist to bed BEFORE removing first pair for treatment; double lock; constant observation even when cuffed
- MTP must accompany prisoner at ALL times until no longer in NYPD custody — HHC is entitled to a photocopy

---

# Section 210 — Release of Prisoners

Covers P.G. 210-13, 210-15, 210-16, 210-17

---

## P.G. 210-13: Release of Prisoners

### Two Types of Release

| Type | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **Voided Arrest** | Reasonable cause to believe prisoner did NOT commit the offense, or that no offense was committed | P.G. 210-13 |
| **Decline Prosecution** | DA exercises discretion not to prosecute; requires a **Decline Prosecution Letter** from an ADA | P.G. 210-13 |

> **Exam Alert:** A voided arrest and a decline prosecution are fundamentally different. A **voided arrest** means the arrest itself was improper (wrong person or no crime). A **decline prosecution** means the arrest was valid but the DA chooses not to prosecute. Know the distinction — exam questions frequently test this.

### Supervisor Conferral Requirement

| Step | Requirement |
|------|-------------|
| Primary authority | Arresting officer must confer with **patrol supervisor** for consent to release |
| If unavailable | Confer with **desk officer** or **BCS supervisor** |

> **Sergeant Focus:** The patrol supervisor is the first-line authority for consent to release a prisoner. This conferral is mandatory — the arresting officer cannot unilaterally void an arrest or release a prisoner. If the patrol supervisor is unavailable, the desk officer or BCS supervisor serves as the alternate authority.

### Release Timing

Release may occur at any time from **initial custody** until **arraignment**.

### Notification Requirements — Voided Arrest

After a voided arrest, the following must be notified:

| Notify | Purpose |
|--------|---------|
| **Desk officer of command of occurrence** | Must be informed in ALL cases |
| **Patrol borough commander** | Notification required |
| **Identification Unit** | Notification required |
| **Borough Court Section (BCS)** | Notification required |
| **Commanding officer of arresting officer** | Notification required |

### Voided Arrest Documentation

| Requirement | Detail |
|-------------|--------|
| Omniform System | Void arrest through Omniform — note: this **still generates an arrest number** |
| OLBS ARREST WORKSHEET | Must include: reason for void, property returned, witnesses, authorizing supervisor, time of release |

> **Exam Alert:** Even a voided arrest generates an arrest number through the Omniform System. The OLBS Arrest Worksheet must document 5 key elements: (1) reason for void, (2) property returned, (3) witnesses, (4) authorizing supervisor, and (5) time of release.

### Sealing Fingerprint Records

| Situation | Action |
|-----------|--------|
| Voided arrest + prisoner was fingerprinted | **BCS intake supervisor** faxes DCJS to seal the record |

> **Sergeant Focus:** When a voided arrest involves a fingerprinted prisoner, the BCS intake supervisor must fax DCJS to seal the record. Sergeants should verify this step is completed — failure to seal creates a permanent record from an invalid arrest.

### When ADA Requests a Void (Not Decline Prosecution)

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 1 | Desk officer confers with **Legal Bureau** |
| 2 | Legal Bureau confers with **ADA** |
| 3 | Legal Bureau attempts to **resolve** the issue |

> **Exam Alert:** When an ADA requests that an arrest be voided (as opposed to simply declining prosecution), the desk officer does NOT void the arrest directly. The desk officer contacts the **Legal Bureau**, which then confers with the ADA and attempts to resolve the matter. The authority to release rests solely with the **Police Department**.

### Authority to Release

| Rule | Detail |
|------|--------|
| **Authority** | Rests **solely with the Police Department** |
| ADA role | DA has discretion to decline prosecution but does NOT have authority to direct release |

---

## P.G. 210-15: Release of Prisoners Arrested by Other Agencies

### Conflict Resolution

| Situation | Resolution |
|-----------|-----------|
| Conflict between desk officer/BCS supervisor and arresting officer | **Precinct commander** or **duty captain** makes the final decision |

> **Sergeant Focus:** When there is a disagreement between the desk officer or BCS supervisor and an arresting officer from another agency regarding prisoner release, the matter is escalated to the precinct commander or duty captain. Sergeants should not attempt to resolve inter-agency disputes at their level — escalate promptly.

---

## P.G. 210-16: Release at Complaint Room by Direction of ADA

### Decline Prosecution Process

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| ADA designation | ADA designates case as "Decline Prosecution" |
| Documentation | Arresting officer obtains **Decline Prosecution Letter** from ADA |
| Warrant check | Desk officer conducts **warrant check** before release |
| Release | Prisoner released after warrant check is clear |

### Key Rules

| Rule | Detail |
|------|--------|
| Rapsheet delay | Release is **NOT delayed** solely because rapsheet has not been received |
| Active warrant found | Process prisoner **solely on warrant authority** (NOT on original charges) |
| Hospitalized prisoner | Desk officer directs release after confirming **no active warrant** |

> **Exam Alert:** If a prisoner is being released on a decline prosecution and a warrant check reveals an active warrant, the prisoner is processed **solely on the warrant** — the original charges are dropped per the ADA's decline. The prisoner is NOT processed on both.

> **Exam Alert:** Release cannot be delayed just because the rapsheet has not arrived. If the ADA has issued a Decline Prosecution Letter and the warrant check is clear, the prisoner is released.

---

## P.G. 210-17: Special Category Prisoners

> **Updated:** 07/02/25 | R.O. 56 | 3 pages

### Purpose

To ensure that all pre-arraignment prisoners in custody at precinct, transit district, police service area, and borough court section holding pens are medically screened by Emergency Medical Service (EMS) personnel located at the borough court sections.

### Scope

EMS personnel are assigned to all patrol borough court sections **with the exception of the Staten Island Court Section**. On Staten Island, the supervisor at the court section (not EMS) makes the determination as to whether a prisoner will be designated as Special Category.

### Definition — Special Category Prisoner

A prisoner who should be removed from the general population for any of the following reasons:

| Reason | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| a. Medical condition or physical disability | Prisoner has a condition requiring separation |
| b. Safety risk | Placing prisoner in general population may pose a **safety risk** to that prisoner or other prisoners |
| c. Health risk | Placing prisoner in general population may pose a **health risk** to that prisoner or other prisoners |

> **Exam Alert:** Know the 3 reasons for Special Category designation: (1) medical/physical disability, (2) safety risk, (3) health risk. The exam tests the distinction between safety and health risks — they are separate categories.

### Exclusions — NOT Special Category

| Exclusion | Reference |
|-----------|-----------|
| a. Hospitalized prisoners | Refer to P.G. 210-02, "Hospitalized Prisoners" |
| b. Prisoners issued DATs or summonses and released directly from precinct/transit district/PSA | Not designated Special Category |

> **Exam Alert:** Hospitalized prisoners and those released directly with DATs/summonses are specifically **excluded** from the Special Category designation. A common exam trap is presenting a hospitalized prisoner as a Special Category — they are handled under a different procedure.

### Procedure — Upon Transporting to Borough Court Section

| Step | Role | Action |
|------|------|--------|
| 1 | UMOS | Ensure prisoner is **medically screened by EMS** as soon as possible |
| 2 | BCS Supervisor | Determine, **in conjunction with EMS personnel**, whether prisoner should be designated "Special Category Prisoner" |

### Guidelines for Handling Special Category Prisoners

| Step | Role | Action |
|------|------|--------|
| 3 | BCS Supervisor | Ensure notation in **"Detention Alert"** caption of Prisoner Movement Slip (reason: medical, safety risk, disability, etc.) |
| 4 | BCS Supervisor | Direct Special Category Prisoner be lodged in a **separate cell** from general pre-arraignment population |
| 5 | BCS Supervisor | Ascertain, in consultation with EMS, whether it is advisable to house **more than one** Special Category Prisoner in a cell |
| 6 | BCS Supervisor | Have **MEDICAL TREATMENT OF PRISONER (PD244-150)** form prepared for **each** Special Category Prisoner |
| 7 | BCS Supervisor | Ensure "Yes" box in caption **"Recommend Prisoner Be Separated From General Population"** on MTP form is checked |
| 8 | BCS Supervisor | Ensure all Special Category Prisoners originally screened by EMS at BCS are **subsequently screened** upon returning from any hospital, as applicable |
| 9 | BCS Supervisor | **Oversee and expedite** the processing of all Special Category Prisoners and their co-defendants, if any |
| 10 | BCS Supervisor | Ensure prisoners lodged in separate cells at precinct, district, or PSA commands are **transported to the appropriate arraignment part** at court, when necessary |

> **Exam Alert:** The BCS Supervisor has 8 specific duties (steps 3-10) for handling Special Category Prisoners. Know especially: (1) MTP prepared for EACH Special Category Prisoner, (2) "Recommend Prisoner Be Separated" box must be checked on MTP, (3) re-screening by EMS after hospital return, (4) expedited processing.

> **Sergeant Focus:** The BCS Supervisor is the key decision-maker for Special Category designation, working in conjunction with EMS. The sergeant must ensure all 8 handling steps are completed, especially the MTP preparation and the "Recommend Prisoner Be Separated" checkbox.

### Lodging Special Category Prisoners

| Rule | Detail |
|------|--------|
| Preference | Lodge in **separate cells** at BCS |
| If not possible | BCS Supervisor directs temporary lodging in separate cell at precinct, district, or PSA |
| Multi-prisoner cells | Decision based on whether prisoners pose safety or health risk **to one another** |
| Avoid handcuffing | Make **every reasonable effort** to avoid handcuffing prisoners to chairs, railings, etc. for extended periods |

> **Exam Alert:** It is **preferable** to lodge Special Category Prisoners in separate cells at BCS, but if not possible, the BCS Supervisor directs temporary lodging at a precinct/district/PSA. The "reasonable effort to avoid handcuffing to chairs/railings" language is testable.

### Operational Considerations

| Rule | Detail |
|------|--------|
| Subsequent designation | Prisoner may be designated Special Category at a **subsequent** EMS screening |
| Priority processing | Special Category Prisoners given **priority status** at every stage of pre-arraignment, including placement on the **"Medical Expedite" list** at BCS |
| Contagious disease | Prisoners identified as being infected with or suspected of carrying an actively contagious disease (e.g., tuberculosis) will be **sent to the hospital immediately** |

> **Exam Alert:** Special Category Prisoners are placed on the **"Medical Expedite" list** for priority processing at BCS. Also, contagious disease (e.g., tuberculosis) requires **immediate** hospital transport — not just separation.

### Wheelchair/Mobility Impaired Prisoners

If a prisoner is confined to a wheelchair or is otherwise mobility impaired:
- **Desk officer, command of arrest** will **confer with the BCS Supervisor** prior to transporting to a court section facility
- Be guided by BCS Supervisor's direction regarding lodging and further processing
- Comply with **P.G. 210-19**, "Department Transportation Services for Arrestees Who Have a Mobility Disability," when necessary

> **See Also:** P.G. 210-19 for ADA-compliant van transport procedures.

---

## P.G. 210-19: Department Transportation Services for Arrestees with a Mobility Disability (NEW)

> **New Procedure:** 07/02/25 | R.O. 56 | 3 pages

### Purpose

To provide a safe method of transportation for arrestees with mobility disabilities.

### Centralized ADA-Accessible Locations

The following commands are designated as centralized locations for arrest processing of mobility-impaired arrestees:

| Borough Code | Precinct |
|-------------|----------|
| **PBBN** | 83rd Precinct |
| **PBBS** | 67th Precinct |
| **PBMN** | 28th Precinct |
| **PBMS** | 9th Precinct |
| **PBSI** | 121st Precinct |
| **PBQN** | Precinct of arrest |
| **PBQS** | Precinct of arrest |
| **PBBX** | Precinct of arrest |

> **Exam Alert:** There are **8 centralized borough locations** for mobility-impaired arrestee processing. For PBQN, PBQS, and PBBX, the arrestee is processed at the **precinct of arrest** (not a centralized location). Know the specific precincts for PBBN (83rd), PBBS (67th), PBMN (28th), PBMS (9th), and PBSI (121st).

### Procedure

| Step | Role | Action |
|------|------|--------|
| 1 | Patrol Borough Commander | Ensure training via Training Bureau for appropriate number of MOS to provide **24-hour coverage** |
| 1a | | Maintain list of **qualified, van-trained** MOS |
| 2 | Patrol Borough Commander | Ensure a Department **ADA compliant van** equipped with wheelchair lift is readily available |
| 3 | Arresting/Escorting Officer | **Interview arrestee** and determine need for accessible transportation based on mobility impairment |
| 4 | Arresting/Escorting Officer | Notify desk officer that a Department ADA compliant van is needed at scene |
| 5 | Desk Officer | Notify **Patrol Borough** to dispatch qualified van operator with ADA van to scene |
| 5a | | Record notification in **Command Log** |
| 6 | Patrol Borough/PS | Dispatch qualified van operator to scene to transport arrestee to **ADA accessible location** for arrest processing |
| 6a | | Record dispatch in **Command Log and/or Digital Activity Log** |
| 7 | Arresting/Escorting Officer | Comply with **P.G. 208-05** ("Arrests - General Search Guidelines") prior to transport in ADA van |
| 7a | | Perform **field search** and utilize hand-held metal detector or similar device |

> **Exam Alert:** The arresting officer must **interview the arrestee** about their mobility impairment (step 3) and perform a **field search with metal detector** (step 7a) before transport in the ADA van.

### Medical Condition Limitation

If an arrestee claims a medical condition that would limit the scope of a field search or prevent safe transport in the ADA van:
- Request response of an **ambulance**
- Remove the arrestee to a **hospital**
- Comply with **P.G. 210-04** ("Prisoners Requiring Medical/Psychiatric Treatment")

### Transport Phase 1 — Scene to Command

| Step | Role | Action |
|------|------|--------|
| 8 | Patrol Supervisor | Ensure A/O-E/O **accompanies** arrestee during transportation to command |
| 9 | Qualified ADA Van Operator | Safely transfer arrestee onto van using **wheelchair lift**; request assistance if needed |
| 10 | Qualified ADA Van Operator | Properly **secure** arrestee using belts provided in van |
| 11 | Arresting/Escorting Officer | Take position in van; ensure arrestee is **observed at all times** during transport |
| 12 | Arresting/Escorting Officer | Upon arrival, assist van operator in **removing** arrestee if requested |

> **Note:** Upon completion of transport, the van will be **returned to command concerned**.

### Transport Phase 2 — Command to Borough Court Section

| Step | Role | Action |
|------|------|--------|
| 13 | Desk Officer | Comply with **P.G. 208-03** ("Arrests - General Processing") and confer with **BCS Supervisor** for direction on lodging and processing |
| 14 | Desk Officer | Notify appropriate command and request ADA van to respond; transport arrestee to BCS |
| 15 | Desk Officer | Assign **escorting officer** to bring arrestee to BCS |
| 16 | Qualified ADA Van Operator | Safely transfer arrestee onto van using wheelchair lift; request assistance if needed |
| 17 | Qualified ADA Van Operator | Properly secure arrestee using belts provided in van |
| 18 | Escorting Officer | Take position in van; observe arrestee at all times during transport |
| 19 | Escorting Officer | Upon arrival at BCS, assist van operator in removing arrestee if requested |
| 20 | BCS Personnel | Comply with **P.G. 210-17** ("Special Category") upon arrestee's arrival at BCS |

> **Exam Alert:** There are **two transport phases**: (1) scene to command (steps 8-12), and (2) command to BCS (steps 13-20). The desk officer must confer with the **BCS Supervisor** before the second transport. BCS personnel comply with P.G. 210-17 upon arrival.

> **Sergeant Focus:** The Patrol Supervisor ensures the A/O-E/O accompanies the arrestee during transport (step 8). The desk officer is responsible for arranging both transports and conferring with BCS. The van operator must be **qualified and trained** in wheelchair lift operation.

---

## Common Exam Angles

1. **Voided arrest vs. decline prosecution** — Know the fundamental difference: invalid arrest vs. valid arrest that DA won't prosecute
2. **Supervisor conferral** — Arresting officer MUST confer with patrol supervisor (or desk officer/BCS supervisor if unavailable) before release
3. **Authority to release** — Rests solely with the Police Department, not the DA
4. **ADA requests void** — Desk officer contacts Legal Bureau, NOT direct action
5. **Warrant check before release** — Mandatory for decline prosecution releases
6. **Active warrant found** — Process on warrant ONLY, not original charges
7. **Rapsheet delay** — Does NOT delay release
8. **Voided arrest documentation** — 5 elements on OLBS Arrest Worksheet
9. **Sealing records** — BCS intake supervisor faxes DCJS for fingerprinted voided arrests
10. **Other agency conflicts** — Precinct commander/duty captain resolves
11. **Special Category definition** — 3 reasons: medical/disability, safety risk, health risk
12. **Special Category exclusions** — Hospitalized prisoners and DAT/summons releases are NOT Special Category
13. **BCS Supervisor duties** — 8 specific steps (3-10) for handling Special Category Prisoners
14. **MTP for each Special Category Prisoner** — Must prepare MTP and check "Recommend Prisoner Be Separated" box
15. **Medical Expedite list** — Special Category Prisoners get priority status at BCS
16. **Contagious disease** — Immediate hospital transport (not just separation)
17. **EMS screening** — All pre-arraignment prisoners screened by EMS at BCS (except Staten Island, where supervisor screens)
18. **Staten Island exception** — Supervisor at court section (not EMS) makes Special Category determination
19. **ADA van — 8 centralized locations** — PBBN=83rd, PBBS=67th, PBMN=28th, PBMS=9th, PBSI=121st; PBQN/PBQS/PBBX=precinct of arrest
20. **ADA van — two transport phases** — Scene to command, then command to BCS
21. **Field search with metal detector** — Required before ADA van transport
22. **Wheelchair/mobility prisoner** — D.O. confers with BCS Supervisor BEFORE transport to court section

## Sergeant Study Notes

- The **patrol supervisor** is the primary authority for consent to release — this is a direct supervisory responsibility
- **Authority to release** rests solely with the Police Department — even when an ADA requests a void, the process goes through the Legal Bureau
- When an ADA requests a void (not decline prosecution), the desk officer contacts the **Legal Bureau** first — never void directly at ADA request
- The **5 elements** of the OLBS Arrest Worksheet for voided arrests must be complete: reason, property returned, witnesses, authorizing supervisor, time of release
- **Sealing fingerprint records** after a voided arrest is mandatory — the BCS intake supervisor handles this by faxing DCJS
- For decline prosecution releases, the **warrant check** is mandatory before release — but do not delay release solely for a rapsheet
- If a warrant is found during release processing, the prisoner is held on the **warrant only** — original charges are dropped per the decline
- Inter-agency release conflicts are resolved at the **precinct commander/duty captain** level — do not attempt to resolve at the sergeant level
- Notification requirements after a voided arrest are extensive — patrol borough commander, Identification Unit, BCS, CO of arresting officer, and desk officer of command of occurrence
- **Special Category designation** is a joint decision between the BCS Supervisor and EMS — not a unilateral call
- The BCS Supervisor must prepare an **MTP for each Special Category Prisoner** and check the "Recommend Prisoner Be Separated" box
- **Medical Expedite list** placement gives Special Category Prisoners priority at every stage of pre-arraignment processing
- **Contagious disease** (e.g., tuberculosis) requires immediate hospital transport — this is non-negotiable
- For **wheelchair/mobility-impaired** prisoners, the desk officer must confer with BCS Supervisor BEFORE transport to court
- The **ADA van** must be operated by a qualified, trained MOS; the Patrol Borough Commander maintains the list of qualified operators
- A **field search with metal detector** is mandatory before placing any arrestee in the ADA van
- If a mobility-impaired arrestee has a medical condition preventing safe ADA van transport, call an ambulance and comply with P.G. 210-04

---

# Section 210 — Key Terms, Forms, and Critical Numbers

---

## Core Prisoner Definitions

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **Holdover Prisoner** | A prisoner who has entered the court process, has NOT yet been arraigned, and has been returned to police custody for overnight detention | P.G. 210-01 |
| **Voided Arrest** | Release of a prisoner when reasonable cause exists to believe the prisoner did NOT commit the offense, or that no offense was committed | P.G. 210-13 |
| **Decline Prosecution** | DA exercises discretion not to prosecute a validly arrested prisoner; requires a Decline Prosecution Letter from an ADA | P.G. 210-13 |
| **High Risk Prisoner** | A prisoner assessed as high risk based on: seriousness of offense, refusal to identify, forcible resistance, threats of violence/escape, history of violence, weapons possession, or escape/attempted escape history | P.G. 210-01 |
| **Prisoner Security Guidelines** | All prisoners will be searched on entry to and exit from any police facility, detention facility, DA facility, or court facility; also searched before transport departure and upon arrival | P.G. 210-01 |

## Medical and Arraignment Terms

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **Mobile Livescan Device (MLD)** | Portable fingerprint scanner that transmits via cellular to DCJS; used exclusively for fingerprinting hospitalized prisoners; requires at least 2 MOS present | P.G. 210-05 |
| **Bedside Arraignment** | Arraignment conducted at the hospital to reduce manpower required to guard hospitalized prisoners | P.G. 210-05 |
| **Prison Ward** | Designated area within a hospital for the treatment and detention of prisoners under guard | P.G. 210-02 |

## Transport and Restraint Terms

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **Transport Chains** | Restraint system used when transporting more than 2 prisoners; cuffs must be double locked | P.G. 210-01 |
| **Leg Restraints** | Additional restraint applied to legs; MUST be used when prisoner refused fingerprinting and for ALL hospital transports; may NOT be used for punitive purposes; does not replace rear handcuffing | P.G. 210-01 |
| **Rear Cuff** | Standard restraint for ALL prisoners during transport; hands cuffed behind the back | P.G. 210-01 |

## Police Action Terms

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **Urgent Police Action** | Response to an emergency, active pursuit, or moving surveillance | P.G. 209-30 |
| **Necessary Police Action** | Less than urgent; enhances safety of an operation; NOT solely for the member's convenience | P.G. 209-30 |

## Hospitalized Prisoner Terms

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **ACR (Ambulance Call Report)** | Report number obtained from ambulance attendant when prisoner is transported by EMS; recorded in Section I of MTP | P.G. 210-04 |
| **PCR (Patient Care Report)** | Report number obtained from ambulance attendant when prisoner is transported by RMP or other means (not EMS); recorded in Section I of MTP | P.G. 210-04 |
| **PIN (Patient Identification Number)** | Number obtained from hospital intake people when prisoner is transferred to Bellevue/Elmhurst for psychiatric admission; NOT the same as ACR or PCR | P.G. 210-04 |
| **UF-50** | A memo with the Official Seal of NYC preprinted in the upper left corner; used for communications outside the Department; issued to authorize family visits to hospitalized prisoners | P.G. 210-02 |
| **Plastic Flex Cuffs** | Additional data restraint used when a hospitalized prisoner admitted to ICU is assessed as having escape potential | P.G. 210-02 |
| **Long Chain Cuffs** | Restraint used when prisoner is comatose, paralyzed, or in last stage of pregnancy | P.G. 210-02 |
| **Prisoner Unusual** | An unarraigned adult prisoner or juvenile under 18 in NYPD custody or Corrections who: assaults MOS, is sexually assaulted/harassed (or alleges such), escapes/attempts, or other unusual occurrence | P.G. 210-07 |
| **Typed Letterhead (49)** | Report prepared by the C.O./Duty Captain for prisoner unusual occurrences; forwarded to Chief of Department (3 copies adult, 2 copies child DOA, 1 copy child in custody) | P.G. 210-07 |

## Other Agency Terms

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **Other Police Agencies** | DOC, NYS DOCCS, Court Officers, Port Authority Police, MTA Police, Amtrak Police, Sea Gate Police, FDNY Fire Marshals, DEP Police, TBTA Police | P.G. 210-03 |

---

## Forms

| Form Name | Form Number | Purpose | Reference |
|-----------|-------------|---------|-----------|
| Prisoner Roster | **PD244-145** | Tracks all prisoners in custody at a command | P.G. 210-01 |
| Prisoner Holding Pen Roster | **PD244-1410** | Tracks all prisoners placed in holding pens; includes 30-minute inspection records | P.G. 210-08 |
| Medical Treatment of Prisoner | **PD244-150** | Documents medical treatment provided to or requested by a prisoner | P.G. 210-04 |
| Transport Dispatch | **PD171-132** | Records prisoner transport details including destination and escort | P.G. 210-01 |
| Meal Voucher | **PD144-051** | Documents prisoner meal purchases; max $1.80 per meal | P.G. 210-06 |
| Arrest Report Supplement | **PD244-157** | Records attorney visit details: name, address, phone, who retained, interview status, arrival/departure times | P.G. 210-01 |
| Medical Treatment of Prisoner (MTP) | **PD244-150** | Documents medical treatment; use "Reserve" function in FORMS app; copy accompanies prisoner until no longer in NYPD custody | P.G. 210-04 |
| Decline Prosecution Letter | — | Issued by ADA when DA declines to prosecute; required before prisoner release | P.G. 210-16 |
| OLBS Arrest Worksheet | — | Must include reason for void, property returned, witnesses, authorizing supervisor, and time of release for voided arrests | P.G. 210-13 |
| Expense Report | — | Submitted by the 15th of each month for prisoner meal expenditures | P.G. 210-06 |

---

## Abbreviations

| Abbreviation | Full Name | Context |
|-------------|-----------|---------|
| **BCS** | Borough Court Section | Coordinates cell space, intake processing, court appearances |
| **DCJS** | Division of Criminal Justice Services | Receives fingerprint transmissions; records sealed for voided arrests |
| **DOC** | Department of Correction (NYC) | Listed as "other police agency" for hospitalized prisoner guarding |
| **DOCCS** | Department of Corrections and Community Supervision (NYS) | State corrections; listed as other police agency; also authorized to interview prisoners |
| **MLD** | Mobile Livescan Device | Portable fingerprint scanner for hospitalized prisoners |
| **MOS** | Member of Service | NYPD personnel |
| **OLBS** | On-Line Booking System | Used to process arrest worksheets including voided arrests |
| **TBTA** | Triborough Bridge and Tunnel Authority | Listed as other police agency |
| **ACR** | Ambulance Call Report | Number for EMS transport; recorded on MTP Section I |
| **PCR** | Patient Care Report | Number for RMP/other transport; recorded on MTP Section I |
| **PIN** | Patient Identification Number | Number from hospital intake for psych transfer; NOT same as ACR/PCR |
| **MTP** | Medical Treatment of Prisoner | Form documenting medical treatment; "Reserve" function in FORMS app |
| **HHC** | NYC Health and Hospitals Corporation | Fiscal Affairs/General Accounting handles billing for non-HHC hospitals |
| **NP** | Nurse Practitioner | At BCS; administers medication given by doctor for prisoner to take |
| **ESU** | Emergency Service Unit | Supervisors may consider ESU for extraordinary hospital transport circumstances |
| **IAB** | Internal Affairs Bureau | Notified via IAB Command Center for prisoner escape/attempt |
| **CJB** | Criminal Justice Bureau | C.O. receives copy of unusual occurrence report |

---

## Critical Numbers

| Number | What It Represents | Source |
|--------|-------------------|--------|
| **$1.80** | Maximum cost per meal per prisoner | P.G. 210-06 |
| **15 minutes** | Maximum duration for parent/guardian visit (prisoner age 18-21) | P.G. 210-01 |
| **30 minutes** | Inspection interval for prisoners in holding pens | P.G. 210-08 |
| **1 hour** | Follow-up notification deadline if other agency does not provide guard relief | P.G. 210-03 |
| **3 hours** | Maximum detention time in holding pen (standard; extension requires approval) | P.G. 210-08 |
| **4 hours** | Minimum detention before parent/guardian visit is permitted (prisoner age 18-21) | P.G. 210-01 |
| **6 days** | Notification threshold — notify borough court section if bedside arraignment delayed beyond this | P.G. 210-05 |
| **2 prisoners** | Threshold — transport chains MUST be used for more than this number | P.G. 210-01 |
| **9 prisoners** | Threshold — additional escort required for more than this number on transport chains | P.G. 210-01 |
| **2 MOS** | Minimum staffing for MLD fingerprinting of hospitalized prisoners | P.G. 210-05 |
| **3 meal periods** | 0500-0600, 1200-1300, 1800-1900 | P.G. 210-06 |
| **15th of month** | Deadline for prisoner meal expense report submission | P.G. 210-06 |
| **September 30th** | Due date for annual Prisoner Security Survey report | P.G. 210-01 |
| **Last week of September** | Timing for annual Prisoner Security Survey | P.G. 210-01 |
| **7** | Number of high risk prisoner factors | P.G. 210-01 |
| **6** | Items removed from prisoners in holding pens (belts, neckties, shoelaces, drawstrings, jackets, overcoats) | P.G. 210-08 |
| **5** | Required elements on OLBS Arrest Worksheet for voided arrests | P.G. 210-13 |
| **10** | Number of agencies classified as "other police agencies" | P.G. 210-03 |
| **4 hours** | Deadline for EO to respond to DOC prison ward to take custody of released prisoner | P.G. 210-04 |
| **3** | Copies of typed letterhead (49) to Chief of Dept for adult prisoner unusual occurrence | P.G. 210-07 |
| **2** | Copies of typed letterhead (49) to Chief of Dept for child in custody DOA | P.G. 210-07 |
| **1** | Copy of typed letterhead (49) to Chief of Dept for child in custody (not DOA) | P.G. 210-07 |
| **1 tour** | Maximum time Pct of Hospitalization may guard a Housing/Transit prisoner if A/O has other arrests | P.G. 210-02 |
| **Wednesday** | Day of weekly telephone message to BCS when guard removed from hospitalized prisoner | P.G. 210-02 |
| **3 notifications** | Times PS and Communications Section notified when prisoner ingests narcotics/dangerous substance | P.G. 210-04 |

---

# Section 210 — Review Questions

15 multiple-choice questions covering key Sergeant exam topics from Section 210.

---

### Question 1
**What is the maximum amount that may be spent per meal per prisoner?**

A. $1.50
B. $1.80
C. $2.00
D. $2.50

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**B.** Per P.G. 210-06, the maximum cost is **$1.80 per meal per prisoner**. The three meal periods are 0500-0600, 1200-1300, and 1800-1900. No hot liquids (coffee, tea, hot chocolate) are permitted.
</details>

---

### Question 2
**How often must a designated MOS inspect prisoners in a holding pen?**

A. Every 15 minutes
B. Every 20 minutes
C. Every 30 minutes
D. Every 60 minutes

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**C.** Per P.G. 210-08, a designated MOS must inspect prisoners every **30 minutes**. In addition, a supervisor must personally and periodically inspect prisoners, recording their condition (sleeping, alert) and position (sitting, lying down).
</details>

---

### Question 3
**What is the standard maximum detention time for a prisoner in a holding pen?**

A. 1 hour
B. 2 hours
C. 3 hours
D. 4 hours

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**C.** Per P.G. 210-08, the standard maximum detention in a holding pen is **3 hours**. Extensions require desk officer or detective squad supervisor approval, and the extension must be recorded in the Command Log.
</details>

---

### Question 4
**When transporting prisoners, at what number must transport chains be used?**

A. 2 or more prisoners
B. More than 2 prisoners
C. 3 or more prisoners — only if high risk
D. More than 4 prisoners

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**B.** Per P.G. 210-01, transport chains MUST be used when transporting **more than 2 prisoners**. Transport chain cuffs must be double locked. Two prisoners may be individually rear cuffed; three or more require transport chains.
</details>

---

### Question 5
**Under what TWO circumstances MUST leg restraints be used on a prisoner?**

A. When the prisoner is combative and when transporting to court
B. When the prisoner refused fingerprinting and for ALL hospital transports
C. When the prisoner is high risk and when transporting more than 2 prisoners
D. When the prisoner threatened violence and when the prisoner has a weapons history

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**B.** Per P.G. 210-01, leg restraints MUST be used when (1) the prisoner **refused fingerprinting** and (2) for **ALL hospital transports**. Leg restraints may NOT be used for punitive purposes and do NOT replace rear handcuffing.
</details>

---

### Question 6
**Where must attorney interviews with prisoners be conducted at the precinct?**

A. The cellblock, under supervision
B. The detective squad room
C. The muster room ONLY
D. Any private room designated by the desk officer

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**C.** Per P.G. 210-01, attorney interviews at the precinct must be conducted in the **muster room ONLY**. At borough court, the interview is conducted at a location designated by the supervisor. The attorney visit must be documented on the Arrest Report Supplement (PD244-157).
</details>

---

### Question 7
**A prisoner is 19 years old and has been detained for 5 hours. The prisoner's parent requests a visit. What are the rules for this visit?**

A. The visit is not permitted — parent visits are only for juveniles under 18
B. Maximum 15 minutes, in the muster room, in the presence of the desk officer or BCS supervisor
C. Maximum 30 minutes, in any designated area, no supervision required
D. Maximum 15 minutes, in the cellblock, in the presence of the arresting officer

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**B.** Per P.G. 210-01, parent/guardian visits are permitted for prisoners aged **18-21** who have been detained **more than 4 hours**. The visit is limited to **15 minutes**, must take place in the **muster room**, and must be in the presence of the **desk officer or BCS supervisor**. This prisoner (age 19, detained 5 hours) meets all criteria.
</details>

---

### Question 8
**A pregnant prisoner is brought into the command. What is the initial restraint method, and who determines if a less restrictive method is appropriate?**

A. Front cuff initially; the desk officer determines if less restrictive method is needed
B. Rear cuff initially; the immediate supervisor (sergeant) determines if less restrictive method is appropriate
C. No restraints initially; the commanding officer determines restraint level
D. Rear cuff initially; the arresting officer determines if less restrictive method is needed

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**B.** Per P.G. 210-01, pregnant prisoners are initially **rear cuffed**, absent exceptional circumstances. The **immediate supervisor** (sergeant) determines if a less restrictive method is appropriate (front cuff, or one hand cuffed to a stationary post) based on factors including safety, escape likelihood, crime circumstances, demeanor, late-stage pregnancy/fall risk, and labor.
</details>

---

### Question 9
**Which of the following is NOT one of the seven high risk prisoner factors?**

A. Seriousness of offense
B. Prior criminal record length
C. Forcible resistance
D. History of escape or attempted escape

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**B.** Per P.G. 210-01, the 7 high risk factors are: (1) seriousness of offense, (2) unwillingness to identify self, (3) forcible resistance, (4) threats of violence or escape, (5) history of violence, (6) weapons possession, and (7) escape/attempted escape history. "Prior criminal record length" is NOT one of the factors.
</details>

---

### Question 10
**An arresting officer wants to void an arrest. With whom must the officer confer to obtain consent?**

A. The desk officer only
B. The commanding officer
C. The patrol supervisor; if unavailable, the desk officer or BCS supervisor
D. The district attorney

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**C.** Per P.G. 210-13, the arresting officer must confer with the **patrol supervisor** for consent to release. If the patrol supervisor is unavailable, the officer confers with the **desk officer or BCS supervisor**. The authority to release rests solely with the Police Department.
</details>

---

### Question 11
**An ADA issues a Decline Prosecution Letter. Before releasing the prisoner, the desk officer conducts a warrant check and discovers an active warrant. What is the correct action?**

A. Release the prisoner on the decline prosecution and issue a new summons for the warrant
B. Hold the prisoner on both the original charges and the warrant
C. Process the prisoner solely on the warrant authority, NOT on the original charges
D. Contact the ADA to request prosecution on the original charges

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**C.** Per P.G. 210-16, if a warrant check reveals an active warrant during a decline prosecution release, the prisoner is processed **solely on the warrant authority** — NOT on the original charges. The ADA has already declined prosecution on the original charges.
</details>

---

### Question 12
**How many MOS must be present when using the Mobile Livescan Device (MLD) to fingerprint a hospitalized prisoner?**

A. 1 MOS
B. At least 2 MOS
C. At least 3 MOS
D. The number depends on the prisoner's risk level

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**B.** Per P.G. 210-05, at least **2 MOS** must be present during MLD fingerprinting of a hospitalized prisoner. The MLD is prohibited for any purpose other than fingerprinting hospitalized prisoners. If bedside arraignment is delayed more than 6 days, the borough court section must be notified.
</details>

---

### Question 13
**When is the annual Prisoner Security Survey conducted, and when is the report due?**

A. First week of January; report due January 7th
B. Last week of September; report due September 30th
C. Last week of December; report due December 31st
D. First week of June; report due June 7th

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**B.** Per P.G. 210-01, the Prisoner Security Survey is conducted annually during the **last week of September**, with the report due by **September 30th**.
</details>

---

### Question 14
**Which of the following prisoners may be placed in a holding pen?**

A. An intoxicated prisoner who is cooperative
B. An emotionally disturbed prisoner under observation
C. A prisoner with a minor laceration who has been treated and cleared by EMS
D. An ill prisoner awaiting transport to the hospital

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**C.** Per P.G. 210-08, ill, intoxicated, and emotionally disturbed prisoners are NOT permitted in holding pens — they must be removed to a hospital under P.G. 210-04. A prisoner who has been **treated and medically cleared** by EMS may be placed in a holding pen, as they are no longer in the ill/injured category requiring hospital treatment.
</details>

---

### Question 15
**A voided arrest was processed and the prisoner was fingerprinted. Who is responsible for faxing DCJS to seal the fingerprint record?**

A. The arresting officer
B. The desk officer
C. The patrol supervisor
D. The BCS intake supervisor

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**D.** Per P.G. 210-13, when a voided arrest involves a fingerprinted prisoner, the **BCS intake supervisor** faxes DCJS to seal the record. This step is critical to ensure that an invalid arrest does not create a permanent fingerprint record.
</details>

---

---

### Question 16
**A hospitalized prisoner's doctor requests that the guarding officer remove the prisoner's handcuffs. What is the correct procedure?**

A. Remove the cuffs immediately as directed by the doctor
B. Explain the circumstances of the arrest; if the doctor insists, request the Patrol Supervisor to respond — do NOT remove without the PS present and authorizing
C. Refuse the request and document it in the Activity Log
D. Contact the desk officer for permission to remove the cuffs

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**B.** Per P.G. 210-04, if a doctor requests cuffs be removed, the guarding officer explains the circumstances of arrest. If the doctor still wants them off, the officer requests the **Patrol Supervisor** to respond. Cuffs are NOT removed without the PS on scene and authorizing. Note: this is different from a doctor asking the officer to **leave the room**, which does NOT require the PS.
</details>

---

### Question 17
**A prisoner ingests narcotics during arrest. How many times must the Patrol Supervisor and Communications Section be notified?**

A. Once — when the ingestion is observed
B. Twice — when observed and when arriving at the hospital
C. Three times — when observed, when transport initiated, and when arrived at hospital
D. Four times — when observed, when transport initiated, when arrived, and when treated

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**C.** Per P.G. 210-04, the Patrol Supervisor AND Communications Section are notified **three times**: (1) upon observing or suspecting ingestion, (2) when transport to hospital is initiated, and (3) when 84 (arrived) at hospital. The prisoner goes DIRECTLY to the nearest hospital — never to the stationhouse first.
</details>

---

### Question 18
**In P.G. 210-02, a prisoner's lawyer requests to interview the prisoner at the hospital. Under the Patrol Guide, the lawyer is classified as:**

A. An Interviewer — same as at the precinct under 210-01
B. A Visitor — "if requested" by the prisoner
C. Not permitted to visit hospitalized prisoners
D. An Interviewer only if approved by the Commanding Officer

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**B.** Under P.G. 210-02, a lawyer drops from the "Interviewer" list (where they appear in 210-01 at the precinct) to the **"Visitor"** list at the hospital. The lawyer may visit "if requested" by the prisoner. This is one of the key differences between the 210-01 and 210-02 interviewer/visitor lists.
</details>

---

### Question 19
**A prisoner requires psychiatric admission. Generally, male prisoners are transported to which facility, and by what means?**

A. Elmhurst General by FDNY EMS
B. Bellevue by FDNY EMS
C. Bellevue by private ambulance contract
D. Kings County Hospital by RMP

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>
