# Section 212 — Crime Scene and Investigative Encounters

## P.G. 212-04 — Crime Scene

### Police Line vs. Crime Scene Tape

| Tape Type | Color / Letters | Purpose |
|-----------|----------------|---------|
| **Police Line Do Not Cross** | White tape with **blue** letters | Establish a "secure area" for police operations (close streets, secure transit facilities, etc.) |
| **Crime Scene Do Not Cross** | Yellow tape with **black** letters | Safeguard a specific area where objects may be evidence; may be **within** the Police Line |

- Both tapes also display: **NYPD Crime Stoppers 1-800-577-TIPS (8744)**
- Crime Scene tape: Only authorized, trained, equipped, actively involved personnel whose presence is **absolutely required** will be permitted — absent exigents involving life or safety
- Deployment of Crime Scene tape for any other reason is **strictly prohibited**

> **Exam Alert:** Know the color distinction: Police Line = white tape/blue letters (secure area). Crime Scene = yellow tape/black letters (evidence area). Crime Scene tape may exist **within** the Police Line perimeter.

### Crime Scene Evidence Cone

- 5-inch-tall orange cone with "NYPD" in black letters
- Placed **next to** small evidence to prevent disturbance (shell casings, bullet fragments, etc.)

> **Exam Alert:** Per PG 219-01, the sergeant's vehicle should contain **5** Crime Scene Evidence Cones ("Five by Five").

### Barrier Tape Duty — Key Distinction

> **PG Conflict:** In PG 212-04 (Crime Scene), it is the **UMOS** duty to "utilize barrier tape as appropriate." In PG 212-05 (Transit Crime Scene), it is the **Patrol Supervisor** who utilizes barrier tape. Know which role applies to each procedure.

### UMOS Responsibilities

- Request through radio dispatcher:
  - Patrol Supervisor
  - PDS / Specialty Unit required
- Detain witnesses and persons with pertinent information
- Secure Crime Scene:
  - Remove unauthorized persons
  - Do not disturb evidence
  - Initially secure a **LARGE** area when uncertain
  - Utilize barrier tape and Crime Scene Evidence Cones as necessary
- Advise Patrol Supervisor and Detectives of witnesses detained and other case info
- Document: Digital Activity Log entries (observations, suspect/witness info, statements)

### Patrol Supervisor Responsibilities

- Assess the scene and determine if CSU services are required
- Call CSU **direct on landline** or if no landline available, through radio dispatcher

> **Exam Alert:** On test day, the correct answer for how the Patrol Supervisor contacts CSU is via **landline** (direct call). Do NOT choose "department-issued cell phone" — the PG specifies landline first, radio dispatcher only if landline unavailable.

- Provide specific details: cross streets, apt #, time/date, crime, weapon, # of victims, hospital
- If Patrol Supervisor cannot respond to scene: detective **on scene** MAY request CSU and other required notifications

> **Exam Alert:** The procedure says the detective may request CSU — it does NOT say the detective requests "Shots Fired." Watch for that distinction on the exam.

- Confer with Crime Scene Unit upon arrival
- Notify Desk Officer of details and request additional assistance if required
- Secure Crime Scene — have "Crime Scene" signs posted

### When to Request Crime Scene (CSU) — The HARBOR Mnemonic

> **Memory Aid — HARBOR:** When Crime Scene Must Respond
> - **H**omicide
> - **A**ggravated assault with dangerous instrument AND victim likely to die
> - **R**ape (forcible)
> - **B**urglary involving forced safe OR circumvented alarm
> - **O**ther crime in which CSU services will assist the investigation
> - **R**obbery OR hijacking with injury caused by firearm

> **Exam Alert:** For burglary, CSU responds when the safe is **forced** OR the alarm is **circumvented**. Compare this carefully with the "Refer to Squad" criteria in PG 207-07 where the safe merely needs to be **involved** and the gun merely **involved**.

### Refer to Squad (207-07) vs. Request Crime Scene (212-04)

| Crime | Refer to Squad (207-07) | Request Crime Scene (212-04) |
|-------|------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Assault** | SPI | **Likely** & D.I. |
| **Robbery** | Firearm or D.I. **Used**; Victim 60+ | Firearm **Caused Injury** |
| **Burglary** | Safe **Involved**; Gun **Involved**; >$5,000 stolen (Manhattan >$10,000); Person Present | Safe **Forced** OR Circumvented Alarm |

> **Prior Test:** Past exams have mixed up the Squad referral rules from 207-07 with the CSU request rules from 212-04. The key difference: Squad referrals use broader criteria (involved/used), while CSU requests use narrower criteria (forced/caused injury).

### DOA Cross-Reference

> **See Also:** PG 216-04 — In a DOA case, the **detective** notifies CSU, not the patrol supervisor.

---

## P.G. 212-11 — Investigative Encounters (Stop, Question & Frisk)

*(Updated R.O. 53, effective 06/26/25 — 16 pages)*

### BWC Requirement

> **Exam Alert:** Per PG 212-11, **always activate BWC** if assigned one, no matter what level the stop is. This applies to Level 1, 2, and 3 encounters.

### Three Levels of Encounter

| Element | Level 1 — Request for Information | Level 2 — Common Law Right of Inquiry | Level 3 — Terry Stop |
|---------|-----------------------------------|---------------------------------------|---------------------|
| **Based on** | Objective **credible** reason | **Founded** suspicion | **Reasonable** suspicion of Felony or PL Misdemeanor |
| **ID Self** | Verbally & display shield if practicable | Verbally: Rank, Name, Shield, Command; display shield, absent exigent circumstances | Verbally: Rank, Name, Shield, Command; display shield, absent exigent circumstances |
| **Ask** | Only **related** questions | May ask **accusatory** questions | May ask **accusatory** questions |
| **Detain/Force** | May NOT detain or use force | May NOT detain or use force | **May** detain or use force |
| **Search** | May NOT even ask to search | **May** ask to search | **May** ask to search |
| **Paperwork** | BWC categorization | CLRI - Consent Search Report (PD541-161), RTKBC | Stop Report (PD383-151), RTKBC, Digital Activity Log entry |

> **Exam Alert:** The critical distinction is that at Level 1, you may NOT even **request** consent to search. At Level 2, you MAY ask. At Level 3, you MAY ask and document the request on a **Stop Report** (not a CLRI).

### Definitions (Updated)

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **Objective Credible Reason** | Based on more than a hunch or a whim; may relate to public safety/service OR law enforcement, but need NOT be based on any indication of criminality |
| **Founded Suspicion** | Some present indication of criminality based on observable conduct or reliable hearsay information |
| **Reasonable Suspicion** | Information known to the member would make an ordinarily prudent and cautious police officer believe a felony or PL misdemeanor has been, is being, or is about to be committed |
| **Frisk** | A carefully limited running of the hands over the outside of a person's clothing for a deadly weapon or instrument readily capable of causing serious physical injury |
| **Search After Frisk** | When the officer places hands inside a pocket or other interior portions of clothing to remove an object that the member felt during a frisk and reasonably suspects is a weapon or dangerous instrument |
| **Protective Measures** | Tactics short of a frisk (e.g., ordering hands out of pockets, stepping away from objects that could be used as weapons, grabbing hands if circumstances suggest weapon) |

### False or Inconsistent Information

- At **any** level of encounter, false or inconsistent information **may** escalate the encounter

### DeBour vs. Terry — Legal Framework

| Case | Court | Levels |
|------|-------|--------|
| **People v. DeBour** | NYS Court of Appeals | Articulates 4 levels: (1) Request for info — no suspicion; (2) CLRI — **founded** suspicion; (3) Forcible stop — **reasonable** suspicion; (4) Arrest — probable cause |
| **Terry v. Ohio** | Federal (Supreme Court) | Provides authority for Level 3: forcibly stop and **possibly** frisk, based on reasonable suspicion |

> **Exam Alert:** DeBour is the **state** case (Court of Appeals) and provides the 4-level framework. Terry is the **federal** case (Supreme Court) and provides Level 3 stop-and-frisk authority. Know which court decided each.

### Level 1 Steps — "Request for Information"

1. Activate BWC, if assigned, for encounters where activation is required per P.G. 212-123.
2. Approach the person if there is an **objective credible reason** to do so.
3. ID self: Verbally AND display shield conspicuously, **if practicable**.
4. Do NOT detain the person, use or threaten the use of force.
5. Do NOT request consent to search.
6. Ask general, non-threatening questions **related to the reason for the approach**.
   - Pointed and accusatory questions are **NOT** permitted.
   - Person may refuse to answer and is free to leave.
7. May inform person free to leave, but NOT required to UNLESS person **specifically asks**.
8. **Should** provide an explanation for the encounter, UNLESS it would impair a criminal investigation.
9. **Protective measures:** In rare cases, may engage protective measures for safety (because of the nature of the approach or the individual's behavior).

### Level 1 — RTKBC and ID Requirements

- Offer **RIGHT TO KNOW BUSINESS CARD (PD142-012)** or **RIGHT TO KNOW BUSINESS CARD - GENERAL (PD142-013)**, as appropriate, per A.G. 304-11.

> **Exam Alert (Updated):** At Level 1, there are specific situations where you are NOT required to proactively identify yourself, explain the reason, or offer an RTKBC:
> - Engaged in undercover activity or operations
> - Exigent circumstances require your immediate action
> - Reasonably expect danger of physical injury, imminent property damage, imminent escape, imminent destruction of evidence
> - Conducting a security search of person entering a public building/facility/event/gathering (including MTA random checks, UNLESS requested by subject)
> - Verifying the identity of a person seeking entrance to a restricted area due to public health, safety, or security concern

### Level 2 Steps — "Common Law Right of Inquiry"

10. Activate BWC, if assigned, per P.G. 212-123.
11. Approach the person if you have a **founded suspicion of criminality**.
12. ID self: Rank, Name, Shield, Command; display shield conspicuously, absent exigent circumstances.
13. Do NOT detain or use force.
14. May ask pointed and accusatory questions.
15. Person may refuse and walk away. If person **runs**, UMOS may **pursue**.
16. Request consent to search (voluntary; elicit clear **"yes" or "no"**):
    a. Ask in a manner that elicits a clear "yes" or "no" response. Example: *"I can only search you, if you consent. Do you understand. May I search you?"*
    b. If a person does not consent, you **cannot** conduct a search.
    c. If seeking consent, you **must** video record the request and the person's response if you have a BWC.
    d. Document request, consent, refusal, and search (if performed) on the **COMMON LAW RIGHT OF INQUIRY - CONSENT SEARCH REPORT (PD541-161)**.
    e. Offer RTKBC in all cases; provide info on how to obtain a copy of the video record.
17. May inform person free to leave (not required unless person specifically asks).
18. May engage protective measures when you have a reasonable concern for your safety.
19. Provide individual with an explanation for the encounter, unless it would impair a criminal investigation.
20. Do NOT offer RTKBC if encounter ends in **arrest or summons**, unless requested.

> **Exam Alert (Updated):** At Level 2, the **CLRI - Consent Search Report** is only prepared for Level 2 requests to search. If you seek consent during a Level 3 Terry Stop, document that request on your **Stop Report**, not a CLRI. The CLRI is NOT prepared when conducting security searches, during exigent circumstances, or when danger of physical injury/imminent escape/evidence destruction exists.

### Level 2 Note — Creating a Situation

> *During a Level 1 or Level 2 encounter, an officer may not create a situation (either by words or actions) where a reasonable person would not feel free to walk away. A person may be detained only if a properly conducted Level 1 or Level 2 encounter yields information to support a reasonable suspicion that the person committed, was committing, or was about to commit a felony or Penal Law misdemeanor.*

### Level 3 Steps — "Terry Stop"

22. Activate BWC, if assigned, per P.G. 212-123.
23. Upon **reasonable suspicion** that the person has committed, is committing, or is about to commit a felony or PL misdemeanor, **stop and detain** the person.
24. Notify the radio dispatcher: location, number of persons being stopped, and whether additional units are needed.
25. ID self: Rank, Name, Shield, Command; display shield conspicuously, absent exigent circumstances.
26. Question the suspect to determine whether there is probable cause to make an arrest.
27. May ask pointed and accusatory questions. **Refusal to answer or produce identification does NOT establish probable cause.**
    a. Offer RTKBC as appropriate.
28. May request consent to search (same rules as Level 2 — elicit clear "yes" or "no", video record if BWC assigned, document on **STOP REPORT (PD383-151)**).
29. Reasonable force may be used to stop a person.
30. May engage protective measures for safety.
31. May frisk if at any point during the encounter you have RS that the person is armed and dangerous.
32. Detention: only as long as necessary to confirm or dispel suspicion. Authority to detain ends when tasks tied to the stop are completed or reasonably should have been completed.
33. Provide individual with an explanation for the encounter, unless it would impair a criminal investigation.
34. Obtain suspect's name, address, and additional information for your digital Activity Log entry regarding the stop.
35. Do NOT transport or move suspect from location UNLESS:
    - Voluntary consent, or
    - Exigency (hostile crowd, threat to safety, hospital show-up, etc.)
36. Release the person immediately after completing the investigation if probable cause to arrest does not exist.
37. Do NOT offer RTKBC if encounter ends in **arrest or summons**, unless requested.

### Level 3 — When RTKBC/ID Not Required

38. Same exceptions as Level 1 (undercover, exigent circumstances, security search, restricted area verification, danger of physical injury/imminent escape/evidence destruction).

### Frisk Rules (Updated)

39. If a police officer develops reasonable suspicion that a person is **armed and dangerous**, the officer may frisk for:
    - A deadly weapon or any instrument, article, or substance readily capable of causing serious physical injury
    - Of a sort not ordinarily carried in public places by law-abiding persons

    RS that a person is armed and dangerous may arise from:
    a. RS that the suspect has committed, is committing, or is about to commit a **violent crime** (assault with deadly weapon, burglary, rape, robbery, etc.)
    b. Observation of something on the person the officer reasonably suspects is a weapon
    c. A statement by the suspect that she/he is armed
    d. Information known that the suspect may be carrying a weapon (victim/witness statements)
40. Purpose of the frisk: ensure **officer safety** — NOT to locate evidence of a crime (such as drugs).
41. **No requirement** to question suspect before conducting a lawful frisk.
42. Conduct the frisk by carefully running your hands down the outside of the person's clothing.
43. If the frisk reveals an object that the member reasonably suspects may be a weapon: search **only** those interior portions of the stopped person's clothing to remove the weapon.
44. **Bag/personal property** — need RS person is armed and dangerous AND bag could contain weapon AND within person's reach:
    - Soft bag: run hands down outside; open ONLY if you feel the contours of what she/he believes is a weapon
    - Rigid and unlocked: open to ensure no weapon
    - Rigid and locked: need **search warrant** or **consent** to search

> **Exam Alert:** Mass transit checkpoint inspections of backpacks/containers for explosive devices follow different rules and are NOT governed by the frisk rules above.

### Requesting Identification Documents

> *At any level, an officer may ask an individual to verbally identify herself/himself or present an identification document. During Level 1 or 2 encounters, the officer must not create a situation where the person does not feel free to leave. Other than the operator of a motor vehicle/motorcycle, members of the public are NOT required to possess or present identification documents. Refusal or inability to produce identification alone will NOT elevate the level of the encounter. Absent probable cause, a person may not be arrested or removed to a Department facility merely because she/he refused to produce identification.*

### Vehicle Report (New)

48. Complete **VEHICLE REPORT** in FORMS for every vehicle, bicycle, motorized scooter, e-scooter, or e-bike stop, regardless of whether enforcement action is taken or not.

> **Exam Alert (Updated):** A VEHICLE REPORT must be completed in FORMS for every vehicle/bicycle/scooter/e-bike stop. This is a new requirement in the R.O. 53 revision.

### Stop Report Documentation (Updated)

47. For all Terry Stops/Level 3 encounters, prepare a **STOP REPORT** using FORMS for **EACH** person stopped.
    a. Complete all applicable captions and follow the directions for each section prior to end of tour.
    b. Check "REFUSED" if the person stopped refused to identify herself/himself.
       - (1) Request the patrol supervisor to respond to verify refusal.
       - (2) Do not detain the person, however, if the investigation is complete and there is no probable cause.
    c. Select all relevant factors that led to the stop if more than one descriptive term applies.
    d. Describe in **plain language** (rather than numeric Penal Law section) the specific felony or PL misdemeanor you suspected.
    e. Describe in your own words, under the "Narrative (Describe the Circumstances That Led to the Stop)" caption, all of the facts and information relied upon to conclude RS existed.
    f. If frisked/searched, describe under the "Narrative (Describe the Circumstances That Led to the Frisk and/or Search)" caption all facts relied upon, the specific area searched, and whether a weapon or other contraband was recovered.
49. When prompted to identify "Reviewing Supervisor" in FORMS, enter the name or tax ID of a supervisor currently performing duty with the platoon/unit and select that supervisor from the dropdown.
    a. Select immediate squad/unit supervisor if assigned and available.
    b. If not available, enter another supervisor performing duty with the squad/unit (patrol supervisor, detail supervisor, etc.).
50. Notify the reviewing supervisor that the STOP REPORT was prepared and submitted to the supervisor's electronic "INBOX" folder in FORMS for review.

### Stop Report — Key Notes

> *The STOP REPORT is NOT prepared for Level 1 and Level 2 encounters, unless the encounter escalates to a Level 3 Terry Stop. The STOP REPORT is NOT prepared when an officer makes a summary arrest for an observed offense/crime or issues a Criminal Court summons or civil summons returnable to OATH for an observed violation, UNLESS the suspect was initially detained for investigation in a Level 3 Terry Stop. The STOP REPORT is NOT prepared for traffic stops based on violations of Vehicle and Traffic Law, unless the suspect is frisked.*

> **Exam Alert:** FORMS will time out after **55 minutes**. UMOS should periodically click "Save" to ensure data is not lost.

51. Record details in digital Activity Log entry:
    a. Date, time and location of stop
    b. Pedigree information (name, DOB, address, telephone number), unless refused, and detailed description
    c. Document refusal to provide pedigree information, if applicable
    d. ICAD number, if applicable
52. Prior to end of tour, submit the STOP REPORT and digital Activity Log printout to the patrol supervisor/unit supervisor for review.
    a. Reviewing supervisor must be at least **1 rank higher** than member submitting the STOP REPORT.

> **Exam Alert (Updated):** Pedigree information is NOT captured electronically and will ONLY be recorded in the member's digital Activity Log. Do NOT put pedigree information into the STOP REPORT narratives.

### Patrol Supervisor/Unit Supervisor Review Duties (Updated)

54. Respond to the scene of stops **when feasible**.
55. Discuss the circumstances of the stop with the member of the service and review the **STOP REPORT** in FORMS by selecting "Signoff."
    a. Determine whether all captions are completed and all relevant check boxes are checked.
    b. Confirm narrative states a specific suspected felony or PL misdemeanor in **plain language** (not Penal Law section numbers).
    c. Determine whether the "Narrative (Describe the Circumstances That Led to the Stop)" includes the facts and circumstances relied upon to conclude RS existed.
    d. If person was frisked, determine whether the "Narrative (Describe the Circumstances That Led to the Frisk and/or Search)" includes facts relied upon to conclude RS that the person was armed and dangerous, and if searched, the basis for the search, the area searched, and whether a weapon or contraband was recovered.
    e. Complete the "Supervisory Action (Must Complete)" caption. Consider the facts and determine whether:
       - (1) The stop was based upon RS of a felony or PL misdemeanor
       - (2) If frisked, whether the frisk was supported by RS the person was armed and dangerous
       - (3) If searched, whether there was a sufficient basis for the search
    f. If appropriate, instruct/refer for additional training and/or other remedial action, including disciplinary action.
56. Complete all captions in the "Reviewing Supervisor" section.
57. If the STOP REPORT requires additional information:
    a. Check "No" following "Report Accurate and Complete?"
    b. Enter instruction to the reporting member in the "Note" section in FORMS.
    c. Select the "Check" icon to return the STOP REPORT for correction.
58. If accurate and complete but **stop was NOT proper**:
    a. Select the action taken from the "Follow-Up Action (If appropriate)" section.
    b. **Approve** the STOP REPORT.
59. "Approve" and "Finalize" the STOP REPORT following the prompts in FORMS.
60. Review the member's digital Activity Log entry (detailed description and pedigree info).
61. If force was used, determine whether the use of force was reasonable under the circumstances.
62. Prior to end of tour, electronically "sign-off" on STOP REPORT and return digital Activity Log back to the member.

> **Exam Alert:** In all scenarios — whether the stop was proper, improper, or the report was prepared in error — the Stop Report is **approved** — never voided. Even when the stop was improper, the supervisor approves and documents the issue. If prepared in error (was Level 1 or 2, or arrest/summons not the result of a Level 3), note in "Comments" section.

### UMOS — Upon Rejection of Stop Report

63. Access the report from FORMS "Inbox," go to "Actions" tab, select "Edit" and make the directed corrections.
    a. Select the "Check" icon to resubmit to supervisor for review and approval.
64. Prior to end of tour, print the digital Activity Log entry detailing the Terry Stop and submit to the desk officer/designee.

### Desk Officer/Designee Duties

65. Log into FORMS and select "SEARCH." A search must be conducted of all STOP REPORTS prepared during the tour.
66. If a STOP REPORT was prepared, print out the "approved" STOP REPORT from the queue and attach it to the printed digital Activity Log entry.
    a. Place STOP REPORT and photocopy of printed digital Activity Log entry into the command's **STOP REPORT binder** maintained at the desk in **sequential order**.

> **Exam Alert:** The Stop Report binder is maintained at the desk in **sequential order** — not chronological. This means filed by report number order, not by date/time.

### ICO Duties (Updated)

67. Personally conduct, in conformance with the Quality Assurance Division's self-inspection program, the command self-inspection of STOP REPORTS.
68. Ensure the patrol supervisor/unit supervisor reviews STOP REPORTS and digital Activity Log and that appropriate actions are taken.
    a. In assessing the patrol/unit supervisor's review: determine whether the supervisor appropriately reviewed the stop and, if conducted, the frisk and search, and any force used.
    b. Take appropriate remedial action if warranted, including discipline.
    c. Inform commanding officer and training sergeant of any matters of importance, including deficiencies or patterns in stop/frisk conduct or STOP REPORT preparation.

> **See Also:** PG 202-30 — The XO has the exact same duty as the ICO regarding stop report review.

### Executive Officer

69. Personally conduct the Quality Assurance Division's command self-inspection of "POLICE INITIATED ENFORCEMENT."

### Commanding Officer Duties (Updated)

70. Assume responsibility for the integrity of the administration of this procedure.
71. Consult with the XO, ICO, platoon commanders, special operations lieutenant, training sergeant, patrol supervisors/unit supervisors to ensure the constitutionality and effectiveness of stops.
    a. Identify training needs and necessary remedial or disciplinary actions required.
    b. Prepare a report on **Typed Letterhead** addressed to CO, Legal Bureau requesting remedial training for any members identified as having a deficient understanding of the law pertaining to street encounters.

### Training Sergeant Duties (Updated)

72. Conduct command level training to help ensure compliance with the Department's policy regarding investigative encounters.
    a. Periodically review and identify command-wide and individual training needs and necessary remedial actions.
    b. Record training sessions in the **Training Attendance Certification Transcript Integrated Collection System (TACTICS)** to assist with future review and analysis of command's compliance and training.
    c. Identify members who have been referred for training in STOP REPORTS and ensure that the training is conducted.
       - (1) Track, record and report such training to the commanding officer on a **quarterly** basis.

### Furtive Movements and High Crime Area

- "Furtive movements" or mere presence in "high crime area" alone are **insufficient** for a stop or frisk
- Even when used in combination with other stop factors, the stopping officer must be able to specifically describe the suspicious nature of the "furtive movements" observed
- Must not define "high crime area" too broadly (e.g., an entire precinct or borough)
- A person may not be stopped merely because he or she matches a **generalized description** of a crime suspect (such as an 18-25 year old male of a particular race)
- If a physical description is the **only factor** relied on, it must be more specific than race, age, and gender
- Individuals may not be targeted for stops and frisks because they are members of a racial or ethnic group that appears more frequently in local crime suspect data

> **Exam Alert:** Race may only be considered when the stop is based on a specific and reliable suspect description that includes not just race, age, and gender, but **other identifying characteristics and information**. When a police officer carries out a stop based on reasonable suspicion that a person fits such a description, the officer may consider the race of the suspect, just as the officer may consider height or hair color.

### Additional Data — Binder Maintenance

- Commands other than patrol precincts, PSAs, and transit districts will designate a supervisor to perform desk officer duties regarding STOP REPORTS.
- Photocopies of STOP REPORTS will be sent via Department mail to the precinct of occurrence daily. The precinct places them in their STOP REPORT command binder in sequential order.
- Desk officers/designees in commands other than patrol precincts, PSAs, or transit districts will maintain a standardized STOP REPORT command binder. A corresponding **Stop Report Index** will be printed out daily and maintained in the binder.
- Commanding officers will ensure photocopied STOP REPORTS maintained in the command binder are **removed and filed** in the command by year of occurrence every **January 1st** and quarterly thereafter (**April 1st, July 1st, and October 1st**).

> **Exam Alert (Updated):** STOP REPORT binders are purged quarterly (Jan 1, Apr 1, Jul 1, Oct 1) and filed by year of occurrence. The Stop Report Index is printed daily.

### Forms and Reports Referenced

| Form | Number |
|------|--------|
| Right to Know Business Card | PD142-012 |
| Right to Know Business Card - General | PD142-013 |
| Common Law Right of Inquiry - Consent Search Report | PD541-161 |
| Stop Report | PD383-151 |
| Investigative Encounters | PD383-090 |
| Supplemental Instructions for Preparation of Stop Report | PD383-151A |

---

# Section 212 — Elections

## P.G. 212-86 — Prior to Opening of Polls

### Arrival Time
- Arrive at the poll site at least **1 hour** prior to opening.

### Duties Before Polls Open
1. Deliver election supplies and obtain receipt from Board of Elections members.
2. Inspect **ALL** scanner machine "public counters."
3. Confirm Board of Elections posted no-electioneering signs within **100 feet** of entrance.
4. Check for suspicious packages and Election Law violations.

### Scanner Machine Inspection

| Counter Type | Expected Reading | Action if Not Expected |
|-------------|-----------------|----------------------|
| **Public counter** | Must be **ZERO (0)** | Notify desk officer/supervisor immediately. Scanner **NOT** used if counter is not zero. |
| **Protective counter** | Will likely **NOT** be zero | This is normal — do NOT confuse with public counter |

> **Exam Alert:** The **public counter** must read zero. The **protective counter** will likely NOT be zero — do not mistake one for the other. If the public counter is not zero, the scanner shall NOT be used and the supervisor must be notified immediately.

---

## P.G. 212-87 — While Polls Are Open

### Election District Staffing

| Requirement | Details |
|------------|---------|
| Full staffing | **4 members** — 2 from each of the top 2 political parties |
| Minimum staffing | **2 members** from different parties |

### Officer Duties
- Remain in poll site unless required for other duty or meal.
- Remain **outside the guardrail** except to perform a specific duty.
- Do **NOT** take part in the election process.

### Persons Permitted Within the Guardrail
- Election inspectors
- Voters
- Authorized Board of Elections representatives
- Attorney General
- Authorized poll watchers
- Persons admitted for the purpose of maintaining order

### Poll Watcher Rules

| Rule | Detail |
|------|--------|
| Maximum per candidate per ED | **3** poll watchers |
| Inside guardrail per candidate per ED | Only **1** |
| Entry time | May enter before opening (**0500**) |
| Departure | May remain after closing |
| Prohibited actions | Electioneering, tampering, interfering, accompanying voter inside booth |

### Close of Polls

> **Exam Alert:** Polls close at **2100 hours**. Persons already on line at 2100 **may** vote. **NO** new persons may join the line after 2100.

### Person Denied Right to Vote
- Refer to the **Supreme Court** of the county concerned.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Supervisors must ensure officers understand the guardrail boundaries, do not participate in the election process, and properly handle disputes about voting rights by referring to the county Supreme Court.

---

## P.G. 212-88 — After Close of Polls

### Post-Closing Duties
- Secure election materials according to Board of Elections procedures.
- Inspect scanner machines after closing.
- Ensure orderly conclusion of voting process.
- Maintain security of poll site until materials are properly transferred.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Ensure all election materials are accounted for and properly secured. Verify that the transition from active voting to post-closing procedures occurs smoothly and without interference.

---

# Section 212 — Firearms and Licensing

## P.G. 212-44 — Response to Firearms Dealers Locations

- Follow established response procedures for incidents occurring at licensed firearms dealer locations.
- Ensure proper coordination with License Division and investigative units.

---

## P.G. 212-46 — Unlawful Possession of Rifles/Shotguns

- Follow procedures for handling illegal rifle and shotgun possession.
- Ensure proper vouching and notification of investigative units.

---

## P.G. 212-84 — Verification of Pistol License

### Verification Steps
1. Request documentary ID **in addition to** the pistol license.
2. Check for valid license features.

### Valid License Features

| Feature | Detail |
|---------|--------|
| Seal | Embossed Police Department seal |
| Validity period | **2 years** |
| Expiration | On licensee's **birth date** |

### Verification Resources

| Resource | Availability |
|----------|-------------|
| License Division (telephone) | **0900-1700, Monday-Friday** |
| ALPS (Automated License and Permit System) | Electronic verification |

### If License Division Closed / ALPS Unavailable
- Desk officer contacts License Division at **0900 next business day** for verification.

### Questionable Validity
1. Retain the license and firearms.
2. Have a Complaint Report prepared.
3. Forward Typed Letterhead to CO, License Division.

> **Exam Alert:** Pistol licenses are valid for **2 years** and expire on the licensee's **birth date**. Two features to remember.

---

## P.G. 212-85 — Rifle/Shotgun Permit Suspended/Revoked/Disapproved

### Assignment
- CO assigns MOS (**preferably a sergeant**) for immediate investigation.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Sergeants are the preferred rank for conducting rifle/shotgun permit surrender investigations.

### Procedure at Licensee Address
1. Respond to the licensee's address.
2. Request surrender of firearms, permit, and registration.
3. Prepare 3 copies of Surrender Notice:

| Copy | Destination |
|------|------------|
| Original | Rifle/Shotgun Section |
| Copy | Precinct file |
| Copy | Receipt for permittee |

### Investigation Deadline

> **Exam Alert:** Investigations must be completed and returned within **60 days**.

### Refusal to Surrender
- If permit holder refuses or fails to surrender: serve summons for Admin Code **section 10-303** (Criminal Court).
- Do **NOT** serve summons for unrenewed permits (administrative matter).

### Priority Cases
- Serious crime conviction
- Mental incompetence
- Consult **Legal Bureau** regarding search warrant for priority cases.

---

## P.G. 212-118 — Incidents Involving Holders of Handgun Licenses or Rifle/Shotgun Permits

- Follow established procedures for incidents involving licensed firearm holders.
- Ensure proper documentation and notification of License Division.

---

## P.G. 212-128 — Extreme Risk Protection Order (ERPO)

### Definition
A court-issued Order of Protection prohibiting the purchasing or possessing of **any** firearm.

### Triggering Factors
- Threat, violence, or stalking
- Order of Protection violation
- Pending weapons charge
- Reckless firearm display
- History of ERPO violations
- Substance or alcohol use
- Recent firearm acquisition combined with one of the above factors (a-f)

### Evaluation Process

| Step | Responsibility |
|------|---------------|
| Initial evaluation and confirmation | Immediate supervisor |
| Record in Activity Log | Immediate supervisor |
| Determine probable cause (self-harm/harm to others) | Immediate supervisor |
| Final determination on filing | Legal Bureau ERPO Unit |

### Legal Bureau ERPO Unit Availability

| Time | Contact |
|------|---------|
| Monday-Friday, 0900-1700 | ERPO Unit |
| 1700-2300 and weekends | Duty attorney |

### Service and Execution
- **Temporary ERPO** served by **NYC Sheriff's Office** — NOT NYPD.
- Intelligence Division assists if a search provision is granted.
- Department attorney appears with MOS at all hearings.

> **Exam Alert:** Temporary ERPOs are served by the **NYC Sheriff's Office**, not NYPD. This is a commonly tested distinction.

### License Division Role
- Conducts background check for final hearing:
  - Prior convictions and pending charges
  - Parole/probation status
  - Registered firearms
  - Any Orders of Protection

### Firearm Retention

> **Exam Alert:** Firearms seized under ERPO are retained for a minimum of **2 years**. No release without written authorization from CO, License Division (plus CO, Medical Division if the subject is a MOS).

### ERPO Renewal
- ERPO Unit reviews **30 days before expiration** to determine if renewal is warranted.

### District Attorney ERPOs
- DA Offices can file ERPOs for arrests/open complaints **independently** of NYPD.

### Exigent Circumstances Exception
- ERPO is **NOT** required when:
  - Imminent harm exists, AND
  - Firearm is on person or readily accessible

### MOS as Subject of ERPO
- Notify CO **without delay**.
- Modified Assignment per A.G. 318-08.

> **Sergeant Focus:** When a subordinate MOS becomes the subject of an ERPO, the supervisor must ensure immediate CO notification and initiation of the Modified Assignment process.

---

# Section 212 — Hazardous Materials, Bombs, and Fire

## P.G. 212-37 — Hazardous Materials

### On-Scene Coordinator

> **Exam Alert:** The On-Scene Coordinator for a hazmat incident is the **highest ranking uniformed supervisor** at the scene — this could be a Patrol Supervisor.

### Inter-Agency Command Center (IACC)

- Separate and distinct from the police temporary HQ (Command Post)
- IACC Log maintained by assigned UMOS

### Trigger for Response

- Involving **suspected** hazmat OR where **legality** of hazmat shipment is questionable
- If legality is questionable, Motor Carrier Safety Unit (MCSU) member will clarify

### Minimal Outdoor Distances

| Distance | Condition |
|----------|-----------|
| **300 feet** | Any spill; any fire EXCEPT military shipment or tanker truck |
| **1,000 feet** | Any explosion OR suspected device; fire involving military shipment or tanker truck |

> **Exam Alert:** Memorize the two outdoor distances: 300 feet for spills/fires, 1,000 feet for explosions/suspected devices/military/tanker fires. These distances may be **expanded** as needed.

### Indoor Distances

| Condition | Distance |
|-----------|----------|
| **Spill** | Just that room |
| **Fire or Explosion** | All rooms/areas where person may be exposed, including floors **above and below** |

### Radiation

- Evacuate to the distance where the radiation pager alarm shuts off (per PG 212-102)

### UMOS Duties

- Notify Desk Officer AND Communications Section
- Establish minimum "frozen areas"
- Request response of Patrol Supervisor, ESU Supervisor/Personnel, AND Highway District MCSU

### Patrol Supervisor Duties

- Evaluate and confer with ESU Supervisor/member OR MCSU member
- If radiation: notify NYC Dept. of Health
- Notifications and request for Bomb Squad: request **Communications** to notify **Operations** Unit and have Bomb Squad respond

> **PG Conflict:** In HazMat (212-37), the **Sergeant** calls the Bomb Squad through Communications Unit and Operations. In PG 218-37 (Exploding Currency), the **UMOS** requests the Bomb Squad if bank personnel confirm they passed a dye pack. Know who makes the request in each procedure.

- Maintain frozen area security until hazmat is secured/neutralized
- Establish Command Post
- Advise Operations by phone with all info (location, time, source, danger, materials, markings, other agencies, action taken)
- Highway Units: if vehicle involved, detain for safety check; notify Highway Supervisor for escort duties

### Frozen Area Rules

> **Exam Alert:** Exclude vehicle and pedestrian traffic from the frozen area. Restrict entry to those properly **trained and equipped** for hazmat — EXCEPT when **absolutely necessary** to save life or prevent serious injury.

### When Incident Involves "SECTAR"

- **S**ignificant Fire, **E**xplosion, **C**hemical Burn OR **T**oxic **A**ir Release / **R**adiation Hazard to personnel/public
- This elevates from suspected/questionable to an actual hazmat incident

### On-Scene Coordinator Expanded Duties

- Assume control; establish IACC (as close as possible to incident, separate from Command Post)
- Ensure communications link between Operations and IACC
- Assign UMOS to maintain IACC Log
- Notify Operations immediately with all material details (spell out chemical names carefully)
- Request Mobile Decontamination Vehicle (MDV) **through Operations**
  - If on-scene coordinator unavailable: FDNY Deputy Chief+, EMS Director+, or DEP Director/Asst Commissioner+ may also request (through Ops)
- Establish police lines; evacuate to sufficient distance
- Parking: designate on high ground and/or **upwind** to avoid contamination
- Traffic Plan "C" (PG 213-13): consider implementing
- Detain exposed persons (except those needing medical treatment); obtain names/addresses for Command Post Log
- Coordinate with FD, DEP, Sanitation to prevent hazmat from entering sewer system
- After Action Critique: critique with agency reps and advise OEM of results

### Entries in Logs

| Entry | Command Post Log | IACC Log |
|-------|-----------------|----------|
| Medical treatment (names/addresses of exposed not needing emergency care) | Yes | Yes (same) |
| Significant info, personnel arrival/departure | Yes | Yes (same) |
| Radiation (ESU reports name/shield of exposed MOS) | Yes | Not specifically required |
| Safety Officer ID | Yes | Not specifically required |

### Substances Other Than Liquids Rendered Safe

- ESU Supervisor advises Patrol Supervisor to have Dept. of Sanitation notified to clean up and dispose of substances **other than liquids** that have been rendered safe

### C.O. Precinct of Occurrence

- Document on Report on Typed Letterhead (49)
- Have transcript of all Command Post Log entries put on 49
- Forward to **C.O. Police Lab**

> **Exam Alert:** The CO does NOT prepare the 49 — the CO has it prepared. The unusual recipient is the C.O. Police Lab, not Operations or Borough.

---

## P.G. 212-40 — Bombs

### Key Notification Rules

> **Exam Alert:** For bomb incidents (212-40): No Desk Officer notification, No Borough notification, No Bureau notification. Only Communications Unit and Operations.

### Key Numbers

> **Exam Alert:** Stop search **20 minutes** before threatened explosion time. Do NOT resume search for at least **1 hour after** threatened time. Evacuate to at least **1,000 feet**.

### MOS Receiving Threat

- Obtain all relevant info and details
- Immediately notify Communications Unit (C.U.) and give details
- Provide caller's location and telephone number if available

### Responding UMOS

- Respond to location; ID and interview caller
- Ascertain caller's location/phone; obtain nature of threat (time, location, device type)
- Request response of Patrol Supervisor and ESU

### Patrol Supervisor/Ranking Officer on Scene

- Review actions taken
- Determine if additional personnel or equipment needed
- Confer with premise owner/representative (not the caller)
- Search: ensure preliminary search/evacuation based on type of premise, location, nature of business, prior history

### Threatening Letter/Package by Mail/Delivery

- UMOS: request Patrol Supervisor response
- Safeguard letter/package in suitable envelope/bag to minimize handling
  - Mark "For Latent Fingerprint Processing"
  - **Do NOT copy** contents of letter/package
- Make list of ALL who handled it (include Tax & Shield #s)
- Request Laboratory Examination; immediately forward with Request for Laboratory Examination form

> **See Also:** PG 218-09 — Questioned documents may be secured in PSE using cardboard inserts. Do not write on envelope once document is inside.

### Unattended Article

- Do NOT move or disturb — **ONLY** examined/disturbed by UMOS in the Bomb Squad
- Assess threat to public safety based on: prior history, sensitive location, physical indications (exposed wires, oil stains, odor), recent political events, high-traffic area
- If owner identified: have owner retrieve property
- If **no owner** and/or **possible danger to public safety**: treat as Suspected Explosive Device

### Suspected/Reported Explosive Device

- UMOS: evacuate if necessary
- Notify C.U. via **landline** (if available)
  - If landline unavailable: use radio or cellphone from position of hard cover, as far as possible from item, after everyone cleared from immediate area

> **Exam Alert:** Do NOT use cellphone or radio **near** a suspected explosive device. The proper method is landline first. Only use radio/cellphone from hard cover at maximum distance.

- Request: Operations notified, Patrol Supervisor, ESU, and **Duty Captain**

### Search Teams

- Use stairs, NOT elevators (exception: elevators inaccessible to public)
- Do NOT use radio or cellphone while anyone is in **direct proximity** to discovered item
- Do NOT touch or disturb a suspected explosive device under any circumstances
- Discontinue search **20 minutes prior** to threatened explosion time, regardless of evacuation status
- Do NOT resume search for at least **1 hour after** threatened time

### Evacuation Distance

- At least **1,000 feet** from unattended article or suspected device
- May increase or decrease based on circumstances, location, ESU/Bomb Squad recommendation

### ESU Safeguards Device

- ESU safeguards device pending arrival of Bomb Squad
- Absent an ESU Supervisor, Senior ESU member on scene determines necessary preliminary steps

### Post-Explosion Bomb Scene

- Ensure Bomb Squad notified and responding
- Search for deceased, injured, trapped persons immediately
- Instruct searchers to be cautious of **secondary devices**
- Limit search teams to minimum number of UMOS
- Once search/evacuation done: no one allowed in (including FD) **unless** a fire exists
- Do NOT correct dangerous conditions UNTIL Bomb Squad declares area free of secondary devices AND crime scene search is completed
- Direct **ESU** to remove injured to safe location for medical treatment (not EMS)

> **Exam Alert:** At a post-explosion scene, direct **ESU** to remove injured persons — NOT EMS. Watch for exam answers that say "direct EMS to remove" — the correct answer is ESU.

- Safeguard but do NOT move bodies of deceased persons
- Have **EMS** administer medical treatment at **safe locations**
- All UMOS evacuate to safe distance (1,000+ feet) and await Bomb Squad after initial search
- Document: Complaint Report for bomb threat, suspected device, or post-explosion incident

---

## P.G. 212-58 — Fire

### Radio Dispatcher Duties

- Immediately assign available unit to respond
- Notify Patrol Supervisor
- Broadcast new information received via ICAD or from scene
- Notify Patrol Supervisor and any other responding supervisors of all available information

### UMOS Duties

- Park vehicles to prevent interference with firefighting
- Ensure area in front of building secured for FDNY; fire hydrants free of vehicles
- Notify dispatcher: give 10-84; ensure FDNY responding; provide pertinent details
- Direct responsible **person** to remain in front to direct fire apparatus if fire not in view
- Warn/assist evacuated persons; prevent persons from reentering building; secure lobby

### Patrol Supervisor Duties

- Respond to **ALL** building/structure fires in command

> **See Also:** PG 202-19 — Patrol Supervisor responds to: **W**eapons, **E**mergency, **B**urglary, **S**erious Crime.

- Confer with FDNY **supervisor** (not just any firefighter)
- Tactical Retreat: if smoke and heat suddenly present, advise MOS to evacuate/tactically retreat to predetermined exit
- If fire is suspicious: notify Desk Officer AND request precinct detective squad
- Ensure access for emergency vehicles; confirm fire hydrants accessible; maintain clear path for ambulances

### Persons Permitted Through Fire Lines

- The Mayor and vehicle
- Ambulance, Police, and FDNY vehicles
- Members of govt. agencies and vehicles, **in performance of duty**
- Employees of public service corporations and vehicles, in performance of **emergency** duty
- Red Cross personnel **in performance of duty** (NO vehicles)
- Persons holding valid **unexpired** press credentials (NO vehicle)

> **Exam Alert:** Red Cross personnel and press credential holders are allowed through fire lines on foot only — NO vehicles. Public service corporation employees must be performing **emergency** duty specifically.

### UMOS Documentation

- Digital Activity Log: location, time, persons displaced/injured, cause, extent of damage, suspicion of arson, dangerous conditions
- If fire is suspicious: Complaint Report
- Aided Report for each injured person
- Secure premises once firefighting concluded

### Desk Officer — FRUITS Notification

> **Memory Aid — FRUITS:** When Desk Officer notifies Operations AND Borough:
> - **F**orced entry by F.D.
> - **R**elocation of tenants
> - **U**nusual fire
> - **I**njury/death
> - **T**hree alarms or more
> - **S**uspicious fire

### Two Alarm Fires

- When fire reaches 2nd alarm: **FD** notifies NYPD Operations Unit, who directs duty captain to scene

> **Exam Alert:** At **2 alarms**, FD notifies Operations. At **3 alarms**, the Desk Officer notifies Operations. Know who makes the notification at each alarm level.

### First NYPD Supervisor at Scene

- Proceed immediately to FDNY Incident Command Post
- Remain until relieved or Command Post demobilized
- Advise FDNY Incident Commander of MOS inside affected building (number, unit, last known location)

### CIMS Primary Agency Matrix — Fire Suppression

- Incident Commander (IC) is the **ranking FDNY officer**
- NYPD co-locates its command post with FDNY's
- Highest-ranking NYPD uniformed supervisor: remain with IC, work and coordinate with Primary Agency
- PD responsibilities: site management; accountable for inner perimeter, crowd/traffic control

### MOS Entering Building on Fire — Last Resort

- NYPD not trained to fight fires; should AVOID entering
- Notify dispatcher of all observations **PRIOR to entry**
- Inform FDNY on scene how many MOS entered, including last known location
- Use stairs IN TEAMS OF TWO, NOT elevator
- Inspect floor **below** fire floor; note other stairways, exits, areas of refuge
- Stairwell door: place back of hand to detect heat
  - Unusually warm door = "immediately dangerous to life and health" (requires PPE)
  - Do NOT open — drawing smoke
- Absent exigents, MOS should NOT operate on any floor **ABOVE** the fire
- Do NOT hold elevator on any floor — FDNY will recall elevators

> **Exam Alert:** When approaching a fire floor, MOS should inspect each floor **below** the fire floor. They should NOT operate above the fire. Use prone position/crawl, stay close to wall, use wall as guide in smoke conditions.

---

# Section 212 — Housing Operations

## P.G. 212-26 — Inspection of NYCHA Facilities

### Covered Facilities
- Community centers
- Child care centers
- Maintenance shops
- Health centers
- Senior citizen centers
- Laundry rooms
- Boiler rooms

### Inspection Schedule
- Check each facility on post during the **first AND last hour** of each tour, if possible.
- **Frequently** inspect security during the tour.
- Give special attention to **"Post Condition"** facilities.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Patrol supervisors shall inspect Activity Logs to ensure NYCHA facilities are being inspected by members on post. Supervisors should recommend Departmental Recognition for burglary arrests at NYCHA facilities.

> **Exam Alert:** Inspections are required during **both** the first and last hour of each tour — not just one or the other.

---

## P.G. 212-28 — Disabled Housing Authority Elevator

### Key Points
- Procedure governs handling of passengers trapped in NYCHA elevators.
- Respond promptly to reports of disabled elevators with trapped occupants.
- Notify appropriate NYCHA maintenance personnel.
- Ensure safety of trapped individuals until professional assistance arrives.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Patrol supervisor should respond to ensure proper coordination between NYPD and NYCHA maintenance staff, particularly when occupants include elderly or medically vulnerable individuals.

---

## P.G. 212-60 — Interior Patrol of Housing Authority Buildings

### Purpose
Covers systematic interior patrols of NYCHA buildings to maintain safety and deter criminal activity.

### Key Procedures
- Conduct thorough interior patrols of NYCHA buildings on post.
- Check stairwells, hallways, rooftops, and common areas.
- Document patrol activity in Activity Log.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Supervisors shall review Activity Logs to verify interior patrols are being conducted and documented. Ensure members are varying patrol times and routes to maximize deterrent effect.

---

# Section 212 — Interaction Procedures

## P.G. 212-104 — Interaction with Persons Who Are Deaf or Hard of Hearing

### Legal Foundation
- **Americans with Disabilities Act** (ADA)
- State and local law
- Department policy

All require **effective communication** with persons who are deaf or hard of hearing.

### Primary Consideration
- The type of auxiliary aid **requested by the individual** is the primary consideration.

> **Exam Alert:** The individual's own preference for the type of auxiliary aid is the **primary consideration**. The Department does not unilaterally decide the accommodation.

### Cost
- Must **NOT** charge the person for auxiliary aids or services.

### Qualified Interpreter Requirements

| Requirement | Detail |
|------------|--------|
| Must interpret | Effectively, accurately, and impartially |
| Family member/child/friend | May **NOT** be qualified — especially in domestic disputes |

> **Exam Alert:** A family member, child, or friend may **not** serve as a qualified interpreter, particularly in domestic dispute situations where impartiality is compromised.

### Communication Resources

| Resource | Details |
|----------|---------|
| **TRS (Telecommunications Relay Service)** | Dial **711** |
| **TTY number** | (212) 504-4115 for 311 system |
| Sign language interpreter | Contact **Operations Unit** |

### Arrest Procedures
- Arrest proceeds normally with probable cause.
- Auxiliary aids used for communication regarding charges, interrogation, etc.
- Arrestee permitted to use **TRS** for phone calls.
- **Qualified interpreter** provided for communication during processing.

### Pre-Planned Events
- CO prepares a **Typed Letterhead** request to the **Chief of Personnel** when a sign language interpreter is needed for a pre-planned event.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Supervisors must ensure that communication accommodations are provided promptly and that the individual's preferred method of communication is the primary consideration. For pre-planned events, arrange interpreter services in advance through the Chief of Personnel.

---

# Section 212 — Media and Information

## P.G. 212-49 — Incidents Involving Media Representatives

*(Updated R.O. 93, effective 10/03/25)*

### Purpose
To cooperate with members of the press by not interfering or allowing others to interfere with media personnel acting in their news gathering capacity.

### Definitions

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **Member of the Press** | An individual who gathers, reports, or licenses news or content for publishing, broadcasting, or cablecasting (radio, TV, newspapers, magazines, wire, books, Internet). Includes employees of news-gathering organizations, independent contractors, freelancers, and self-employed persons. |
| **MOME Press Credential** | City of New York Press Card issued by the Mayor's Office of Media and Entertainment (MOME) Press Credentials Office allowing bearer to pass NYPD/city official lines and attend official city-government sponsored events open to the press. |

### MOME Press Card Holders

| Right | Details |
|-------|---------|
| Cross police/fire lines | Access **WILL NOT** be denied |
| Interior crime scenes | Access **NOT** included |
| Security-restricted areas | Access **NOT** included |
| Proximity | As close as possible with clear line of sight and hearing range (unaided by electronic device) |

> **Exam Alert:** **Only** MOME-issued Press Card holders are entitled to cross police and fire lines. However, this does **not** include access to interior crime scenes or areas designated off-limits for security reasons.

### Press Access Rules (UMOS)

1. Adhere to the following guidelines unless safety or police duties require otherwise:
   a. Press given access as close as possible with clear line of sight and hearing range (unaided by electronic device)
   b. Press access to demonstrations on private property shall not be impeded
   c. Press shall **NOT** be arrested for criminal trespass on private property **unless** owner expressly indicates the press is not to enter or remain
   d. If ranking officer determines press access must be restricted, they retain the discretion to do so
2. Determine if any threat to safety of press exists; take appropriate action.
3. Request response of patrol supervisor.
4. Take enforcement action as necessary.

### First Amendment Activity Rules

> **Exam Alert:** During First Amendment Activity, MOME press are **NOT** required to leave the area even if NYPD orders persons to leave. They shall move to a **safe location** instead. At the discretion of the incident commander, press may be directed to a different location where they may still see and hear ongoing newsworthy events and will **not** be subject to arrest for documenting police activity or for not leaving the general area. See P.G. 213-21.

### Patrol Supervisor Duties

5. Cooperate with and assist members of the press; provide safe access to the scene.
6. Conduct **immediate investigation** if a member of the press is assaulted, harassed, or their vehicle/equipment is vandalized/damaged.
7. Attempt to obtain third party witnesses if confrontation with MOS or others.
8. Request all parties, including witnesses, to report to precinct of occurrence for further investigation when necessary.
9. Notify **without delay**:
   a. Office of the Deputy Commissioner, Public Information (DCPI)
   b. Desk officer
   c. Lieutenant platoon commander

> **Sergeant Focus:** Patrol supervisor must investigate immediately when a press member is assaulted or harassed. Notification to DCPI must be made without delay. DCPI is available via phone **24/7**.

### Desk Officer

10. Notify:
    a. CO/XO/duty captain
    b. Operations Unit

### Lieutenant Platoon Commander

11. Respond to command (if in the field) and under supervision of CO/XO/duty captain, conduct investigation.
12. Prepare report on **Typed Letterhead** containing details of incident and results of investigation. Forward to:
    a. Chief of Patrol/bureau chief concerned
    b. Borough commander concerned
    c. CO, Public Information Division
    d. Command file

> *NOTE: The CO/XO/duty captain will perform the duties of the lieutenant platoon commander if the LPC is unavailable.*

### Commanding Officer/Duty Captain

13. Forward supplementary report, if necessary.
14. Respond to command and supervise the investigation and preparation of the report.

### Recording and Photography Rules
- Members **WILL NOT** interfere with videotaping or photographing in public places.
- Blocking or obstructing cameras or harassing the photographer constitutes **censorship**.
- Members may **NOT** set up police lines or frozen zones to prevent press from viewing or recording the scene from a public place.

### Credential Seizure

> **Exam Alert:** Members may **NO LONGER** seize press credentials under **any** circumstances, except when seized in the normal course of an arrest and held for safekeeping like other types of non-evidentiary, non-investigatory personal property.

### Prohibited Retaliatory Actions
- May **NOT** take police action in retaliation for:
  - Expressing a viewpoint
  - Engaging in First Amendment Activities
  - Exercising the right to witness, observe, record, comment on, or protest police activity

### "Red Light" Offense Arrest of Press

- If arrested for a "Red Light" offense (per P.G. 213-21) and presents a MOME-issued credential or official government-issued press credential: processing **shall** be approved by the **incident commander** or ranking member from **DCPI**.
- **Presumption:** summons issued **on scene**, if eligible.
- Press member may be removed to arrest processing facility **only** if incident commander determines issuing summons on scene would create a health/safety risk.
- Applies to all "Red Light" arrests of press regardless of whether it occurs at a First Amendment Activity event or not.

### Government-Issued Press Credentials from Other Jurisdictions

- Shall be verified to the extent possible at the time of encounter.
- If unable to verify on scene, officers **shall** consider the following indicia of validity:
  - Name matches another form of ID
  - Contains a photograph
  - Details the issuing city or agency
  - Includes the associated media outlet
  - Indicates whether the individual is independent
  - Includes an expiration date, serial, or ID number
- Members **should** confer with DCPI if necessary.

### Press Card Age Requirements

| Type | Age Requirement |
|------|----------------|
| Standard/Reserve Press Card | At least **18 years old** |
| Single Event Press Card | Under **18** may apply |
| Under 16 with Single Event card | Must be accompanied by an **adult** (1 adult per child in restricted area) |

### DCPI Notification
- Notify DCPI **immediately** for **ANY** incident involving the press, regardless of outcome.
- DCPI available **24/7**.
- DCPI will maintain a central repository of all reports received of incidents involving members of the press.

### MOME Verification
- MOME is available to confirm validity of press credentials.
- Contact the **Operations Unit** for the MOME phone number and contact information.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Supervisors must ensure members understand that press access cannot be restricted through police lines or frozen zones designed to block recording. Any incident involving the press requires immediate DCPI notification. The patrol supervisor must conduct an immediate investigation if press members are assaulted or harassed.

---

## P.G. 212-77 — Release of Information to News Media

### General Rule

- **Media inquiries should be directed to DCPI**
- MOS provide assistance ONLY when:
  - It does not pose a threat to personal safety of MOS, the public, or the news media
  - It does not interfere with police operations, crime scene investigations, or other emergency service providers
  - It does not infringe on the rights of the accused or privacy/security of crime victims
- Even if other agencies are involved, ALL media requests go through DCPI
- Unauthorized communication with news media without conferral with DCPI is **PROHIBITED**

> **Exam Alert:** MOS are required to **immediately** notify DCPI of any interaction with the media. This is an absolute requirement.

### Highest Ranking UMOS Addressing Media

- Prior to addressing media, confer with DCPI regarding info to be released
- Briefly describe incident: how called to scene, type of job, location, # of aideds, etc.

### Crime Scenes or Frozen Areas

> **Exam Alert:** Media access to an incident does NOT extend to interior crime scenes or frozen areas. This applies to all media, including MOME press card holders (per PG 212-49).

### Information NOT to Be Released

- **Juvenile under 18** taken into custody — EXCEPT Juvenile Offender or Adolescent Offender (can be released)
  - If case drops from Criminal Court to Family Court: offender becomes Juvenile Delinquent and ID can NOT be released from that point forward
- **Abused child** — neglected or abused child or child complainant under 18
- **Sex crime** victim ID or location of sex assault if victim's residence or perp's residence (if not apprehended)
- **Communicable disease** info indicating a person has one
- **Arrest/booking photo** unless it would serve a law enforcement purpose
- **Witness or confidential informant** information
- **MOS, complainant, or witness** — address or phone # of any of these three; info that may hinder prosecution or jeopardize safety

### Civil Rights Law — Sex Crime Victim ID

- Allows victims to bring private action if their ID is disclosed
- MOS releasing records **must** ensure sex offense victim identity is NOT divulged
- Proper release of sex crime victim ID is limited to:
  - Defendant and their counsel or guardian
  - Public officers/employees investigating, prosecuting, and keeping records relating to offense

### Communicable Disease — Confidentiality

- Prisoner or victim status regarding communicable disease (HIV, AIDS, etc.) is confidential
- Generally do NOT release to public, media, family, friends, or other prisoners
- Any release requires written consent of the **DCLM** (Deputy Commissioner, Legal Matters)

### Prisoner Volunteers Medical Status

- If prisoner volunteers positive medical status or is treated medically: use isolation cells if available
- Document on Prisoner Movement Slip **or** Medical Treatment of Prisoner (either form)

### Releasable Information Post Arrest

- **Pedigree:** name, age, residence, employment, marital status, similar background (after ID confirmed)
- **Charge:** substance or text of charge
- **Complainant info:** only when appropriate, ID of complainant
- **Agency involved:** identity of investigating/arresting agency and length of investigation
- **Arrest information:** circumstances surrounding arrest (time, place, resistance, pursuit, possession/use of weapons, description of items seized)

### Release ONLY After Conferral with DCPI AND Chief of Detectives — "A RAT COP"

> **Memory Aid — RATCOP:** Information released only after conferral with DCPI AND Chief of Detectives:
> - **R**eputation — statements concerning reputation/character of accused or witness
> - **A**nticipated testimony of prospective witness
> - **T**est — performance of tests, results of tests, OR refusal by accused to take a test
> - **C**redibility of prospective witness
> - **O**pinion/speculation concerning evidence or argument in the case
> - **P**hotos, films, videotape and/or mug shot

> **Exam Alert:** RATCOP items require conferral with **both** DCPI and Chief of Detectives before release. This is a commonly tested mnemonic.

---

## P.G. 212-76 — Information Concerning Official Business of Department

### General Principles
- Treat official business as **CONFIDENTIAL**.
- Understand the public's right to be informed.
- Exercise discretion when divulging authorized information.

### Authorized Releases
- Specific agencies may receive specific categories of information per Department guidelines.

### Requests for Statistics/Surveys
- Forward to **Office of Management Analysis and Planning**.

### Medical Records

| Requestor | Requirements | Fee |
|-----------|-------------|-----|
| MOS (own records) | Written request to CO, Medical Division | **No fee** |
| Outside parties | Written request with notarized authorization | **$5 fee** |

### Grants and Equipment Offers

> **Exam Alert:** Members may **NOT** accept offers of grants, equipment, or funding from elected officials, agencies, or private entities. All such offers must be directed to the **Police Commissioner** in writing.

### Sensitive Information from Elected Officials
- Submit the request in writing to the **Police Commissioner**.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Ensure subordinates understand that all grant/equipment/funding offers must be forwarded to the Police Commissioner — members have no authority to accept directly.

---

# Section 212 — Miscellaneous Procedures

## P.G. 212-14 — Traffic Intelligence Report

*(Updated R.O. 108, effective 10/24/25)*

### Purpose
To report traffic conditions that need to be addressed by the NYPD or other outside agencies (i.e., collision-prone locations, chronic traffic congestion-prone locations, double parking, requests for signs or signal lights, pavement markings, construction projects, etc.).

### When Prepared
Whenever a uniformed member observes a traffic condition that is not immediately correctable and further assistance is required, OR when a collision-prone location is identified by the Traffic Accident Management System (TAMS).

### Uniformed Member of the Service

1. Prepare **TRAFFIC INTELLIGENCE REPORT (PD178-150)** when a traffic condition is observed and is not immediately correctable by available resources.
2. Indicate on the report:
   a. Specific type of condition(s)
   b. Times
   c. Place(s) of occurrence
   d. Any corrective measures taken or recommended
3. Utilize reverse side for detailed description, diagram(s), and additional comments.
4. Submit completed report to the **administrative QOL officer**, command of occurrence, before end of tour.

> *NOTE: If the condition was observed on a limited access highway, submit the completed report to the highway unit concerned.*

### Administrative Quality of Life Officer

5. Review Traffic Intelligence Report(s) daily and indicate corrective action taken and recommendations.
6. Maintain a **Precinct/Highway Unit Traffic Intelligence Report Log** captioned:
   - Precinct Serial #, Date of Report, Location, Condition, Corrective Action Taken, Recommendation, Date

> *NOTE: Reports concerning the same condition at the same location during the same week (Mon-Sun) will be grouped together and given one precinct serial number.*

### When a Collision-Prone Location Has Been Identified by TAMS

7. Conduct a survey of each collision-prone location to determine contributing factors.
8. Prepare TRAFFIC INTELLIGENCE REPORT for **each** collision-prone location.
9. Confer with executive officer regarding conditions correctable at the precinct level.
10. Establish liaison with outside agencies (NYC DOT, Parks, DEP, Buildings, etc.) to address conditions that cannot be corrected by precinct personnel.

> *NOTE: If the report concerns an engineering recommendation, a copy is forwarded to the DOT borough commissioner's office. A telephone notification is also made.*

11. Forward copies of TRAFFIC INTELLIGENCE REPORTS to the borough traffic safety coordinator **WEEKLY** and indicate:
    a. Corrective actions taken and/or recommendations for further measures
    b. File copies for precinct/highway unit records

> *NOTE: Conditions requiring immediate attention (potholes, missing traffic signs, inoperable signal lights, etc.) will NOT be forwarded to the borough traffic safety coordinator. Corrective action must be initiated at the scene, including notification to the precinct telephone switchboard operator for entry on the HIGHWAY CONDITION RECORD (PD311-151).*

### Operations Coordinator

12. Ensure each Monday the **WEEKLY STREET CONDITIONS SURVEY (PD311-150)** is reviewed and appropriate agencies/members notified.

### Borough Traffic Safety Coordinator

13. Maintain a borough Traffic Intelligence Report Log with additional column for borough serial number.
14. Confer with borough executive officer regarding conditions correctable at borough level.
15. Forward copies to **Traffic Management Center ONLY** where precinct or borough personnel cannot correct the condition AND it is not reportable on the Weekly Street Conditions Survey or Highway Condition Record.

### Traffic Management Center Traffic Safety Officer

16. Review and analyze TRAFFIC INTELLIGENCE REPORTS and:
    a. Assist precinct or patrol borough in correcting condition(s)
    b. Confer with outside agencies, if applicable
    c. Report findings to the commanding officer

> *NOTE: A TRAFFIC INTELLIGENCE REPORT will be prepared for all incidents involving a construction site (refer to P.G. 214-16). Include all permit numbers, indicate if a MOSAICS computer check was made and results.*

> **Exam Alert:** The Administrative QOL Officer reviews Traffic Intelligence Reports **daily**. Reports are forwarded to the borough traffic safety coordinator **weekly**. Conditions requiring immediate attention (potholes, missing signs) are NOT forwarded -- they require immediate action at the scene.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Preparation of a Traffic Intelligence Report does not relieve precinct personnel from taking other corrective action to expedite traffic flow and ensure pedestrian safety.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Patrol supervisor must respond personally when forced entry is detected at a DOE facility and assign a member to guard until the premises are secured.

## P.G. 212-52 — Labor Dispute

### UMOS

- Preserve peace and prevent destruction of property
- Request Patrol Supervisor
- Notify Desk Officer

### Patrol Supervisor

- Respond to the scene
- Interview management and union representatives **separately**
- Advise both sides of the following rules:
  - No sticks/poles that may be injurious; no professional agitators/offensive language
  - Precinct **C.O.** determines the number of pickets allowed
  - Force/violence not tolerated; right of public to use streets/sidewalk protected
  - Law enforced impartially
- Obtain information for Strike Report
- Notify: telephone D/O for **immediate** assignment of personnel, if required
- Document: deliver Strike Report to D/O
- Command Post: set up if required (**NOT** in facilities of Management or Labor)

> **Exam Alert:** The Command Post for a labor dispute must NOT be set up in the facilities of either management or labor. This ensures the appearance of neutrality.

### Desk Officer

- Confer with C.O./Duty Captain regarding personnel assignments
- Make assignments as directed; obtain personnel from Pct. if available, or request from Borough
- Notify Operations
- Document: have Strike Report prepared and forwarded; sign above "Reviewed By" caption

### Harassment and Labor Disputes

> **Exam Alert:** Certain Penal Law sections of harassment will NOT be enforced at labor disputes:
> - **Harassment 1st degree (PL 240.25):** Prohibits intentionally and repeatedly harassing by following in public place; engaging in conduct placing person in reasonable fear of physical injury
> - **Harassment 2nd degree (PL 240.20):** Course of conduct that alarms/seriously annoys with no legal purpose
>
> However, any **violence or physical harassment** WILL be enforced — if they strike/shove/kick or attempt/threaten physical contact.

- Picketing, leafleting, and demonstrating are exempt from harassment arrest
- May refer **victim** to Court for injunction or NLRB (National Labor Relations Board)

---

- Covers bicycle patrol operations and procedures.
- Members assigned to bicycle patrol follow specific equipment and safety guidelines.

## P.G. 212-66 — City Policy Concerning Identifying Information

## P.G. 212-67 — Police/Clergy Program

*(Updated R.O. 86, effective 09/23/25)*

### Purpose
To formalize police/clergy cooperation; provide instruction and direction to both police and clergy; and to aid the Department in its effort to advance the concept of Community Policing.

### Definitions

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **Police/Clergy Liaisons** | Designated members available to assist the Department in: (1) Maintaining calm, (2) Seeking community cooperation during and after emergencies (bias incidents, hostage cases, EDPs), (3) Acting as unofficial recruiters, (4) Attending appropriate events (Precinct Community Council meetings, Youth Councils, etc.) |
| **Police/Clergy Volunteers** | Selected by precinct commanders based on precinct needs. Function within precinct boundaries as recruiters, advisers, and a bridge between community and precinct. |

### Precinct Commander — When a Vacancy Occurs

1. Notify Deputy Commissioner, Community Affairs Bureau, in writing, as to cause of vacancy.
2. Nominate a member of the clergy to fill vacancy using the vacancy nomination form.
3. Forward nomination to Deputy Commissioner, Community Affairs Bureau, through channels.

> *NOTE: Nominations should not exceed more than **five per precinct**. Deputy commissioners and bureau chiefs may also make nominations direct to the Deputy Commissioner, Community Affairs Bureau.*

4. Direct maintenance of a log listing all recruited and selected clergy (liaisons and volunteers) captioned:
   - Title, Field Name/Address, Telephone Numbers, Religious Institutions, Times Available, Languages Spoken, Other Special Qualifications

### Police/Clergy Review Committee

5. Review nominations.
6. Forward recommendations to the Police Commissioner.

> **Exam Alert:** Maximum of **five** clergy nominations per precinct. The Police/Clergy Review Committee reviews nominations and forwards recommendations to the PC.

## P.G. 212-100 — Auxiliary Police

### Coordinator Requirement
- A **coordinator** is required for **ALL** patrol duties.

### Age Restrictions

| Age | Permitted Activities |
|-----|---------------------|
| 17 | **Administrative functions only** |
| Under 18 | **NO** patrol duties, **NO** undercover QOL assignments |
| 18+ | Full auxiliary duties with coordinator |

### If Coordinator Unavailable
- Auxiliary members assigned to **command facility ONLY**.
- **NEVER** patrol without a coordinator.

> **Exam Alert:** Auxiliary police shall **NEVER** patrol without a coordinator. If the coordinator is unavailable, auxiliaries are assigned to the command facility only. Members under 18 may not patrol or perform undercover QOL — age 17 is limited to administrative functions.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Supervisors must verify coordinator availability before any auxiliary patrol deployment. No exceptions to the coordinator requirement.

# Section 212 — Special Incidents

## P.G. 212-36 — Incidents Involving Former/Retired MOS or Off-Duty Law Enforcement

### Initial Response
1. Ascertain identity of the individual.
2. Determine if the individual is armed.
3. Request personal ID and pistol license.
4. Request to see NYPD ID Card (PD416-091) or other official law enforcement ID.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Patrol supervisor shall respond and approve resolution (arrest, summons, court referral, or no action).

> **Exam Alert:** These individuals shall **NOT** receive preferential treatment based on former or present law enforcement status. This is a critical principle tested on exams.

### Notifications
- Desk officer notifies Operations Unit for incidents involving agents from **other agencies**.

---

## P.G. 212-43 — Vacate Orders for Residential Buildings

### Authority
- Issuing agency (Buildings, Health, Fire, HPD) serves the order.
- NYPD **assists** — does not serve the order.

### Issuing Agencies
- Department of Buildings
- Department of Health
- Fire Department
- HPD (Housing Preservation and Development)

### Procedure
1. Verify credentials and authority of agency representative.
2. NYPD assists in execution of the vacate order.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Patrol supervisor shall consult the duty captain when:
> - Doubt exists about the authority of the agency representative
> - Large-scale relocations are involved
> - Occupants refuse to vacate
> - Potential for violence exists

### Key Timelines

| Responsibility | Deadline |
|---------------|----------|
| HPD seals building | Within **72 hours** |
