| Investigation and Review Section contacted | **24 hours** in advance |

> **Exam Alert:** HPD is responsible for sealing the building within **72 hours**. Investigation and Review Section must be contacted **24 hours in advance**.

---

## P.G. 212-45 — Foreign National Requesting Asylum

- Handle asylum seekers in accordance with Department policy.
- Ensure proper notifications and referrals to appropriate agencies.

---

## P.G. 212-47 — Counterfeit Money

### When Counterfeit Money Is Detected

- Determined that the passer is an innocent victim, OR
- No indication who passed it

### Investigating Officer

- Document: have person **last in possession** of the bill:
  - Write name/date across the **face of the bill** or scratch initials **on** a coin
- Sign: rank, name, shield, date on bill or scratch initials on coin

### Desk Officer

- Document: prepare **4 copies** of report on Typed Letterhead (49x4) to **Chief of Patrol**
- Deliver:
  - Have messenger sign itemized receipt in **Property Receipt Book**
  - Deliver 49x4 and counterfeit bill to **Patrol Borough** office
  - Messenger brings back 1 copy of the 49 as a receipt from Borough
- Receipt: attach receipted copy of 49 to the entry in the Property Receipt Book

> **Exam Alert:** No voucher is prepared for counterfeit money. The bill goes to the Patrol Borough office with the 49x4, not to the Property Clerk.

### Cross-References for Counterfeiting

> **See Also:** PG 208-47 — Arrests for counterfeiting: D/O or counterpart notifies Dept. of Treasury, US Secret Service **by phone direct**.

> **See Also:** PG 207-06 — Complaints of counterfeiting: D/O notifies Operations; Operations notifies US Treasury Dept., Secret Service Bureau.

---

## P.G. 212-54 — Person Threatening to Jump from Structure

### Immediate Actions
1. Notify radio dispatcher.
2. Request patrol supervisor, ESU, and ambulance.
3. Request Harbor Unit if location is over navigable waters.

### Intervention Techniques
- Attempt to persuade or prevent person from jumping.
- Seek assistance of relatives, friends, or clergy.
- Confine person to the **side facing the street**, if possible.
- Rope off area below.

### Cross-Reference
- Follow **P.G. 221-13** (Mentally Ill or Emotionally Disturbed Persons) as appropriate.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Patrol supervisor shall take command of the scene, coordinate with ESU, and ensure the area below is secured. Consider requesting additional resources (negotiators, mental health professionals) early.

---

## P.G. 212-55 — Investigation of Aided Cases — Illegal Fireworks Injuries

### Procedure
1. Request patrol supervisor and precinct detective squad.
2. Issue summons or arrest for illegal fireworks possession.

### Aided Under 18 Years Old
- Determine extent of adult involvement.
- Arrest for **Endangering Welfare of Child** if probable cause exists.

### Database and Notifications
- **Intelligence Division** maintains database of all fireworks injury incidents.
- **Detective Bureau Wheel** notified 24/7 for ongoing investigations.

> **Exam Alert:** When the aided person is under 18, determine adult involvement and consider charges of Endangering Welfare of Child.

---

## P.G. 212-57 — Service of Family Court/Supreme Court Orders of Protection

*(Updated R.O. 104, effective 10/20/25)*

### Purpose
To assist in the service of all orders of protection and any associated papers issued by Family Court or Supreme Court.

### Desk Officer — When Order of Protection Is Presented at a Department Facility

1. Accept the order of protection from the member of the public or Court.

> *NOTE: Courts and advocacy organizations encourage petitioners to contact the NYC Sheriff in the borough of issuance so that the NYC Sheriff may serve the order. However, if a member of the public appears at a Department facility seeking assistance, the member of the service must accept the order.*

2. Have the petitioner complete the **NYC Dept. of Finance, Office of the Sheriff, Service of Process Intake (SH-0609)** form.

### Monday Through Thursday (0300-2300) and Friday (0500-2300)

3. Fax a copy of the order and the SH-0609 form directly to the NYC Sheriff's Operations Desk.
4. Confirm receipt via telephone.
5. Make an entry in the **Order of Protection Log**: petitioner's name, respondent's name, name of court, docket number, and confirmation of NYC Sheriff's receipt.

### Order of Protection Log Format

| Left Page | Right Page |
|-----------|------------|
| Pct. Serial #, Petitioner's Name, Respondent's Name, Docket #, 1st Tour UMOS Assigned/D.O., Time/Date of Attempt(s) | Date & Time of Attempt(s), (Through), 6th Tour UMOS Assigned/D.O., Date & Time of Attempt(s), Final Disposition, Time/Date NYC Sheriff Office Received, Time/Date DVP Officer Entered In Registry |

### All Other Times

6. Make appropriate entries in the Order of Protection Log.
7. Assign a uniformed member to serve the order of protection.
   a. Indicate name under caption "1st TOUR UMOS."

### If Order of Protection Is Served

8. (UMOS) Attempt to serve the order.
9. Prepare **STATEMENT OF PERSONAL SERVICE (PD260-152)** and a photocopy.
   a. Examine and account for all documents served; ensure appropriate box is checked.
10. Make a digital **Activity Log** entry.
11. Deliver form to desk officer.
12. Forward photocopy of the Statement of Personal Service to **domestic violence prevention officer**.

> *NOTE: When preparing Statement of Personal Service forms for orders issued by Supreme Court, strike out "Family Court" and write in "Supreme Court."*

### Desk Officer

13. Enter into the Order of Protection Log that the order was served.

### Domestic Violence Investigator

14. Coordinate all tasks relating to service and recording of orders of protection (filing, forwarding, etc.).
15. Enter applicable information for any order of protection served by the Department into the **State Order of Protection Registry**, via the FINEST system, upon receipt of each photocopy of a Statement of Personal Service.
16. Enter the date and time entry into the Registry was made under the appropriate caption in the Order of Protection Log.
    a. Place initials inside same caption.
17. Forward Statement of Personal Service to Family Court or Supreme Court clerk for any order of protection served by the Department.

### If Unable to Serve Order of Protection

18. (UMOS) Make a digital Activity Log entry of **each attempt** to serve the order.
19. Return the order and related forms at end of tour to the desk officer.

### Desk Officer

20. Repeat steps "6" and "7" at least once **each consecutive tour** until the NYC Sheriff's Office reopens or the order is served.
    a. Indicate name under caption "2nd Tour UMOS Assigned/Desk Officer."
21. Repeat steps "3" and "4" for further processing when assigned member is unable to serve and the NYC Sheriff's Office has reopened.
22. Enter into the Order of Protection Log that the order was forwarded to NYC Sheriff.

### Key Rules

- Each attempt to serve **shall** be documented in the member's digital Activity Log (respondent's name, docket number, pertinent information).
- Service must be attempted **at least once each tour** until effected or forwarded to the NYC Sheriff.
- Assigned UMOS will inquire of neighbors, superintendent, etc. as to respondent's whereabouts.
- If respondent has moved, attempt to obtain new address; if within precinct, attempt service at new location.
- If new address is outside the precinct, indicate in digital Activity Log and return order to desk officer for forwarding to appropriate command.

### Exclusionary Order of Protection

> **Exam Alert:** When an exclusionary order has been served (prohibits contact and/or excludes respondent from a location), uniformed members may **NOT** assist respondents who wish to remove clothing/property ("clothes jobs"). The respondent should be advised to return to court to have the order amended. However, this does **NOT** prevent a member from accompanying the **PETITIONER** if the petitioner has safety concerns about returning to a shared location to retrieve clothing/personal property.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Ensure desk officers are maintaining the Order of Protection Log properly and that service is attempted at least once per tour. The Domestic Violence Investigator is responsible for entering service information into the State Order of Protection Registry via FINEST.

---

## P.G. 212-56 — Diplomatic Incidents

### Diplomatic Immunity — Core Rules

| Action | Permitted? |
|--------|-----------|
| **Arrest** | **NO** — shall not be arrested |
| **Issue summons** | **YES** — may be issued summons |
| **Enter UN HQ** | **NO** — not without Secretary General consent |
| **Detain** | **YES** — pending verification, if danger to self/public |

> **Exam Alert:** Diplomats with immunity shall **NOT** be arrested but **MAY** be issued a summons. This is a frequently tested distinction.

### Notifications (Immediate, by Telephone)
1. Operations Unit
2. Intelligence Division

### Verification Process
- Intelligence Division verifies diplomatic status through:
  - Mayor's Office for International Affairs
  - US Mission to the United Nations
  - US Department of State

### DWI and Diplomatic Immunity

| Element | Rule |
|---------|------|
| Sobriety test/breathalyzer | May **ASK** — diplomat entitled to **REFUSE** |
| Documentation | Document request and response in Activity Log and Unusual Occurrence Report |

> **Exam Alert:** MOS **may ask** a diplomat to take a breathalyzer test, but the diplomat is entitled to **refuse**. Always document the request and response.

### Even with Full Immunity
- Prepare **ALL** normally required reports.

### Parking Summons
- Issuing a parking summons on diplomat/consul plates for a safety hazard is **NOT** a diplomatic incident.

### Emergency Child Removal
- With diplomatic immunity: directly supervised by **CO or duty captain**.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Do NOT detain a properly identified and verified diplomat. All reports must still be prepared even when immunity applies.

---

## P.G. 212-61 — Burglary/Robbery Apprehension Module (BRAM)

*(Updated R.O. 27, effective 05/01/25)*

### Purpose
To reduce the occurrence of burglaries/robberies by identifying and apprehending those individuals committing burglaries/robberies.

### Scope
The Precinct Detective Squad/Burglary Robbery Apprehension Module (PDS/BRAM) will maintain a dedicated focus on burglary/robbery investigations and shall be under the direct organizational control and supervision of the Precinct Detective Squad Commander.

### Uniformed Member of the Service — Upon Receiving a Burglary or Robbery Complaint

1. Determine whether a burglary or robbery has in fact been committed by conducting preliminary investigation.
2. Broadcast description of perpetrator(s) and conduct search, if appropriate.
3. Safeguard evidence; follow crime scene procedures per P.G. 212-04.
4. Interview complainant and initiate investigation.
   a. If perpetrator(s) can be identified, transport complainant to station house to view photos.
5. Prepare **COMPLAINT REPORT WORKSHEET (PD313-152A)** for robbery.
   a. Prepare for burglary if Burglary Apprehension Team member is not working.
   b. Comply with P.G. 207-01 (Complaint Reporting System).
6. Notify **Detective Borough Evidence Collection Team (ECT)** to respond to **all** burglary incidents, including situations where an arrest is made at the scene.
7. Notify Precinct Detective Squad who will immediately notify the designated PDS/BRAM investigator, when available.
   a. Provide all available information necessary to continue investigation.
   b. Notify Detective Borough Night Watch if PDS/BRAM office is closed (0100-0800 hours).

> *NOTE: Copies of robbery/burglary complaints recorded on COMPLAINT REPORTS in the precinct on late tours will be picked up at 0800 hours by a PDS/BRAM member.*

### Desk Officer

8. Review and finalize the completed **COMPLAINT REPORT** in OMNIFORM System.
9. Have a copy forwarded to the Precinct Detective Squad/BRAM.
10. Ensure additional copies are sent to other units/agencies, as required.

### PDS/BRAM Investigator

11. Review burglary and robbery complaint reports and assist in identifying patterns or trends.
12. Confer with the Precinct Field Intelligence Officer, when assigned, prior to and following the issuance of a pattern.
13. Compile a pattern case folder for all identified patterns, documenting all investigative steps taken.
14. Maintain photo books in accordance with established Legal Bureau guidelines.
15. Confer with precinct and borough patrol and investigative units and disseminate all information regarding burglary and robbery trends, patterns, and wanted perpetrators.
16. Update and maintain the Precinct Crime Information Center with active **INVESTIGATION CARD (PD373-163)**, Field Information posters, serial burglar lists, and photos.
17. Conduct an investigation when the subject of a "Home Visit" cannot be located by the Precinct Burglary Apprehension Team (BAT).
18. Cultivate and maintain sources of information within command (confidential informants, building superintendents, landlords, block watchers, etc.).
19. Debrief all suspects arrested for burglary/robbery to:
    a. Identify accomplices
    b. Identify fencing locations
    c. Identify other victims
20. Be responsible for reviewing the list of persons selling items to pawnshops or secondhand dealers.
    a. Conduct INVESTIGATION CARD check, warrant investigation, criminal record check.
21. Assist the Burglary Apprehension Team with "Fencing Sting Operations."
22. Take major case prints for every person arrested for the crime of burglary.

### PDS/BRAM Supervisor

23. Be responsible for the review of all burglary/robbery complaints received at the office to determine case status.
24. Review all **COMPLAINT REPORTS (PD313-152)**, Omniform Complaint Revisions, and associated Enterprise Case Management (ECMS) entries to determine whether cases should remain ACTIVE or CLOSED, and to ensure all necessary investigative steps have been taken.
25. Assign robbery and burglary cases amongst all detectives in the unit.
26. Ensure the crime information centers are updated accordingly.
27. Ensure pawnshop/secondhand dealer inspections are properly investigated.
28. Verify major case prints are taken for every person arrested for burglary.

### Key Elements of BRAM Program

| Element | Detail |
|---------|--------|
| Specialized BRAM unit | Created within **every** Precinct Detective Squad |
| Command structure | Under direct control of PDS Commander |
| Personnel allocation | Commensurate with reported burglary/robbery cases |
| Liaison | One PDS/BRAM member designated liaison with Borough Robbery Squad and Intelligence Division |
| Pattern identification | Detective Bureau notifies Patrol Borough Pattern ID Modules on all alerts |
| Photo books | Required — contain photos of persons previously arrested for burglary, robbery, and grand larceny |

> **Exam Alert:** The ECT (Evidence Collection Team) shall be notified to respond to **ALL** burglary incidents, including those where an arrest is already made at the scene. This is a frequently tested point -- ECT responds even when an arrest has occurred.

> **Sergeant Focus:** The PDS/BRAM Supervisor is responsible for reviewing all burglary/robbery complaints to determine case status (ACTIVE or CLOSED) and ensuring all investigative steps have been taken. Major case prints must be taken for every burglary arrest.

---

# Section 212 — Technology and Systems

## P.G. 212-12 — Citywide Intelligence Reporting System

- General intelligence reporting system for sharing and tracking intelligence information across commands.
- Follow established procedures for submitting and accessing intelligence reports.

---

## P.G. 212-78 — Utilization of Real Time Crime Center (RTCC)

- RTCC provides intelligence and investigative support to field personnel.
- Utilize RTCC resources for real-time information during investigations and critical incidents.

---

## P.G. 212-99 — AMBER Alert System

### Activation Criteria
- Child must be **17 or younger**.
- Police must believe the child is in **imminent danger** of serious bodily harm or death.
- May be activated for abduction by vehicle, foot, or any other means.

### Who May Request Activation
- Only a **Captain or above** from the **Detective Bureau** may request activation through the NYS Police Communications Section.

> **Exam Alert:** Only a **Captain or above from Detective Bureau** may request AMBER Alert activation. Not a patrol captain — specifically Detective Bureau.

### Activation Process

| Step | Responsibility |
|------|---------------|
| Notify NYS Police | Detective Bureau Duty Captain |
| Notify Operations Unit | Detective Bureau Duty Captain |
| Broadcast description over radio divisions | Communications Section |
| NCIC File 6 completed | Investigative unit |
| NYSPIN File 11A (Abduction Message) | Generated automatically |
| Major Case Unit notified | Detective Bureau |

### Vehicle Involvement
- If a vehicle is involved: special radio message regarding **License Plate Reader System** — manually enter the plate number.
- If vehicle description AND plate available: fax to **Traffic Management Center** for Variable Message Signs.

### Cancellation

| Timeframe | Method |
|-----------|--------|
| Within **8 hours** | Fax to NYS Police |
| After 8 hours | Via **DCPI** media notification |

> **Exam Alert:** AMBER Alert cancellation within **8 hours** is by fax to NYS Police. After 8 hours, cancellation is through DCPI media notification. The Detective Bureau Duty Captain ensures proper cancellation.

### Non-Eligible Cases
- **DCPI** available 24/7 for cases not eligible for AMBER Alert activation.

---

## P.G. 212-108 — NCIC/VGTOF Notifications

### VGTOF — Violent Gang and Terrorist Organization File

### Handling Codes

| Code | Meaning | Action |
|------|---------|--------|
| **Handling Code 1** | **ARREST** | Warrant exists — approach with caution |
| **Handling Code 2** | **DETAIN** | Reasonable suspicion — active investigation |
| **Handling Code 3** | **INVESTIGATION** | Do NOT alert individual — possible terrorism ties |
| **Handling Code 4** | **INFORMATION GATHERING** | Do NOT alert individual |

> **Exam Alert:** Memorize all four VGTOF Handling Codes. Code 1 = Arrest (warrant), Code 2 = Detain (reasonable suspicion), Code 3 = Investigation (do not alert, terrorism), Code 4 = Information gathering (do not alert).

### No Hit but Terrorism Suspected
- Contact **Intelligence Division Criminal Intelligence Section**.

### FBI Unable to Confirm
- Do **NOT** arrest unless probable cause exists for a federal, state, or local law violation.

---

## P.G. 212-123 — Body Worn Cameras (BWC)

### Who Must Wear BWC

> **Exam Alert:** Desk Officer, Telephone Switchboard Operator, and Station House Security are **required** to wear BWC while performing duty. These are positions you might not expect to need a BWC.

### Platoon Commander Duties (If No P/C: D.O. or Unit Sup)

- At start of tour: account for all BWCs assigned to command and enter details **in the Command Log**
- Once per tour: review BWC video footage; log in to ID videos not properly categorized; instruct users to categorize as appropriate; document in Command Log Entry
- Prohibited recording: notify C.O./Duty Captain
- At EOT: account for all BWCs; ensure all returned to docking station for upload and recharging

### UMOS Rules

- Prior to roll call/start of tour: obtain BWC from docking station; turn on and inspect
- Position: attach to outermost garment near breast pocket area for optimal recording field of view (use mounting hardware provided)
- Use: ONLY if personally issued AND authorized by Dept. to record official activity while on-duty
- Use of non-Department device is **strictly prohibited**

> **Exam Alert:** Non-Department recording devices are strictly prohibited. The BWC must be personally issued to the member using it.

### Supervisor Conducting Roll Call

- After start of tour but prior to roll call: provide time for UMOS to obtain BWC from docking station
- Inspect: ensure BWC properly affixed to uniform/outermost garment, turned on, and working

### BWC Damaged/Unaccounted For

- Platoon Commander (or D.O./Unit Sup) immediately investigates
- Comply with PG 217-10 (Accidents — Dept Property) or PG 219-20 (Loss or Theft of Dept Property)
- Document in Command Log; notify ITB Service Desk for replacement

### Recording Rules — "Police Action"

**Police Action Defined:** Police service, law enforcement, or investigative activity conducted in furtherance of official duties, including:
- Responding to calls for service
- Addressing QOL conditions
- Handling pick-up assignments
- Any self-initiated investigative or enforcement actions (witness canvass, car stop, investigative encounters)

**NOT considered Police Action:**
- Routine consensual conversations with the public as part of community engagement
- Admin duty or non-enforcement function
- Routine activities in Dept facilities
- Dept meetings or training

> **Exam Alert:** Even though meetings/training are NOT police action, the D.O., Telephone Switchboard Operator, and Station House Security ARE required to wear BWC while performing duty.

### Timing

- Begin recording **prior** to arrival at incident location OR prior to any self-initiated police action
- Exceptions:
  - Vehicle operator may activate immediately **after** arrival if necessary for safe vehicle operation
  - Activate as soon as feasible after taking necessary action for human health/safety during unanticipated or exigent occurrences

### Mandatory Activation

- Police action (as defined above)
- Any interaction that escalates and becomes adversarial with potential evidentiary value
- Person under arrest or otherwise in custody
  - Immediately inquire if med/psych treatment necessary (ensure BWC activated when asking)

### Discretionary Activation

- Non-mandatory recording activities where officer judges it would be beneficial
- May discontinue recording if victim or witness requests anonymity during interview

### Prohibited Activation

- Off-duty employment (including paid detail)
- Sex crime victim interview (as soon as it becomes apparent)
- Strip searches
- Inside medical facility UNLESS necessary for police action (arrest, EDP, etc.)
- Certain briefings (tactical discussions, resource capabilities, after-action debriefs involving ESU)
- Confidential or undercover involved (CI/UC interviews or when CIs/UCs are present)

> **Exam Alert — Mandatory vs. Prohibited:** Mandatory takes precedence over prohibited. Example: effecting an arrest where undercovers and informants are involved on scene — **activate BWC** because the arrest is mandatory.

### If UC or CI Is Recorded

- Enter note in comments section of video management system that a UC is **visible**
- Detective Bureau supervisor prepares Report on Typed Letterhead to DCLM with description of officer and clothing

### Deactivation Rules

- Verbally document: state aloud on camera the reason prior to deactivation
- Crime scene established: may deactivate after arrival at active scene
- Subject NOT present: may deactivate after attempted apprehension/warrant execution when subject is not present

### Political Activity

- Handschu Guidelines: prohibit indiscriminate recording of political activity
- TARU documents protests, demos, political events by photo/video
- UMOS activate BWC at protest/demo/political event only when:
  - Enforcement action being taken
  - Unlawful activity occurring (whether or not enforcement action taken)
  - Directed by the **incident commander**

### IDTU Responds to Hospital/Medical Facility

- Tech and A/O both activate BWCs
- Record questions on Interrogation Warnings to Persons in Police Custody
- Record blood draw (whether by consent or court order)
- If medical personnel request stop recording: capture request if possible, turn off BWC, record request in Digital A/L

### BWC Documentation — Video Management System

- Select all applicable categories from dropdown list
- Arrests: enter complete arrest # beginning with borough letter in "ID" field
- Investigative encounters NOT ending in arrest/summons: select "Investigative Encounter" and select **final level** in "Add Metadata"
- All Level 2 encounters not escalating beyond Level 2: document apparent race, gender, more than one individual in "Add Metadata"
- Categorize **all** BWC videos within the **next two scheduled tours**

### End of Tour

- Insert BWC into docking station at station house to upload video and recharge battery

### Arrest Process

- Arresting/assigned officer: identify all BWC videos associated with arrest plus any pertinent video from **previous incidents**
- Prepare **two** checklists: District Attorney Body-Worn Camera Checklist AND Arrest Checklist
- Transmit video to DA via "Case Share"
- Desk Officer: ensure A/O shared video with DA and DA BWC Checklist prepared

### Viewing Recordings — "GO 15" (A.G. 318-11)

- Member may view own BWC video as subject or witness in official Dept investigation (GO 15 statement) prior to making statement at time/place deemed appropriate by supervisor in charge

### Failed to Record

- Patrol/Unit Supervisor: investigate when notified member failed to record mandatory encounter
- Document in **uniformed member's** Digital A/L (not supervisor's)
- Prepare Report on Typed Letterhead (49) to Professional Standards Bureau through channels

### Prohibited Recording

- Patrol/Unit Supervisor notifies Desk Officer (or Unit Commander if no D.O.)
- Commanding Officer: investigate; prepare Report on Typed Letterhead (49) to **Chief of Department** through channels, with copies to DC Information Technology and Professional Standards Bureau

### Typed Letterhead Comparison

| Fail to Record (Mandatory Event) | Record a Prohibited Incident |
|----------------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Patrol/Unit Supervisor** prepares 49 to Professional Standards Bureau through channels | **Commanding Officer** prepares 49 to Chief of **Department** through channels (copies to DC IT and PSB) |

> **Exam Alert:** For failure to record, the **Patrol Supervisor** writes the 49 (to PSB). For a prohibited recording, the **Commanding Officer** writes the 49 (to Chief of Dept). Know who writes and who receives in each scenario.

### BWC — Additional Data

- Access Code: cover the lens when inputting access code to keypad at Dept facility
- BWC is **Department property** (hardware, software, video, audio, captured data)
- Do NOT share with anyone except MOS for official purposes, prosecutors as described, or as authorized by PC
- Audit trail: video management system maintains audit trail for all transactions
- Video deleted after **39 months** (certain categories retained longer: arrest, homicide)
- CO may request longer retention through channels to DC Information Technology
- Disclosure: ONLY share with DA, US Attorney, or Special Prosecutor
  - Do NOT share with CCRB, ACS, NYC Law Dept, Corp Counsel (except Family Court prosecutions), or other police jurisdictions
  - Refer all requests to Legal Bureau's BWC Unit
- Show-ups: do NOT use still shot of video for a show-up
- Photo arrays: MAY use (print and paste) for photo arrays
- Witnesses/victims/civilians: cannot view recordings; refer to prosecutor or Legal Bureau Subpoena Litigation Unit
- FOIL requests: FOIL@NYPD.ORG or a860-openrecords.nyc.gov/new

> **Exam Alert:** BWC video is automatically deleted after **39 months**. Do NOT use BWC video stills for show-ups, but MAY use for photo arrays.

---

## P.G. 212-127 — Gunshot Detection

### UMOS

- Respond: canvass for perps, victims, evidence, witnesses; ensure BWC activated
- Ascertain whether alert is confirmed or unconfirmed
- Confirmed activations include:
  - Person shot or evidence of such (blood, blood trail)
  - Ballistics or firearms evidence
  - Property damage from gunfire
  - 1 or more 911 calls for shots fired
  - Eye/earwitness to the shooting (if necessary, establish crime scene)
- Whether confirmed or unconfirmed: notify Patrol Supervisor AND Communications
- Safeguard potential evidence of possible shooting
- Request TARU to retrieve video evidence
- Confirmed or unconfirmed — document:
  - Prepare 61 for ALL alerts; leave **OPEN** to Precinct Detective Squad
  - Note in narrative that incident originated as a gunshot detection alert
  - Ensure "Shots Fired" caption is checked regardless of classification

### Crime Classification for Complaint Report

| Condition | Classification | PD Code |
|-----------|---------------|---------|
| No other offense; no ballistics evidence | "Investigate Shots Fired" | **083** |
| No other offense; ballistics evidence found | "Investigate Shot Fired, Ballistics Recovered" | **088** |
| Shotspotter alert; no ballistics | "Investigate Gunshot Detection Alert" | **080** |
| Shotspotter alert with ballistics | "Investigate Gunshot Detection Alert, Ballistics Recovered" | **089** |

> **Exam Alert:** Know the four PD codes for gunshot detection classification. The key distinction is whether it is a Shotspotter alert (080/089) vs. general shots fired (083/088), and whether ballistics evidence was recovered.

### Patrol Supervisor

- Respond to **confirmed** alerts
- Notify and request response of PDS, C.O./X.O./Duty Captain, CSU or ECT as appropriate
- Notify Desk Officer with pertinent details

> **Exam Alert:** Operations and Borough are NOT specifically notified by anyone in the initial gunshot detection response — they receive notifications directly through the system.

### Desk Officer

- Confirmed alerts: update Operations, Patrol Borough, and Pct/PSA/TD C.O./X.O./Duty Captain

### C.O./X.O./Duty Captain

- Respond to all **confirmed** alerts
- Supervise response: ensure thorough canvass for evidence and victims

### 1st Platoon Desk Officer

- Review all 61s for previous 24 hours; ensure all gunshot alerts are **open** to the squad
- If 61 says no evidence recovered: review alert and determine if additional 911 calls exist
- Determine if daylight canvass necessary (review ICAD narratives, Complaint Report, audio)

### Neighborhood Coordination Officer

- Open companion case for confirmed alerts in Enterprise Case Management System (ECMS)
- Conduct daylight canvass for cameras, ballistics, witnesses, etc.

### Commanding Officer

- Review alert reports; ID problematic locations requiring additional resource deployment
- Chronic false alerts: document and notify **Information Technology Bureau (ITB)**

---

## P.G. 212-121 — Unauthorized Radio Transmission

- Follow established procedures for handling unauthorized use of police radio frequencies.
- Notify appropriate investigative and technical units.

---

## P.G. 212-129 — Facial Recognition Technology

### Fundamental Principle

> **Exam Alert:** Facial recognition does **NOT** establish probable cause by itself. It generates **investigative leads only**.

### Authorized Uses (ONLY)
1. Basis to believe person committed, is committing, or is about to commit a crime
2. Missing person, victim, or witness
3. Deceased person identification
4. Person incapacitated or unable to self-identify
5. Arrestee without valid ID
6. Imminent threat to health or public safety

### Key Definitions

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **Probe Image** | Image of an unidentified person from witnesses, victims, or reliable sources |
| **Photo Repository** | **ONLY** arrest and parole photographs — the approved database |
| **Possible Match Candidate** | An investigative lead ONLY — does **NOT** constitute probable cause |

> **Exam Alert:** The photo repository contains **only** arrest and parole photographs. No other image sources are in the approved database.

### Process Flow
1. Investigator submits probe image to **RTCC-FIS** (Facial Identification Section).
2. FIS assigns a tracking number.
3. Investigator confirms authorized use.
4. FIS runs the query against the photo repository.
5. Visual comparison performed.
6. Background check conducted.
7. **Peer review** of results.
8. **Supervisor final review**.
9. FIS Possible Match Report issued.

### Report Requirements
- Must include notification that a match alone does **NOT** constitute probable cause.

### Prohibited Uses
- Use of images **outside** the photo repository is **PROHIBITED** unless approved by:
  - Chief of Detectives
  - Intelligence and Counterterrorism Bureau
  - Chief of Intelligence

### Privacy
- All records are subject to privacy and confidentiality restrictions.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Supervisors conduct the final review in the facial recognition process. Ensure all subordinates understand that a possible match is an investigative lead only and must never be presented as establishing probable cause.

---

# Section 212 — Tour Operations

## P.G. 212-01 — Roll Call Formations

*(Updated R.O. 109, effective 10/24/25)*

### Purpose
To inspect, instruct and conduct roll call of uniformed members of the service.

### Timing
- **Five minutes** after the start of tour of duty.

### Patrol Supervisor Duties

1. Assemble platoon in ranks in the sitting or muster room.
2. Make assignments.
3. Inspect the platoon:
   a. Allow uniformed members to reasonably demonstrate that a **protective vest** is being worn.
   b. List members deficient in uniform, equipment or personal appearance.
      - Pay special attention to seasonal/optional uniform items (AG 305-03, AG 305-04).
   c. Reprimand and instruct deficient members **privately**.
   d. Follow up to determine if corrections have been made; take disciplinary action if necessary.
4. Ensure QOL officers and administrative QOL officers are **not** utilized as backfill for other precinct assignments (shooting posts, prisoner transports, hospital posts, patrol supervisor, desk officer, etc.) without authorization of the commanding officer.
5. Ensure QOL officers, administrative QOL officers, and QOL sergeants are not assigned to out-of-precinct details during their regularly scheduled tours without permission of the Bureau Chief concerned, the Chief of Department, or the Police Commissioner.
6. Ensure members are familiar with integrity concerns.
7. Ensure members are aware of active Paid Detail locations and times of assignments at these locations within the precinct.
8. Conduct a **"platoon briefing"** informing uniformed members of:
   a. Information compiled by training sergeant,
   b. Incident critiques,
   c. Updates regarding crime patterns, crime trends, and wanted persons, and
   d. Precinct conditions and tactics for addressing the conditions, etc.
9. Report personally to ranking officer, members absent and reason, if known.
10. Instruct platoon not to congregate in groups around the stationhouse.
11. Direct members of platoon to leave stationhouse for post or assignments at selected intervals.

> **Exam Alert:** The platoon briefing is a **Patrol Supervisor** responsibility. It covers training sergeant info, incident critiques, crime patterns/trends/wanted persons, and precinct conditions/tactics.

### Protective Vest Inspection

> **Exam Alert:** Upon notification that a protective vest inspection will be conducted, the uniformed member must take **appropriate actions** to demonstrate the vest is being worn:
> - Raising, removing, unbuttoning top garment to expose the vest to visual inspection, OR
> - Patting on the front and rear of the vest to produce a sound, OR
> - Any other manner indicating to the patrol supervisor that the vest is being worn

> **Sergeant Focus:** Only authorized/approved protective vests will be worn.

### Lieutenant-Platoon Commander

12. Supervise roll call.
    a. Personally inspect the outgoing platoon to ensure personal appearance meets Department standards.

### Special Operations Lieutenant

13. Ensure QOL officers and admin QOL officers are **not** utilized as backfill without authorization of the Commanding Officer.
14. Ensure QOL officers, admin QOL officers, and QOL sergeants are not assigned to out-of-precinct details without permission of the Bureau Chief, Chief of Department, or Police Commissioner.

### Travel Time Note

> *Sergeants, police officers and detectives required to report in uniform to a location within the geographic boundaries of the precinct to which assigned at the beginning of their tour, shall be given sufficient travel time within the tour.*

---

## P.G. 212-02 — Meal Period

*(Updated R.O. 6, effective 01/07/26)*

### Purpose
To provide for meal periods, obtain authorization for out of command meal, obtain approval from competent authority for meal period, and report meal location to radio dispatcher.

### Uniformed Member of the Service

1. Take meal in either a **police facility**, **bonafide restaurant**, or **Department vehicle**.
2. Notify radio dispatcher at beginning of meal, of location where meal is to be taken.
   a. If meal is to be taken in a Department facility, notify the desk officer.

> *Members shall commence and end their meal period on assigned post. Travel time to and from a meal location is not authorized.*

### Foot/Train Patrol

3. Obtain meal on assigned post, if possible, and in a place maintained for eating purposes.
4. Make digital **Activity Log** entry prior to leaving post including meal location address, and again upon returning to post.
5. Inform radio dispatcher upon return to post and make digital Activity Log entry.

### RMP Assignment

6. Notify radio dispatcher, by appropriate code signal, prior to commencing assigned meal period and upon return to patrol and provide:
   a. Address when meal taken in restaurant
   b. Nearest intersection when meal taken in RMP
   c. "Stationhouse" when meal taken in command facility
7. Remain in area designated by commanding officer, if meal is taken within command facility.
   a. Be available for assignment by desk officer.
8. Acknowledge calls directed to unit and:
   a. Remain within assigned sector, when practical
   b. Do not park more than one Department vehicle in the same place at the same time
   c. Comply with any request for police service and make digital Activity Log entry.
9. Notify desk officer when deprived of scheduled meal due to police service.

### Desk Officer

10. Take meal, when appropriate, providing another supervisor is assigned to cover desk officer responsibilities and is able to provide continuous police service within the command.
11. Make accommodations, if possible, so that members are not deprived of meal, if their originally scheduled meal is delayed due to police services rendered.
    a. Assign a police officer for relief of RMP operator or recorder, when necessary.
    b. Assign a police officer to provide meal relief for member assigned to fixed post, if available.

### Assigned Meal Relief Officer

12. Remain in view on post at time of relief when assigned to relieve radio motor patrol unit for meal.
    a. Assume all duties of recorder during relief period.

### RMP Operator

13. Transport relieved member directly to meal location.

### Uniformed Member on Meal

14. Notify desk officer if radio motor patrol fails to return within **fifteen minutes** after end of meal period.

> **Exam Alert:** If RMP fails to return within **15 minutes** after end of meal period, the member on meal must notify the desk officer. This is a specific time limit frequently tested.

### Patrol Supervisor

15. Notify radio dispatcher, via **landline**, from meal location when commencing meal period.
    a. Make digital Activity Log entry at beginning of meal (include address of meal location) and upon conclusion of meal period.

### Duty in Civilian Clothes

16. Request permission from immediate supervisor prior to taking meal.
17. Notify immediate supervisor of intended meal location.
    a. If immediate supervisor is unavailable, notify bureau/borough operations desk/Field Operations Desk (FOD) of intended meal location.
18. Document name of supervisor notified and meal location in appropriate Department record (digital Activity Log, movement log, etc.).

### Additional Data — Key Rules

| Rule | Detail |
|------|--------|
| **Intoxicants** | All on-duty members are **strictly prohibited** from consuming intoxicants in any amount during meal period |
| **Civilian clothes exception** | Members in civilian clothes may be granted permission by bureau chief/counterpart |
| **Meal eligibility** | Must be scheduled for **8 consecutive hours** of duty |
| **8/9-hour tour** | One hour meal period |
| **12-hour tour** | Two, non-consecutive, **45-minute** meal periods |
| **Not during first/last** | Members are not to be assigned meal periods during the **first hour or last hour and one-half** of their tour, except in emergency |
| **3-hour excusal rule** | If a police officer requests excusal of 3 hours or less, they are eligible for a meal period provided the request for partial excusal is made **prior to** start of the scheduled tour |

> **Exam Alert:** Know the meal period rules for different tour lengths: 8/9-hour tour = 1 hour; 12-hour tour = two non-consecutive **45-minute** periods. Meals are NOT assigned during the first hour or last 1.5 hours of the tour.

### Extended Tour Overtime Meal Rules

- Meal period on extended tour overtime may be granted by a supervisor **only** if assigned meal period was denied during the regularly scheduled tour.
- Such meal period may **not** be granted if it interferes with arrest processing or needs of the service.
- Under **no circumstances** will a rescheduled meal on extended tour overtime be assigned during the **last hour of overtime**.
- Once the event necessitating the overtime ends, a member who has not yet taken a meal period is **precluded** from taking any meal thereafter.

### Outside Details — Police Officers

- Police officers assigned to outside details (parades, events, etc.) **should** be granted a one-hour meal period absent exigent circumstances.
- Police officers denied a meal period are entitled to **one hour compensatory time** at straight time.
- This credit is **not** available if meal was denied due to exigent circumstances.
- Detail supervisors **shall** assign meal periods prior to posting the detail.
- If exigent circumstances prevent granting meals, commanding officers of outside details must submit a written communication to the Commanding Officer, Office of Labor Relations detailing the actual exigent circumstances. The Police Commissioner makes the final determination.

### Meal in Adjoining Precinct

- CO of a precinct may designate a portion of an adjoining precinct where members may obtain meals during hours when a suitable eating facility is not available within the precinct.
- Such designation forwarded on **Typed Letterhead** to the appropriate borough commander.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Supervisors must ensure meal periods are properly scheduled and documented. Key responsibilities include: assigning meal periods prior to posting details, ensuring members are not deprived of meals, and properly documenting any exigent circumstances that prevent granting meals.

---

## P.G. 212-03 — Expiration of Tour

### Foot Patrol
- Leave post and proceed directly to stationhouse at end of tour.
- Contact desk officer **1 hour prior** to end of tour when on assignment requiring relief.

### RMP Crew
- Proceed to stationhouse, park in relief area.
- Remain until relieved by incoming crew.
- Inform relieving operator of any incomplete assignments or vehicle defects.
- If RMP will **not** be used next tour: operator secures auto and delivers keys to desk officer.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Desk officer shall check CPR System Roll Call to account for all members from previous tour. Conduct **immediate investigation** if any member failed to scan out.

### 4th Platoon Rule

| Element | Detail |
|---------|--------|
| Platoon | 4th (1800x0200) |
| Trigger | Subpoenaed for next-day court (2nd platoon) |
| Reassignment | 3rd platoon (1600x2400) |
| Not notified in advance | Excused at 2400 hours |
| Overtime | **NO OT** for 2400-0200 period |
| Working beyond 0200 | Entitled to OT compensation |

> **Exam Alert:** The 4th platoon rule reassigns members to the **3rd platoon** (1600x2400), not the 2nd platoon. Members are excused at 2400 if not notified in advance, and there is **no overtime** for the 2400-0200 gap.

---

## P.G. 212-08 — Activity Logs

### Who Uses Digital vs. Paper

| Format | Who |
|--------|-----|
| **Digital Activity Log** (Department smartphone) | All uniformed MOS below captain rank (except permanent admin/clerical) |
| **Paper Activity Log** (PD112-145) | **Undercovers** — shall NOT use digital |

> **Exam Alert:** Undercovers use the **paper** Activity Log (PD112-145), never the digital version.

### Required Entries
- Day, date, and tour
- Assignment
- Meal time
- Operator and recorder name
- Vehicle inspection (condition, odometer, gas level)
- Chronological activities

### RMP Activity Log Rules
- **Only the recorder** makes chronological entries.
- Operator may note "See Recorder's Activity Log."
- **BOTH** operator and recorder **must** record their own enforcement actions.

### Digital Activity Log Features
- Can capture non-MOS signatures (physician, DA, etc.)

### Rosario Material Warning

> **Exam Alert:** Activity Logs are potential **Rosario material** — they may be reviewed by the Department, outside agencies, DAs, and defense attorneys.

### Restrictions
- **DO NOT** upload official Department arrestee photos to the digital Activity Log (sealed upon termination of criminal action).
- Disclosure or sale of Activity Logs is **prohibited** under NY City Charter sections 2604(b)(4) and 2604(d)(5) — violation carries financial penalties and possible misdemeanor charges.

---

## P.G. 212-10 — Interrupted Patrol Log

### Purpose
Controls time spent in the stationhouse by platoon members during their tour.

### Procedure
1. Member reports to desk officer with reason for presence in stationhouse.
2. Desk officer makes entries **in own handwriting**:

| Column | Content |
|--------|---------|
| Time In | When member arrived |
| Rank | Member's rank |
| Name | Member's name |
| Assignment | Current assignment |
| Reason | Why member is in stationhouse |
| Time Out | When member departed |
| Total Time | Duration in stationhouse |
| Desk Officer | Desk officer initials/signature |

> **Sergeant Focus:** The desk officer shall maintain the Interrupted Patrol Log under **direct control** at all times and review it frequently during the tour to ensure member presence is being expedited.

> **Exam Alert:** Entries are made in the **desk officer's own handwriting** — not the member's. Commanding officers shall frequently review this log to determine if stops are necessary and being expedited.

---

# Section 212 — Transit Operations

## P.G. 212-16 — Evacuations of NYC Transit Trains

### Key Definitions

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **Reach Train** | Passengers walk through stalled train to reach a train positioned in front or behind |
| **Rescue Train** | Brought alongside; passengers cross via emergency evacuation devices |
| **Emergency Evacuation Devices** | Located at south end of southbound platform near blue light |

### Power Removal Rules

| Evacuation Type | Power Removal Required? |
|----------------|------------------------|
| Reach train | **NO** |
| Rescue train | **NO** |
| Evacuation to benchwall/roadbed | **YES — MUST ensure power is removed first** |

> **Exam Alert:** Power must be removed **only** when evacuating to benchwall or roadbed. Reach trains and rescue trains do **not** require power removal.

### Notifications and Responsibilities
- **MOS:** Notify patrol supervisor/duty captain; report who ordered evacuation and why.
- **Patrol supervisor:** Establish command post; keep dispatcher and duty captain informed.
- **Duty captain:** Contact **RTOCC** (Rapid Transit Operations Command Center); maintain constant communication.
- Typed Letterhead report submitted to Chief, Transit Bureau for all incidents.

---

## P.G. 212-17 — Critical Situations on NYC Transit

### Types of Critical Situations
- Fires, floods, collisions, derailments
- Persons under trains
- Major service disruptions

### Key Procedures
- Remove power when necessary.
- Post a member in the street to direct emergency units.
- **DO NOT** permit removal of sick/injured firefighter without fire officer consent.
- Remove persons from under trains **EXCEPT** when deceased and possibly a crime victim.

### Train Crew Signal for Police
- Horn/whistle pattern: **LONG-SHORT-LONG-SHORT**

> **Exam Alert:** The train crew signal for police is **LONG-SHORT-LONG-SHORT**. Remember this distinctive pattern.

### Required Reporting Details
Report to duty captain/incident commander:
- Train operator information
- Conductor information
- Line and direction of travel
- Car numbers
- Full incident details

---

## P.G. 212-19 — Animals on NYC Public Transportation

### General Rule
- Section 1050.9(h)(1) NYCRR: **No animals** unless enclosed in a container.

### Exemptions

| Category | Details |
|----------|---------|
| Law enforcement dogs | Permitted without container |
| Service animals | Seeing eye, hearing, physical disability, non-apparent disabilities (epilepsy, heart, lung disease) |
| Animals in training | Permitted |

> **Exam Alert:** Members **CANNOT** require proof of disability or documentation for a service animal. May **only** inquire if the animal is providing disability assistance.

### Stray Dogs on Roadbed
- Request patrol supervisor and ESU.
- **DO NOT** enter roadbed without supervisor permission.
- **DO NOT** chase after the dog (causes it to run).
- Restraining pole shall **not** be waved as a weapon.

### Dead Animal
- Notify radio dispatcher.
- Contact Sanitation Department for removal.

---

## P.G. 212-20 — Ejection from New York City Transit Property

*(Updated R.O. 90, effective 10/02/25)*

### Purpose
To provide procedures for uniformed members having occasion to eject a passenger from New York City Transit property.

### Scope
Any person who violates any portion of NYCRR Part 1050, regardless of whether or not that person has also been issued a TAB summons/NOV or Criminal Court Summons for that violation, is subject to ejection from NYC Transit facilities. Issuance of a Notice of Violation does **not** preclude a member from ejecting a passenger from the transit system, if the member concerned deems it appropriate.

### Procedure — When Necessary to Eject a Passenger

### Uniformed Member of the Service

1. Advise passenger of ejection and reason.
2. Permit passenger who voluntarily leaves or shows willingness to do so, to leave without interference.
3. Use **no more force** than is necessary to effect the ejection, if passenger refuses to leave.
   a. Generally, grasping the upper arm and forearm to escort the person off the system is considered using reasonable force necessary to effect the ejection.

> *NOTE: If the passenger's conduct requires more force than is ordinarily needed to assist and guide the passenger out of the transit system, an arrest may be appropriate.*

4. Make digital **Activity Log** entries and prepare a **TRANSIT SYSTEM EJECTION REPORT** using Finest Online Records Management System (FORMS) each time an ejection is effected.
   a. Submit completed report **prior to completion of tour**.
   b. Use radio code disposition **"10-93E"** to finalize assignment anytime an individual is ejected from the NYC Transit System.

### Transit District Supervisor

5. Review **TRANSIT SYSTEM EJECTION REPORT** using FORMS and ensure all required captions are complete and accurate.
   a. If unable to verify accuracy and completeness, return to submitting member for necessary attention.
   b. **Approve** TRANSIT SYSTEM EJECTION REPORT using FORMS, after verifying accuracy and completeness.

> **Exam Alert:** Radio code **10-93E** is used to finalize the assignment when an individual is ejected from transit. The Transit District Supervisor reviews and approves the ejection report via FORMS.

> **Sergeant Focus:** The supervisor shall review every Transit System Ejection Report for accuracy and completeness before approving it in FORMS. If the report is deficient, it must be returned to the member for correction.

---

## P.G. 212-21 — Photography on NYC Transit System

### General Rule
- Photography, filming, and video recording are **PERMITTED** on the transit system.
- **Ancillary equipment** (lights, reflectors, tripods) may **NOT** be used.

### Exception
- Press with valid **MOME** (Mayor's Office of Media and Entertainment) ID **may** use ancillary equipment.

### When to Take Action

| Situation | Action |
|-----------|--------|
| Camera alone, no hazard | **No action** |
| Ancillary equipment without press ID | Take action |
| Creating a hazard | Take action |

> **Exam Alert:** Camera alone = no action needed. Only act if ancillary equipment is used without a MOME press ID or if a hazard is being created.

---

## P.G. 212-22 — Malfunction of Transit Bureau Radio Network

### Dispatch Procedures
- Dispatch re-routes Transit calls to Patrol Services Bureau.
- If a call is not finalized in **30 minutes**, dispatch another unit.
- Prioritize 911 calls from field MOS.

### Command Responsibilities

> **Sergeant Focus:** During radio malfunction:
> - Radio check of strategic areas at **30-minute intervals**
> - Assign officers to **paired patrols** with UHF radios
> - **Suspend** assignments in civilian clothes
> - Members make **hourly signals** to command
> - Strategic Response Group placed on **"Alert"** for Transit use if required

### Critical Limitation
- NYPD radios (other than Transit Bureau) **DO NOT** work underground, except in Manhattan stations equipped with repeaters.

---

## P.G. 212-23 — Inspection of Rapid Transit Stations on Post

### Radio Code Signals

| Code | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **10-75T** | Transit patrol by non-Transit member |
| **10-75S** | Station inspection by Transit member |
| **10-75O** | TOMS |
| **10-75M** | Mobile order maintenance |

### Inspection Requirements
- Confer with railroad clerk at each station about conditions.
- Inspect concessions, toilets, stairways, mezzanines, platforms, and **all** token booths (open and closed).
- Visit each station **at least once per tour**.

### Opposite Sex Toilet Inspection
- Knock and announce **"Police Inspection."**
- **WILL NOT** enter until departure of occupants.
- **UNLESS** informed of criminal activity inside.

> **Exam Alert:** The opposite-sex toilet rule requires knock and announce first. Entry is delayed until occupants depart, unless criminal activity is reported.

### RMP Recorder
- Shall inspect each uncovered station in the sector.

---

## P.G. 212-24 — Removal of Power in Subway

### Authorization
- **ONLY** in extreme emergencies involving life-threatening situations.
- **Patrol supervisor** determines necessity.

### Factors to Consider
- Time of day (rush vs. non-rush)
- Weather (extreme heat)
- Station type (elevated vs. below ground)
- Train locations in vicinity
- Extent of injuries
- Risk to public

### Emergency Alarm Box

| Feature | Detail |
|---------|--------|
| Spacing | **400-600 feet** apart |
| Location | Beneath **blue lights** |
| Operation | Pull lever **down** as far as possible, then release |
| Effect | Removes power on **ALL tracks** in vicinity |

> **Exam Alert:** Emergency alarm boxes are spaced **400-600 feet** apart under **blue lights**. They remove power on **ALL** tracks in the vicinity, not just one track.

### Critical Warnings
- NYC Transit will **automatically restore** power if not notified immediately via the telephone at the alarm box.
- Officers shall **NOT** personally shut off power **UNLESS** grave risk of **imminent danger** and **no other** safe alternative exists.

### Notification
- Notify Operations Unit of time of request; notify again when power is restored.

### Alternative to Power Removal
- Ride in lead cars through the area to search for suspects.

---

# Section 212 — Key Terms and Numbers

## Critical Terms

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **CPR System** | Centralized Personnel Resource — attendance and roll call tracking system |
| **Digital Activity Log** | Primary activity recording tool on Department smartphone; required for all uniformed MOS below captain rank |
| **Interrupted Patrol Log** | Desk officer log tracking time members spend in the stationhouse during tour |
| **Rosario Material** | Documents, notes, or logs potentially discoverable in criminal proceedings |
| **Reach Train** | Train brought in front of or behind a stalled train so passengers can walk through |
| **Rescue Train** | Train brought alongside for cross-platform transfer via emergency evacuation devices |
| **RTOCC** | Rapid Transit Operations Command Center — primary transit coordination hub |
| **Emergency Alarm Box** | Located 400-600 feet apart under blue lights; removes power on ALL tracks in vicinity |
| **MOME** | Mayor's Office of Media and Entertainment — issues press credentials |
| **DCPI** | Deputy Commissioner, Public Information — notified for all press incidents (24/7) |
| **ERPO** | Extreme Risk Protection Order — court order prohibiting firearm purchase/possession |
| **VGTOF** | Violent Gang and Terrorist Organization File within NCIC |
| **RTCC-FIS** | Real Time Crime Center — Facial Identification Section |
| **Probe Image** | Image of unidentified person submitted for facial recognition comparison |
| **Photo Repository** | Approved facial recognition database containing ONLY arrest and parole photographs |
| **Possible Match Candidate** | Investigative lead ONLY — does NOT establish probable cause |
| **AMBER Alert** | Child abduction alert for victims 17 or under in imminent danger of serious harm/death |
| **ALPS** | Automated License and Permit System — electronic pistol license verification |
| **Public Counter** | Scanner machine counter that MUST read zero before polls open |
| **Protective Counter** | Scanner counter that is NOT expected to be zero — do not confuse with public counter |
| **TRS** | Telecommunications Relay Service — dial 711 for deaf/hard of hearing communication |
| **4th Platoon Rule** | Members on 1800x0200 subpoenaed for next-day court reassigned to 3rd platoon (1600x2400) |

## Investigative Encounter Terms

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **Level 1 Encounter** | Request for Information — based on objective credible reason; no detention, no search request |
| **Level 2 Encounter** | Common Law Right of Inquiry — based on founded suspicion; may ask accusatory questions and request consent to search |
| **Level 3 Encounter** | Terry Stop — based on reasonable suspicion of felony or PL misdemeanor; may detain, use force, frisk |
| **DeBour** | NYS Court of Appeals case articulating the 4-level encounter framework (request for info, CLRI, forcible stop, arrest) |
| **Terry v. Ohio** | Federal Supreme Court case providing authority for Level 3 forcible stop and possible frisk |
| **RTKBC** | Right to Know Business Card — pre-printed card given to persons at Level 2 and 3 encounters |
| **RTKBC-G** | Right to Know Business Card — General — blank version used when regular RTKBCs are exhausted |
| **CLRI** | Common Law Right of Inquiry — Consent Search Report; prepared when consent to search is requested at Level 2 |
| **Stop Report** | Documentation prepared for each Level 3 (Terry) stop; submitted through FORMS; reviewed by supervisor |
| **TACTICS** | Training Attendance Certification Transcript Integration Collection System — records training sessions for investigative encounter compliance |
| **Protective Measures** | Tactics short of a frisk (order hands from pockets, instruct person to put down object) used when officer safety is at risk |
| **Frisk** | Carefully running hand down outside of person's clothing to check for weapons — NOT to locate evidence |

## Crime Scene and Incident Terms

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **Police Line Do Not Cross** | White tape with blue letters; establishes secure area for police operations |
| **Crime Scene Do Not Cross** | Yellow tape with black letters; safeguards area where evidence may be present |
| **Crime Scene Evidence Cone** | 5-inch orange cone marked "NYPD" in black; placed next to small evidence items |
| **HARBOR** | Mnemonic for mandatory CSU response: Homicide, Aggravated assault (likely to die), Rape (forcible), Burglary (forced safe/circumvented alarm), Other (CSU assists), Robbery/hijacking (firearm caused injury) |
| **IACC** | Inter-Agency Command Center — separate from police Command Post; established at hazmat/SECTAR incidents |
| **SECTAR** | Significant fire, Explosion, Chemical burn, Toxic Air Release, Radiation hazard — triggers full hazmat response |
| **MDV** | Mobile Decontamination Vehicle — requested through Operations by on-scene coordinator |
| **FRUITS** | Mnemonic for when D.O. notifies Ops AND Borough for fires: Forced entry by FD, Relocation of tenants, Unusual fire, Injury/death, Three alarms+, Suspicious fire |
| **RATCOP** | Mnemonic for info released only after conferral with DCPI AND Chief of Detectives: Reputation, Anticipated testimony, Test results, Credibility, Opinion/speculation, Photos/films/mug shot |

## BWC and Technology Terms

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **BWC** | Body Worn Camera — Dept property; must be personally issued; activate for all police action |
| **Police Action** | Police service, law enforcement, or investigative activity in furtherance of official duties — triggers mandatory BWC activation |
| **GO 15 (A.G. 318-11)** | Rule allowing member to view own BWC video as subject/witness in Dept investigation prior to making statement |
| **Gunshot Detection** | Alert system (Shotspotter) that detects potential gunfire; generates confirmed or unconfirmed alerts |
| **ECMS** | Enterprise Case Management System — NCO opens companion case for confirmed gunshot alerts |

## Critical Numbers

| Number | Significance |
|--------|-------------|
| **1 hour** | Contact desk officer before end of tour (foot patrol needing relief); arrive at poll site before opening |
| **30 minutes** | Dispatch another unit if Transit call not finalized; radio check intervals during malfunction |
| **100 feet** | No-electioneering zone around poll site entrance |
| **400-600 feet** | Spacing of emergency alarm boxes (under blue lights) in subway |
| **2100 hours** | Poll closing time — persons on line may vote, no new entries |
| **72 hours** | HPD deadline to seal vacated premises |
| **60 days** | Rifle/shotgun surrender investigation completion deadline |
| **2 years** | Pistol license validity period; minimum firearm retention under ERPO |
| **8 hours** | Window to cancel AMBER Alert via fax to NYS Police |
| **30 days** | ERPO Unit reviews before expiration for renewal determination |
| **$15** | Subpoena fee — Civil/State |
| **$40** | Subpoena fee — Civil/Federal |
| **$5** | Medical records fee for outside parties |
| **711** | TRS dial code for deaf/hard of hearing relay service |
| **(212) 504-4115** | TTY number for 311 system |
| **0900-1700** | License Division hours (Mon-Fri); Legal Bureau ERPO Unit hours (Mon-Fri) |
| **300 feet** | Minimum outdoor hazmat distance for spills and most fires |
| **1,000 feet** | Minimum outdoor hazmat distance for explosions/suspected devices/military/tanker fires; bomb evacuation distance |
| **20 minutes** | Discontinue bomb search before threatened explosion time |
| **1 hour** | Do not resume bomb search after threatened explosion time |
| **39 months** | BWC video automatic deletion period |
| **PD Code 080** | Investigate Gunshot Detection Alert (Shotspotter, no ballistics) |
| **PD Code 083** | Investigate Shots Fired (no ballistics) |
| **PD Code 088** | Investigate Shot Fired, Ballistics Recovered |
| **PD Code 089** | Investigate Gunshot Detection Alert, Ballistics Recovered |

## Handling Codes (VGTOF)

| Code | Action | Key Detail |
|------|--------|-----------|
| **1** | ARREST | Warrant exists — approach with caution |
| **2** | DETAIN | Reasonable suspicion — active investigation |
| **3** | INVESTIGATION | Do not alert — possible terrorism ties |
| **4** | INFORMATION GATHERING | Do not alert individual |

## Transit Radio Codes

| Code | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **10-75T** | Transit patrol by non-Transit member |
| **10-75S** | Station inspection by Transit member |
| **10-75O** | TOMS |
| **10-75M** | Mobile order maintenance |
| **10-90N** | Unnecessary Alarm (DOE facilities, no forced entry) |

## Train Crew Signal

| Signal | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| **LONG-SHORT-LONG-SHORT** | Train crew requesting police assistance |

---

# Section 212 — Review Questions

Test your knowledge of Command Operations procedures. Click each answer to reveal the correct response.

---

**1. Who is required to use a paper Activity Log (PD112-145) instead of the digital Activity Log?**

- A) Members on light duty
- B) Auxiliary police officers
- C) Undercover officers
- D) Members above the rank of captain

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) Undercover officers.** Per P.G. 212-08, undercovers use the paper Activity Log (PD112-145) and shall NOT use the digital version.

</details>

---

**2. Entries in the Interrupted Patrol Log are made in whose handwriting?**

- A) The member reporting to the stationhouse
- B) The desk officer's own handwriting
- C) The patrol supervisor on duty
- D) The platoon commander

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) The desk officer's own handwriting.** Per P.G. 212-10, the desk officer makes all entries in the Interrupted Patrol Log in their own handwriting and maintains the log under direct control.

</details>

---

**3. Activity Logs are considered potential what type of material?**

- A) Brady material
- B) Rosario material
- C) Classified material
- D) Privileged material

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) Rosario material.** Per P.G. 212-08, Activity Logs are potential Rosario material and may be reviewed by the Department, outside agencies, DAs, and defense attorneys.

</details>

---

**4. Under the 4th platoon rule, members subpoenaed for next-day court are reassigned to which platoon?**

- A) 1st platoon
- B) 2nd platoon
- C) 3rd platoon (1600x2400)
- D) They remain on the 4th platoon

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) 3rd platoon (1600x2400).** Per P.G. 212-03, members on the 4th platoon (1800x0200) who are subpoenaed for next-day court (2nd platoon) are reassigned to the 3rd platoon. If not notified in advance, they are excused at 2400 hours with no OT for the 2400-0200 period.

</details>

---

**5. Before evacuating transit passengers to the benchwall or roadbed, what must happen first?**

- A) A rescue train must be positioned
- B) Power must be removed
- C) The duty captain must approve
- D) ESU must arrive on scene

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) Power must be removed.** Per P.G. 212-16, when evacuating passengers to the benchwall or roadbed, power MUST be removed first. Power is NOT removed for reach trains or rescue trains.

</details>

---

**6. What distinguishes a "reach train" from a "rescue train"?**

- A) Reach trains use emergency devices; rescue trains do not
- B) Reach trains involve walking through the stalled train; rescue trains involve cross-platform transfer via emergency devices
- C) Reach trains require power removal; rescue trains do not
- D) There is no difference; the terms are interchangeable

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) Reach trains involve walking through the stalled train to a train positioned in front or behind; rescue trains are brought alongside for cross-platform transfer using emergency evacuation devices.** Per P.G. 212-16, neither requires power removal.

</details>

---

**7. The transit radio code 10-75T indicates what?**

- A) Transit station inspection by Transit member
- B) Transit patrol by a non-Transit member
- C) Transit emergency evacuation
- D) Mobile order maintenance

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) Transit patrol by a non-Transit member.** Per P.G. 212-23, 10-75T is for transit patrol by non-Transit personnel. 10-75S is station inspection by Transit, and 10-75M is mobile order maintenance.

</details>

---

**8. Emergency alarm boxes in the subway are spaced how far apart and located where?**

- A) 200-400 feet apart, under red lights
- B) 400-600 feet apart, under blue lights
- C) 600-800 feet apart, under yellow lights
- D) 100-200 feet apart, under green lights

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) 400-600 feet apart, under blue lights.** Per P.G. 212-24, emergency alarm boxes are located 400-600 feet apart beneath blue lights. Pulling the lever removes power on ALL tracks in the vicinity.

</details>

---

**9. Can members require proof of disability or documentation for a service animal on public transportation?**

- A) Yes, written documentation is required
- B) Yes, but only a verbal confirmation
- C) No, but members may inquire if the animal is providing disability assistance
- D) No, members may not ask any questions

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) No, but members may inquire if the animal is providing disability assistance.** Per P.G. 212-19, members CANNOT require proof of disability or documentation. They may only ask if the animal is providing disability assistance.

</details>

---

**10. What photography/recording is permitted on the transit system without a press ID?**

- A) No photography of any kind
- B) Camera alone with no ancillary equipment
- C) Camera with a tripod only
- D) Camera with lights and reflectors

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) Camera alone with no ancillary equipment.** Per P.G. 212-21, photography and recording are permitted, but ancillary equipment (lights, reflectors, tripods) may NOT be used without a valid MOME press ID.

</details>

---

**11. MOME Press Card holders are entitled to what access?**

- A) Access to interior crime scenes
- B) Access to cross police and fire lines
- C) Access to security-restricted areas
- D) Access to all areas without restriction

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) Access to cross police and fire lines.** Per P.G. 212-49, MOME press card holders are entitled to cross police and fire lines. This does NOT include interior crime scenes or security-restricted areas.

</details>

---

**12. During First Amendment Activity, if NYPD orders persons to leave an area, what happens to MOME press?**

- A) They must leave with everyone else
- B) They are arrested if they refuse to leave
- C) They are NOT required to leave but should move to a safe location
- D) They may stay only with incident commander approval

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) They are NOT required to leave but should move to a safe location.** Per P.G. 212-49, during First Amendment Activity, MOME press are not required to leave even if NYPD orders persons to leave — they should be directed to a safe location.

</details>

---

**13. Before polls open, the scanner machine public counter must read what?**

- A) Any number below 100
- B) The same as the protective counter
- C) Zero (0)
- D) The number of registered voters

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) Zero (0).** Per P.G. 212-86, ALL scanner machine public counters must read zero before polls open. If not zero, the desk officer/supervisor must be notified immediately and the scanner shall NOT be used. The protective counter will likely NOT be zero — do not confuse the two.

</details>

---

**14. No-electioneering signs must be posted within how many feet of the poll site entrance?**

- A) 50 feet
- B) 75 feet
- C) 100 feet
- D) 200 feet

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) 100 feet.** Per P.G. 212-86, the Board of Elections must post no-electioneering signs within 100 feet of the poll site entrance.

</details>

---

**15. Polls close at what time, and what is the rule for persons on line?**

- A) 2000 hours; all persons must leave immediately
- B) 2100 hours; persons on line may vote, no new entries after 2100
- C) 2200 hours; persons on line may vote
- D) 2100 hours; no one may vote after 2100

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) 2100 hours; persons on line may vote, no new entries after 2100.** Per P.G. 212-87, polls close at 2100 hours. Persons already on line at closing may vote, but NO new persons may join the line after 2100.

</details>

---

**16. Temporary Extreme Risk Protection Orders (ERPOs) are served by whom?**

- A) NYPD patrol officers
- B) The precinct detective squad
- C) NYC Sheriff's Office
- D) US Marshals Service

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) NYC Sheriff's Office.** Per P.G. 212-128, temporary ERPOs are served by the NYC Sheriff's Office, NOT by NYPD.

</details>

---

**17. Firearms seized under an ERPO are retained for a minimum of how long?**

- A) 6 months
- B) 1 year
- C) 2 years
- D) 5 years

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) 2 years.** Per P.G. 212-128, firearms are retained for a minimum of 2 years. No release is permitted without written authorization from CO, License Division (and CO, Medical Division if the subject is a MOS).

</details>

---

**18. What is the maximum age for an AMBER Alert subject?**

- A) 12 or younger
- B) 15 or younger
- C) 17 or younger
- D) 18 or younger

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) 17 or younger.** Per P.G. 212-99, the child must be 17 or younger and police must believe the child is in imminent danger of serious bodily harm or death.

</details>

---

**19. Who may request AMBER Alert activation?**

- A) Any sergeant or above
- B) The precinct commanding officer
- C) A Captain or above from the Detective Bureau
- D) The Operations Unit duty officer

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) A Captain or above from the Detective Bureau.** Per P.G. 212-99, only a Captain or above from the Detective Bureau may request activation through the NYS Police Communications Section.

</details>

---

**20. Within what timeframe can an AMBER Alert be cancelled by fax to NYS Police?**

- A) 4 hours
- B) 6 hours
- C) 8 hours
- D) 12 hours

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) 8 hours.** Per P.G. 212-99, cancellation within 8 hours is by fax to NYS Police. After 8 hours, cancellation is through DCPI media notification.

</details>

---

**21. Facial recognition technology establishes what level of evidence?**

- A) Probable cause for arrest
- B) Reasonable suspicion for a stop
- C) Investigative lead only — NOT probable cause
- D) Proof beyond reasonable doubt

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) Investigative lead only — NOT probable cause.** Per P.G. 212-129, facial recognition does not establish probable cause by itself. A possible match candidate is an investigative lead only.

</details>

---

**22. The facial recognition photo repository contains only what type of images?**

- A) Driver's license photos
- B) Social media photos
- C) Arrest and parole photographs
- D) Surveillance camera captures

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) Arrest and parole photographs.** Per P.G. 212-129, the photo repository contains ONLY arrest and parole photographs. Use of images outside this repository is prohibited unless approved by the Chief of Detectives, Intelligence and Counterterrorism Bureau, or Chief of Intelligence.

</details>

---

**23. VGTOF Handling Code 1 means what?**

- A) Detain — reasonable suspicion
- B) Arrest — warrant exists, approach with caution
- C) Investigation — do not alert individual
- D) Information gathering only

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) Arrest — warrant exists, approach with caution.** Per P.G. 212-108, Handling Code 1 = Arrest (warrant exists), Code 2 = Detain, Code 3 = Investigation (do not alert), Code 4 = Information gathering (do not alert).

</details>

---

**24. Can a diplomat with full immunity be arrested?**

- A) Yes, for any felony
- B) Yes, but only for violent felonies
- C) No, but may be issued a summons
- D) No, and may not be issued a summons

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) No, but may be issued a summons.** Per P.G. 212-56, diplomats with immunity shall NOT be arrested but MAY be issued a summons. Pending verification, they may be detained if a danger to self or public.

</details>

---

**25. In a Level 2 investigative encounter, the UMOS requests consent to search. What document must be prepared?**

- A) Stop Report
- B) Common Law Right of Inquiry — Consent Search Report (CLRI)
- C) Level 2 Encounter Report only
- D) Arrest Report

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) Common Law Right of Inquiry — Consent Search Report (CLRI).** Per P.G. 212-11, when consent to search is requested at Level 2, the UMOS prepares a CLRI. At Level 3, the request is documented on the **Stop Report** instead.

</details>

---

**26. What does the HARBOR mnemonic stand for regarding when Crime Scene Unit must respond?**

- A) Homicide, Assault (any), Robbery, Burglary (any), Officer-involved, Rape
- B) Homicide, Aggravated assault (likely to die), Rape (forcible), Burglary (forced safe/circumvented alarm), Other (CSU will assist), Robbery/hijacking (firearm caused injury)
- C) Homicide, Arson, Robbery, Burglary, Obstruction, Rape
- D) Homicide, Assault (SPI), Robbery (any), Burglary (safe involved), Other crimes, Rape

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) Homicide, Aggravated assault (dangerous instrument AND likely to die), Rape (forcible), Burglary (forced safe OR circumvented alarm), Other crime (CSU will assist investigation), Robbery OR hijacking (firearm caused injury).** Per P.G. 212-04, these are the specific criteria for mandatory CSU response.

</details>

---

**27. At a hazmat scene, what is the minimum outdoor evacuation distance for an explosion or suspected device?**

- A) 300 feet
- B) 500 feet
- C) 1,000 feet
- D) 1,500 feet

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) 1,000 feet.** Per P.G. 212-37, the minimum outdoor distance for any explosion or suspected device is 1,000 feet. For spills and most fires, the distance is 300 feet. The 1,000-foot distance also applies to fires involving military shipments or tanker trucks.

</details>

---

**28. During a bomb threat search, teams must discontinue searching how long before the threatened explosion time?**

- A) 10 minutes
- B) 15 minutes
- C) 20 minutes
- D) 30 minutes

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) 20 minutes.** Per P.G. 212-40, search teams must discontinue 20 minutes prior to the threatened explosion time, regardless of whether the premise is evacuated or occupied. Search must NOT resume for at least 1 hour after the threatened time.

</details>

---

**29. At a post-explosion bomb scene, who removes injured persons to safe locations for medical treatment?**

- A) EMS
- B) FDNY
- C) ESU
- D) Responding UMOS

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) ESU.** Per P.G. 212-40, the patrol supervisor/ranking officer directs **ESU** to remove injured to safe locations — not EMS. EMS administers medical treatment at the safe locations, but the physical removal is an ESU responsibility.

</details>

---

**30. The RATCOP mnemonic identifies information that may only be released after conferral with DCPI AND which other official?**

- A) Borough Commander
- B) Chief of Patrol
- C) Chief of Detectives
- D) Police Commissioner

<details><summary>Answer</summary>
