## P.G. 216-19: Public Access Defibrillation Program

**Date Effective:** 11/18/21 | **Revision:** I.O. 104 | **Pages:** 3

### Purpose

To account for and properly document the use of Automated External Defibrillators (AEDs) in assigned commands, and to provide for training and certification of uniformed, civilian, and auxiliary members of the service in their use.

### Key Definitions

**AUTOMATED EXTERNAL DEFIBRILLATOR (AED)** — An electronic device used to defibrillate the heart by applying an electric shock to it.

**UTILIZATION OF AED** — Once an AED is **placed on an aided**, the Department considers that as utilized, **even if a shock is not administered**. When the AED pads are placed on an aided, the machine will perform an analysis and advise what actions should be taken.

> **Exam Alert:** "Utilized" means pads are placed on the patient — a shock does NOT need to be delivered for it to count as utilized. Once AED pads are **placed on aided**, whether you shock or not, it is considered **USED** per P.G. 216-19.

**CARDIO PULMONARY RESUSCITATION (CPR) ADMINISTERED** — The Department considers CPR administered once a member performs rapid **chest compressions** OR **mouth-to-mouth** ventilation on an aided. Either one counts as CPR per P.G. 216-19.

### AED Kit Contents

> **Exam Alert:** Memorize the kit contents — this is frequently tested.

| Item | Quantity |
|------|----------|
| Operational AED device | 1 |
| Electrode pads with wire leads (sealed packages, each containing two pads) | 2 packages |
| Pocket mask | 1 |
| Gloves | 1 pair |
| Cloth/gauze compress | 1 |
| Razor | 1 |

### Procedure — Desk Officer (Steps 1-6)

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 1 | Account for **all AED kits** assigned to command at the **beginning of each tour** |
| 2 | Assign an AED at start of each tour to a member trained and certified as AED qualified |
| 3 | Notify radio dispatcher of the unit(s) assigned an AED kit |
| 4 | Make a **Command Log entry** accounting for all AED kits and notification to radio dispatcher |
| 4a | Note assignment of AED kits by their serial numbers, with prefix "AED" (e.g., AED 1234) |
| 5 | Ensure assignment of AED kits are noted on the roll call |
| 6 | Secure AED kits not in use in appropriate location (e.g., radio room, property room) |

> **Exam Alert:** AED serial numbers are prefixed with "AED" (e.g., AED 1234) in the Command Log. Ensure assignment is also documented on the **Roll Call** per P.G. 216-19.

> **Sergeant Focus:** AED accountability happens **every tour** — the desk officer must account for all kits, assign them, notify dispatch, and make Command Log entries.

### Member of the Service — Kit Inspection (Steps 7-8)

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 7 | Inspect AED kit to ensure all items present |
| 8 | Notify desk officer of any deficiencies/missing equipment; make digital Activity Log entries |

### Upon Arriving at Scene of Cardiac Aided Case (Steps 9-13)

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 9 | Advise radio dispatcher of aided's condition and request EMS response |
| 10 | Utilize AED in compliance with Department guidelines/training |
| 10a | AED will **only** be attached to an **unconscious adult patient** after confirming: **not breathing** and **no pulse** |
| 10b | Provide care until relieved by advanced-level emergency medical personnel |
| 11 | Brief responding EMS crew of: (a) condition when found, (b) nature of care provided (rescue breathing, CPR, defibrillation, etc.) |
| 12 | Prepare AIDED REPORT; note in "Details" if AED utilized or CPR administered and results achieved; include AED serial number if utilized |
| 12b | If CPR administered, check appropriate caption on AIDED REPORT |
| 13 | Submit AIDED REPORT immediately to desk officer after returning to command |

> **Exam Alert:** AED is **only** for unconscious adults with **no breathing** and **no pulse**. All three conditions must be confirmed.

### Prisoner AED/CPR Use

> **Exam Alert:** If AED/CPR is administered on a **prisoner** in custody, a **MEDICAL TREATMENT OF PRISONER (PD244-150)** form will be completed **instead of** an AIDED REPORT. This is the same distinction as Medic Alert emblems on prisoners per P.G. 216-19.

### Desk Officer — Post-Use (Steps 14-15)

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 14 | Telephone ESU desk to obtain an **AED/CPR Log number**; enter on AIDED REPORT or MEDICAL TREATMENT OF PRISONER form |
| 14a | Forward copy of completed form to ESU-AED Coordinator via **fax** (to one person's attention) and **Department mail** to ESU, Attention: EMT School (mailed to another) |
| 14b | Ensure AED is secured until delivery and is **not reassigned** |
| 15 | Schedule appointment; ensure AED is delivered to ESU-AED Coordinator for downloading of information after each use |

### Training — "BLASTT" Course

> **Exam Alert:** AED training is a one-day course at the **Police Academy** called **"BLASTT"** — Basic Life Support and Trauma Treatment CPR-AED-Naloxone-BTK.

- The commanding officer designates the command's **training sergeant** as the AED liaison
- The command training sergeant schedules members via **T.A.C.T.I.C.S.** (Training Attendance Certification Transcript Integrated Collection System)

### Loss or Theft

If an AED kit or its contents (**except** cloth/gauze compress and plastic gloves) are lost or stolen, comply with P.G. 219-20, "Loss or Theft of Department Property."

> **Exam Alert:** Replacement supplies = call ESU AED coordinator. Questions about training = call the Police Academy. Someone wants to donate an AED = confer with ESU per P.G. 216-19.

> **See Also:** P.G. 305-08 — If trained in opioid antagonist kit, it is **mandatory** to carry it per P.G. 216-19.

### Key Forms

| Form | Number |
|------|--------|
| AIDED REPORT | (Electronic) |
| MEDICAL TREATMENT OF PRISONER | PD244-150 |

---

## P.G. 216-20: Handling Shelter Clients with Tuberculosis

**Date Effective:** 11/18/21 | **Revision:** I.O. 99 | **Pages:** 2

### Purpose

To assist the Department of Health and Mental Hygiene in the removal of recalcitrant patients sheltered in Human Resources Administration facilities, who have tuberculosis and are refusing treatment.

### Procedure

#### Operations Unit Personnel (Steps 1-2)

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 1 | Upon notification by DOHMH that a removal and detention order has been issued: (a) ascertain location of removal, (b) enter in Command Log the name of DOHMH personnel and pedigree information of subject |
| 2 | Notify Communications Section to assign a patrol supervisor, an RMP unit, and ESU to respond |

#### Patrol Supervisor (Steps 3-6)

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 3 | Respond to scene and assess the situation |
| 4 | Determine if services of ESU are necessary; cancel ESU if not required |
| 5 | Confirm that the **Removal and Detention Order** is possessed by the DOHMH physician/peace officer at the shelter site |
| 6 | Assign uniformed member to accompany subject and DOHMH physician/peace officer in DOHMH transportation vehicle to the pre-designated hospital |

> **Sergeant Focus:** The patrol supervisor must **confirm the Removal and Detention Order** before proceeding. This is a legal document that authorizes the removal.

### Department's Role — Critical Limitations

> **Exam Alert:** The Department's primary responsibility is to **assist in the arrest and preserve the peace**. Members of the service are:

| Allowed | NOT Allowed |
|---------|-------------|
| Use physical force to restrain subject until hospital delivery (only extent necessary) | Personally assist in **forcing entry** to premises |
| Accompany patient to hospital | **Safeguard property** at location |
| Wear protective equipment | **Secure premises** afterward |

> **Exam Alert:** The Department does NOT force entry, safeguard property, or secure premises — those duties are performed by other agencies at the direction of the DOHMH physician/peace officer.

### Protective Equipment

Responding members will take appropriate precautions including wearing **plastic gloves and facial masks**. Facial masks will be provided at the shelter site by DOHMH.

### Transportation

- DOHMH provides the vehicle
- If HRA cannot provide a vehicle, an ambulance will be requested through Communications
- **Except in extreme cases**, Department vehicles will NOT be used for transportation
- Transport **only** to the designated HHC facility (even if subject is arrested for other conduct)

#### Uniformed Member of the Service (Steps 7-10)

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 7 | Accompany patient along with DOHMH physician/peace officer to pre-designated hospital |
| 8 | Enter details of removal and transport in digital Activity Log |
| 9 | Ensure DOHMH physician/peace officer name and patient name are recorded in Activity Log and Department forms |
| 10 | Comply with A.G. 330-09, "Exposure of Members of the Service to Infectious Diseases or Hazardous Materials" |

### If Patient Becomes Violent or Barricades

Comply with P.G. 221-13, "Mentally Ill or Emotionally Disturbed Persons" and P.G. 221-14, "Hostage/Barricaded Person(s)," as appropriate.

---

## P.G. 216-21: Response to Carbon Monoxide Alarms

**Date Issued:** 10/18/16 | **Pages:** 2

### Key Definition

**CARBON MONOXIDE** — A **colorless and odorless** gas that interferes with the delivery of oxygen to the cells of the body.

| Signs and Symptoms |
|--------------------|
| Headache |
| Nausea |
| Vomiting |
| Confusion and disorientation |
| Cherry red skin (severe cases) |

Carbon monoxide is created by incomplete combustion of burning fuels (gasoline, oil, wood, paper).

> **Exam Alert:** CO is **colorless and odorless**. Cherry red skin indicates a **severe** case.

### Procedure — Uniformed Member of the Service (Steps 1-8)

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 1 | Promptly respond; identify type of alarm (residential, central station monitored, EMS call) |
| 2 | Interview persons with information regarding location of alarm |
| 3 | **Refrain from entering location** |
| 4 | Request response of **patrol supervisor**, if alarm confirmed |
| 5 | Establish frozen area |
| 6 | Render reasonable aid to victims, if present |
| 7 | Request EMS to respond if necessary |
| 8 | Observe affected individuals for signs/symptoms of CO poisoning; direct them to EMS for treatment |

> **Exam Alert:** Members **DO NOT enter** the location. This is critical — CO is invisible and can incapacitate quickly.

### Patrol Supervisor (Steps 9-11)

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 9 | Respond to location |
| 10 | Evaluate situation upon arrival |
| 11 | Maintain frozen area and extend if necessary, until hazard is removed |

> **Sergeant Focus:** Patrol supervisors should be guided by P.G. 212-37, "Hazardous Materials," when responding to CO alarms and setting up frozen areas.

### Patrol Supervisor — Continued (Steps 12-16)

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 12 | Establish crime scene, if necessary |
| 13 | Request response of **ESU personnel** |
| 14 | Prevent all non-ESU police personnel from entering location |
| 15 | Notify duty captain and precinct desk officer |
| 16 | Direct Communications to notify Department of Buildings if location is City-owned |

### Emergency Service Unit Member (Steps 17-19)

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 17 | Respond to location and determine whether CO gases are present |
| 18 | Advise patrol supervisor on appropriate frozen area boundaries |
| 19 | **Determine whether location is safe for re-entry** |

> **Exam Alert:** Only **ESU** determines whether the location is safe for re-entry. Not the patrol supervisor, not FDNY — ESU.

### After ESU Assessment (Steps 20-25)

| Step | Role | Action |
|------|------|--------|
| 20 | MOS | Remain outside location until ESU deems location habitable |
| 21 | MOS | Assist EMS with removal of aided(s) if location properly vented and ESU advises safe to enter |
| 22 | MOS | Comply with P.G. 216-01, "Aided Cases General Procedure" |
| 23 | P.S. | Direct preparation of AIDED REPORT and COMPLAINT REPORT WORKSHEET (PD313-152A), if appropriate |
| 24 | P.S. | Confer with ESU to determine if location is safe for occupants to re-enter prior to allowing re-entry |
| 25 | P.S. | Comply with P.G. 212-37, "Hazardous Materials," if a continuing CO threat exists |

### Key Forms

| Form | Number |
|------|--------|
| AIDED REPORT | (Electronic) |
| COMPLAINT REPORT WORKSHEET | PD313-152A |

---

## P.G. 216-22: Mobile Crisis Outreach Teams and Assertive Community Treatment Teams

**Date Effective:** 06/11/24 | **Revision:** R.O. 39 | **Pages:** 2

### Purpose

To safeguard and remove mentally ill or emotionally disturbed persons at the direction of Mobile Crisis Outreach or Assertive Community Treatment Teams pursuant to **section 9.58 of the New York State Mental Hygiene Law**.

### Key Definitions

**MOBILE CRISIS OUTREACH TEAMS** — Independently operated by local hospitals or community-based mental health organizations; usually funded by DOHMH; may be attached to NYS-licensed "comprehensive emergency programs." Goal: assess, stabilize, and rapidly refer patients to longer-term providers.

**ASSERTIVE COMMUNITY TREATMENT TEAMS (ACT)** — Similar to Mobile Crisis Outreach Teams but are "hospitals on wheels," more richly staffed by mental health workers. Provide a wide range of ongoing services. May order involuntary transport of patients to hospital ERs. Receive contract funding from DOHMH and are licensed by the NYS Office of Mental Health.

**QUALIFIED MENTAL HEALTH PROFESSIONAL (QMHP)** — Members of a Mobile Crisis Outreach or ACT Team who are:

| QMHP Eligible Professionals |
|------------------------------|
| Licensed psychologists |
| Certified social workers |
| Registered professional nurses |

> **Exam Alert:** Only these three types of professionals qualify as QMHPs eligible to direct a removal under Section 9.58.

### Procedure — Uniformed Member of the Service

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 1 | Comply with P.G. 221-13, "Mentally Ill or Emotionally Disturbed Persons," as required |
| 2 | Request the physician or QMHP on scene to present **section 9.58 identification card** along with signed and completed **OMH 482 form** (Mobile Crisis Outreach Team Authorization for Transport) |
| 2a | Record pertinent information in digital Activity Log and AIDED REPORT, including identity of physician/QMHP ordering removal and length of time aided was in police custody |
| 3 | Inform communications dispatcher that the removal is involuntary, when appropriate |
| 4 | Prepare AIDED REPORT and include: |
| 4a | **Upload photographs** of section 9.58 identification card and OMH 482 form to AIDED REPORT |
| 4b | Note that individual was removed pursuant to section 9.58 of NYS Mental Hygiene Law |
| 4c | Name of QMHP who directed the removal and the mobile crisis team name |

> **Exam Alert:** Two documents must be presented: (1) **Section 9.58 ID card** and (2) **OMH 482 form**. Both must be photographed and uploaded to the AIDED REPORT.

### AIDED REPORT Coding

To accurately record section 9.58 removals, select:
- **'Emotionally Disturbed'** in the 'Aided Person' section
- **'Mobile Crisis Team Removal? — Yes'** in 'Additional Information' section
- **'Involuntary Removal? — Yes'** in 'Additional Information' section

### Additional Data

**Transportation:**
- Officer will request an ambulance to provide transportation
- Officer **rides in the body of the ambulance** with the patient

**Frisk Authority:**
- Officer may frisk individual's person for weapons when transporting an EDP to the hospital
- If individual has a bag or similar item, officer may only frisk the bag for a weapon or safety concern **if the bag is in a grabbable area**
- If the officer feels what appears to be a weapon during the frisk, the officer may search the bag for the weapon

**Hospital Responsibility:**
- The mobile crisis team member ordering the removal is responsible to communicate details to hospital staff and provide required documentation
- Once individual is removed to hospital and registered as a patient, uniformed member presence is **no longer required** unless the patient is exhibiting violent behavior

**Property:**
- Mobile Crisis Outreach or ACT Teams ordering the removal will safeguard all personal property (other than contraband) removed from the patient

**Legal Concerns:**
- Contact the **Legal Bureau** if the validity of a removal is in question

> **Exam Alert:** If the validity of a Section 9.58 removal is questioned, contact the **Legal Bureau** — not IAB, not the district attorney.

---

# Section 216 — Response to Suspected Overdose Incidents

## P.G. 216-23: Response to Suspected Overdose Incidents

### Purpose

To establish procedures for responding to suspected overdose incidents, including administration of Naloxone, evidence handling, and reporting requirements.

### Key Definitions

**OPIOIDS** — Opiate, synthetic or otherwise, including but not limited to heroin, morphine, oxycodone, fentanyl, methadone, hydrocodone, and codeine.

**FENTANYL AND FENTANYL ANALOGUES:**
- Fast acting (rapid onset) potent narcotic analgesic and synthetic opioid pain medication
- Prescription opioid, about **30-50x more powerful** than heroin and **80-100x** more powerful than morphine
- Substance can be absorbed through the skin — proper handling is essential to keep all persons safe

> **Exam Alert:** Exercise **extreme caution** handling drugs/paraphernalia at overdose scenes. Even the slightest contact with fentanyl can be lethal. Utilize PPE (gloves, etc.) per P.G. 216-23.

**OPIOID ANTAGONIST** — FDA-approved drug (Naloxone, Narcan, etc.) that negates or neutralizes the effects of an opioid. Shall be limited to naloxone and other meds **approved by the Department of Health** for such purpose.

**OPIOID ANTAGONIST KIT** — Consists of:
- Intranasal mucosal atomization devices
- Safety gloves
- Rescue Breathing Face Shield
- Opioid Antagonist delivery devices

> **Exam Alert:** If trained in opioid antagonist kit, it is **mandatory** that you carry it (also referenced in P.G. 305-08) per P.G. 216-23.

**DRUG PARAPHERNALIA** — Items associated with use, preparation, manufacture, packaging, or storage of narcotics, including but not limited to straws, cutting agents, scales, strainers, pestles, empty or new glassine envelopes or vials, plastic bags, rubber stamps, drug records, etc.

> **Exam Alert:** Hypodermic needles, syringes, and other objects used for injecting controlled substances into the body are **NOT** classified as drug paraphernalia per P.G. 216-23.

**STAMPS** — Image and/or writing that drug dealers use to ID the dealer's "brand" for their product. When applicable, the stamp will be located on wax folds or glassines.

### Naloxone (NLX) — Key Facts

- Naloxone is **NOT a controlled substance** — possession by the public is legal
- Possession does NOT establish probable cause to arrest or prove commission of an offense
- Naloxone rescue kits are distributed to friends and family members of intravenous drug users
- Some rescue kits contain a hypo to administer the Naloxone intravenously
- Possession of a hypodermic instrument, in most circumstances, is **not a crime**

> **Exam Alert:** The 911 Good Samaritan Law says not to arrest a person for possessing a controlled substance, marihuana, drug paraphernalia, or alcohol by someone under 21, when they sought in good faith health care for self or other experiencing a drug or alcohol overdose. **HOWEVER**, P.O.s are mandatory reporters of suspected child abuse — "A" misd if you don't per P.G. 216-23.

### Procedure — Uniformed Member of the Service

#### Medical Attention
| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 1 | Request ambulance |
| 2 | Render reasonable aid |
| 3 | Request UMOS with Naloxone Kit, if not present |

#### Naloxone Administration
| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 4 | Ascertain from witnesses if Naloxone was administered prior to police arrival |
| 5 | If person **unresponsive**: give NLX if appropriate, and have radio notify EMS that Naloxone was administered |
| 6 | If person **not breathing and no pulse**: administer CPR/AED while waiting for additional NLX dose |
| 7 | If person has NOT responded to Naloxone within **3-5 minutes**: administer 1 additional dose |

#### Responding EMS
| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 8 | Inform responding EMS personnel: (a) why suspected opioid overdose (physical signs, statements by witnesses, etc.), (b) any attempts to revive (CPR, AED, etc.) and number of NLX doses given |

#### If Suspected Overdose Person Removed Prior to Police Arrival
| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 9 | Ascertain which hospital |
| 10 | Request response of Patrol Supervisor |
| 11 | Notify detective squad |
| 12 | Notify Desk Officer — inform if NLX was administered and by whom (NYPD, EMS, FDNY, family, friend, other) |

#### Safeguard Scene
| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 13 | Safeguard potential evidence: narcotics (including legally prescribed medicine), drug paraphernalia, pill bottles, **cellphones**, or anything to aid in finding source of substance |

#### Evidence Handling
| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 14 | Use proper precautions — PPE (gloves, etc.) |
| 15 | Document prescription pills found on or in vicinity of person/body |
| 16 | Include name, address, phone number of Dr, Pharmacy, and Patient listed on bottle |

#### Investigation
| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 17 | Attempt to identify witnesses |
| 18 | Obtain from individual/family/witnesses any potential substance responsible for suspected overdose |

#### Documentation
| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 19 | Prepare **Complaint Report**: "Investigate Aided-Drug Overdose" OR "Investigate DOA-*Possible* Drug Overdose" |
| 19a | Open to Squad if: DOA, Likely to die, or NLX administered |
| 19b | "Details" section includes: stamps/markings on glassines or pills, Dr/Pharmacy/Patient info, ICAD #, type of call, type of location, suspected drug type, manner of ingestion, if NLX given + Naloxone Log #, name/position of person who gave NLX, condition of aided before/after NLX |
| 20 | Prepare Aided Report or Medical Treatment of Prisoner (if person arrested) |
| 21 | If UMOS gave NLX, prepare **NYS Public Safety Naloxone Administration Reporting (PSNAR)** — give to Desk Officer |
| 22 | Voucher drug/paraphernalia as directed by ECT as Investigatory Evidence in FORMS-Property |

### Patrol Supervisor

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 1 | Request **ECT** (Evidence Collection Team) to respond |
| 2 | Request detective squad if: DOA, Likely to die, or Naloxone administered |
| 3 | If **DOA**: prepare Report on Typed Letterhead to Chief of Department noting NLX given and by whom, department report serial #s and/or log #s (61, Aided, Naloxone Log #, etc.) |
| 3a | Forward copy of 49 with copies of all reports to: Chief of Dept, Chief of Patrol, Chief of Crime Control Strategies, Crime Analysis Unit, Supervising Chief Surgeon, Borough/Bureau training coordinator, Training Sergeant |

### Desk Officer

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 1 | Notify: C.O./Duty Captain, Patrol Borough, and **Operations** of fatal/non-fatal suspected overdoses |
| 1a | If NLX given, obtain Naloxone Log # and advise who gave it (PD, FD, other person, etc.) |
| 1b | Include any Department report serial or log #s prepared |
| 2 | Document: Ensure notification to Ops and NLX Log # is on Aided, 61, Medical Treatment of Prisoner |
| 3 | Review: Aided, 61, Medical Treatment of Prisoner — verify reports are accurate and complete |
| 4 | Review **PSNAR** if prepared — forward for completeness and **EMAIL a copy to Operations Division** |

### Detective Squad Investigator

- Responds if DOA, Likely to die, or NLX used (those 3 triggers again)
- Investigate and confer with ECT when appropriate

### ECT Personnel

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 1 | Respond to **all** overdoses, fatal and non-fatal |
| 2 | Photo all drugs/paraphernalia and upload photos to **Enterprise Case Management System (ECMS)** |
| 3 | Document any relative stamps/markings on glassines or wax folds |
| 4 | Voucher — ensure evidence is invoiced as "Investigatory Evidence" by initial responding unit |
| 5 | Prints/DNA testing — if needed, follow P.G. 218-09, "Non-Drug Evidence going to the Lab" |

### Detective Squad Supervisor

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 1 | Supervise initial investigation; ensure preliminary steps completed and photos uploaded to ECMS |
| 2 | Prepare a **Detective Bureau Unusual Occurrence Report** and make appropriate notifications |
| 3 | Review prior cases looking for **patterns or clusters** — if yes, notify DB Zone Captain for follow-up with Narcotics counterpart |

### Hypodermic Syringes and Needles — Additional Data

**Safe Handling and Invoicing:**
- Utilize hypo container (comes in a plastic bag with instructions)
- Hypo containers on **separate voucher** apart from other property/evidence in same occurrence
- Place hypo containers in serialized **Plastic Security Envelope (PSE)** if involved in same occurrence
- No other evidence (glassines, etc.) will be placed in the same security envelope

**Storage:**
- Invoice and PSE in property locker forwarded to **Property Clerk facility**

**Critical Rules:**
- **NEVER** attempt to remove or dislodge a needle from a hypodermic syringe
- If hypo syringe/needles may bear fingerprints or other forensic evidence — safeguard until examined by Crime Scene Unit
- If NOT evidence — make safe using hypo container, dispose in "sharps" container at medical facility

> **Exam Alert:** Hypo needles and syringes are advisable to collect at overdose scenes as evidence in an overdose investigation, BUT **NOT** as evidence of the crime of possession of drug paraphernalia per P.G. 216-23.

### Changes to the Law

**Penal Law 220.45** (criminally possessing a hypodermic instrument) — **REPEALED**
- Legalized the possession of hypodermic needles
- No longer need to get from a needle exchange program or pharmacy to legally possess

**Penal Law 220.03** (criminal possession of a controlled substance 7th) — **AMENDED**
- No longer a violation to possess residue of a controlled substance on any hypodermic needle, regardless of where the needle was obtained

**General Business Law Section 850:**
- Hypo — no longer considered drug paraphernalia by statute

**Public Health Law Section 3381(1)(c)(ii) — AMENDED:**
- No limit on number of hypos available for sale
- No longer a violation to purchase, possess with intent to sell, or offer for sale a hypo needle or syringe
- **Lawful** to obtain/possess hypo syringe or needle regardless of whether possession was authorized

> **Exam Alert:** If questions arise regarding the validity of an arrest related to these law changes, contact the **Legal Bureau** per P.G. 216-23.

---

# Section 216 — Special Aided Cases

## P.G. 216-07: Firearm Safety Stations at Psychiatric Wards and Admitting Areas

**Date Effective:** 07/01/20 | **Pages:** 2

### Purpose

To utilize firearms safety stations, when available, for the safe unloading and reloading of firearms at psychiatric wards or psychiatric admitting areas of city hospitals.

### Procedure — Uniformed Member of the Service

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 1 | Escort mentally ill or emotionally disturbed person to designated patient waiting area |
| 2 | Sign in and complete entries under captions in **Psychiatric Admitting Log** maintained by hospital security police |
| 3 | Proceed to designated firearms safety station, when available, and **unload firearm(s)** |
| 3a | Replace unloaded firearm(s) in holster and put loose ammunition, and/or magazine containing ammunition, **into pocket** |
| 4 | Confer with psychiatric admitting staff and provide necessary information |
| 5 | Safeguard patient at hospital until examined by a psychiatrist |
| 6 | Inform psychiatrist of circumstances which brought patient into police custody |
| 7 | Enter details in digital Activity Log and prepare AIDED REPORT (indicate name of psychiatrist and admission number) |
| 8 | Proceed to designated firearms safety station and **reload firearm(s)** prior to leaving hospital facility |
| 9 | Complete "time-out" entry in Psychiatric Admitting Log |
| 10 | Submit AIDED REPORT to desk officer |

> **Exam Alert:** If the psychiatrist **refers a patient to the hospital emergency room** for medical evaluation and clearance, the member shall **reload** firearm(s) at the safety station before leaving the psychiatric area, and **unload upon return**.

> **Exam Alert:** Interim entries must be made in "Remarks" column of the Psychiatric Admitting Log for each unload/reload cycle.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Patrol supervisors/integrity control officers shall comply with unloading procedures and make a **visit entry** in the Psychiatric Admitting Log when supervising subordinates.

### Relieving Member

If safeguarding extends beyond expiration of tour, the relieving uniformed member will:
- Comply with sign-in and firearms unloading procedures
- Inform psychiatrist of circumstances regarding aided

### DOC Forensic Psychiatry

When a mentally ill or emotionally disturbed prisoner is escorted to a Department of Correction Forensic Psychiatry Ward, comply with the regulations of the Department of Correction regarding unloading and safeguarding firearms.

### HHC Facilities for Emergency Psychiatric Admission

| Hospital |
|----------|
| Bellevue Hospital Center |
| Bronx Municipal Hospital Center |
| City Hospital Center at Elmhurst |
| Coney Island Hospital |
| Harlem Hospital Center |
| Kings County Hospital Center |
| Lincoln Hospital |
| Metropolitan Hospital Center |
| North Central Bronx Hospital |
| Queens Hospital Center |
| Woodhull Hospital |

---

## P.G. 216-08: Persons Overcome by Gas, Drugs, or Other Poisonous Substances

**Date Effective:** 07/01/20 | **Pages:** 2

### Purpose

To secure special assistance and obtain samples of poisonous substances in appropriate cases.

### Procedure — Uniformed Member of the Service

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 1 | Call an ambulance |
| 2 | Request Emergency Service Unit, if oxygen required |
| 3 | Give ambulance attendant information regarding the nature of the poisoning |
| 4 | Permit doctor or attendant to take the substance, prescription, or container, **if not required as evidence** |
| 4a | If evidence, give **sample** to doctor/attendant and deliver remainder to desk officer or detective |
| 5 | Notify and make samples available to Poison Control Center of the Health Department, if required |
| 6 | Obtain a receipt for such materials |
| 7 | Make digital Activity Log entry of facts and time notifications were made |
| 8 | Prepare AIDED REPORT |
| 9 | Prepare COMPLAINT REPORT WORKSHEET (PD313-152A), if necessary |

### Desk Officer Notification

The desk officer will notify the **Operations Unit** in:
- Poisoned or infected food cases
- Wood or denatured alcohol poisoning cases

> **Exam Alert:** The **Operations Unit** (not Poison Control) is notified for poisoned food and denatured alcohol cases.

### Additional Guidelines for Contaminated Food/Drug Samples

| Scenario | Action |
|----------|--------|
| Spoiled/contaminated food from restaurants | Refer to Dept. of Health, Food Poisoning Investigation Unit |
| Person treated at hospital for poisoning (sample available) | Take sample to DOH Laboratories, 455 First Avenue, Manhattan, with REQUEST FOR LABORATORY EXAMINATION REPORT (PD521-168) |
| Pattern of several separate poisoning incidents | Notify Poison Control Center; comply with instructions received |
| Mass removal of potentially dangerous product | Handled case by case; **NEVER** remove items from retail establishment to stationhouse without consultation with city/state/federal agencies via Operations Unit |
| Evidence with criminal sanctions | Deliver to Police Laboratory with REQUEST FOR LABORATORY EXAMINATION attached |
| Non-evidence materials | Invoice and forward to Property Clerk |
| Citizen-delivered substance (no public health/criminal allegation) | **Do NOT accept**; refer to private laboratories for civil cases |

> **Exam Alert:** Mass product removal is handled case by case. Under **no circumstances** will items be removed from retail shelves and brought to a stationhouse before consultation with agencies via the Operations Unit.

### Key Forms

| Form | Number |
|------|--------|
| AIDED REPORT | (Electronic) |
| COMPLAINT REPORT | PD313-152 |
| REQUEST FOR LABORATORY EXAMINATION REPORT | PD521-168 |

---

## P.G. 216-09: Animal Bites

**Date Effective:** 11/18/21 | **Revision:** I.O. 110 | **Pages:** 2

### Purpose

To notify the NYC Department of Health and Mental Hygiene of human exposure to a rabid animal, an animal suspected of being rabid, and/or an animal that has bitten, scratched, or abraded a human being and to provide for observation of the animal involved.

### Key Definition

**HUMAN EXPOSURE TO RABIES** — A bite or a scratch from a rabid animal, an animal suspected of being rabid, or contamination of an open or fresh wound (having bled within the last twenty-four hours) or mucous membranes (eyes, nose, mouth) with infectious material (saliva, nervous tissue) from a rabid or suspected rabid animal.

### Procedure — Uniformed Member of the Service

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 1 | Render reasonable aid and prepare AIDED REPORT, if applicable |
| 2 | Comply with P.G. 219-17, "Use of Patrol Kits" |
| 3 | Advise person to thoroughly **wash affected area with soap and water** and seek medical attention as quickly as possible (even if not serious) |
| 4 | Ascertain owner of animal; obtain description and attempt to locate for examination |
| 4b | If animal is located, comply with P.G. 214-33, "Care and Disposition of Animals" |
| 5 | Prepare **DANGEROUS ANIMAL/BITE REPORT (PD311-152)** in ALL instances involving an animal bite and/or dangerous animal; include ICAD Incident number |
| 5a | If the animal is gone upon arrival, a DANGEROUS ANIMAL/BITE REPORT **must still be prepared**, regardless of whether a person was injured |
| 6 | Deliver DANGEROUS ANIMAL/BITE REPORT and submit AIDED REPORT to desk officer |

> **Exam Alert:** The DANGEROUS ANIMAL/BITE REPORT must be prepared in **ALL instances** — even if the animal is gone and no person was injured.

### Desk Officer (Steps 7-13)

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 7-8 | Review and approve AIDED REPORT; process per P.G. 216-02 |
| 9 | Review and sign DANGEROUS ANIMAL/BITE REPORT; distribute as delineated |
| 10 | Notify DOHMH by telephone of animal bite/exposure |
| 11 | Forward DOHMH copy of report via Mail and Distribution Unit |
| 12 | Notify DOHMH if animal/owner is located after report forwarded; ensure supplementary info recorded on AIDED REPORT in FORMS |
| 13 | File command copy of all DANGEROUS ANIMAL/BITE REPORTS |

### DOHMH Contact Hours

| Time | Contact |
|------|---------|
| 0900-1700, Monday-Friday | Dept. of Health and Mental Hygiene, **Animal Bite Unit** |
| After hours | **Poison Control Center** of DOHMH |

> **Exam Alert:** After-hours animal bite notifications go to **Poison Control**, not the Animal Bite Unit.

### Critical Additional Data

**Raccoons:** Raccoons are primarily **nocturnal** animals; any raccoon seen active during the **daytime** should be considered rabid until proven otherwise. Any raccoon acting friendly or aggressively should be considered rabid.

> **Exam Alert:** Daytime raccoon = presumed rabid.

**Firearms and Animals:** Uniformed members shall **not discharge their firearms** at a dog or other animal **except** to protect themselves or another person from physical injury and there is **no other reasonable means** to eliminate the threat.

**Snakebite:** Requires notification to the Poison Control Center of DOHMH. Enter notification on AIDED REPORT.

### Key Forms

| Form | Number |
|------|--------|
| AIDED REPORT | (Electronic) |
| DANGEROUS ANIMAL/BITE REPORT | PD311-152 |

---

## P.G. 216-10: Taxicab Drivers

**Date Effective:** 08/01/13 | **Pages:** 1

### Purpose

To report certain aided cases involving taxicab drivers to the NYC Taxi and Limousine Commission.

### Procedure

When a taxicab is involved and the driver has epilepsy, vertigo, heart disease, or other condition making him/her unfit to operate a taxi:

| Step | Role | Action |
|------|------|--------|
| 1 | MOS | Follow normal aided case procedures |
| 2 | MOS | Obtain driver's credentials and give **RECEIPT FOR CREDENTIALS (PD616-011)** |
| 3 | Desk Officer | Forward credentials and facts on **REPORT OF VIOLATION (PD672-151)** to NYC Taxi and Limousine Commission |
| 4 | Desk Officer | Note on REPORT OF VIOLATION if unable to obtain credentials |

### Key Forms

| Form | Number |
|------|--------|
| RECEIPT FOR CREDENTIALS | PD616-011 |
| REPORT OF VIOLATION | PD672-151 |

---

## P.G. 216-11: Communicable Disease

**Date Effective:** 11/18/21 | **Revision:** I.O. 109 | **Pages:** 1

### Purpose

To assist in the orderly removal of a person with a communicable disease.

### Definition

**COMMUNICABLE DISEASE** — An infectious disease capable of being passed to another by direct contact with an infected individual or his/her discharges.

### Procedure

| Step | Role | Action |
|------|------|--------|
| 1 | Desk Officer | Assign a uniformed member to assist in the removal of patient |
| 2 | MOS | Comply with A.G. 330-09, "Exposure of Members of the Service to Infectious Diseases or Hazardous Materials" |
| 3 | MOS | Accompany patient to the hospital when requested by attendant or physician |

### Confidentiality Rules

> **Exam Alert:** Information concerning a prisoner's or victim's communicable disease status must be kept **CONFIDENTIAL**.

- Generally should **NOT** be released to: the public, the media, the person's family and friends, or other prisoners
- Any release may only be made with the **written consent of the Deputy Commissioner — Legal Matters**

> **Exam Alert:** Only the **Deputy Commissioner — Legal Matters** can authorize release of communicable disease information (with written consent).

### Prisoner Segregation

- Prisoners who **volunteer** their positive status indicating the need for segregation or medical treatment should be placed in **isolation cells**, if available
- This information may be recorded on a Prisoner Movement Slip or on form **MEDICAL TREATMENT OF PRISONER (PD244-150)** to alert Corrections, DA's offices, or courts

### Key Forms

| Form | Number |
|------|--------|
| MEDICAL TREATMENT OF PRISONER | PD244-150 |

---

## P.G. 216-12: School Crossing Guard (SCG) Injured Line of Duty

### Purpose

To report and investigate injuries to School Crossing Guards sustained in the line of duty.

### Procedure

#### Uniformed Member of the Service
- Request **Patrol Supervisor** to respond

#### Patrol Supervisor
- Interview School Crossing Guard (get hospital, EMS, and doctor info if removed)
- Interview witnesses and have them make a **Witness Statement**
- Document:
  - **Employee's Notice of Injury (Law Department Form WCD 23)** — have SCG prepare in own handwriting, if possible, **2 copies**
  - Submit to **C.O. of Precinct of occurrence** (breaks the chain of command)
  - Have the UMOS prepare either an **Aided Report** or the **PAR**, if vehicle involved
- Notify:
  - Report circumstances to the Desk Officer
  - If SCG brought to hospital, notify Ops promptly
  - A **2nd notification** is required before final reports are prepared to update condition

> **Exam Alert:** No LODI Report for Crossing Guards — it is a WCD 23 "Employee Notice of Injury" instead per P.G. 216-12.

> **Exam Alert:** The WCD 23 is submitted to the **C.O. of the Precinct of occurrence** — this breaks the chain of command (not through the desk officer) per P.G. 216-12.

#### Desk Officer
- Document:
  - Command Log entry regarding circumstances and result of investigation
  - Report on Typed Letterhead (49) — attach a COPY of the Command Log entry and deliver to C.O.
- Notify:
  - Employee Resources Section (ERS), Civilian Employee Services Unit — **NEXT BUSINESS DAY**
  - Operations — **WITHIN 24 HOURS**

> **Exam Alert — Timely Reporting:** Desk Officer calls Operations within **24 hours**. C.O. directs "Supervisor's Report of Injury" within **48 hours** per P.G. 216-12.

#### Commanding Officer
- Forward 2 copies of (WCD 23, 49, and Witness Statement) to ERS, Attn: Civilian Employee Unit
- Crossing Guard is **Not Paid** — keep Guard "on payroll, WITHOUT PAY" until they return and cause the "Election of Rate of Change Against Annual and/or Sick Leave Balances" form to be prepared
- Notify ERS, Civilian Employee Unit when Guard returns to duty, goes sick again on this injury, and when returns again from that last sick
- Document:
  - Direct "Supervisor's Report of Injury" gets made within **48 hours**
  - Direct "Report to Employee Benefits Unit" be prepared
- C.O. directs that 3 reports get made (doesn't specifically say by whom):
  1. Supervisor's Report of Injury — **WITHIN 48 HOURS**
  2. Report to Employee Benefits Unit
  3. Election of Rate of Change Against Annual and/or Sick Leave Balances

#### Forms and Reports Summary

| Who | Form/Report |
|-----|-------------|
| UMOS | Aided Report or PAR |
| School Crossing Guard | Employee's Notice of Injury (WCD 23) — given to C.O. |
| Desk Officer | Typed Letterhead (given to C.O.) |
| Desk Officer | Command Log entry (given to C.O.) |
| Witness | WITNESS STATEMENT — Injury To Member Of Department |

---

# Section 216 — Key Terms, Forms, and Critical Numbers

## Definitions

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| Aided Case | Any occurrence requiring a person OTHER THAN A PRISONER to receive medical aid or assistance (sick/injured, dead, lost, mentally ill, abandoned child, runaway, homeless) | P.G. 216-01 |
| Routine Sick at Home | Aided at residence who is conscious, identified, no dependent adults/uncared children, no other police service needed — requires only Activity Log entry | P.G. 216-01 |
| Routine Sick in Nursing Home | Uniformed members WILL NOT respond; exceptions: injury, cardiac, D.O.A., etc. | P.G. 216-01 |
| Human Exposure to Rabies | Bite/scratch from rabid or suspected rabid animal, or contamination of open wound (bled within 24 hours) or mucous membranes with infectious material | P.G. 216-09 |
| Communicable Disease | Infectious disease capable of being passed by direct contact with an infected individual or their discharges | P.G. 216-11 |
| Line of Duty (Auxiliary) | After "sign-in" on Auxiliary Police Roll Call (APS #10) until sign-out; NO off-duty designation | P.G. 216-13 |
| Carbon Monoxide | Colorless, odorless gas; symptoms include headache, nausea, vomiting, confusion; severe cases show cherry red skin | P.G. 216-21 |
| AED Utilization | Once AED pads are placed on an aided — even if no shock is administered | P.G. 216-19 |
| CPR Administered | Once a member performs rapid chest compressions or mouth-to-mouth ventilation | P.G. 216-19 |
| QMHP | Qualified Mental Health Professional — licensed psychologist, certified social worker, or registered professional nurse eligible to direct a Section 9.58 removal | P.G. 216-22 |
| Mobile Crisis Outreach Teams | Hospital/community-based teams funded by DOHMH that assess, stabilize, and refer patients | P.G. 216-22 |
| ACT Teams | "Hospitals on wheels" — richly staffed mental health teams licensed by NYS Office of Mental Health; may order involuntary transport | P.G. 216-22 |
| Opioids | Opiate, synthetic or otherwise — includes heroin, morphine, oxycodone, fentanyl, methadone, hydrocodone, codeine | P.G. 216-23 |
| Fentanyl | Fast-acting synthetic opioid, 30-50x more powerful than heroin, 80-100x more powerful than morphine; can be absorbed through the skin | P.G. 216-23 |
| Opioid Antagonist | FDA-approved drug (Naloxone/Narcan) that negates or neutralizes effects of an opioid; limited to meds approved by Dept of Health | P.G. 216-23 |
| Naloxone (NLX) | NOT a controlled substance; possession is legal; does NOT establish probable cause to arrest | P.G. 216-23 |
| Drug Paraphernalia | Items associated with narcotics use/preparation/storage (straws, scales, glassines, etc.); hypodermic needles are NOT classified as drug paraphernalia | P.G. 216-23 |
| Stamps | Image/writing drug dealers use to ID their "brand"; located on wax folds or glassines | P.G. 216-23 |
| PSNAR | NYS Public Safety Naloxone Administration Reporting — prepared by UMOS who administers NLX; given to Desk Officer | P.G. 216-23 |
| ECMS | Enterprise Case Management System — ECT uploads overdose photos here | P.G. 216-23 |
| 911 Good Samaritan Law | Protects from arrest for possessing controlled substance/paraphernalia/alcohol (under 21) when seeking help in good faith for drug/alcohol overdose | P.G. 216-23 |
| School Crossing Guard LOD | No LODI Report; uses Employee's Notice of Injury (WCD 23) instead; SCG prepares in own handwriting if possible | P.G. 216-12 |

## Mnemonics

| Mnemonic | Meaning | Source |
|----------|---------|--------|
| **CHILD-ERS** | **C**hild, **H**omeless, **I**njured, **L**ost, **D**OA, **E**DP, **R**equires care, **S**ick — the 8 aided case categories | P.G. 216-01 |
| **TED the M.D. from D.C.** | **T**echnician (Highway CIS), **E**SU, **D**octor/Paramedic/EMT, **M**edical examiner, **D**istrict attorney, **D**etective Bureau, **C**rime Scene Unit — persons who may disturb a dead body | P.G. 216-04 |
| **I C no P.A.I.N.** | **I**dentified and **C**onscious, no **P**olice service, no dependent **A**dults/uncared children, no **I**nvestigation, no **N**otifications — Routine Sick at Home criteria (Activity Log only) | P.G. 216-01 |
| **3 "UNs"** | **UN**-identified, **UN**-conscious, **UN**-sound mind (EDP) — ride in back of ambulance with aided | P.G. 216-01 |

## Key Forms and Reports

| Form Name | Form Number | Used In |
|-----------|-------------|---------|
| AIDED REPORT | Electronic (FORMS) | All aided cases |
| IDENTIFICATION TAG | PD317-091 | Dead bodies, body parts, fetuses delivered to morgue |
| COMPLAINT REPORT | PD313-152 | Deaths, suspicious circumstances, DOC deaths |
| COMPLAINT REPORT WORKSHEET | PD313-152A | Dead body cases, CO alarm cases |
| MISSING/UNIDENTIFIED PERSONS REPORT | PD336-151 | Unidentified deceased or aided |
| Police Accident Report | MV104AN | Vehicle collisions, auxiliary police vehicle collisions |
| DANGEROUS ANIMAL/BITE REPORT | PD311-152 | ALL animal bite/dangerous animal incidents |
| RECEIPT FOR CREDENTIALS | PD616-011 | Taxicab driver cases |
| REPORT OF VIOLATION | PD672-151 | Taxicab driver cases (to TLC) |
| REQUEST FOR LABORATORY EXAMINATION REPORT | PD521-168 | Poison/substance cases sent to DOH Laboratories |
| MEDICAL TREATMENT OF PRISONER | PD244-150 | Communicable disease prisoners; AED/CPR on prisoners |
| Employee's Notice of Injury | WCD 23 (Law Department) | Auxiliary police LOD injury |
| WITNESS STATEMENT — INJURY TO MEMBER OF DEPARTMENT | PD429-065 | Auxiliary police LOD injury witnesses |
| UNUSUAL OCCURRENCE REPORT | PD370-152 | Serious auxiliary police injuries/deaths |
| THREAT, RESISTANCE OR INJURY (T.R.I.) INCIDENT REPORT | — | Auxiliary police force incidents |
| OMH 482 Form | OMH 482 | Mobile Crisis Team Authorization for Transport |
| Section 9.58 Identification Card | — | QMHP identification for involuntary removal |
| NYS Public Safety Naloxone Administration Reporting (PSNAR) | — | Prepared when UMOS administers NLX; emailed to Operations Division |
| Opioid Antagonist Kit | — | Intranasal atomization devices, safety gloves, rescue breathing face shield, OA delivery devices |
| Complaint Report (Overdose) | PD313-152 | "Investigate Aided-Drug Overdose" or "Investigate DOA-Possible Drug Overdose" |

## Critical Numbers and Time Limits

| Item | Value | Source |
|------|-------|--------|
| Second ambulance call | **20 minutes** after first call if ambulance does not arrive | P.G. 216-01 |
| ME follow-up call | **1 hour** if ME fails to respond | P.G. 216-04 |
| Morgue identification (homicide) | **0900 hours, next day** — officer who discovered body reports to morgue | P.G. 216-04 |
| Reports to Auxiliary Police Section | Within **10 days** | P.G. 216-13 |
| DOHMH Animal Bite Unit hours | **0900-1700, Monday-Friday** | P.G. 216-09 |
| After-hours animal bite notification | **Poison Control Center** | P.G. 216-09 |
| 9-8-8 Hotline availability | **24 hours/day, 7 days/week** in 200+ languages | P.G. 216-01 |
| Rabies wound definition | Open wound that **bled within last 24 hours** | P.G. 216-09 |
| NLX re-dose window | **3-5 minutes** — if no response to Naloxone, administer 1 additional dose | P.G. 216-23 |
| Waterproof body covers in P/S car | **4** covers required | P.G. 219-01 |
| SCG — D.O. notify Operations | **Within 24 hours** | P.G. 216-12 |
| SCG — Supervisor's Report of Injury | **Within 48 hours** (C.O. directs) | P.G. 216-12 |
| 3 triggers for detective squad (OD) | DOA, Likely to die, or Naloxone administered | P.G. 216-23 |
| 3 requirements to close UF 61 (DOA) | Identified, ME says natural, relatives present and notified | P.G. 216-04 |

## Critical Notification Contacts

| Situation | Notify |
|-----------|--------|
| Poisoned/infected food or denatured alcohol | Operations Unit (via desk officer) |
| Homicide, suspicious death, suicide | Operations Unit, detective squad, Crime Scene Unit (via detective) |
| DOC suspicious death/suicide | Detective squad, CO/duty captain, patrol borough, **IAB** (get log number), Operations Unit |
| Scaffold injury/death | Department of Buildings |
| MTA bus injury/death | NYC Transit |
| Construction site, Parks property, factory/store injury/death | Operations Unit |
| Firefighter killed/injured at fire | DO NOT notify family without **fire officer** permission |
| DOC inmate death | DO NOT notify family unless **Correction supervisor** requests |
| Auxiliary police injury requiring hospital | Operations Unit (promptly) |
| Carbon monoxide alarm (City-owned building) | Department of Buildings (via Communications) |
| Section 9.58 removal validity in question | Legal Bureau |

| Woodhull Hospital | Brooklyn |

| Queens Hospital Center | Queens |

## Persons Authorized to Disturb a Dead Body

| Letter | Authorized Person |
|--------|-------------------|
| a | Paramedic, E.M.T., or doctor (caution: do not disturb evidence) |
| b | Medical Examiner or assistant |
| c | District Attorney or assistant |
| d | Members of Detective Bureau, detective squad, or Crime Scene Unit |
| e | Highway District member assigned as "collision technician" or "collision investigator" |

| Electrode pads with wire leads (sealed packages) | 2 packages (2 pads each) |
| Pocket mask | 1 |
| Gloves | 1 pair |

| Razor | 1 |

## Investigating Supervisor Hierarchy — Auxiliary Police

| Priority | Role | Condition |
|----------|------|-----------|
| 1st | Lieutenant platoon commander | If available |
| 2nd | Patrol supervisor | If LPC unavailable |
| 3rd | CO/XO/duty captain | Serious physical injury, likely to die, or death |

# Section 216 — Review Questions

## Question 1
A uniformed member responds to a residence where a person is conscious, properly identified, and requesting an ambulance for stomach pain. There are no dependent adults or uncared-for children, and no other police service is required. What documentation is needed?

- A) An AIDED REPORT and digital Activity Log entry
- B) Only a digital Activity Log entry
- C) An AIDED REPORT, COMPLAINT REPORT, and Activity Log entry
- D) No documentation is required for routine sick at home

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**B) Only a digital Activity Log entry**

P.G. 216-01 — A "Routine Sick at Home" requires ONLY a digital Activity Log entry when the aided is at a residence AND is conscious and properly identified, no other police service is required, no dependent adults or uncared-for children are present, and no other investigation is needed. All four conditions are met here.
</details>

---

## Question 2
Which of the following persons is NOT authorized to disturb a dead body at a scene?

- A) A member of the Emergency Service Unit, if the body is on train tracks
- B) A member of Highway District assigned as a collision technician
- C) The patrol supervisor directing the investigation
- D) The Medical Examiner or assistant Medical Examiner

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**C) The patrol supervisor directing the investigation**

P.G. 216-04 — Only six categories of persons may disturb a body: (a) paramedic/EMT/doctor, (b) ME or assistant, (c) DA or assistant, (d) Detective Bureau/squad/Crime Scene Unit, (e) Highway District collision technicians/investigators, (f) ESU on train tracks/subway tunnel. The patrol supervisor is not among them.
</details>

---

## Question 3
When a uniformed member responds to an aided case involving a suspected drug overdose (cocaine psychosis), what is the proper disposition of the aided?

- A) Bring the aided to the precinct stationhouse for processing
- B) Have the aided brought to an appropriate hospital facility
- C) Release the aided to a responsible family member
- D) Have the aided transported to a police detention facility for evaluation

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**B) Have the aided brought to an appropriate hospital facility**

P.G. 216-01 — The aided is NOT to be brought to a police facility. The patrol supervisor determines if the aided should be removed to the appropriate hospital by RMP or await the ambulance. Drug overdose/cocaine psychosis aided persons go to the hospital, never to a police facility.
</details>

---

## Question 4
At a psychiatric ward, the attending psychiatrist refers a patient to the hospital emergency room for medical evaluation. What should the uniformed member do regarding their firearm?

- A) Keep the firearm unloaded for the entire duration
- B) Reload at the firearms safety station before leaving the psychiatric area, and unload upon return
- C) Secure the firearm with hospital security
- D) Leave the firearm unloaded and in the holster at all times while in the hospital

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**B) Reload at the firearms safety station before leaving the psychiatric area, and unload upon return**

P.G. 216-07 — If the psychiatrist refers a patient to the hospital ER for medical evaluation and clearance, the member shall reload firearm(s) at the safety station before leaving the psychiatric admitting area, and unload upon return. Interim entries must be made in the Psychiatric Admitting Log.
</details>

---

## Question 5
A raccoon is observed in a park during mid-afternoon, approaching people in a friendly manner. How should this animal be treated?

- A) As a normal animal; raccoons sometimes adjust their schedules
- B) As potentially rabid until proven otherwise
- C) As a danger only if it displays aggressive behavior
- D) As requiring no police action unless it bites someone

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**B) As potentially rabid until proven otherwise**

P.G. 216-09 — Raccoons are primarily nocturnal animals; any raccoon seen active during the daytime should be considered rabid until proven otherwise. Any raccoon acting friendly or aggressively should be considered rabid.
</details>

---

## Question 6
Under what circumstances may a uniformed member discharge a firearm at a dog or other animal?

- A) When the animal has bitten a person
- B) When the animal is suspected of being rabid
- C) To protect themselves or another person from physical injury when there is no other reasonable means to eliminate the threat
- D) When directed to do so by a supervisor

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**C) To protect themselves or another person from physical injury when there is no other reasonable means to eliminate the threat**

P.G. 216-09 — Members shall not discharge their firearms at a dog or other animal except to protect themselves or another person from physical injury and there is no other reasonable means to eliminate the threat.
</details>

---

## Question 7
Who may authorize the release of information about a prisoner's communicable disease status?

- A) The commanding officer of the precinct
- B) The Deputy Commissioner — Legal Matters, with written consent
- C) The precinct desk officer, in an emergency
- D) The patrol supervisor on scene

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**B) The Deputy Commissioner — Legal Matters, with written consent**

P.G. 216-11 — Information concerning a prisoner's communicable disease status must be kept confidential. Any release may only be made with the written consent of the Deputy Commissioner — Legal Matters.
</details>

---

## Question 8
An auxiliary police officer sustains a serious physical injury while on duty. Who acts as the investigating supervisor?

- A) The patrol supervisor, precinct of occurrence
- B) The lieutenant platoon commander, precinct of occurrence
- C) The precinct commanding officer/executive officer/duty captain
- D) The commanding officer of the Auxiliary Police Section

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**C) The precinct commanding officer/executive officer/duty captain**

P.G. 216-13 — The CO/XO/duty captain will be the investigating supervisor if the injured auxiliary police officer sustains a serious physical injury, is likely to die, or dies. The lieutenant platoon commander handles non-serious cases.
</details>

---

## Question 9
How must the Employee's Notice of Injury (WCD 23) be prepared for an injured auxiliary police officer?

- A) Typed by the desk officer
- B) Completed electronically in FORMS
- C) In the auxiliary police officer's own handwriting, if possible, in two copies
- D) Dictated to the patrol supervisor for transcription

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**C) In the auxiliary police officer's own handwriting, if possible, in two copies**

P.G. 216-13 — The patrol supervisor will have the auxiliary police officer prepare, in own handwriting if possible, two copies of Employee's Notice of Injury (WCD 23). If the member is unable, the patrol supervisor will prepare and submit the report.
</details>

---

## Question 10
An aided case occurs on a NYC Transit train. What is the primary concern regarding service interruption?

- A) Delays may cause commuter complaints
- B) Unnecessary interruption can increase temperatures significantly and may cause panic on crowded trains stopped in tunnels
- C) Service interruptions require MTA authorization
- D) Union regulations prohibit stopping trains for more than 10 minutes

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**B) Unnecessary interruption can increase temperatures significantly and may cause panic on crowded trains stopped in tunnels**

P.G. 216-14 — Unnecessary interruption of train service can inconvenience thousands of passengers and endanger public welfare. In addition, unnecessary interruptions can increase temperatures significantly in trains and stations, and may cause panic on crowded trains stopped in tunnels. Service will be interrupted only when absolutely necessary.
</details>

---

## Question 11
A homicide victim's family needs to be notified. Who is responsible for making that notification?

- A) The desk officer at the precinct of occurrence
- B) The patrol supervisor on scene
- C) The assigned detective
- D) The Operations Unit

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**C) The assigned detective**

P.G. 216-15 — The assigned detective will make notification to relatives or friends of a homicide victim.
</details>

---

## Question 12
An inmate dies in a Department of Correction facility. Regarding notification of the inmate's family, what is the correct procedure?

- A) The desk officer will immediately notify the family
- B) The detective squad will notify the family
- C) Notifications WILL NOT be made unless requested by the Correction supervisor in charge
- D) The Operations Unit will coordinate notification

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**C) Notifications WILL NOT be made unless requested by the Correction supervisor in charge**

P.G. 216-16 — Notifications will NOT be made to relatives or friends of an inmate who dies in a DOC facility unless requested to do so by the Correction supervisor in charge.
</details>

---

## Question 13
When notified of a suspicious death in a DOC facility, which agency must the desk officer notify to obtain a log number?

- A) Operations Unit
- B) Crime Scene Unit
- C) Internal Affairs Bureau
- D) Detective Borough Command

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**C) Internal Affairs Bureau**

P.G. 216-16 — The desk officer must notify the Internal Affairs Bureau and obtain a log number for DOC suspicious deaths and suicides.
</details>

---

## Question 14
The Department considers an AED to have been "utilized" when:

- A) A shock is delivered to the patient
- B) The AED is removed from its carrying case
- C) The AED pads are placed on the aided, even if no shock is administered
- D) The AED is turned on and the analysis is completed

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**C) The AED pads are placed on the aided, even if no shock is administered**

P.G. 216-19 — Once an AED is placed on an aided, the Department considers that as utilized, even if a shock is not administered. When the AED pads are placed, the machine performs an analysis and advises what actions should be taken.
</details>

---

## Question 15
An AED should only be attached to a patient who meets which criteria?

- A) Unconscious adult, not breathing, no pulse
- B) Any unconscious person regardless of age
- C) Any person experiencing chest pain
- D) Unconscious adult or child, not breathing

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**A) Unconscious adult, not breathing, no pulse**

P.G. 216-19 — The AED will only be attached to an unconscious adult patient after it has been confirmed that the patient is not breathing and does not have a pulse. All three criteria must be met.
</details>

---

## Question 16
If AED/CPR is administered on a prisoner in Department custody, what form is completed?

- A) AIDED REPORT
- B) MEDICAL TREATMENT OF PRISONER (PD244-150)
- C) Both AIDED REPORT and MEDICAL TREATMENT OF PRISONER
- D) COMPLAINT REPORT WORKSHEET (PD313-152A)

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**B) MEDICAL TREATMENT OF PRISONER (PD244-150)**

P.G. 216-19 — If AED/CPR is administered by a uniformed member on a prisoner in custody, a MEDICAL TREATMENT OF PRISONER (PD244-150) form will be completed instead of an AIDED REPORT.
</details>

---

## Question 17
During the removal of a TB shelter client, what is the Department's primary responsibility?

- A) Force entry to the premises and remove the patient
- B) Safeguard the patient's property
- C) Assist in the arrest and preserve the peace
- D) Provide transportation to the hospital

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**C) Assist in the arrest and preserve the peace**

P.G. 216-20 — The primary responsibility of the Department during TB shelter removals is to assist in the arrest and preserve the peace. Members are NOT to personally assist in forcing entry, securing premises, or safeguarding property. Those duties are performed by other agencies at DOHMH direction.
</details>

---

## Question 18
Upon responding to a residential carbon monoxide alarm, what should a uniformed member do?

- A) Enter the location immediately to check for victims
- B) Refrain from entering the location and request the patrol supervisor if alarm is confirmed
- C) Enter the location only if wearing a filter mask
- D) Enter the location after confirming the CO detector is not malfunctioning

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**B) Refrain from entering the location and request the patrol supervisor if alarm is confirmed**

P.G. 216-21 — The uniformed member will refrain from entering the location and request the response of the patrol supervisor if the alarm is confirmed. Only ESU determines whether the location is safe for re-entry.
</details>

---

## Question 19
When a Mobile Crisis Team requests police assistance for an involuntary removal under Section 9.58, what two documents must the QMHP present?

- A) Hospital admission form and physician's order
- B) Section 9.58 identification card and OMH 482 form
- C) Mental Hygiene warrant and court order
- D) DOHMH removal order and patient consent form

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**B) Section 9.58 identification card and OMH 482 form**

P.G. 216-22 — The uniformed member will request the physician or QMHP to present section 9.58 identification card along with signed and completed OMH 482 form (Mobile Crisis Outreach Team Authorization for Transport). Both must be photographed and uploaded to the AIDED REPORT.
</details>

---

## Question 20
If the validity of a Section 9.58 involuntary removal is questioned, whom should the uniformed member contact?

- A) Internal Affairs Bureau
- B) The district attorney's office
- C) The Legal Bureau
- D) The Operations Unit

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**C) The Legal Bureau**

P.G. 216-22 — Uniformed members of the service are reminded to contact the Legal Bureau if the validity of a removal is in question.
</details>

---

## Question 21
Using the CHILD-ERS mnemonic, which of the following is NOT one of the eight aided case categories?

- A) Homeless individual
- B) Prisoner requiring medical care
- C) Lost person
- D) Emotionally Disturbed Person

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**B) Prisoner requiring medical care**

P.G. 216-01 — CHILD-ERS stands for: Child (runaway/abandoned/destitute/abused/neglected), Homeless, Injured, Lost, DOA, EDP, Requires care (due to arrest/hospitalization/death of guardian), Sick. Prisoners are specifically EXCLUDED from aided case definitions. Use the Medical Treatment of Prisoner form instead.
</details>

---

## Question 22
A uniformed member responds to a residence where an elderly person is conscious, identified, and complaining of feeling ill. There are no dependent adults or uncared-for children, and no other police service is needed. Using the "I C no P.A.I.N." mnemonic, what documentation is required?

- A) Full Aided Report and Activity Log entry
- B) Only a digital Activity Log entry
- C) Complaint Report and Aided Report
- D) No documentation required

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**B) Only a digital Activity Log entry**

P.G. 216-01 — "I C no P.A.I.N." = Identified and Conscious, no Police service needed, no dependent Adults or uncared-for children, no Investigation needed, no Notifications required. All conditions are met, making this a "Routine Sick at Home" requiring only a digital Activity Log entry.
</details>

---

## Question 23
At a DOA scene, which of the following persons may disturb the body using the "TED the M.D. from D.C." mnemonic?

- A) The patrol supervisor directing the investigation
- B) A Highway District member on general patrol
- C) A Technician/investigator from Highway's CIS (Collision Investigation Squad)
- D) A nurse who happens to be on scene

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**C) A Technician/investigator from Highway's CIS (Collision Investigation Squad)**

P.G. 216-04 — TED the M.D. from D.C.: Technician (Highway collision tech/investigator), ESU (subway/train tracks), Doctor/Paramedic/EMT, Medical examiner (or assistant), District attorney (or assistant), Detective Bureau/squad, Crime Scene Unit. A general Highway member or nurse is NOT authorized.
</details>

---

## Question 24
What three conditions must be met to close a UF 61 (Complaint Report) at the precinct level for a dead body case?

- A) DOA identified, ME certifies death as natural, and detectives have completed investigation
- B) DOA identified, ME certifies death as natural, and relatives are present and notified
- C) DOA identified, Crime Scene Unit has processed scene, and desk officer has reviewed reports
- D) DOA identified, Operations has been notified, and relatives have been contacted

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**B) DOA identified, ME certifies death as natural, and relatives are present and notified**

P.G. 216-04 — To close the UF 61 at precinct level, all three must be met: (1) DOA is identified, (2) M.E. states cause is natural, and (3) relatives are present and notified.
</details>

---

## Question 25
A uniformed member arrives at a suspected overdose scene. The person is unresponsive and not breathing with no pulse. Naloxone was administered but the person has not responded within 3-5 minutes. What should the member do?

- A) Administer CPR/AED only and wait for EMS
- B) Administer 1 additional dose of Naloxone
- C) Transport the person immediately via RMP
- D) Request the patrol supervisor to determine next steps

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**B) Administer 1 additional dose of Naloxone**

P.G. 216-23 — If person has NOT responded to Naloxone within 3-5 minutes, administer 1 additional dose. While waiting, administer CPR/AED if not breathing and no pulse.
</details>

---

## Question 26
At a suspected overdose scene, hypodermic needles are found. How should they be handled for evidence purposes?

- A) Classify as drug paraphernalia evidence
- B) Collect as evidence in the overdose investigation, but NOT as evidence of possession of drug paraphernalia
- C) Leave them for EMS to dispose of in a sharps container
- D) Place in the same security envelope with other drug evidence

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**B) Collect as evidence in the overdose investigation, but NOT as evidence of possession of drug paraphernalia**

P.G. 216-23 — Hypodermic needles and syringes are advisable to collect at overdose scenes as evidence in an overdose investigation, but NOT as evidence of the crime of possession of drug paraphernalia. Hypo containers must be placed on a SEPARATE voucher and in a serialized Plastic Security Envelope (PSE) — no other evidence in the same envelope.
</details>

---

## Question 27
A School Crossing Guard is injured in the line of duty. What form replaces the LODI Report, and who prepares it?

- A) Aided Report, prepared by the UMOS
- B) Employee's Notice of Injury (WCD 23), prepared by the SCG in their own handwriting if possible
- C) Supervisor's Report of Injury, prepared by the patrol supervisor
- D) Unusual Occurrence Report, prepared by the desk officer

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**B) Employee's Notice of Injury (WCD 23), prepared by the SCG in their own handwriting if possible**

P.G. 216-12 — There is no LODI Report for Crossing Guards. Instead, it is an Employee's Notice of Injury (WCD 23) prepared by the SCG in their own handwriting, if possible, in two copies. If unable, the patrol supervisor will prepare and submit it.
</details>

---

## Question 28
At a suspected overdose scene, when must the patrol supervisor request the detective squad to respond?

- A) Only when the overdose is confirmed as fatal
- B) When the person is DOA, likely to die, or Naloxone was administered
- C) For all suspected overdoses, fatal and non-fatal
- D) Only when fentanyl is suspected

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

**B) When the person is DOA, likely to die, or Naloxone was administered**

P.G. 216-23 — The patrol supervisor requests ECT (Evidence Collection Team) to respond to ALL overdoses. However, the detective squad is requested only for the three triggers: DOA, likely to die, or Naloxone was administered.
</details>

---

# Section 217 — Vehicle Collisions

## Chapter Overview

Section 217 of the NYPD Patrol Guide governs procedures for **vehicle collisions**, including fatal and serious injury collisions, leaving the scene investigations, the Directed Accident Response Program (D.A.R.P.) for towing, city and NYCHA liability cases, aircraft accidents, and chemical test warrant applications for DWI suspects.

Sergeants must understand towing protocols, notification responsibilities, agency routing for city-involved incidents, and the chemical test warrant process — particularly the critical timeframes and escalation procedures.

## Learning Objectives

After studying this chapter, you should be able to:

1. Explain the mechanical defect investigation procedures for fatal/serious injury collisions.
2. Define "City Involved" and route reports to the correct city agency.
3. Describe the procedures for investigating leaving-the-scene collisions, including VTL Section 602 authority.
4. Articulate the full D.A.R.P. tow dispatch process, including rotation rules, 30-minute wait, and disposition codes.
5. State the rules for unsolicited tow trucks at collision scenes.
6. Explain aircraft accident procedures, including the pilot's 10-hour reporting obligation.
7. Describe NYCHA-involved accident reporting and notification requirements.
8. Identify the correct reference marker procedures for highway, bridge, and tunnel collisions.
9. Define "Chemical Test Warrant" and explain when it can and cannot be applied for.
10. List the escalation timeline for contacting the ADA in chemical test warrant cases.

## Procedure Index

| File | Procedures Covered |
|------|-------------------|
| section-217-collisions-general | 217-01 (cross-ref), 217-02 (cross-ref), 217-03 |
| section-217-city-agency-involved | 217-04, 217-11 |
| section-217-leaving-scene-aircraft | 217-05, 217-06 (cross-ref), 217-08, 217-10 |
| section-217-darp-towing | 217-09 |
| section-217-highways-chemical-test | 217-14, 217-16 |
| key-terms | Definitions, forms, critical numbers |
| review-questions | 15 multiple-choice exam prep questions |


---

# Section 217 — City and Agency Involved

## P.G. 217-04: Accidents and Collisions — City Involved

**Date Issued:** 10/18/16 | **Pages:** 2

### Purpose

To assist the City in processing cases in which the City may be a plaintiff or defendant.

### Key Definition

**CITY INVOLVED** — Any reported incident involving personal injury and/or property damage which may involve the City in a civil action.

> **Exam Alert:** "City Involved" is broadly defined — any incident that **may** involve the City, not just confirmed liability situations.

### Procedure

When it is determined that the City may be involved in a civil suit:

#### Uniformed Member of the Service (Steps 1-4)

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 1 | Prepare **Police Accident Report (MV104AN)** if collision, and check appropriate boxes under "DUPLICATE COPY REQUIRED FOR" |
| 2 | **OR** prepare **AIDED REPORT** in cases of personal injury where City may be involved |
| 3 | **OR** prepare **ACCIDENT REPORT — CITY INVOLVED (PD301-155)** only in cases where City may be involved and **no personal injury** resulted (property damage only) |
| 4 | Ensure AIDED REPORT contains indication that City is involved and name of City agency |

> **Exam Alert:** Three possible reports depending on circumstances: MV104AN (collision), AIDED REPORT (personal injury, non-collision), or PD301-155 (property damage only, no personal injury).

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 5 | Inform persons involved that a claim against the City must be filed with the **Comptroller of the City of New York** within **ninety (90) days** |
| 6 | Submit reports to desk officer |

> **Exam Alert:** Claims against the City must be filed with the **Comptroller** within **90 days**.

#### Desk Officer (Steps 7-8)

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 7 | Review and sign/approve reports |
| 8 | Forward reports: Police Accident Report in normal manner; ACCIDENT REPORT — CITY INVOLVED per instructions on form |
| 8a | Dispose of buff agency copy if Police Department is the **ONLY** agency involved |
| 8b | Determine City agency involved per the routing table below |

### Agency Routing Table

| Condition | Agency |
|-----------|--------|
| Traffic signs, stanchions, signal lights | Department of Transportation |
| Lampposts | Department of Transportation |
| Parkway, highway, stone wall, curb, fence, guide rail, post | DOT, Bureau of Highways, Legal Department |
| Property damage on Parks Department property; trees on parkland, city streets, or public property | Department of Parks |
| Damaged fire alarm box/post | FDNY, Actions and Claims Unit |
| City hydrants | Department of Environmental Protection **AND** FDNY, Actions and Claims Unit |
| School bus collision | Department of Education, Office of Pupil Transportation |
| Property damage from transit facilities (e.g., falling debris from elevated MTA line) | NYC Transit Law Department |
| Accidents on/near ferry, bridge, tunnel, dock, airport, or seaplane base | Agency concerned |

> **Exam Alert:** City hydrants require notification to **two** agencies: DEP **and** FDNY Actions and Claims.

> **Exam Alert:** School bus collisions require notification to DOE Office of Pupil Transportation regardless of whether children are on the bus — the PG says "with OR without kids, private or public school." *(P.G. 217-01 "Dup. Copy Required For" table)*

> **Memory Aid — Dup Copy Table (PAR back):** For the "Duplicate Copy Required For" section, know the full table from 217-01 — the following conditions on the back of the PAR trigger sending a copy to the named agency:
> - Fire Trucks/EMS/Hydrants/Alarm Boxes → FDNY Action & Claims
> - Tow Truck or Pedicab licensed by DCWP → Dept. Consumer and Worker Protection (DCWP)
> - Crash Cushion or Impact Attenuator → Dept. of Transportation
> - City Park (person killed/injured OR damage to **any** City Tree) → Dept. of Parks
> - School Bus (with OR without kids, private or public) → DOE Office of Pupil Transportation
> - Taxi or For Hire vehicle (TLC licensed — Uber/Lyft) → TLC
> - NYC personnel or property involved → Comptroller
> - DOA → Highway (CIS unit) AND Missing Persons (M.E.'s unit)
> - Bicycle Collisions ("Killed," SILD, or Critically Injured determined by EMS) → Highway CIS

> **Key Note — DOA/SILD/CI and 217-17 (Bicycle Collisions):** In P.G. 217-02, the DOA/SILD/CI and 217-17 bicycle collision cases require the UMOS to have the Sgt respond. The Sgt is NOT mentioned in 217-01. Watch for exam questions testing whether the Sgt responds under 217-01 or only 217-02/217-17. *(P.G. 217-01 Key Note)*

> **Exam Alert — DOA/SILD or Critical Injury (CI):** CI is determined by EMS on scene. A Designated MOS from CIS (Highway) coordinates the investigation and presents facts to the D.A. *(P.G. 217-01)*

> **Sergeant Focus:** It is City policy to **pursue claims in its favor**. Members must detail circumstances supporting City claims and obtain witness information. **Full cooperation** with the involved agency or Law Department is **mandatory**.

> **Prior Test:** Scenario — a city vehicle collides with a private vehicle and the city may be liable. The UMOS prepares the MV104AN and checks the "Duplicate Copy Required For" box for **Comptroller** (NYC personnel or property involved). The "City Involved" box is checked on the PAR back. Additionally, persons involved are informed they must file claims with the **Comptroller** within **90 days**. *(P.G. 217-04)*

> **See Also:** P.G. 217-01 — "Dup. Copy Required For" table on back of PAR: check "Comptroller" for NYC personnel or property involved in any collision.

### Key Forms

| Form | Number |
|------|--------|
| ACCIDENT REPORT — CITY INVOLVED | PD301-155 |
| AIDED REPORT | (Electronic) |
| Police Accident Report | MV104AN |

---

## P.G. 217-11: Accidents — New York City Housing Authority Involved

**Date Issued:** 04/13/21 | **Pages:** 2

### Purpose

To assist the New York City Housing Authority in processing cases in which NYCHA may be a plaintiff or defendant.

### Key Definition
