| **Incident Command Post** — Check-In with Resources Unit |
| **Intelligence/Investigations Section Post** — When applicable |
| **Helibase(s)** — Absent extraordinary circumstances, not used in NYC |

### Check-In Forms

**DETAIL ROSTER / ASSIGNMENT SHEETS (PD406-141)** or other appropriate forms.

### Critical Rule

Resources must formally **Check-In before** reporting to Operations Section for assignment (absent extraordinary circumstances).

> **Exam Alert:** Resources must **Check-In before assignment** to the Operations Section. The 4 NYC Check-In locations are: Staging Area, ICP, I/I Section Post, and Helibase.

---

## P.G. 220-25: CIMS Resources

**Date Effective:** 07/19/16 | **Pages:** 5

### Definitions

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **Resources** | Personnel, teams, equipment, supplies, vehicles, aircraft, watercraft, facilities |
| **Kind** | Broad class characterizing similar resources (e.g., marked patrol car, van, motorcycle) |
| **Type** | Level of capability: Roman numeral I (greatest) to IV (least) |

### Resource Categories

| Category | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| **Single Resource** | One vehicle/watercraft/aircraft + crew, single personnel, K-9 team, or pre-designated team (Squad, Mobile Field Force) |
| **Resource Team** | Same Kinds and Types of Single Resources assembled for a tactical assignment; must have designated Leader, transportation, common communications |
| **Special Response Team** | Any combination of different Kinds and Types assembled for a tactical assignment; must have Leader, transportation, common communications |

### NYPD Resource Examples

| Resource | Composition |
|----------|-------------|
| **Squad** | 1 Sergeant + 8 Police Officers |
| **Mobile Field Force** | 2 to 6 Squads |
| RMP | 1 vehicle + 2 Officers |
| Highway Motorcycle Squad | 1 Sergeant + 5 Officers on motorcycles |
| Mounted Squad | 1 Sergeant + 5 Mounted Officers on horses |
| ESU Entry Team | 1 Sergeant + 7–8 ESU Officers + vehicle(s) |
| ESU CAT Car | Nondescript Suburban + 1 Sergeant + 4 Officers |
| Bomb Squad Response Team | Van + 2 Officers + EDC + RMI |

### Resource Status Conditions

| Status | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| **Assigned** | Performing work tasks under supervisor direction |
| **Available** | Ready for assignment; deploy within **3 minutes** |
| **Out-of-Service** | Not able to be assigned or available (mechanical, rest, illness, injury, etc.) |

### Status Change Authority

Command Element, Operations Section chief(s), Branch Directors, Sector Supervisors, Group Supervisors, Resource Team/Special Response Team Leaders, I/I Section chief, Unit Leaders, Managers.

> **Exam Alert:** The 3 resource statuses are **Assigned, Available, Out-of-Service**. Available resources deploy within **3 minutes**.

> **Exam Alert:** A **Squad** = 1 Sergeant + 8 Officers. A **Mobile Field Force** = 2 to 6 Squads.

---

## P.G. 220-26: CIMS Resource Assignments

**Date Effective:** 08/01/13 | **Pages:** 2

### Key Points

- Resources assigned to tactical operations via the Operations Section
- Resource assignments documented in the Incident Action Plan
- Span of Control principles apply to all resource assignments

---

# Section 220 — General Staff Sections

## P.G. 220-09: CIMS Operations Section Chief

**Date Effective:** 08/01/25 | **Last Revision:** R.O. 66 | **Pages:** 14

### Purpose

To outline the duties of the Operations Section chief in CIMS.

### Key Responsibilities

| Duty |
|------|
| Direct and coordinate **all tactical operations** at the incident |
| Implement tactical portion of the Incident Action Plan |
| Establish Operations Section Post |
| Manage/supervise Sectors, Groups, Branches |
| Maintain accountability for all resources assigned to Operations Section |

### Subordinate Elements

| Element | Supervised By | Managed By |
|---------|-------------|------------|
| **Branch** | Branch Director | Major functional or geographic areas |
| **Sector** | Sector Supervisor | Geographic area of operations |
| **Group** | Group Supervisor | Functional area of operations |

> **Memory Aid — Sectors vs. Groups:** Think **S**ectors = **S**patially (geographic area). Think **G**roups = **G**iven function (functional area). *(PG 220-09)*

### Operations Section Post

- Separate from the ICP (generally NOT co-located)
- Located within the Site Access Control / Outer Perimeter
- Where Operations Section chief and staff direct tactical operations

### Unified Operations Section

Activated when:
- Unified Command — Command Element is managing the incident
- Single Command at a CBRN/HazMat incident

Each Primary Agency designates an **Agency Operations Section chief** based on Core Competencies.

> **Exam Alert:** The Operations Section directs **all tactical operations**. The Operations Section Post is generally **NOT co-located** with the ICP.

> **Prior Test:** The Sergeant exam tested: "At a CBRN incident with suspected terrorism, who is the primary agency for overall site management?" — correct answer is **NYPD** (NYPD has the Core Competency for Overall Site Management and Assessment/Investigation at CBRN incidents). *(PG 220-02, 220-03)*

> **See Also:** PG 220-03 — Core Competencies chart describing which agency handles which operational functions within the Unified Operations Section.

---

## P.G. 220-10: CIMS Planning Section Chief

**Date Effective:** 08/01/13 | **Pages:** 9

### Purpose

To outline the duties of the Planning Section chief in CIMS.

### Key Responsibilities

| Duty |
|------|
| Collect, evaluate, and disseminate incident information |
| Prepare/distribute the **Incident Action Plan** |
| Prepare the **Demobilization Plan** |
| Track resource status |
| Conduct planning meetings |
| Provide technical specialists |

### Subordinate Units

| Unit | Function |
|------|----------|
| **Resources Unit** | Track resource status; perform Check-In function |
| **Situation Unit** | Collect/analyze situation data; prepare situation displays |
| **Demobilization Unit** | Plan/execute orderly demobilization |
| **Documentation Unit** | Maintain incident files and records |

> **Memory Aid — Planning Units (RSDD):** **R**esources, **S**ituation, **D**emobilization, **D**ocumentation. The four units under the Planning Section. *(PG 220-10)*

> **Exam Alert:** The **Planning Section** prepares the Incident Action Plan and the Demobilization Plan. The **Resources Unit** tracks resource status and performs the Check-In function.

> **Exam Alert:** Planning Section has **no Branches and no Groups/Sectors** — only Units. If the Resources Unit is not activated, Check-In falls to the Planning Section Chief. If Planning is not activated, Check-In falls to the Command Element. *(PG 220-10, 220-24)*

> **See Also:** PG 220-24 — Check-In process, where Planning Section's Resources Unit is the primary performer.

---

## P.G. 220-11: CIMS Logistics Section Chief

**Date Effective:** 08/01/25 | **Last Revision:** R.O. 66 | **Pages:** 9

### Purpose

To outline the duties of the Logistics Section chief in CIMS.

### Key Responsibilities

| Duty |
|------|
| Provide **all service and support** for the incident |
| Manage communications infrastructure |
| Manage food, water, supplies, equipment |
| Manage facilities and transportation |
| Manage medical support for incident personnel (not victims) |

### Subordinate Structure

| Branch | Units |
|--------|-------|
| **Service Branch** | Communications Unit, Medical Unit, Food Unit |
| **Support Branch** | Supply Unit, Facilities Unit, Ground Support Unit |

> **Memory Aid — Service Branch (CMF):** **C**ommunications, **M**edical, **F**ood. *(PG 220-11)*

> **Memory Aid — Support Branch (SFG):** **S**upply, **F**acilities, **G**round Support. *(PG 220-11)*

> **Exam Alert:** Logistics provides **service and support** (NOT tactical operations). Medical Unit provides care for **incident personnel**, not victims.

> **Prior Test:** A recent exam asked: "Under what section would you find the Facilities Unit?" — The correct answer is **Logistics Section** (Support Branch), NOT the Command Element. Trick: "facilities" sounds like it could relate to ICP/infrastructure, but it is a Logistics subordinate unit. *(PG 220-11)*

> **Exam Alert:** Logistics can have **both Branches AND Units** — it is the only non-Operations section that can have Branches. The Service Branch and Support Branch both report to the Logistics Section Chief. *(PG 220-11)*

> **See Also:** PG 220-19 — Incident Facilities (ICP, Staging Area, etc.) — separate from the Logistics Facilities Unit.

---

## P.G. 220-12: CIMS Finance/Administration Section Chief

**Date Effective:** 08/01/13 | **Pages:** 5

### Purpose

To outline the duties of the Finance/Administration Section chief in CIMS.

### Key Responsibilities

| Duty |
|------|
| Track all incident costs |
| Manage financial/administrative functions |
| Process personnel time records |
| Manage procurement |
| Process claims |

### Subordinate Units

| Unit | Function |
|------|----------|
| **Time Unit** | Record personnel time |
| **Procurement Unit** | Manage contracts and purchasing |
| **Compensation/Claims Unit** | Process injury claims |
| **Cost Unit** | Track overall incident costs |

> **Memory Aid — Finance/Admin Units (TPCC):** **T**ime, **P**rocurement, **C**laims, **C**ost. The four units under Finance/Administration. *(PG 220-12)*

> **Exam Alert:** Finance/Admin has **no Branches and no Groups/Sectors** — only Units. If finance/admin tasks are minimal (only managing OT), this section may not be a full General Staff section and could reduce to one specialist under Planning. *(PG 220-12, step 1a)*

---

## P.G. 220-13: CIMS Intelligence/Investigations Section Chief

**Date Effective:** 02/18/15 | **Pages:** 7

### Purpose

To outline the duties of the Intelligence/Investigations Section chief in CIMS.

### Key Concept

When there is a **considerable investigative or intelligence component**, the Command Element should establish an Intelligence/Investigations Section as part of the General Staff. In unusual situations where it's not considerable, it may be organized as a Branch or Group within Operations, or even a Command Staff position.

### Subordinate Groups

| Group | Function |
|-------|----------|
| **Investigative Operations Group** | Direct investigative operations |
| **Intelligence Group** | Intelligence collection and analysis |
| **Forensics Group** | Forensic analysis and processing |
| **Electronic Communications, Surveillance and Evidence Group** | Surveillance operations, evidence collection |
| **Missing/Unidentified Person and Human Remains Group** | Track missing or unidentified persons |
| **Investigative Support Group** | Support investigative operations |

> **Memory Aid — Intel Section Groups:** The Intel/Investigations Section has **Groups but no Branches and no Units** — it is the only General Staff section that can have Groups but no Branches. *(PG 220-13)*

### Intelligence/Investigations Section Post

- Generally **NOT co-located** with the ICP or Operations Section Post
- Located at a **secure location** within reasonable distance

> **Exam Alert:** The Intelligence/Investigations Section may be organized as a **General Staff Section** (considerable component), an **Operations Branch/Group** (unusual), or a **Command Staff position/Planning function** (extremely unusual).

> **PG Conflict:** The PG specifically calls out the Intelligence/Investigations Section as "optional" among General Staff sections. However, The Key notes this is misleading — in practice, **ALL** General Staff positions are optional. The IC picks only what is needed for the incident. The "optional" label on Intel/Invest is not unique. *(PG 220-01, 220-13)*

> **See Also:** PG 220-09 — Operations Section, where Intel/Investigations may alternatively be placed as an Operations Branch or Group during unusual situations.

---

# Section 220 — Incident Operations and Procedures

## P.G. 220-14: CIMS Supporting Agencies and Agency Representatives

**Date Effective:** 08/01/13 | **Pages:** 2

### Purpose

To define Supporting Agencies and Agency Representatives.

### Definition

**Supporting Agency** — An agency that is NOT a Primary Agency but has relevant resources, information, expertise, or jurisdictional authority.

**Agency Representative** — A representative designated by a Supporting Agency to function as the agency's spokesperson at the incident.

### Key Rules

- Agency Representative reports to the **Liaison Officer** (or Command Element if no Liaison Officer)
- Must have **authority** to make decisions on behalf of their agency
- All Supporting Agencies co-locate at the ICP when directed

---

## P.G. 220-15: CIMS Coordinating Agency

**Date Effective:** 08/01/25 | **Last Revision:** R.O. 66 | **Pages:** 1

### Definition

**NYC Emergency Management (NYCEM)** is New York City's designated **Coordinating Agency**.

### Key Roles

- Facilitate, coordinate, and support inter-agency activities
- May respond to multi-agency incidents
- Participate in Unified or Single Command organization
- Facilitate and support **Recovery/Restoration Operations**
- Ensure ICP and Operations Section Post are established and staffed

> **Exam Alert:** **NYCEM** is the Coordinating Agency — they facilitate and support, but do NOT command the incident.

---

## P.G. 220-16: CIMS Integrated Communications

**Date Effective:** 08/01/25 | **Last Revision:** R.O. 66 | **Pages:** 2

### Key Points

- Use **clear text** for inter-agency communications (no codes)
- Use **CIMS nomenclature/terminology**
- Observe strict radio and phone communications discipline
- Develop and implement a **Communications Plan** (component of the IAP)
- When appropriate, use Borough/Citywide interoperable radio frequencies
- NYC Agencies' Citywide interoperable **800 MHz** radio frequency

> **Exam Alert:** All incident personnel must use **clear text** (no 10-codes) for inter-agency communications and use **CIMS terminology**.

---

## P.G. 220-17: CIMS Transfer of Command

**Date Effective:** 02/04/15 | **Pages:** 2

### Purpose

To describe the formal Transfer of Command process.

### Options Upon Arrival of Ranking Member

| Option | Action |
|--------|--------|
| 1 | Assume command (if no Command Element exists) |
| 2 | Assume command from current Command Element |
| 3 | Make **no change** to current Command Element |
| 4 | Designate a **third party** to assume command |

### When Transfer of Command Occurs

- Higher-ranking person arrives and elects to assume command
- Incident stabilized — transfer to less senior person
- New Operational Period — designated personnel assume command
- Command Element dismissed at end of tour

### Transfer of Command Checklist

| Item |
|------|
| CIMS organization implemented? CIMS terminology in use? |
| One single ICP established? |
| Face-to-face transfer if possible? |
| Incoming Command Element personally assesses situation with outgoing? |
| Comprehensive briefing provided? |
| Appropriate time chosen for formal transfer? |
| Incoming Command Element formally accepted command? |
| Notifications made? |

### Transfer of Command Briefing Should Include

Concise history, current situation, objectives/strategy/priorities, IAP, organizational structure, resource assignments, status of tactical/investigative operations, intelligence/threat information, incident facilities status, communications plan, constraints/limitations, current concerns, future predictions, delegation of authority issues.

> **Exam Alert:** A ranking member arriving at an incident has **four options**: assume command, assume from current CE, make no change, or designate a third party. Transfer should be **face-to-face** when possible.

> **Sergeant Focus:** An arriving sergeant may elect to **make no change** to the command element if the current officer is managing effectively — assuming command is not mandatory.

---

## P.G. 220-18: CIMS Incident Sequence

**Date Effective:** 08/01/25 | **Last Revision:** R.O. 66 | **Pages:** 3

### Purpose

To describe the general CIMS Incident Sequence.

### Incident Sequence (In Order)

| Phase | Activities |
|-------|-----------|
| **1. Initial Response** | Agencies respond; first arrivals assess scene |
| **2. Life Safety Operations** | Immediate #1 priority; rescue, EMS |
| **3. Site Management Operations** | NYPD establishes perimeters; excludes non-essential personnel |
| **4. Investigative Operations** | May run concurrently with Life Safety (with CE approval); life safety concerns take precedence |
| **5. Recovery/Restoration Operations** | Final mitigation; return to non-emergency; may be delayed for investigation |
| **6. Demobilization** | Operations Section Post and ICP demobilize; incident concludes |

### Key Sequencing Rules

- **Life Safety** is always the immediate #1 priority
- Investigative Operations may run **concurrently** with Life Safety (life safety concerns take precedence)
- Recovery/Restoration may be **delayed** to minimize disturbance to crime scenes
- Recovery/Restoration **may begin concurrently** with Life Safety and/or Investigation (with CE approval)

> **Exam Alert:** The incident sequence is: Response → Life Safety → Site Management → Investigation → Recovery/Restoration → Demobilization. Phases may **overlap** with CE approval.

---

## P.G. 220-22: CIMS Demobilization

**Date Effective:** 08/01/25 | **Last Revision:** R.O. 66 | **Pages:** 2

### Key Points

- Orderly, safe, efficient release of resources
- Demobilization Plan prepared by **Planning Section** (Demobilization Unit)
- Command Element orders demobilization
- Resources released based on established priorities

---

## P.G. 220-27: CIMS 201 Incident Briefing Form (ICS Form 201)

**Date Effective:** 08/01/13 | **Pages:** 6

### Purpose

To standardize incident briefing documentation using ICS Form 201.

### Form Components

| Section | Content |
|---------|---------|
| Incident Name/Number | Identification |
| Map/Sketch | Incident area |
| Current Organization | Organizational chart |
| Resources Summary | Personnel and equipment assigned |
| Actions Taken | Current and planned |

---

## P.G. 220-28: CIMS Incident Management Team (IMT) Activation/Deployment

**Date Effective:** 08/01/13 | **Pages:** 3

### Purpose

To activate and deploy an Incident Management Team at an emergency or planned event.

### Activation Authority

| IMT Type | Activated By |
|----------|-------------|
| Citywide IMT | Police Commissioner, First Deputy Commissioner, or Chief of Department |
| Borough IMT | Chief of Patrol, or Patrol Borough Commander (or Duty Chief) |
| Transit Bureau IMT | Chief of Transit (or Duty Chief) |
| Housing Bureau IMT | Chief of Housing (or Duty Chief) |

### Situations Requiring IMT Activation

| Situation |
|-----------|
| "Stand Alone" protocols activated |
| Incident extends into **multiple Operational Periods (>24 hours)** — formal written IAP required |
| Unified Command with multi-agency resources expected to exceed 24 hours |
| Declaration of Disaster |
| National Special Security Event (e.g., UNGA, RNC) |
| Incident of National Significance outside NYC with NYC impact |
| Substantial city/state/federal resources required |
| Natural disaster affecting NYC |

### Key Definitions

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **Incident Management Team (IMT)** | Command Element + appropriate Command Staff + General Staff assigned to an incident |
| **Operational Period** | Period for execution of a given set of operations per the IAP; usually not over **24 hours** |
| **Incident Action Plan (IAP)** | Contains Incident Objectives, strategy, tactical actions for next Operational Period; may be oral or written |

> **Exam Alert:** When incident extends beyond **24 hours**, a formal **written IAP** is required. Operational Periods are usually not over 24 hours.

> **Exam Alert:** **Citywide IMT** activated by Police Commissioner, First Deputy, or Chief of Department. **Borough IMT** activated by Chief of Patrol or Patrol Borough Commander.

---

# Section 220 — CIMS Organizational Structure, Primary Agency Matrix, and Agency Roles

## P.G. 220-01: CIMS Organizational Structure

**Date Effective:** 08/01/13 | **Pages:** 2

### Purpose

To outline the CIMS Organizational Structure.

### Key Concepts

- CIMS structure begins with the **Command Element**
- The structure is **modular** — expanded as needed regardless of incident nature, size, scope, duration, location, or complexity
- Organization may range from Command Element + several Single Resources (small) to Command Element + 3 Command Staff + 5 General Staff Sections (large)

> **Memory Aid — MODULAR:** The keyword for how CIMS is designed — the Incident Commander can run an operation with one or two people *or* open any combination of staff positions as needed. *(PG 220-01)*

> **Exam Alert:** The PG specifically describes the Intelligence/Investigations Section as "optional," but The Key notes that in reality ALL positions above are optional — the IC picks only what is needed. *(PG 220-01)*

### CIMS Organizational Elements

| Level | Elements |
|-------|----------|
| **Command Element** | Single Command or Unified Command |
| **Command Staff** | Public Information Officer, Safety Officer, Liaison Officer |
| **General Staff — Sections** | Operations, Planning, Logistics, Finance/Administration, Intelligence/Investigations (optional) |

> **Exam Alert:** The PG uses the term "Command Element" (not "Incident Commander") because NYPD may **share command with leaders from other agencies** — thus there could be more than one Incident Commander. *(PG 220-01)*

> **Memory Aid — General Staff Chiefs rank:** Although the PG uses the word "Chief" for General Staff Section Chiefs, the assigned MOS could be **any rank** — including a civilian MOS. The title describes the role, not the rank. *(PG 220-01)*

### Operations Section Subordinate Elements

| Element | Activated By |
|---------|-------------|
| Sectors and/or Groups | Operations Section chief |
| Branches | Operations Section chief (if necessary) |

### Other Section Subordinate Elements

| Section | Can Activate |
|---------|-------------|
| Intelligence/Investigations | Groups |
| Logistics | Units, Branches |
| Planning | Units |
| Finance/Administration | Units |

> **Prior Test:** A recent Sergeant exam asked: "Under what section would you find the Facilities Unit?" — The correct answer is **Logistics Section** (Support Branch). The Facilities Unit is one of three Support Branch units under Logistics. *(PG 220-01, 220-11)*

### Role Comparison — Subordinate Elements by Section Chief

| Section Chief | Can Have Branches | Can Have Groups/Sectors | Can Have Units |
|---------------|:-----------------:|:-----------------------:|:--------------:|
| **Operations** | YES | YES | no |
| **Planning** | no | no | YES |
| **Logistics** | YES | no | YES |
| **Finance/Admin** | no | no | YES |
| **Intel/Invest** | no | YES | no |

> **Exam Alert:** Operations and Logistics can have Branches. Operations and Intel/Investigative can have Groups/Sectors. Planning, Logistics, and Finance/Admin can have Units — but of those three, **only Logistics** can also have a Branch. *(PG 220-01)*

> **PG Conflict:** The Air Operations Branch of the Operations Section is **NOT listed** in PG 220-09 (Operations Section Chief duties) but **IS listed** in PG 220-11 (Logistics Section Chief). This means the Air Operations Branch organizationally appears in Logistics' scope — watch for this distinction on the exam. *(PG 220-09, 220-11)*

> **Exam Alert:** None of the branches, sub-groups, sectors, or units are mandatory — only open what is needed. However, responsibilities of **unopened** units/groups revert to the responsible Section Chief and **cannot be moved** to another opened branch, group, or unit. *(PG 220-01)*

### Organizational Principles

1. Observe recommended **Span of Control** guidelines
2. Activate and staff **only** organizational elements necessary to manage required functions
3. If one individual can manage all functions, **no further organization** is required
4. **Deactivate** elements no longer required — their functions revert to the overhead supervising element

> **Exam Alert:** The CIMS structure is **modular** — only activate what is needed. Non-activated functions are managed by the overhead element.

---

## P.G. 220-02: CIMS Primary Agency Matrix

**Date Effective:** 08/01/25 | **Last Revision:** R.O. 66 | **Pages:** 3

### Purpose

To list incident types and designate which Command Element structure (Single or Unified) and which Agency/Agencies have command responsibility.

### Unified Command Matrix (Multiple Primary Agencies)

| Incident Type | Primary Agencies | Potential Primary Agencies |
|--------------|-----------------|--------------------------|
| Aviation Incident | FDNY, NYPD | PANYNJ, USCG, NTSB |
| CBRN / HazMat Incident | NYPD, FDNY | USCG, DEP, DOHMH |
| Citywide Public Health Emergency | DOHMH, NYPD, FDNY | HHC, GNYHA |
| Explosion | FDNY, NYPD | DDC, DOB, HPD |
| Natural Disaster / Weather Emergency | NYCEM, NYPD, FDNY, DOT, DSNY | DDC, DEP, DOB, DOHMH, Con Ed |
| Rail Incident | FDNY, NYPD | MTA, PANYNJ, NJT, Amtrak |
| Utility Incident (Electric/Gas/Steam) | NYPD, FDNY | Con Ed, National Grid/LIPA |
| Utility Incident (Water/Wastewater) | DEP, FDNY, NYPD | Con Ed, National Grid/LIPA |
| Utility Incident (Telecom) | DOITT, NYPD, FDNY | Verizon |

### Single Command Matrix (One Primary Agency)

| Incident Type | Primary Agency |
|--------------|---------------|
| Auto Extrication | NYPD/FDNY (first to arrive) |
| Boat in Distress | NYPD/FDNY (first to arrive) |
| Citywide Cyber Incident | NYPD and/or DOITT |
| **Civil Disturbance** | **NYPD** |
| Confined Space Rescue | FDNY |
| Correctional Facility Disturbance | DOC |
| **Emotionally Disturbed Person** | **NYPD** |
| **Explosive Device, Bomb Threat** | **NYPD** |
| Fire | FDNY |
| **Hostage Incident** | **NYPD** |
| **Sniper Incident** | **NYPD** |
| **Special Event** | **NYPD** |
| Structural Collapse | FDNY |
| **Suspicious Package** | **NYPD** |
| **Water/Ice Rescue** | **NYPD** |

### CBRN/HazMat Special Rules

| Situation | Command Structure |
|-----------|------------------|
| Actual or suspected crime/terrorism | NYPD **Single Command** |
| No actual or suspected crime/terrorism | **Unified Command** |
| Anticipated/suspected/actual nuclear weapon detonation | **Unified Command** |

> **Exam Alert:** NYPD is the **sole Incident Commander** at CBRN/HazMat incidents with actual or suspected criminal activity/terrorism (Single Command). If NYPD determines **no** crime/terrorism, it switches to Unified Command.

> **Exam Alert:** For auto extrication and boat in distress, the primary agency is determined by **whoever arrives first** (NYPD or FDNY).

### Three Types of CIMS — Role Comparison

| Feature | Type I: Single (220-04) | Type II: Unified (220-03) | Type III: Single-CBRN (220-02) |
|---------|------------------------|--------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Who is IC? | One IC from Core Agency | Co-ICs from each agency | NYPD IC (initially) |
| Who picks staff? | IC picks all positions | Co-ICs jointly pick most; each picks own Safety Officer & Ops Chief | Same as Unified for Ops Section |
| PIO | IC picks (Mayor's Press Office may override) | Co-ICs jointly pick one | Same as Unified |
| Liaison | IC picks (NYCEM may override) | Co-ICs jointly pick one | Same as Unified |
| Safety Officer | IC picks one | Each agency picks own (or jointly one) | Each agency (NYPD, FDNY, DEP, DOHMH) picks own |
| Ops Section | IC picks | Each agency picks own Ops Chief | Ops Section is Unified — each agency has Ops Chief |
| Planning, Logistics, Finance, Intel | IC picks | Co-ICs jointly pick ONE each | Same as Unified |

> **Prior Test:** This table was tested on ALL THREE exams — Sergeant, Lieutenant, and Captain. The Primary Agency Matrix for single command incidents is high-frequency exam content. *(PG 220-02)*

> **Exam Alert:** In **CBRN/Single Command**, even though it starts as "Single" (NYPD only), the **Operations Section is Unified** — each agency (NYPD, FDNY, DEP, DOHMH) has its own Ops Chief and Safety Officer. This makes it function similarly to Unified despite the Single Command designation. *(PG 220-02)*

> **Exam Alert:** The "N" in CBRN stands for Nuclear. However, if there is an **anticipated, suspected, or actual detonation of a nuclear weapon**, the command **always goes Unified** — it never stays Single-CBRN. This is the one exception where CBRN does not default to NYPD Single Command. *(PG 220-02)*

> **Exam Alert:** In Unified Command, all Agency Incident Commanders are on **equal footing regardless of their rank** in their own organization. An FDNY Captain has the same authority as an NYPD Deputy Inspector in a Unified Command Element. *(PG 220-04)*

### Unified Command — What ICs Jointly Pick vs. Each Agency Picks

> **See Also:** PG 220-05 — Detailed rules on what Unified Command ICs pick jointly vs. individually.

| Jointly Picked (one each) | Each IC Picks Own |
|--------------------------|-------------------|
| Public Information Officer | Safety Officer (each agency) |
| Liaison Officer | Operations Section Chief (each agency) |
| Planning Section Chief | — |
| Logistics Section Chief | — |
| Finance/Admin Section Chief | — |
| Intelligence Section Chief | — |

> **Exam Alert:** In Unified Command, NYPD IC should always try to get NYPD personnel into the jointly-picked spots — or at minimum, get an NYPD member as the deputy in those positions. *(PG 220-05)*

### Unified Operations Section Responsibilities in Single-CBRN/HazMat

| Agency | Responsibility |
|--------|---------------|
| **NYPD** | Overall site management; Assessment and investigation (Crime/Terrorism) |
| **FDNY** | Life Safety Operations; Search & Rescue; EMS/97H; Mass Decontamination |
| **DEP** | Chemical — Set Hot/Warm/Cold zones; Direct mitigation effort |
| **DOHMH** | Biological/Radiological — Set zones (same as DEP); mitigation |

> **See Also:** PG 220-02, page 2 — Unified Operations Section Responsibilities in Single-CBRN/HazMat incidents.

---

## P.G. 220-03: CIMS Agency Core Competencies and Unified Operations Section

**Date Effective:** 08/01/25 | **Last Revision:** R.O. 66 | **Pages:** 3

### Purpose

To define Agency Core Competencies at incidents.

### Key Concept

**Core Competencies** are functional areas of expertise assigned to **one single agency**. Even if multiple agencies can perform similar operations, CIMS assigns the Core Competency to only one agency.

> **Exam Alert:** The 220-03 two-page chart shows which agencies have what capabilities — it **only describes capability, not management authority**. It does NOT assign who commands what. *(PG 220-03)*

> **Exam Alert:** Tactical Operations are managed by the **Operations Section**. If the Operations Section is not activated, the **Command Element** manages Tactical Operations directly. *(PG 220-03)*

> **Exam Alert:** The 220-02 chart (Primary Agency Matrix) shows which agency will be in command. The 220-03 chart shows who is capable of what. These are **two separate charts with different purposes** — exam questions may try to confuse them. *(PG 220-02, 220-03)*

> **Exam Alert:** "Potential Primary Agencies" (State/Federal agencies) may have to share command as Primary Agencies at incidents on military bases, terrorism incidents, etc. Nothing in the procedure restricts naming them as Primary Agencies. *(PG 220-03)*

### NYPD Core Competencies

| Core Competency |
|----------------|
| Law Enforcement and Investigation |
| Intelligence Collection and Analysis |
| Crime Scene Processing / Evidence Preservation |
| **Site Management** (Perimeter Control, Traffic Control, Crowd Control) |
| Site Security and Force Protection |
| Evacuation (Area and/or Law Enforcement related) |
| Water Search and Rescue |
| CBRN/HazMat Overall Site Management, Assessment and Investigation |
| Collision Investigation |
| VIP Protection |
| Arson Investigation (Major Case) |

### FDNY Core Competencies

| Core Competency |
|----------------|
| Fire Suppression |
| Pre-hospital Emergency Medical Care |
| Search and Rescue |
| Structural Evacuation |
| CBRN/HazMat Life Safety and Mass Decontamination |
| Arson Investigation (Cause and Origin) |

### Other Agency Core Competencies

| Agency | Core Competency |
|--------|----------------|
| DOB | Damage Assessment (Structural), Building Re-occupancy |
| DDC | Technical Stabilization, Remediation and Demolition |
| DEP | Environmental Monitoring/Mitigation (Chemical) |
| DOHMH | Disease Surveillance, Mass Prophylaxis, Environmental Mitigation (Radiological/Biological) |
| OCME | Fatality Management |
| NYCEM | Interagency Coordination and Support |
| DSNY | Debris Management (Non-Toxic), Snow Removal |
| DOT | Bridges/Roadways, Transportation Systems Management |

> **Exam Alert:** The agency with the Core Competency gives **tactical direction** to other agencies performing operations within that competency — even if those agencies are capable of doing the work.

---

## P.G. 220-04: CIMS Agency Roles

**Date Effective:** 08/01/25 | **Last Revision:** R.O. 66 | **Pages:** 2

### Definitions

| Role | Description |
|------|-------------|
| **Primary Agency** | One agency designated to command a Single Command; designates one Incident Commander |
| **Primary Agencies** | Multiple agencies commanding a Unified Command; each designates an Agency Incident Commander |
| **Potential Primary Agencies** | State/federal agencies with jurisdictional authority under specific conditions (airports, military facilities, terrorism) |
| **Supporting Agencies** | Agencies assisting but not commanding the incident |
| **Subject Matter Expert Agencies** | Agencies with specific technical expertise but no personnel in tactical assignments |

### Unified Command Key Rules

- All Agency Incident Commanders **co-locate** at one single ICP
- Jointly set Incident Objectives and create **one single** Incident Action Plan
- All Agency Incident Commanders are at an **equal level of authority** regardless of rank

> **Exam Alert:** In Unified Command, all Agency Incident Commanders are at **equal authority** regardless of their agency rank (Fire Captain = Police Sergeant = EMS Lieutenant in the Command Element).

---

# Section 220 — Key Terms and Definitions

## CIMS Organizational Terms

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **CIMS** | Citywide Incident Management System — NYC's adaptation of NIMS/ICS |
| **Command Element** | Individual(s) responsible for overall command function; may be Single or Unified. PG uses "Command Element" not "Incident Commander" because command may be shared with other agencies |
| **Single Command** | One Primary Agency with one Incident Commander |
| **Unified Command** | Multiple Primary Agencies, each with an Agency Incident Commander; all co-locate at one ICP |
| **Single-CBRN** | Hybrid command type for Chemical, Biological, Radiological, Nuclear or HazMat incidents — NYPD is sole IC until no criminal/terror activity is determined, then reverts to Unified; Ops Section is Unified throughout |
| **Primary Agency** | Agency designated by the Primary Agency Matrix to command the incident |
| **Supporting Agency** | Agency assisting but not commanding |
| **Coordinating Agency** | NYCEM — facilitates inter-agency coordination and support |
| **Potential Primary Agency** | State/federal agencies with jurisdictional authority under specific conditions |
| **Subject Matter Expert Agency** | Agency with technical expertise but no tactical personnel deployed |
| **Core Competency** | Functional area of expertise assigned to one single agency — describes capability only, not management authority |
| **OEM** | Former name for NYCEM (NYC Emergency Management); older exam questions may still use "OEM" |
| **DCPI** | Deputy Commissioner of Public Information — preferred source for the PIO when NYPD is in command |
| **MODULAR** | Key word describing CIMS structure — IC can run an operation alone or open any combination of staff positions as needed |

## Command Staff Positions

| Position | Designation Authority (Override) | Note |
|----------|-------------------------------|------|
| **Public Information Officer** | Mayor's Press Office may designate | Preferably from DCPI when NYPD is in command |
| **Safety Officer** | No external override — IC designates | In Unified Command, each IC picks own; if all agree, may share one |
| **Liaison Officer** | NYCEM may designate | NYCEM formerly known as OEM |

## General Staff Sections

| Section | Chief Manages | Subordinate Elements | Memory Aid |
|---------|-------------|---------------------|------------|
| **Operations** | All tactical operations | Branches, Sectors (geographic), Groups (functional) | Sectors = Spatial; Groups = Given function |
| **Planning** | Information collection, IAP preparation, resource tracking | Resources Unit, Situation Unit, Demobilization Unit, Documentation Unit | RSDD |
| **Logistics** | All service and support | Service Branch (Communications, Medical, Food); Support Branch (Supply, Facilities, Ground Support) | Service = CMF; Support = SFG |
| **Finance/Administration** | Financial/administrative functions | Time Unit, Procurement Unit, Claims Unit, Cost Unit | TPCC |
| **Intelligence/Investigations** | Investigative/intelligence operations | Investigative Operations Group, Intelligence Group, Forensics Group, Electronic Communications/Surveillance/Evidence Group, Missing/Unidentified Person and Human Remains Group, Investigative Support Group | Groups only — no Branches, no Units |

## Unified Command — Joint vs. Individual Picks

| Jointly Picked (one for entire command) | Each IC Picks Own |
|----------------------------------------|-------------------|
| Public Information Officer | Safety Officer (each agency picks own) |
| Liaison Officer | Operations Section Chief (each agency picks own) |
| Planning Section Chief | — |
| Logistics Section Chief | — |
| Finance/Admin Section Chief | — |
| Intelligence Section Chief | — |

## Three Types of CIMS

| Type | PG Section | Key Feature |
|------|-----------|-------------|
| **Single Command** | 220-04 | One IC from core agency; IC picks all staff |
| **Unified Command** | 220-03 | Multiple Co-ICs; jointly pick most staff; each picks own Safety Officer and Ops Chief |
| **Single-CBRN** | 220-02 | NYPD IC until no crime/terror determined; Ops Section is always Unified; nuclear weapon = Unified always |

## Single Command Primary Agency Matrix (High-Frequency Exam Items)

| Incident Type | Primary Agency |
|--------------|---------------|
| Civil Disturbance | NYPD |
| EDP | NYPD |
| Explosive Device / Bomb Threat | NYPD |
| Fire | FDNY |
| Hostage Incident | NYPD |
| Sniper Incident | NYPD |
| Special Event | NYPD |
| Structural Collapse | FDNY |
| Suspicious Package | NYPD |
| Water/Ice Rescue | NYPD |
| Auto Extrication | NYPD/FDNY (first to arrive) |
| Boat in Distress | NYPD/FDNY (first to arrive) |
| Confined Space Rescue | FDNY |
| Downed Tree | Dept of Parks & Recreation / FDNY |
| Elevator Incident | FDNY |
| Correctional Facility Disturbance | DOC |

## Incident Facilities

| Facility | Key Rule |
|----------|----------|
| **Incident Command Post (ICP)** | ONE per incident (except nuclear); identified by incident name |
| **Operations Section Post** | NOT co-located with ICP |
| **I/I Section Post** | NOT co-located with ICP or Operations Post |
| **Staging Area** | Resources in "Available" status; deploy within 3 minutes; named by geography/number |
| **Incident Base** | NOT activated in NYC (absent extraordinary circumstances) |
| **Camp** | NOT activated in NYC (absent extraordinary circumstances) |

## Resource Terms

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **Single Resource** | One vehicle + crew, single personnel, K-9 team, or pre-designated team |
| **Resource Team** | Same Kinds/Types assembled for a tactical assignment; requires Leader + communications |
| **Special Response Team** | Different Kinds/Types assembled for a tactical assignment; requires Leader + communications |
| **Kind** | Broad class of similar resources |
| **Type** | Level of capability: I (greatest) to IV (least) |
| **Squad** | 1 Sergeant + 8 Police Officers |
| **Mobile Field Force** | 2 to 6 Squads |

## Resource Status Conditions

| Status | Description | Readiness |
|--------|-------------|-----------|
| **Assigned** | Performing work tasks under supervisor | Working |
| **Available** | Ready for assignment | Deploy within **3 minutes** |
| **Out-of-Service** | Not able to be assigned | Mechanical, rest, illness, injury |

## Incident Management Priorities

| Priority | Operations |
|----------|-----------|
| **#1** | Life Safety Operations (immediate, supersedes all) |
| #2 | Site Management Operations |
| #3 | Investigative Operations |
| #4 | Recovery/Restoration Operations |

## Span of Control

| Context | Ratio |
|---------|-------|
| General | **1:3 to 1:7** |
| Law Enforcement | **1:8 to 1:10** |

## Key Principles

| Principle | Definition |
|-----------|-----------|
| **Span of Control** | Number of personnel one supervisor can effectively manage |
| **Unity of Command** | Every subordinate has only ONE designated supervisor |
| **Chain of Command** | Direction given through established authority levels |
| **Management by Objectives** | Incident Objectives must be Attainable, Measurable, Adaptable/Flexible |

## Critical Timeframes and Thresholds

| Item | Threshold |
|------|-----------|
| Operational Period | Usually not over **24 hours** |
| Written IAP required | When incident extends beyond **24 hours** (multiple Operational Periods) |
| Available resource readiness | **3 minutes** to deploy |
| Check-In | Required **before** assignment to Operations |

## CBRN/HazMat Command Rules

| Situation | Command Structure |
|-----------|------------------|
| Actual/suspected crime or terrorism | NYPD **Single Command** (Incident Commander) |
| No crime/terrorism | **Unified Command** (NYPD, FDNY, DEP, DOHMH) |
| Nuclear weapon (anticipated/suspected/actual) | **Unified Command** with borough-centric ICPs |

## IMT Activation Authority

| IMT Type | Activated By |
|----------|-------------|
| Citywide | Police Commissioner, First Deputy Commissioner, or Chief of Department |
| Borough | Chief of Patrol or Patrol Borough Commander (or Duty Chief) |
| Transit | Chief of Transit (or Duty Chief) |
| Housing | Chief of Housing (or Duty Chief) |

## Key Forms

| Form | Number | Used For |
|------|--------|----------|
| DETAIL ROSTER / ASSIGNMENT SHEETS | PD406-141 | Check-In recording |
| ICS Form 201 (Incident Briefing) | — | Standardized incident briefing |

## Transfer of Command Options

| Option | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| 1 | Assume command (no current CE) |
| 2 | Assume from current CE |
| 3 | Make no change |
| 4 | Designate third party |

## NYPD Single Command Incidents

Civil Disturbance, EDP, Explosive Device/Bomb Threat, Hostage, Sniper, Special Event, Suspicious Package, Water/Ice Rescue

*Note: Downed Tree, Elevator Incident, Confined Space Rescue, and Structural Collapse are NOT NYPD — commonly tested trap.*

## NYPD Core Competencies

Law Enforcement & Investigation, Intelligence Collection & Analysis, Crime Scene Processing/Evidence, Site Management (Perimeter, Traffic, Crowd Control), Site Security & Force Protection, Evacuation, Water Search & Rescue, CBRN/HazMat Site Management/Assessment/Investigation, Collision Investigation, VIP Protection, Arson Investigation (Major Case)

---

# Section 220 — Review Questions

## Multiple Choice

**1. What is the #1 priority in CIMS incident management?**

- A) Site Management Operations
- B) Investigative Operations
- C) Life Safety Operations
- D) Recovery/Restoration Operations

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) Life Safety Operations**

Life Safety is always the immediate #1 priority and supersedes all other operations. (P.G. 220-05)

</details>

---

**2. What is the span of control ratio for law enforcement operations under CIMS?**

- A) 1:3 to 1:5
- B) 1:3 to 1:7
- C) 1:5 to 1:10
- D) 1:8 to 1:10

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**D) 1:8 to 1:10**

The general span of control is 1:3 to 1:7, but law enforcement operations use a ratio of 1:8 to 1:10. (P.G. 220-05)

</details>

---

**3. How many Incident Command Posts (ICPs) may be established per incident?**

- A) One per borough involved
- B) One per agency
- C) Only ONE (except nuclear incidents)
- D) As many as needed based on incident size

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) Only ONE (except nuclear incidents)**

There is only ONE ICP per incident. The exception is a nuclear weapon incident (anticipated, suspected, or actual), which uses borough-centric ICPs under Unified Command. (P.G. 220-19)

</details>

---

**4. What is the composition of a Squad under CIMS?**

- A) 1 Sergeant + 5 Police Officers
- B) 1 Sergeant + 8 Police Officers
- C) 1 Lieutenant + 8 Police Officers
- D) 2 Sergeants + 10 Police Officers

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) 1 Sergeant + 8 Police Officers**

A Squad consists of 1 Sergeant and 8 Police Officers. (P.G. 220-25)

</details>

---

**5. Who has authority to designate the Public Information Officer at an incident?**

- A) The Chief of Department
- B) NYCEM
- C) The Mayor's Press Office
- D) The Operations Section chief

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) The Mayor's Press Office**

While the Incident Commander or Agency Incident Commanders may designate the PIO, the Mayor's Press Office has overriding authority and may designate the PIO. (P.G. 220-06)

</details>

---

**6. Resources in "Available" status at a Staging Area must be ready for deployment within how many minutes?**

- A) 1 minute
- B) 3 minutes
- C) 5 minutes
- D) 10 minutes

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) 3 minutes**

Resources at a Staging Area are in "Available" status and must be ready for deployment within 3 minutes. (P.G. 220-20)

</details>

---

**7. Which agency is designated as the Coordinating Agency under CIMS?**

- A) NYPD
- B) FDNY
- C) NYC Emergency Management (NYCEM)
- D) Mayor's Office of Operations

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) NYC Emergency Management (NYCEM)**

NYCEM is the designated Coordinating Agency. It facilitates, coordinates, and supports inter-agency activities but does NOT command the incident. (P.G. 220-15)

</details>

---

**8. When a CBRN/HazMat incident involves actual or suspected crime or terrorism, what command structure is used?**

- A) Unified Command with NYPD, FDNY, DEP, and DOHMH
- B) NYPD Single Command with an Incident Commander
- C) FDNY Single Command
- D) Unified Command with borough-centric ICPs

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) NYPD Single Command with an Incident Commander**

When a CBRN/HazMat incident involves actual or suspected crime or terrorism, NYPD exercises Single Command. (P.G. 220-02)

</details>

---

**9. A ranking member arriving at an incident has how many options regarding the Command Element?**

- A) Two — assume command or make no change
- B) Three — assume command, make no change, or leave the scene
- C) Four — assume command (no CE), assume from current CE, make no change, designate a third party
- D) Five — the four options above plus request a higher-ranking officer

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) Four — assume command (no CE), assume from current CE, make no change, designate a third party**

The four Transfer of Command options are: (1) Assume command when no CE exists, (2) Assume command from current CE, (3) Make no change, (4) Designate a third party. (P.G. 220-17)

</details>

---

**10. The Safety Officer may exercise emergency authority to directly stop unsafe activities under what condition?**

- A) When any safety concern is identified
- B) When authorized by the Command Element
- C) When life or safety of incident personnel is in imminent danger
- D) Only at HazMat incidents

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) When life or safety of incident personnel is in imminent danger**

The Safety Officer may exercise emergency authority to directly prevent, stop, and/or correct hazardous activities when life or safety is in imminent danger — without waiting for chain of command approval. (P.G. 220-07)

</details>

---

**11. Which General Staff section prepares the Incident Action Plan?**

- A) Operations Section
- B) Logistics Section
- C) Planning Section
- D) Finance/Administration Section

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) Planning Section**

The Planning Section chief is responsible for preparing and distributing the Incident Action Plan (IAP). (P.G. 220-10)

</details>

---

**12. A formal written Incident Action Plan is required when an incident extends beyond what timeframe?**

- A) 12 hours
- B) 18 hours
- C) 24 hours
- D) 48 hours

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) 24 hours**

When an incident extends beyond 24 hours (multiple Operational Periods), a formal written IAP is required. (P.G. 220-28)

</details>

---

**13. Who may activate a Citywide Incident Management Team (IMT)?**

- A) Any Deputy Chief or above
- B) Chief of Patrol or Patrol Borough Commander
- C) Police Commissioner, First Deputy Commissioner, or Chief of Department
- D) Chief of Department or any Bureau Chief

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) Police Commissioner, First Deputy Commissioner, or Chief of Department**

The Citywide IMT is activated by the Police Commissioner, First Deputy Commissioner, or Chief of Department. The Borough IMT is activated by the Chief of Patrol or Patrol Borough Commander. (P.G. 220-28)

</details>

---

**14. What is the correct CIMS incident sequence?**

- A) Life Safety → Investigation → Site Management → Recovery → Demobilization
- B) Response → Life Safety → Site Management → Investigation → Recovery/Restoration → Demobilization
- C) Response → Site Management → Life Safety → Investigation → Demobilization
- D) Life Safety → Site Management → Recovery → Investigation → Demobilization

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) Response → Life Safety → Site Management → Investigation → Recovery/Restoration → Demobilization**

The correct CIMS incident sequence is: Initial Response, Life Safety Operations, Site Management Operations, Investigative Operations, Recovery/Restoration Operations, Demobilization. Phases may overlap with CE approval. (P.G. 220-18)

</details>

---

**15. Which of the following is NOT a CIMS resource status condition?**

- A) Assigned
- B) Available
- C) Standby
- D) Out-of-Service

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) Standby**

The three CIMS resource statuses are Assigned (performing work tasks), Available (ready for assignment, deploy within 3 minutes), and Out-of-Service (not able to be assigned). "Standby" is not a CIMS status. (P.G. 220-25)

</details>

---

**16. The Operations Section Post is generally:**

- A) Co-located with the ICP for efficiency
- B) NOT co-located with the ICP
- C) Located at the Staging Area
- D) Located outside the Outer Perimeter

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) NOT co-located with the ICP**

The Operations Section Post is generally NOT co-located with the ICP. The I/I Section Post is also NOT co-located with either the ICP or Operations Post. (P.G. 220-09, 220-19)

</details>

---

**17. What form is used for Check-In recording at CIMS incidents?**

- A) ICS Form 201
- B) Complaint Report
- C) DETAIL ROSTER / ASSIGNMENT SHEETS (PD406-141)
- D) Unusual Occurrence Report

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) DETAIL ROSTER / ASSIGNMENT SHEETS (PD406-141)**

The PD406-141 (DETAIL ROSTER / ASSIGNMENT SHEETS) or other appropriate forms are used for the Check-In process. (P.G. 220-24)

</details>

---

**18. A Mobile Field Force consists of:**

- A) 1 to 3 Squads
- B) 2 to 6 Squads
- C) 4 to 8 Squads
- D) 3 to 5 Squads

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) 2 to 6 Squads**

A Mobile Field Force consists of 2 to 6 Squads. Each Squad = 1 Sergeant + 8 Police Officers. (P.G. 220-25)

</details>

---

**19. Under CIMS, the Incident Base and Camp are:**

- A) Required at every incident with more than 50 personnel
- B) Activated only during Unified Command
- C) NOT activated in NYC absent extraordinary circumstances
- D) Managed by the Logistics Section only during planned events

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) NOT activated in NYC absent extraordinary circumstances**

Incident Base and Camp are NIMS ICS concepts that are NOT activated in NYC absent extraordinary circumstances. (P.G. 220-19)

</details>

---

**20. What type of communications must be used for inter-agency communications at a CIMS incident?**

- A) Standard NYPD 10-codes
- B) Agency-specific codes
- C) Clear text with CIMS terminology
- D) Encrypted digital communications only

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) Clear text with CIMS terminology**

All incident personnel must use clear text (no 10-codes) for inter-agency communications and use CIMS nomenclature/terminology. (P.G. 220-16)

</details>

---

**21. Who has authority to designate the Liaison Officer under CIMS?**

- A) The Mayor's Press Office
- B) NYCEM (NYC Emergency Management)
- C) The Operations Section chief
- D) The Planning Section chief

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) NYCEM (NYC Emergency Management)**

While the Incident Commander may designate the Liaison Officer, NYCEM has overriding authority and may designate the Liaison Officer. The Mayor's Press Office designates the PIO, not the Liaison Officer. (P.G. 220-08)

</details>

---

**22. Resources must formally Check-In before reporting to which section for assignment?**

- A) Planning Section
- B) Logistics Section
- C) Operations Section
- D) Finance/Administration Section

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) Operations Section**

Resources must formally Check-In before reporting to the Operations Section for assignment (absent extraordinary circumstances). (P.G. 220-24)

</details>

---

**23. The Safety Officer function is required by law at which type of incident?**

- A) Any incident with more than 20 personnel
- B) Hazardous materials incidents
- C) All Unified Command incidents
- D) National Special Security Events

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) Hazardous materials incidents**

The Safety Officer function is required by law at hazardous materials incidents. (P.G. 220-07)

</details>

---

**24. Under CIMS, Incident Objectives must be:**

- A) Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound
- B) Attainable, Measurable, Adaptable/Flexible
- C) Clear, Concise, Complete
- D) Immediate, Actionable, Documented

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) Attainable, Measurable, Adaptable/Flexible**

Under Management by Objectives, CIMS Incident Objectives must be Attainable, Measurable, and Adaptable/Flexible. (P.G. 220-05)

</details>

---

**25. Which of the following is an NYPD Single Command incident?**

- A) Building collapse
- B) Structural fire
- C) Hazardous materials release (no crime)
- D) Hostage situation

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**D) Hostage situation**

NYPD Single Command incidents include: Civil Disturbance, EDP, Explosive Device/Bomb Threat, Hostage, Sniper, Special Event, Suspicious Package, and Water/Ice Rescue. Building collapse and structural fire are not NYPD Single Command; HazMat without crime is Unified Command. (P.G. 220-02)

</details>

---

**26. Under a Unified Command, which of the following positions does each Agency Incident Commander pick independently (not jointly with other ICs)?**

- A) Planning Section Chief
- B) Logistics Section Chief
- C) Safety Officer
- D) Liaison Officer

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) Safety Officer**

In Unified Command, each IC independently picks their own Safety Officer and their own Operations Section Chief. PIO, Liaison Officer, Planning Section Chief, Logistics Section Chief, Finance/Admin Section Chief, and Intelligence Section Chief are all jointly picked by the Co-ICs. (P.G. 220-05, 220-07)

</details>

---

**27. At a CBRN/HazMat incident involving suspected terrorism, which agency serves as the primary Incident Commander?**

- A) FDNY
- B) DEP
- C) NYPD
- D) NYCEM

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) NYPD**

When a CBRN/HazMat incident involves actual or suspected crime or terrorism, NYPD serves as the sole Incident Commander (Single Command). NYPD remains primary IC until it is determined there is no criminal or terrorist activity, at which point command reverts to Unified Command. (P.G. 220-02)

</details>

---

**28. Which of the following incident types uses a Unified Command structure with FDNY, NYPD, PANYNJ, USCG, and NTSB as Potential Primary Agencies?**

- A) Civil Disturbance
- B) Aviation Incident
- C) Rail Incident
- D) Explosion

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) Aviation Incident**

Aviation Incidents use a Unified Command structure with FDNY and NYPD as Primary Agencies, and PANYNJ, USCG, and NTSB as Potential Primary Agencies/Subject Matter Experts. Rail Incidents use MTA, PANYNJ, NJT, Amtrak as PPAs. Explosions use DDC, DOB, HPD. (P.G. 220-02)

</details>

---

**29. At a structural collapse incident, which agency is the Primary Agency?**

- A) NYPD
- B) FDNY
- C) DOB
- D) DDC

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) FDNY**

Structural Collapse is an FDNY Single Command incident. This question has been tested on the Sergeant exam. Other FDNY Single Command incidents include: Confined Space Rescue, Elevator Incident, Entrapment/Impalement, and Fire. (P.G. 220-02)

</details>

---

**30. The General Staff Section Chief who uses the keyword "MODULAR" to describe how the Incident Commander can staff the incident is associated with which concept?**

- A) The Incident Commander must use all five General Staff sections at every incident
- B) The IC can run an operation with one or two people or any combination of staff positions
- C) Only the Operations Section must always be activated
- D) The Command Element always requires at least three Command Staff members

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) The IC can run an operation with one or two people or any combination of staff positions**

"MODULAR" is the keyword for how CIMS works — the structure is flexible and scalable. The IC can run an operation with one or two people or open any combination of staff positions as needed. No section is mandatory. (P.G. 220-01)

</details>

---

**31. In a CBRN/HazMat incident that starts as NYPD Single Command, which agency is responsible for setting Hot, Warm, and Cold zones?**

- A) NYPD
- B) FDNY
- C) DEP
- D) DOHMH

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) DEP**

In Single-CBRN incidents, the Operations Section is Unified. DEP is responsible for Chemical — setting Hot, Warm, and Cold zones, and directing the mitigation effort. FDNY handles Life Safety, Search & Rescue, and Mass Decontamination. DOHMH handles Biological/Radiological zone-setting. NYPD handles overall site management and crime/terrorism assessment. (P.G. 220-02)

</details>

---

**32. When the Finance/Administration Section Chief is not needed as a full General Staff section, which section may absorb its functions?**

- A) Operations Section
- B) Planning Section
- C) Logistics Section
- D) Intelligence/Investigations Section

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) Planning Section**

When minimal finance/admin tasks are required (e.g., only managing overtime), the Finance/Admin role may not be a full General Staff section. Instead, it could be one specialist person working for the Planning Section Chief (per PG 220-12, step 1a). (P.G. 220-12)

</details>

---

**33. Under CIMS, which two General Staff sections can have Branches?**

- A) Operations and Planning
- B) Operations and Intelligence/Investigations
- C) Operations and Logistics
- D) Planning and Finance/Administration

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) Operations and Logistics**

Operations and Logistics can have Branches. Operations can also have Groups/Sectors. Planning, Finance/Admin can have Units only. Intelligence/Investigations can have Groups/Sectors only. Of the sections that can have Units, only Logistics can also have Branches. (P.G. 220-01)

</details>

---

**34. The Downed Tree incident type designates which agency as the Primary Agency?**

- A) NYPD
- B) FDNY
- C) DEP
- D) Department of Parks & Recreation / FDNY

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**D) Department of Parks & Recreation / FDNY**

Downed Tree incidents are Primary Agency for Department of Parks & Recreation / FDNY. This is a Single Command incident that is often overlooked — it is not NYPD or FDNY alone. Correctional Facility Disturbance is DOC; Confined Space Rescue is FDNY. (P.G. 220-02)

</details>

---

**35. Which agency has authority to designate the Liaison Officer in CIMS, and what is the history of that agency's name?**

- A) Mayor's Press Office, formerly known as the Mayor's Communications Office
- B) NYCEM, formerly known as OEM (Office of Emergency Management)
- C) DCPI, formerly known as the Press Secretary
- D) DSNY, formerly known as the Sanitation Commission

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) NYCEM, formerly known as OEM (Office of Emergency Management)**

NYCEM (NYC Emergency Management) has authority to designate the Liaison Officer. NYCEM was formerly known as OEM (Office of Emergency Management). Exam questions may use either name. The Mayor's Press Office designates the PIO, not the Liaison Officer. (P.G. 220-08)

</details>

---

# Section 221 — Tactical Operations

## Source
- **Patrol Guide Section:** 221
- **Source PDF:** public-pguide4.pdf (OCR)
- **Priority:** Medium

## Learning Objectives
- Understand force guidelines, firearm prohibitions, and restraint prohibitions
- Know the 4 force incident levels and corresponding investigating entities
- Master less lethal weapons procedures (O.C. pepper spray, CEW, tear gas)
- Know EDP handling procedures including zone of safety, isolate/contain, spit hood rules
- Understand firearms discharge investigation procedures and temporary assignment rules
- Recognize special weapons issuance requirements and distraction device approval process
- Know vehicle checkpoint rules and canine team authorized uses

## Procedures

| Procedure | Title | Status |
|-----------|-------|--------|
| P.G. 221-01 | Force Guidelines | completed |
| P.G. 221-02 | Use of Force | completed |
| P.G. 221-03 | Reporting and Investigation of Force Incident or Injury to Persons During Police Action | completed |
| P.G. 221-04 | Firearms Discharge by Uniformed Members of the Service | completed |
| P.G. 221-05 | Person Dies or Sustains Serious Injury in Police Custody or in Connection with Police Action | completed |
| P.G. 221-06 | Member of the Service Subjected to Force While Performing Lawful Duty | completed |
| P.G. 221-07 | Use of Oleoresin Capsicum Pepper Spray Devices | completed |
| P.G. 221-08 | Use of Conducted Electrical Weapons (CEW) | completed |
| P.G. 221-09 | Use of Tear Gas | completed |
| P.G. 221-10 | Use and Issuance of Shotguns/Long Guns/Automatic Weapons | completed |
| P.G. 221-11 | Guidelines and Approval Process Pertaining to the Use of Distraction Devices | completed |
| P.G. 221-13 | Mentally Ill or Emotionally Disturbed Persons | completed |
| P.G. 221-15 | Vehicle Pursuits | completed |
| P.G. 221-16 | Vehicle Checkpoints | completed |
| P.G. 221-18 | Use of Department Canine Teams | completed |
| P.G. 221-21 | School Safety Agent — Victim of an Offense While Performing Duty | completed |
| P.G. 221-22 | Traffic Enforcement Agent Victim of an Offense While Performing Duty | completed |
| P.G. 221-23 | Alcohol Testing for UMOS Involved in Firearms Discharges Resulting in Injury, Death, or Likelihood of Injury or Death | completed |

> **Note:** Procedure numbers not listed above (221-12, 221-14, 221-17, 221-19, 221-20) are not covered in the public Patrol Guide. P.G. 221-14 (Hostage/Barricaded) is referenced in the EDP section but its content is not included.

## Chapter Files

| File | Content |
|------|---------|
|  | 221-01, 221-02: Force guidelines, firearm/restraint prohibitions, use of force, de-escalation, duty to intervene |
|  | 221-03: Force incident levels 1-4, T.R.I. reports, immediate/investigating supervisor duties, BWC rules |
|  | 221-04, 221-05, 221-23: Firearms discharge investigation, death in custody, alcohol testing |
|  | 221-07, 221-08, 221-09: O.C. pepper spray, CEW (TASER 7), tear gas |
|  | 221-10, 221-11: Shotguns/long guns/automatic weapons, distraction devices |
|  | 221-15: Vehicle pursuits, initiation criteria, prohibited tactics, termination rules, post-pursuit requirements |
|  | 221-06, 221-13, 221-16, 221-18, 221-21, 221-22: MOS subjected to force, EDP, vehicle checkpoints, canine teams, school safety/TEA |
|  | Comprehensive definitions, force levels, approval authorities, timeframes, forms |
|  | 25 multiple-choice questions covering all major tactical operations concepts |

## Sergeant Focus Areas

- **Force incident response**: Immediate supervisor must be NOT involved, one rank higher; for Level 1, responsible for all investigative steps; complete T.R.I. before end of tour
- **Duty to intervene**: Must intervene to stop excessive force — failure = serious misconduct up to dismissal
- **Firearms discharge**: Assume command, establish crime scene, inspect/secure firearms, secure BWC, notify desk officer
- **CEW accountability**: Account for all CEWs each tour (Command Log entry with serial numbers); observe function test; carry CEW on patrol if available
- **EDP handling**: Establish firearms control immediately; maintain 20-foot zone of safety; if contained and believed armed but no immediate threat — NO additional action without CO/duty captain; only sergeant+ can cancel ESU
- **De-escalation**: Supervisor assumes command and coordinates de-escalation; consider whether non-compliance is inability vs. deliberate resistance
- **Less lethal prohibitions**: Never use O.C. spray, CEW, or impact weapons on passively resisting subjects; never use CEW on rear-cuffed prisoner
- **Post-force**: Ensure subject positioned for free breathing; inquire about medical needs; obtain medical attention for any injured person

---

# Section 221 — Vehicle Pursuits

## P.G. 221-15: Vehicle Pursuits

**Date Effective:** 10/30/25 | **Last Revision:** R.O. 114 | **Pages:** 8

### Purpose

To determine the necessity of initiating a vehicle pursuit and the method to be employed in conducting such a pursuit.

### Scope

**Vehicle pursuits are NOT authorized** when the most serious offense committed, or suspected of being committed, is a:
- Traffic infraction
- Violation
- **Non-violent misdemeanor**

> **NOTE:** Reckless Driving (VTL § 1212) constitutes a **non-violent misdemeanor** and **cannot** be the basis for a vehicle pursuit.

### Definitions

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Authorized Emergency Vehicle** | Marked or unmarked Department RMP. Department vans must disengage when another Department vehicle joins. **Unmarked RMPs and two-wheeled motorcycles must disengage when a marked RMP joins.** |
| **Vehicle Pursuit** | Any attempt by a uniformed member operating an authorized emergency vehicle to stop a subject who is **actively seeking to elude custody** while also operating a vehicle. Does not include Aviation Unit or monitoring techniques. |
| **Vehicle Pursuit Termination** | Official ending — cease all pursuit activity, **deactivate emergency signaling devices**, notify radio dispatcher, resume patrol. |
| **Non-Violent Misdemeanor** | Does not cause, threaten, or create substantial risk of physical injury or death. **Reckless Driving (VTL § 1212) = non-violent.** |
| **Primary Vehicle** | Vehicle initiating and/or controlling pursuit. |
| **Secondary Vehicle** | First additional authorized emergency vehicle assisting. |
| **Forcible Stopping** | **PROHIBITED** — Deliberately bringing emergency vehicle into contact with pursued vehicle (ramming, PIM, pinning, etc.). |
| **Roadblock** | **PROHIBITED** — Vehicle/barrier intentionally placed to encourage stop. |
| **Traffic Control** | **PERMISSIBLE** — Techniques to slow pursuit, induce voluntary stop, minimize risk. May direct to preferred path (less populated, StarChase, Aviation). |

### Procedure: Before Initiating Pursuit

**UNIFORMED MEMBER / PRIMARY VEHICLE**

1. **Initiate vehicle stop** (when feasible)
2. **Determine necessity** by considering:
   - **a.** Nature of offense(s)
   - **b.** Time of day
   - **c.** Weather conditions
   - **d.** Location and population density (residential, commercial, schools, playgrounds)
   - **e.** Traffic conditions
   - **f.** Speed of pursuit
   - **g.** Identity of operator (if known)
   - **h.** Emergency signaling devices and siren
   - **i.** Distance from pursuit origin
   - **j.** Capabilities of Department vehicle

3. **Notify radio dispatcher** immediately:
   - Location and direction of travel
   - Reason for pursuit (offense)
   - Vehicle description
   - Occupant information

> **Memory Aid — P.U.R.S.U.I.T.:**
> - **P**opulation density
> - **U**rgency of offense
> - **R**oad/weather conditions
> - **S**peed of pursuit
> - **U**nit capabilities
> - **I**dentity known?
> - **T**raffic conditions

> **Memory Aid — NO STOP:**
> - **N**on-violent misdemeanor = No pursuit
> - **O**ffense must justify risk
> - **S**top if danger > benefit
> - **T**raffic control OK, roadblock prohibited

### Procedure: During Pursuit

**PRIMARY VEHICLE**

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 1 | Maintain constant communication with dispatcher |
| 2 | Continuously assess necessity to continue |
| 3 | **Terminate if**: Danger to public outweighs benefit of apprehension |
| 4 | Notify dispatcher of **any change** in circumstances |

**SECONDARY VEHICLE**

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 1 | Maintain **safe distance** from primary |
| 2 | **Do not pass** primary unless directed |
| 3 | Assist with communication and coordination |
| 4 | Be prepared to become primary if needed |

**SUPERVISOR**

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 1 | **Respond to scene** when feasible |
| 2 | **Monitor pursuit** via radio |
| 3 | **Assess** whether pursuit should continue |
| 4 | **Direct termination** if danger outweighs benefit |
| 5 | Coordinate additional resources (Aviation, StarChase, Highway) |
| 6 | **Notify Duty Captain** of ongoing pursuit |

> **Sergeant Focus:** As a supervisor, you must **respond to the scene** when feasible and **actively monitor** the pursuit via radio. You have the **authority and responsibility** to direct termination if the danger to the public outweighs the benefit of apprehension.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Know the **prohibited tactics** — Forcible stopping and roadblocks are **NEVER authorized** during a vehicle pursuit. Officers may confuse these with permissible tactics like traffic control or PIM (which is only allowed on **stationary** vehicles).

> **Sergeant Focus:** **Reckless Driving (VTL § 1212)** is explicitly defined as a **non-violent misdemeanor** — pursuits for this offense alone are **NOT authorized**. This is heavily tested.

**DUTY CAPTAIN**

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 1 | Monitor pursuit |
| 2 | Coordinate cross-precinct resources |
| 3 | **Authorize/deauthorize** continuation based on risk assessment |
| 4 | Ensure proper documentation |

### Pursuit Termination

**When to Terminate**

| Factor | Consideration |
|--------|---------------|
| **Offense severity** | Non-violent misdemeanor = terminate |
| **Population density** | High density = higher risk |
| **Traffic conditions** | Heavy traffic = higher risk |
| **Weather/road conditions** | Adverse conditions = higher risk |
| **Vehicle capabilities** | Department vehicle limitations |
| **Operator identity known** | Can apprehend later = terminate |
| **Length of pursuit** | Extended pursuit = increasing risk |

**How to Terminate**

1. **Deactivate emergency signaling devices**
2. **Notify radio dispatcher** — "Terminating pursuit"
3. **Resume patrol** (do not follow unless normal traffic)
4. **Document** all actions taken

### Prohibited Tactics

| Tactic | Status | Notes |
|--------|--------|-------|
| **Forcible Stopping** (ramming, PIM, pinning) | PROHIBITED | During vehicle pursuit |
| **Roadblock** | PROHIBITED | During vehicle pursuit |
| **Firearms discharge** | PROHIBITED | From moving vehicle |
| **Two-wheeled motorcycle pursuit** | Limited | Must disengage when marked RMP joins |
| **Unmarked RMP pursuit** | Limited | Must disengage when marked RMP joins |
| **Department van pursuit** | Limited | Must disengage when any other Dept vehicle joins |

> **Exam Alert:** The distinction between **permissible traffic control** vs. **prohibited roadblock** is tested. Traffic control **slows/redirects**; roadblock **blocks all escape**.

> **Exam Alert:** **Unmarked RMPs and motorcycles** must disengage when a **marked RMP** joins. Department vans must disengage when **any other Department vehicle** joins.

### Permissible Tactics

| Tactic | Status | Notes |
|--------|--------|-------|
| **Traffic control** | ALLOWED | Slow down, redirect to safer area |
| **StarChase** | ALLOWED | GPS tagging system |
| **Aviation Unit** | ALLOWED | Monitor and broadcast (non-emergency) |
| **Precision Immobilization** | ALLOWED | **Only** when pursuit has ended and suspect is stationary |

### Post-Pursuit Requirements

**UNIFORMED MEMBER**

| Action | Deadline |
|--------|----------|
| Complete **Vehicle Pursuit Report** | Before end of tour |
| Notify **Precinct Executive Officer** | Immediately |
| **Safeguard BWC footage** | Immediately |
| **Do not view BWC** if injury/death | Await IAB/FID |

**SUPERVISOR**

| Action | Deadline |
|--------|----------|
| Review pursuit footage | Within 24 hours |
| **Notify IAB** if force incident | Immediately |
| **Notify FID** if death/serious injury | Immediately |
| Conduct **preliminary investigation** | Within 48 hours |

> **Exam Alert:** **BWC rules** — If injury or death occurs, supervisor **DO NOT VIEW** footage. Safeguard and await IAB/FID.

> **Exam Alert:** **Extraordinary circumstances** for false statements (A.G. 304-10) — Only the **Police Commissioner** can determine on a case-by-case basis.

### See Also

| Procedure | Title |
|-----------|-------|
| P.G. 221-01 | Force Guidelines |
| P.G. 221-02 | Use of Force |
| P.G. 221-03 | Reporting and Investigation of Force Incidents |
| P.G. 218-48 | Vehicle Seizure |
| A.G. 304-10 | False or Misleading Statements |
---

# Section 221 — EDP, MOS Subjected to Force, and Tactical Procedures

## P.G. 221-06: Member of the Service Subjected to Force While Performing Lawful Duty

**Date Effective:** 11/18/21 | **Last Revision:** I.O. 102 | **Pages:** 5

### Purpose

To record and evaluate incidents when MOS is subjected to force while performing lawful duty, on or off duty, or auxiliary members on duty.

### Definition of Force (Against MOS)

Any physical harassment, assault, reckless endangerment, or menacing, including shots fired at member, brandishing weapon at member, and throwing items at member — whether or not injury resulted.

### Immediate Supervisor Duties

| Step | Duty |
|------|------|
| 6 | Respond to scene and conduct preliminary investigation |
| 7 | Notify desk officer |
| 8 | Notify precinct detective squad if outstanding suspects or further investigation required |
| 9 | Ensure **COMPLAINT REPORT** prepared for offense against member and/or additional charge added |
| 10 | Ensure **Line of Duty Injury Report** completed |
| 11 | Perform duties per P.G. 221-03 |
| 12 | Prepare T.R.I. INCIDENT REPORT |
| 13 | Instruct involved members to complete T.R.I. INTERACTION REPORT |

### Note on Force Incident Levels

Injuries sustained by MOS are **independent** of force incident levels in P.G. 221-03. Force incident levels do NOT apply to injuries sustained by MOS. Instead, this procedure and A.G. 330-03 (Line of Duty Injury or Death) apply.

### Desk Officer — Charging Requirements

Ensure arresting officer charges defendant with appropriate charges:
- **Resisting Arrest** (Penal Law §205.30)
- **Assault 2nd degree** (Penal Law §120.05, sub.3)
- **Assault on Peace Officer/Police Officer/Fireman/EMS Professional** (Penal Law §120.08)
- **Aggravated Assault Upon Police/Peace Officer** (Penal Law §120.11)

### "Resisting Recidivist"

| Criteria | Action |
|----------|--------|
| Prisoner arrested for above charges **more than once within 5 years** (2nd time in 5 years) | Deemed a **"Resisting Recidivist"** |
| Action required | Detective squad **enhances arrest**, interviews subjects, canvasses for video and witnesses |

### If ADA Refuses to Charge

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| CO/XO/Duty Captain | Confer with desk officer; if unsatisfied, notify **Deputy Commissioner, Legal Matters** |
| DC Legal Matters availability | Mon-Fri 0700-2300 directly; midnight tours/weekends through Operations Division |

> **Exam Alert:** A "Resisting Recidivist" is a prisoner arrested for charges against MOS **more than once within 5 years**. Requires detective squad enhancement.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Force incident levels (1-4) do NOT apply to MOS injuries. Follow this procedure and A.G. 330-03 instead.

---

## P.G. 221-13: Mentally Ill or Emotionally Disturbed Persons

**Date Effective:** 10/28/25 | **Last Revision:** R.O. 113 | **Pages:** 7

### Purpose

To safeguard a mentally ill or emotionally disturbed person who does not voluntarily seek medical assistance.

### Scope

**Primary duty** of all MOS is to **preserve human life**. Safety of all persons is **paramount**. Force may be used when reasonable to prevent serious physical injury or death. When there is time to negotiate, **all the time necessary** will be used.

### Key Definitions

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **EDP** | Person apparently mentally ill or temporarily deranged, conducting themselves in a manner reasonably believed likely to result in serious injury to self or others |
| **Zone of Safety** | Distance maintained between EDP and MOS; greater than effective range of weapon (other than firearm); minimum **20 feet** recommended |
| **Establishing/Maintaining Firearms Control** | UMOS will not discharge firearms except to protect from imminent serious physical injury or death; ensure muzzle and trigger finger discipline |
| **Crossfire** | Unintentional placement of any person in position where they may be injured from firearms discharge |
| **Crossfire Awareness** | Being aware of threat location and surroundings; communicate "crossfire" when aware |

### MOS Initial Response

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 1 | Review **"EDP History"** in 911 app on Department smartphone **prior to arrival** |
| 1a | Request **EDP location history** from Communications Section |
| 3 | Transmit **"10-84"** upon arrival |
| 5 | Carry **three-foot polycarbonate shield** with door bag (door rope/wedge), if available |
| 6 | Request: patrol supervisor, ambulance, additional personnel, ESU if necessary |

### Spit Hood Rules

| Rule |
|------|
| Only **ESU personnel** may apply a spit hood |
| ESU member monitors and remains with subject until relieved by EMT (minimum) |
| Only **ESU personnel** or medical professional (EMT minimum) can **remove** |

### Patrol Supervisor Duties

| Step | Duty |
|------|------|
| 11 | Respond to location (even if supervisor from another agency is present) |
