| 12 | Transmit **"10-84"** upon arrival |
| 13 | **Establish firearms control** |
| 14 | Verify ESU is responding — may **cancel ESU only** if sergeant or above |
| 23 | Use **CIT-trained** members as contact officers when appropriate |
| 23a | If non-CIT officer has established rapport, allow that officer to continue; CIT supports |
| 24 | Employ less lethal devices when necessary for safety |
| 25 | Direct removal of door rope/wedge if EDP constitutes immediate threat |
| 26 | Request response of CO/duty captain |
| 30 | Take **no additional action** without CO/duty captain authorization if EDP is contained, believed armed/violent, but poses no immediate threat |

### Isolate and Contain — Door Rope/Wedge

| Rule |
|------|
| Use to isolate and contain EDP |
| Secure door with **only one door rope** at a time (girth hitch knot) |
| If another individual is present with EDP, use only in **exigent circumstances** |
| Do NOT remove without **supervisor authorization** or ESU direction (unless exigent) |

### Three Scenarios

| Scenario | Action |
|----------|--------|
| **EDP unarmed, not violent, willing to leave voluntarily** | MOS may take into custody **without supervisor direction** |
| **EDP isolated/contained but will not leave voluntarily** | Request H.N.T., coordinator, T.A.R.U.; comply with P.G. 221-14 (Hostage/Barricaded) |
| **EDP's actions = immediate threat of serious physical injury or death** | Take reasonable measures; deadly force only as **last resort**; attempt de-escalation |

### When EDP Has Been Restrained

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 41 | Remove property dangerous to life or facilitating escape |
| 42 | Remove to hospital in **ambulance** |
| 43 | Ride in body of ambulance with EDP |
| 44 | Transport by RMP only if ambulance unavailable and at **supervisor's direction** |
| 44a | **Under no circumstances** transport EDP to **police facility** |
| 45 | Inform examining physician of less lethal devices used |
| 46 | Unload firearm at safety station when entering **psychiatric ward** |
| 47 | Safeguard EDP at hospital until examined by **psychiatrist** |

### CIT (Crisis Intervention Team) Requirements

- Training sergeants/operations coordinators maintain updated CIT-trained list at desk (weekly)
- Desk officers ensure list maintained at all times
- Check "CIT Trained UMOS on Scene" on AIDED REPORT

### EDP Radio Codes

| Code | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| 10-97E3 | ESU Assisted in Removing EDP |
| 10-97E4 | Voluntary Surrender to ESU or H.N.T. |
| 10-97E5 | ESU Removal — Less Than Lethal Used |

> **Exam Alert:** **Zone of safety** = minimum **20 feet**. EDP must NEVER be transported to a **police facility**. Only **ESU** may apply or remove a **spit hood**.

> **Exam Alert:** Response of ESU may only be canceled by a **uniformed supervisor in the rank of sergeant or above**.

> **Sergeant Focus:** If EDP is contained and believed armed/violent but poses no immediate threat — take **no additional action** without CO/duty captain authorization. Establish **firearms control** immediately upon arrival. Use CIT-trained members as contact officers.

---

## P.G. 221-16: Vehicle Checkpoints

**Date Effective:** 06/26/25 | **Last Revision:** R.O. 53 | **Pages:** 3

### Purpose

To establish criteria for safe, legal, and effective vehicle checkpoints.

### Authorized Purposes

- **DWI check**
- **Vehicle safety check** (including vehicle and driver documentation)
- Emergency exceptions

Vehicle checkpoints for **all other purposes** require prior written approval from **Deputy Commissioner, Legal Matters**.

### Key Rules

| Rule | Detail |
|------|--------|
| Duration | Should **NOT exceed 2 hours** at one location |
| Stop pattern | **Non-arbitrary** manner (e.g., every third vehicle) — directed by CO |
| Department vehicles | At least **2** Department vehicles required |
| Reflective vests | Required during **hours of darkness** |
| Safety equipment | Turret lights, flares, traffic cones, signs, and/or barriers |
| Supervisor | Must remain at checkpoint for **entire operation** |
| Inclement weather | **Cancel** checkpoint (snow, heavy rain, etc.) |
| VEHICLE REPORT | Complete for **every vehicle stopped**, regardless of enforcement action |

### Member Duties at Checkpoint

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 12 | Identify yourself: rank, name, shield number, command |
| 14 | Explain purpose of checkpoint (unless impairs investigation) |
| 16 | Offer **RIGHT TO KNOW BUSINESS CARD** |

### Permissible Actions

- Request driver's license, registration, insurance card
- Conduct license and VIN check (VIN from **outside** the car only)
- If documentation appropriate, driver must proceed unless **reasonable suspicion** developed

### Prohibited Actions

- Reaching inside car to uncover VIN plate (may **request** motorist uncover it)
- Requesting consent to search without at least **founded suspicion** of criminality (Level 2 Encounter)

> **Exam Alert:** Vehicle checkpoints should NOT exceed **2 hours**. Stops must be **non-arbitrary** (e.g., every 3rd vehicle). At least **2 Department vehicles** required.

---

## P.G. 221-18: Use of Department Canine Teams

**Date Effective:** 08/28/19 | **Pages:** 3

### Authorized Tactical Uses — Patrol Canines

| Use |
|-----|
| Search buildings for break-in suspects (non-suspects NOT present) |
| Track suspects or missing persons |
| Locate lost/abandoned articles or hidden implements of crime |
| Assist in arrest/prevent escape of person with probable cause for **felony** |
| Protect officers and other persons from injury/death |
| Assist in service of arrest/search warrants |
| Safeguard equipment and designated facilities |
| Perform cadaver searches |
| Locate persons in building collapse / search and rescue |
| Search for felony suspects within transit system, including subway tracks |

### Key Rules

| Rule |
|------|
| Tactical use NOT specifically authorized is **prohibited** without prior approval of **borough commander** |
| Canine teams will **NOT** be used for crowd control or deterrent at **peaceful demonstrations** |
| Use at other than peaceful demonstrations requires prior approval of **Chief of Patrol** |
| Extra-jurisdictional use requires approval of patrol borough commander |
| Patrol supervisor determines if circumstances warrant use; consults with canine handler |

### Department Bloodhounds

- Unique scent discrimination ability
- Used when patrol canine insufficient
- Request through **Operations Unit**
- Determination made by supervisor from Chief of Special Operations or ESU on scene, after consultation with duty captain and Canine Unit member
- Require **good, uncontaminated scent article** — not handled by anyone
- Washed clothes are **not acceptable** as scent articles
- Bloodhounds track only — do NOT backtrack, do NOT defend handler

> **Exam Alert:** Canine teams NOT used for **crowd control at peaceful demonstrations**. Non-authorized tactical use requires **borough commander** approval.

---

## P.G. 221-21: School Safety Agent — Victim of an Offense While Performing Duty

**Date Effective:** 10/24/25 | **Last Revision:** R.O. 111 | **Pages:** 3

### Key Points

- School safety agent requests school safety supervisor and special operations lieutenant (or patrol supervisor if unavailable)
- School safety supervisor initiates investigation
- If H.A.R.M. (harassment, assault, reckless endangerment, menacing) — prepare T.R.I. INCIDENT REPORT per P.G. 221-06
- School safety agent who is **not a peace officer** shall **never** be directed to effect an arrest

---

## P.G. 221-22: Traffic Enforcement Agent Victim of an Offense While Performing Duty

**Date Effective:** 10/08/19 | **Pages:** 2

### Key Points

- Patrol supervisor, precinct of occurrence, **must** respond and conduct initial investigation
- Traffic Enforcement District supervisor must respond and assist
- If H.A.R.M. — prepare T.R.I. INCIDENT REPORT per P.G. 221-06
- If serious injury or newsworthy — prepare UNUSUAL OCCURRENCE REPORT and forward to Chief of Transportation
- All TEA on-duty injuries — Communications Section notifies both duty captain and Traffic Management Center

---

# Section 221 — Firearms Discharge and Death in Custody

## P.G. 221-04: Firearms Discharge by Uniformed Members of the Service

**Date Effective:** 04/04/25 | **Last Revision:** R.O. 18 | **Pages:** 7

### Purpose

To record and evaluate incidents in which a UMOS's firearm is discharged by any person.

### Scope Exclusions

Does NOT include discharges during:
- Authorized training session
- Lawful target practice or hunting
- Discharge into a firearms safety station within NYPD, DOC, or H+H facility

### Patrol Supervisor Duties

| Step | Duty |
|------|------|
| 5 | Respond to scene and **assume command** |
| 6 | Assess if involved members have sustained injuries |
| 6a | Direct **seriously injured** members to nearest **trauma center** |
| 6b | Direct members with minor injuries (tinnitus, etc.) to nearest hospital after conferring with CO/duty captain |
| 7 | **Establish crime scene** — all involved vehicles must remain in original positions |
| 8 | Inspect and **secure firearms** of involved MOS for evidence of recent discharge |
| 9 | **Secure body-worn cameras** from all MOS on scene |
| 10 | Notify desk officer |
| 11 | Request immediate response of precinct detective squad |
| 12 | Perform duties of "immediate supervisor" per P.G. 221-03 |

### Desk Officer Notifications (Without Waiting for Details)

| Notification |
|-------------|
| Precinct CO/XO |
| Operations Unit |
| Patrol Borough Command |
| Internal Affairs Bureau Command Center |
| Precinct detective squad |

> **Exam Alert:** If a UMOS discharges a firearm **outside of New York City**, the Operations Unit must be notified within **6 hours** of the discharge. The member must also notify local police authorities immediately.

### Chain of Response

| Who | Action |
|-----|--------|
| Patrol Borough Command | Notify duty captain and duty inspector |
| Operations Unit | Notify **duty chief** and **Force Investigation Division (FID)** |
| CO/XO/Duty Captain | Respond to scene, confer with ranking supervisors, assume command |
| Detective Squad | Respond, assume command of investigation, transition to FID upon arrival |
| FID Supervisor | Respond, confer with detective squad supervisor, **assume command of investigation** |

### FID Investigation Responsibilities

- Complete T.R.I. INCIDENT REPORT and INTERACTION REPORTS
- Ensure PD324-140 (Firearms Discharge Report) completed within **48 hours**
- Notify appropriate prosecutorial agency (DA's Office, State AG)
- Confer with prosecutorial agency **before** interviewing involved UMOS
- Supply discharging member with **temporary replacement firearm**
- Transport discharged firearm to Firearms Analysis Section

### Temporary Assignment After Firearms Discharge Causing Death/Injury

| Requirement | Detail |
|------------|--------|
| Assigned to | Patrol borough office of assignment (or counterpart) |
| Duration | Minimum **three consecutive scheduled tours** (exclusive of sick time or RDOs) |
| Return to permanent command | Requires prior approval of **First Deputy Commissioner** |

### Post-Incident Member Welfare

| Timeframe | Action |
|-----------|--------|
| After initial investigation | CO/XO inquires about member's well-being; offers Counseling Services, Family Assistance, EAU, POPPA |
| Within **24-48 hours** | Follow-up assessment; observe post-trauma reaction; repeat offer of services |
| After 3 working days | Bureau Chief prepares report recommending continuance or discontinuance of temporary assignment |

### CO, FID — Final Report

- Addressed to First Deputy Commissioner
- Includes findings: within/outside guidelines, tactics consistent with guidelines
- Findings categories: Intentional discharge (violation/no violation), Unintentional discharge (violation/no violation)
- 15 copy distribution list (PC, CoD, Chief of Staff, etc.)

### Key Rule — Body Identification

If person is **killed** as direct result of police action, the involved UMOS will **NOT** be assigned to identify the body at the morgue.

> **Exam Alert:** After firearms discharge causing death/injury, member is temporarily assigned for minimum **3 consecutive tours**. Return requires **First Deputy Commissioner** approval.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Patrol supervisor's priorities at firearms discharge: (1) call ambulance/render aid, (2) assume command, (3) establish crime scene, (4) inspect/secure firearms, (5) secure BWC, (6) notify desk officer, (7) request detective squad.

---

## P.G. 221-05: Person Dies or Sustains Serious Injury and Is Likely to Die in Police Custody or in Connection with Police Action

**Date Effective:** 05/03/24 | **Last Revision:** R.O. 24 | **Pages:** 5

### Purpose

To establish command responsibility and notification requirements when a person dies or is seriously injured and likely to die in/immediately prior to police custody.

### Definitions

**Police In-Custody Death** — Death/likely to die while in police custody:
- Police force or restraint may be a factor
- Suicide (e.g., in holding cell)
- Accidental (drug overdose, reaction to medicine)
- Apparent natural causes (heart failure, asthma)

**Police Pre-Custody Death** — Death/likely to die immediately prior to or during apprehension:
- Police use of force/restraint (firearms discharge, etc.)
- Suicide (barricaded subject)
- Accidental (falls from building, killed eluding police)
- Apparent natural causes

### Force Investigation Division Responsibility

FID is responsible for **ALL investigations** when a person dies or is likely to die in police custody or immediately prior to custody.

### Notification to Next of Kin

| Requirement | Detail |
|------------|--------|
| Made by | Member in rank of **lieutenant or above** |
| Assisted by | Community Affairs Bureau, if appropriate |
| Investigator accompanies | Unless deemed inappropriate by PBC and CO, FID |
| Coordination | Confer with CO, FID **before** contacting next of kin |
| Timing | Within a **reasonable amount of time** |
| Reported to | Office of First Deputy Commissioner and Chief of Department |

### NYS Correction Law Section 47(2)

Report required to NYS Commission of Correction when death occurs while prisoner is:
- Lodged in detention or holding cell
- Being transported to courthouse after being in detention/holding cell
- At hospital after being in detention/holding cell

Report (**SCOC M187**) — faxed within **6 hours** of death; mailed within **10 days**.

> **Exam Alert:** Notification to next of kin must be made by a member in the rank of **lieutenant or above**. FID must confer with before contacting.

---

## P.G. 221-23: Alcohol Testing for UMOS Involved in Firearms Discharges Resulting in Injury, Death, or Likelihood of Injury or Death

**Date Effective:** 02/16/22 | **Last Revision:** I.O. 13 | **Pages:** 3

### Purpose

To ensure integrity at the scene of police-involved firearms discharges resulting in reasonable possibility of injury or death.

### Procedure

| Step | Who | Action |
|------|-----|--------|
| 4 | Duty Captain/Inspector | Inform discharging member(s) they will be subject to **alcohol testing** |
| 5 | | Ensure involved member(s) remain on scene pending FID |
| 10 | FID Zone Commander | Direct alcohol test using **PBT device** in **private setting** |
| 10a | | Test conducted by FID supervisor at least **one rank higher** than discharging member |

### Test Results

| PBT Reading | Action |
|-------------|--------|
| Less than **.08** and no indicia of intoxication | **No further testing** required |
| **.08 or greater** and/or indicia of intoxication | Notify Highway IDTU; transport to IDTU facility for **Intoxilyzer** test |

### If Unfit for Duty

- Notify IAB Command Center and obtain log number
- Be guided by A.G. 318-10 (Removal of Firearms from Intoxicated UMOS)

### Key Rules

- Prudent not to ingest alcohol up to **4 hours** before tour
- Undercover members: no more than **2 alcoholic beverages** during tour (absent exigent circumstances)
- Entire Intoxilyzer testing process must be **videotaped**
- Highway District supervisor must be present during all testing phases

> **Exam Alert:** Alcohol testing uses **PBT device** in private setting. Threshold is **.08** — if at or above, Intoxilyzer test at IDTU facility required.

---

# Section 221 — Force Guidelines and Use of Force

## P.G. 221-01: Force Guidelines

**Date Effective:** 07/10/25 | **Last Revision:** R.O. 60 | **Pages:** 4

### Purpose

To provide members of the service with force/restraint and firearm prohibitions.

### Scope

**The primary duty of all members of the service (MOS) is to protect human life, including the lives of individuals being placed in police custody.** Force may be used when it is reasonable to ensure safety of MOS or a third person, or otherwise protect life, or when it is reasonable to place a person in custody or to prevent escape from custody. In all circumstances, any application or use of force must be **reasonable under the circumstances**. All MOS at a police incident must use reasonable force, employ less lethal alternatives, and prioritize **de-escalation**, whenever possible. If the force used is unreasonable under the circumstances, it will be deemed excessive and in violation of Department policy.

All MOS must, when in a position to do so, **intervene** to stop another MOS or members of other law enforcement agencies from using excessive force. Failure to intervene in the use of excessive force, or report excessive force, or failure to request or to ensure timely medical treatment for an individual is **serious misconduct** that may result in criminal and civil liability and **will** result in Department discipline, up to and including **dismissal**. If a member becomes aware of the use of excessive force or failure to request or ensure timely medical treatment, the member **must** report such misconduct to the Internal Affairs Bureau Command Center.

Excessive force will NOT be tolerated. MOS who use excessive force will be subject to Department discipline, up to and including **dismissal**.

### Reporting Misconduct

Reporting misconduct by a member of the service is outlined in P.G. 207-21, *"Allegations of Corruption and Other Misconduct Against Members of the Service."* Additionally, reporting misconduct by other city employees is outlined in P.G. 207-22, *"Allegations of Corruption Against City Employees (Other Than Members of The New York City Police Department)."*

### Factors for Determining Reasonableness of Force

In determining whether the use of force is reasonable, MOS should consider:

| Factor | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| a | Nature and severity of the crime/circumstances |
| b | Actions taken by the subject |
| c | Duration of the action |
| d | Immediacy of the perceived threat or harm to the subject, MOS, and/or bystanders |
| e | Whether the subject is actively resisting custody |
| f | Whether the subject is attempting to evade arrest by flight |
| g | Number of subjects in comparison to the number of MOS |
| h | Size, age, and condition of the subject in comparison to the MOS |
| i | Subject's violent history, if known |
| j | Presence of hostile crowd or agitators |
| k | Subject apparently under the influence of a stimulant/narcotic which would affect pain tolerance or increase the likelihood of violence |

### Key Definitions

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **De-Escalation** | Taking action to stabilize a situation and reduce the immediacy of the threat so that more time, options, and/or resources become available; goal is voluntary compliance |
| **Objectively Reasonable Standard** | Reasonableness based on totality of circumstances known by MOS at the time; examined from perspective of a member with similar training and experience |
| **Excessive Force** | Force greater than what a reasonable officer, in the same situation, would use under the circumstances |
| **Prohibited Methods of Restraint** | Sitting, kneeling, or standing on chest or back compressing diaphragm, reducing ability to breathe |
| **Chokehold** | Any pressure to throat, carotid artery, or windpipe which may prevent or hinder breathing or reduce intake of air or blood flow |
| **Mass Casualty Vehicle Ramming Attack** | Subject deliberately rams motor vehicle into crowd or building |

### Firearm Prohibitions — SHALL NOT:

| Prohibition |
|-------------|
| Discharge when doing so will unnecessarily endanger innocent persons |
| Discharge in defense of **property** |
| Discharge to subdue fleeing felon who presents **no threat** of imminent death/serious physical injury |
| Fire **warning shots** |
| Discharge to summon assistance (except emergency when personal safety endangered and no other means) |
| Discharge at or from a **moving vehicle** unless deadly physical force is being used by means **other than** a moving vehicle |
| Discharge at a dog/animal except to protect from **imminent physical injury** and no opportunity to retreat |
| **Cock** a firearm — must fire **double action** at all times |

### Notes on Drawing a Firearm

Drawing a firearm prematurely or unnecessarily limits a UMOS's options in controlling a situation and may result in an unwarranted or accidental discharge. The decision to display or draw a firearm **should** be based on an articulable belief that the potential for serious physical injury is present. MOS, when feasible, **should** issue an appropriate verbal warning prior to discharging a firearm. When the potential for serious physical injury is no longer present, the UMOS will holster the firearm as soon as practicable.

*Where only a vehicle is used as deadly physical force against MOS, members should safely attempt to move out of the path of an approaching vehicle.*

### Mass Casualty Vehicle Ramming Attack Exception

If a vehicle is being used in a mass casualty ramming attack, UMOS may utilize tactics **otherwise prohibited** within P.G. 221-15 (Vehicle Pursuits) to stop the vehicle if deadly physical force or death is imminent against MOS or other persons present, by means of a moving vehicle as the sole or primary threat.

### Restraint Prohibitions — SHALL NOT:

| Prohibition |
|-------------|
| Use a prohibited method of restraint |
| Use a **chokehold** |
| Use any level of force to **punish, retaliate, or coerce** statements |
| Use force on **handcuffed/restrained** subjects unless necessary to prevent injury, escape, or overcome active physical resistance or assault |
| Connect or tie rear-cuffed hands to cuffed/restrained ankles or legs |
| Transport a subject **facedown** |
| Use force to prevent a subject from **swallowing** alleged controlled substance, or forcibly attempt to remove substance from mouth or body cavity |

### CEW on Rear-Cuffed Prisoner

A CEW should **never** be used in CARTRIDGE or DRIVE STUN mode on a **rear-cuffed prisoner**.

### Use of Force Review Board

Members subject to investigation for violation of prohibitions may request review. The Board may find that under **exigent or exceptional circumstances**, the prohibited action was justified.

> **Exam Alert:** Firearms must be fired **double action** at all times — cocking is prohibited. Members **SHALL NOT** discharge at or from a **moving vehicle** unless deadly force is used by means OTHER than the moving vehicle.

> **Exam Alert:** **Chokeholds** are prohibited. Prohibited methods of restraint (compressing diaphragm) are prohibited. Force to **punish, retaliate, or coerce** is prohibited.

> **Sergeant Focus:** When a subject ingests narcotics, transport **DIRECTLY** to nearest hospital — do NOT use force to prevent swallowing or attempt to remove from mouth.

---

## P.G. 221-02: Use of Force

**Date Effective:** 06/26/25 | **Last Revision:** R.O. 53 | **Pages:** 4

### Purpose

To provide guidelines for MOS regarding the use of force and ensure proper reporting and documentation.

### Additional Definitions

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **Active Resisting** | Physically evasive movements to defeat control (bracing, tensing, pushing, or verbally signaling intent to resist) |
| **Active Aggression** | Threat or overt act of assault coupled with present ability, reasonably indicating imminent assault or injury |
| **Passive Resistance** | Minimal physical action to prevent lawful duty (standing motionless, going limp) |
| **Resisting Arrest (Penal Law)** | Intentionally preventing or attempting to prevent authorized arrest |

### MOS Procedure When Force Is Necessary

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 1 | Take action to protect life and personal safety of **all persons**, including subjects |
| 2 | Utilize **de-escalation** techniques when appropriate and consistent with personal safety |
| 3 | Isolate and contain the subject, if appropriate |
| 4 | **Immediately request a supervisor** to respond |
| 5 | Request additional members, as necessary |
| 6 | Request ESU, if appropriate |
| 7 | Comply with P.G. 221-13 if subject appears to be EDP |

### Supervisor On Scene

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 8 | **Assume command** and coordinate de-escalation |
| 9 | Direct tactics to **minimize possibility of injury** to MOS, subject, or bystanders |

### If De-Escalation Fails (MOS/Supervisor)

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 10a | Advise offender they will be charged with resisting arrest |
| 10b | Devise a **tactical plan** with members present to minimize injury |
| 10c | Advise offender that physical force or devices will be used before applying, if appropriate |
| 11 | Apply **no more than reasonable force** necessary to gain control |
| 12 | **Assess situation continually** and adjust force as necessary |

### Prohibited Uses on Passive Resistance

Members should **NOT** use O.C. Pepper Spray, CEW, or impact weapons on persons who are **passively resisting**.

### Duty to Intervene

All MOS **must**, when in a position to do so, **intervene to stop** another MOS or members of other law enforcement agencies from using **excessive force**. Failure to intervene or report excessive force is **serious misconduct** that may result in criminal/civil liability and Department discipline up to **dismissal**.

### Post-Force Requirements

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 13 | Rear-cuff subject (may front-cuff initially, then transition) |
| 14 | Position subject to promote **free breathing** (sit up or turn on side) |
| 15 | Observe closely for injuries, illness, or difficulty breathing |
| 16 | Inquire if subject requires medical attention; document response |
| 17-18 | Ensure immediate medical attention if difficulty breathing or life-threatening symptoms |
| 20 | Notify immediate supervisor of force type, reason, and injuries |
| 21 | Document use of force in digital Activity Log |
| 22 | Complete VEHICLE REPORT if force resulted from vehicle/bike stop |

> **Exam Alert:** MOS must **intervene** to stop excessive force — failure is **serious misconduct** and may result in **dismissal**.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Supervisor must **assume command**, coordinate de-escalation, and direct tactics to **minimize injury**. Must consider whether non-compliance is deliberate resistance or an inability to comply (medical condition, mental impairment, language barrier, etc.).

---

# Section 221 — Reporting and Investigation of Force Incidents

## P.G. 221-03: Reporting and Investigation of Force Incident or Injury to Persons During Police Action

**Date Effective:** 07/23/25 | **Last Revision:** R.O. 64 | **Pages:** 11

### Purpose

To properly record and investigate use of force incidents by MOS/auxiliary members, and/or injuries to persons sustained as a result of police action or while in police custody.

### Key Definitions

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **Subject** | Individual who is the target/focus of police action (suspect, perpetrator, prisoner, EDP, disorderly person) |
| **Civilian/Non-Member/Bystander** | Non-employee of Department not intended subject of police action but inadvertently injured |
| **Immediate Supervisor** | Available supervisor NOT involved in incident, from same command, at least **one rank higher** than all involved members; if none available, duty captain determines |
| **Investigating Supervisor** | Level 2: CO/XO/duty captain; Level 3: IAB ranking officer; Level 4: FID ranking officer |
| **Physical Illness/Injury** | Impairment including minor swelling, contusions, lacerations, abrasions |
| **Serious Physical Injury/Illness** | Substantial risk of death, or serious disfigurement, protracted impairment of health, or loss of function of organ/limb |

### Serious Physical Injury Includes

- Broken/fractured bone(s)
- Aneurysm
- Injury requiring **hospital admission**
- Heart attack, stroke
- Other life-threatening illness or injury

### Force Incident Levels

| Level | Qualifying Criteria | Investigating Entity |
|-------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Level 1** | Hand/foot strikes, forcible take-downs, wrestling/grappling with actively resisting subject, O.C. pepper spray discharge, mesh restraining blanket, CEW in **cartridge mode**, physical injury to subject/civilian/bystander from police action | **Immediate Supervisor** |
| **Level 2** | Intentional striking with any object (baton, vehicle), police canine bite, CEW in **drive stun mode**, prohibited acts (excluding chokehold/prohibited restraint), alleged/suspected excessive force, attempted prisoner suicide, unconsciousness/loss of tooth/stitches/staples | **CO/XO/Duty Captain** + IAB Investigations Unit |
| **Level 3** | Force readily capable of causing death/serious injury (not firearm), alleged/suspected chokehold or prohibited restraint, excessive force with serious physical injury, attempted prisoner suicide with serious injury, serious physical injury from police action | **Internal Affairs Bureau** |
| **Level 4** | ALL firearms discharges by UMOS (intentional, unintentional, self-inflicted), discharge of UMOS firearm by someone else, subject/civilian seriously injured and likely to die or dies in/prior to custody | **Force Investigation Division** |

> **Exam Alert:** The 4 force incident levels are critical. Key distinctions: CEW **cartridge mode = Level 1**, CEW **drive stun = Level 2**. Chokehold/prohibited restraint = **Level 3**. ALL firearms discharges = **Level 4**.

### Immediate Supervisor Duties (All Levels)

| Step | Duty |
|------|------|
| 6 | Review facts and provide details to desk officer |
| 7 | Make prompt assessment — determine force level and appropriate investigation |
| 8 | Establish crime scene for **Level 3 and Level 4** |
| 9 | Obtain and secure **body-worn cameras** from ALL MOS on scene for Level 3/4 |
| 10 | Reserve T.R.I. INCIDENT REPORT number from FORMS for Level 1 |
| 11 | Direct involved members to complete T.R.I. INTERACTION REPORT |
| 12 | Question subjects/civilians regarding possible injuries |
| 13 | Take **digital photographs** of visible injuries (and where injury claimed but not visible) for Level 1 |
| 14 | Interview witnesses |
| 15 | Review body-worn camera video for **Level 1 only** |
| 17 | Question involved MOS regarding reason for force and type used |
| 20 | Determine tactical recommendations to improve safety |
| 21 | Complete and electronically sign T.R.I. INCIDENT REPORT for Level 1 **prior to end of tour** |

### Body-Worn Camera Viewing Rules

| Level | Who Views |
|-------|-----------|
| Level 1 | Immediate supervisor reviews |
| Level 2 | Guided by investigating supervisor (CO/XO/duty captain) |
| Level 3-4 | Immediate supervisor does **NOT** view; safeguard cameras for IAB/FID |

### Desk Officer Notifications (Level 2, 3, or 4)

| Notification |
|-------------|
| Patrol Borough Command |
| Operations Unit |
| Internal Affairs Bureau Command Center (obtain IAB log number) |
| Precinct detective squad, as appropriate |
| Commanding officer/executive officer, if available |

### Investigating Supervisor Duties (Level 2, 3, 4)

| Step | Duty |
|------|------|
| 28 | Conduct investigation and prepare T.R.I. INCIDENT REPORT |
| 29 | Review body-worn camera videos |
| 30 | Interview involved MOS to assess whether force was necessary and reasonable |
| 31 | Ensure photographs taken and uploaded |
| 32 | Report findings and recommendations to IAB Command Center |
| 33 | Review all T.R.I. INTERACTION REPORTS prior to end of tour |

### Command Training Sergeant

- Review all T.R.I. reports for command
- Identify recommendations for creative/innovative approaches to improve safety
- Submit recommendations to precinct CO on Typed Letterhead

### One Report Rule

Only **ONE** T.R.I. INCIDENT REPORT per incident, regardless of number of MOS involved or number of subjects/civilians/bystanders.

### NOT Reportable Uses of Force

| Situation |
|-----------|
| Ordering person to lie on ground, guiding to ground in controlled manner, or mere use of equipment (Velcro straps, shield) — **unless injury sustained** |
| Subject with pre-existing illness/injury requiring ER (report per P.G. 210-04 or 216-01 instead) |
| Injuries occurring in custody of **another agency** (e.g., DOC) |
| Department vehicle collisions — **unless** person in custody injured/dies, pursuit injury/death, or apprehension injury/death |

### Key Forms

| Form | Use |
|------|-----|
| **T.R.I. INCIDENT REPORT** | One per incident; completed by investigating supervisor |
| **T.R.I. INTERACTION REPORT** | Completed by each involved MOS for every reportable force/injury |
| **PD324-140** (UMOS Report of Firearms Discharge) | Firearms discharges — completed within **48 hours** |

> **Exam Alert:** Immediate supervisor must be at least **one rank higher** than all involved members and NOT involved in the incident.

> **Exam Alert:** FID completes T.R.I. reports for Level 4 incidents. **PD324-140** (Firearms Discharge Report) must be completed within **48 hours**.

> **Sergeant Focus:** For Level 1 incidents, the immediate supervisor is responsible for **all investigative steps**. For Level 2-4, the immediate supervisor is guided by the investigating supervisor. Complete Level 1 T.R.I. INCIDENT REPORT **before end of tour**.

---

# Section 221 — Less Lethal Weapons

## P.G. 221-07: Use of Oleoresin Capsicum Pepper Spray Devices

**Date Effective:** 04/19/24 | **Last Revision:** R.O. 23 | **Pages:** 4

### Purpose

To inform UMOS of circumstances for O.C. pepper spray use and to record instances of discharge.

### Scope

O.C. pepper spray constitutes **physical force** under NYS Penal Law. May be used to gain/maintain control of persons who are **actively resisting** or exhibiting **active aggression**, or to prevent physical injury.

### Authorized Uses

| Situation |
|-----------|
| Gain/maintain control of persons **actively resisting** arrest or exhibiting **active aggression** |
| Prevent individuals from physically injuring themselves, MOS, or others |
| Establish physical control of subject attempting to **flee** |
| Establish physical control of an **EDP** |
| Control a **dangerous animal** |

### Application Method

| Parameter | Requirement |
|-----------|-------------|
| Position | Hold **upright**, aim at subject's **eyes** |
| Burst pattern | **Two, one-second bursts** |
| Minimum distance | **Three feet** |

### Prohibitions

| SHALL NOT Use On |
|-----------------|
| Persons who **passively resist** |
| If possible, avoid: frail health, young children, women believed pregnant, known respiratory conditions |
| Avoid: indiscriminately over a large area for disorder control (unless specifically trained) |
| Avoid: small contained areas (automobiles, closets) |

### Post-Use Requirements

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 3 | Request **EMS** once situation is under control |
| 4 | Remove subject from contaminated area, expose to fresh air |
| 4a | Determine if subject wearing **contact lenses** (recommend removal ASAP) |
| 5 | Position subject on side or sitting — **never facedown** |
| 6 | Flush contaminated skin with **profuse amounts of water** |
| 7 | Transport to ER if difficulty breathing, severe stress, hyperventilation |
| 7a | Keep **windows of transport vehicle open** |
| 7c | Advise hospital staff that O.C. spray was used |

### Canister Capacity

| Type | Bursts | Replace When |
|------|--------|-------------|
| Stream | ~10 one-second bursts | Half empty (after 5 bursts) |
| Gel | ~8 one-second bursts | Half empty (after 4 bursts) |

### Carrying Requirements

| Duty Status | Carry Location |
|-------------|---------------|
| Uniform | Holster on **non-shooting side** of gun belt |
| Civilian clothes | Holster attached to belt or appropriate manner |
| Undercover | May opt **not** to carry |
| Off duty | May carry (optional) |

### Important Decontamination Notes

- Effects do **not dissipate** for **15-30 minutes**
- Do NOT rub or touch contaminated skin
- Do NOT use salves, creams, ointments, commercial eyewashes, or bandages

> **Exam Alert:** O.C. pepper spray: **two one-second bursts** at minimum **three feet** distance. **Do NOT** use on **passively resisting** subjects.

---

## P.G. 221-08: Use of Conducted Electrical Weapons (CEW)

**Date Effective:** 12/02/25 | **Last Revision:** R.O. 124 | **Pages:** 10

### Purpose

To inform UMOS of CEW use circumstances and provide control, accountability, issuance, and investigation procedures.

### Key Definitions

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **CEW** | Hand-held less lethal device delivering electrical charge through probes or direct contact |
| **Function Test** | Mandatory test into firearms safety station under supervisor observation; must use **test mode** |
| **Activate** | Arm CEW by releasing safety — laser/LED illuminators turn on |
| **Laser Warning** | De-escalation technique — placing laser dot on subject to gain compliance |
| **Warning Arc** | Activating and discharging TASER 7 to display visible/audible arcing of electricity |
| **Cartridge Mode** | Probes fired at subject for neuromuscular incapacitation — **Level 1 force** |
| **Drive Stun** | Front electrodes in direct contact with subject's body — **Level 2 force** |

### CEW Issuance Priority

| Priority | Assignment |
|----------|-----------|
| 1 | One operable CEW maintained at **desk** |
| 2 | One to each **supervisor** performing patrol duty |
| 3 | One to authorized UMOS in each **RMP** |
| 4 | One to authorized UMOS per **foot post** |
| 5 | Remaining to second authorized UMOS in RMP |

### Carrying Requirements

| Requirement |
|-------------|
| Holster on **support side** (opposite firearm) |
| **Cross-drawing** is prohibited |
| ESU/canine handlers carry in manner approved by CO, Firearms and Tactics |

### Point of Aim

| Target Area | Direction |
|-------------|-----------|
| **Lower center mass** (below chest) | Frontal discharges |
| **Below neck area** | Discharges at back |
| Avoid: head, neck, chest | If possible |
| **Never** aim at groin | |
| **Preferred**: discharge at subject's back | When practical |

### CEW Prohibitions

| SHALL NOT |
|-----------|
| Use on persons who **passively resist** |
| Use as **coercion or punishment** |
| Use on **rear-cuffed prisoner** (cartridge or drive stun) |
| Generally not on children, elderly, obviously pregnant, frail |
| Generally not against subjects on **moving device/vehicle** (fall risk) |
| Generally not where subject may fall from **elevated surface** |
| Not when **combustible gases or flammable liquids** present |
| **Two CEWs simultaneously** on one subject — prohibited |
| **Fleeing** should NOT be **sole** justification |

### Cycle Duration

| Guideline |
|-----------|
| Standard cycle: **5 seconds** — constantly assess |
| Exposure beyond **15 seconds** (multiple applications or continuous) may increase risk of **death or serious injury** |
| All applications must be **independently justifiable** |

### Post-Use Requirements

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| Request **EMS** | Anyone who received CEW discharge |
| Medical facility | Anyone struck by probe or had drive stun used on them **must be examined** |
| Probes in skin | Only removed by **medical personnel** |
| Break wire | By hand, ~4-6 inches from probe |
| Spent cartridge | Safeguard and invoice (PROPERTY CLERK INVOICE) |
| Battery | Place in charging dock |
| Notify | Firearms and Tactics Section — obtain **FTS CEW Log Number** |

### Command Accountability

| Duty | Responsible Party |
|------|------------------|
| Account for all CEWs/cartridges each tour | Desk officer/unit supervisor |
| Maintain list of CEW-trained members at desk | Command Training Sergeant |
| Monthly inspection of equipment | Command Operations Coordinator |
| Replacement cartridges within **72 hours** | CO |
| Unintentional discharge retraining within **5 business days** | Command Training Sergeant |

> **Exam Alert:** CEW cartridge mode = **Level 1** force. CEW drive stun = **Level 2** force. Standard cycle = **5 seconds**. Beyond **15 seconds** increases risk of death.

> **Exam Alert:** **All supervisors performing patrol** are to carry a CEW if available. CEW holster on **support side**, opposite firearm. Cross-drawing **prohibited**.

> **Sergeant Focus:** Ensure CEWs are accounted for at start of every tour with Command Log entry including serial numbers. Ensure function test is performed into firearms safety station under your observation.

---

## P.G. 221-09: Use of Tear Gas

**Date Effective:** 06/01/16 | **Pages:** 2

### Purpose

To provide for the use of tear gas.

### Key Rules

| Rule | Detail |
|------|--------|
| Decision authority | **Patrol borough commander** or designee |
| Technical decisions | Ranking member of **Emergency Service Unit** at scene |
| Who may use | Trained **ESU members** only |

### Operational Plan Requirements

| Requirement |
|-------------|
| Establish designated mobilization point |
| Station non-engaged personnel at safe distance |
| Evacuate civilians from affected areas |
| Request: Fire Department company, EMS ambulance, EMS borough supervisor, HazMat Decontamination Trailer, Patrol Wagon |

### Decontamination

- Remove persons to open, **upwind** position
- Eyes open, facing the wind
- Flush face, eyes, contaminated skin with **copious cool water**
- Contaminated clothing: thoroughly aired and washed with soap and water
- **Dry cleaning will NOT remove** tear gas residue

> **Exam Alert:** Tear gas use requires approval of **patrol borough commander** (or designee). Technical decisions made by ranking **ESU** member. Dry cleaning does NOT remove tear gas.

---

# Section 221 — Special Weapons and Distraction Devices

## P.G. 221-10: Use and Issuance of Shotguns/Long Guns/Automatic Weapons

**Date Effective:** 08/28/19 | **Pages:** 4

### Purpose

To provide guidelines for use of special weapons and maintain security and control of issuance.

### Definition

**Special Weapons** — Shotguns, long guns, and automatic weapons.

### Special Weapons Record (Three Sections)

| Section | Content |
|---------|---------|
| **Qualification Section** (20 pages) | Member's rank, name, tax registry number, dates qualified |
| **Issuance Section** | Time out, rank, name, signature, shield number, date qualified, reason, weapon serial number, supervisor authorizing, time returned, verifying supervisor |
| **Maintenance Section** (4 pages) | Date received, serial number, date removed, reason, signatures |

### Key Rules

| Rule |
|------|
| NEVER issue to or allow handling by member whose **range qualifications are not current** |
| Unissued weapons stored in **locked gun cabinet** |
| Command Log entry each tour indicating disposition of all special weapons |
| Load/unload at **safety station** maintained at command |
| Only carry weapons and ammunition **authorized and issued by the Department** |
| Shotgun is **mandated** for one-officer sector patrol vehicles and solo Highway District personnel |

### Solo Unit Shotgun Rules

| Rule |
|------|
| Lock and secure windows, doors; place in locking device when leaving unoccupied vehicle |
| Lock when responding to scene where other members present (unless extraordinary need) |
| Return immediately to precinct if defective |
| If solo RMP converted to solo SP10, summons unit, or other unit — shotgun **immediately returned** |

### Detective Bureau Special Weapons

A **captain or above** may authorize issuance when ALL of the following exist:
1. Tactical plan formulated
2. Potential arrest situation
3. Arrest or search warrant issued
4. History of violence on the part of the perpetrator

Captain or above must **accompany** those members and maintain firearms control. Weapon **unloaded, opened, and encased** while transported.

### ESU/Harbor Unit Special Weapons Uses

| Authorized Situation |
|---------------------|
| Barricaded criminal/hostage taker/EDP with firearm (or reasonable belief) |
| Armed hostage taker threatening life of hostage |
| Searches for armed perpetrators, warrant executions |
| VIP escorts and protection |
| Guarding dangerous criminals (with DOC) |
| Guarding payrolls, narcotics, or weapons transfers |
| Captain or above deems appropriate |
| Spontaneous incident requiring immediate action (last resort) |
| NOT for restraining EDP unless firearm involved |

> **Exam Alert:** Special weapons NEVER issued to member whose **qualifications are not current**. Shotgun is **mandated** for solo sector patrol vehicles.

---

## P.G. 221-11: Guidelines and Approval Process Pertaining to the Use of Distraction Devices

**Date Effective:** 06/26/25 | **Last Revision:** R.O. 52 | **Pages:** 2

### Definition

A **distraction device** is a less lethal hand-held apparatus generating startling sound and light energy when deployed into a confined area. Provides short-term tactical cover for high-risk entries.

### Authorization

- Only authorized for use by trained **Emergency Service Unit** members
- Requires direct knowledge and **specific approval** of **Chief of Department** (or designee), absent exigent circumstances

### Approval Process — Search Warrants

| Step | Who | Action |
|------|-----|--------|
| 1 | Captain or above | Contact **borough executive officer** |
| 2 | Borough XO | Review and confer with **Chief of Department** (or designee) for **final approval** |

### Approval Process — EDP/Perpetrator Search/Exigent Circumstances

| Step | Who | Action |
|------|-----|--------|
| 1 | Captain or above | Notify **duty inspector** |
| 2 | Duty inspector | Request approval of **citywide duty chief** |
| 3 | Duty chief | Contact **Chief of Department** (or designee) via Operations Unit for **final approval** |

### Exigent Circumstances Exception

Ranking ESU supervisor may request approval directly from **Chief of Special Operations** or **CO, ESU**. Once stabilized, the captain must **immediately notify the Chief of Department** via Operations Unit.

### Post-Incident Reporting

Captain concerned prepares Typed Letterhead report:
- Obtain **Distraction Device Utilization Control Number** from ESU Desk Officer
- Forward through channels to Chief of Department

> **Exam Alert:** Distraction devices require **Chief of Department** approval (or designee). Only used by trained **ESU** members. At every level, **all alternatives must be considered** before recommending approval.

---

# Section 221 — Key Terms and Definitions

## Force Definitions

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **De-Escalation** | Taking action to stabilize situation, reduce immediacy of threat; gain voluntary compliance |
| **Objectively Reasonable Standard** | Reasonableness based on totality of circumstances; perspective of similarly trained/experienced member |
| **Excessive Force** | Force greater than what a reasonable officer would use under same circumstances |
| **Active Resisting** | Physically evasive movements (bracing, tensing, pushing, verbal intent to resist) |
| **Active Aggression** | Threat or overt act of assault with present ability, indicating imminent assault/injury |
| **Passive Resistance** | Minimal physical action (standing motionless, going limp) |
| **Prohibited Methods of Restraint** | Sitting, kneeling, standing on chest/back compressing diaphragm |
| **Chokehold** | Any pressure to throat, carotid artery, or windpipe affecting breathing or blood flow |

## Force Incident Levels

| Level | Key Criteria | Investigating Entity |
|-------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Level 1** | Hand/foot strikes, take-downs, grappling, O.C. spray, mesh blanket, CEW **cartridge mode**, physical injury from police action | Immediate Supervisor |
| **Level 2** | Striking with object (baton, vehicle), canine bite, CEW **drive stun**, prohibited acts (excl. chokehold), alleged excessive force, attempted prisoner suicide, unconsciousness/lost tooth/stitches | CO/XO/Duty Captain + IAB |
| **Level 3** | Force readily capable of causing death/serious injury (not firearm), chokehold/prohibited restraint, excessive force + serious injury, attempted prisoner suicide + serious injury | Internal Affairs Bureau |
| **Level 4** | ALL firearms discharges, UMOS firearm fired by another, subject seriously injured/likely to die/dies in/prior to custody | Force Investigation Division |

## Immediate Supervisor Definition

Available supervisor NOT involved in the incident, from same command, at least **one rank higher** than all involved members. If none available, **duty captain** determines.

## Injury Classifications

| Term | Examples |
|------|---------|
| **Physical Illness/Injury** | Minor swelling, contusions, lacerations, abrasions |
| **Serious Physical Injury/Illness** | Broken/fractured bones, aneurysm, hospital admission required, heart attack, stroke, life-threatening |

## Less Lethal Weapons

| Weapon | Key Parameters |
|--------|---------------|
| **O.C. Pepper Spray** | Two 1-second bursts, minimum 3 feet, aim at eyes; NOT on passive resistance |
| **CEW (TASER 7)** | Cartridge mode = Level 1; Drive stun = Level 2; standard cycle 5 seconds; never on rear-cuffed; support side holster |
| **Tear Gas** | Borough commander approval; ESU only; dry cleaning does NOT remove residue |
| **Distraction Device** | Chief of Department approval; ESU only |

## CEW Definitions

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **Function Test** | Mandatory test into firearms safety station in test mode under supervisor observation |
| **Laser Warning** | De-escalation — placing laser dot on subject for voluntary compliance |
| **Warning Arc** | Visible/audible arcing of electricity on TASER 7 |
| **Cartridge Mode** | Probes fired for neuromuscular incapacitation (Level 1) |
| **Drive Stun** | Direct/close contact with front electrodes (Level 2) |

## Special Weapons

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **Special Weapons** | Shotguns, long guns, automatic weapons |
| **Special Weapons Record** | Three-section log: Qualification, Issuance, Maintenance |

## EDP Terms

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **EDP** | Person apparently mentally ill/temporarily deranged, likely to result in serious injury to self or others |
| **Zone of Safety** | Distance from EDP > effective range of weapon (not firearm); minimum **20 feet** |
| **Crossfire** | Unintentional placement of person in position to be injured by firearms discharge |
| **Crossfire Awareness** | Being aware of threat/surroundings; communicate "crossfire" when aware |
| **CIT** | Crisis Intervention Team — specially trained members as contact officers |

## "Resisting Recidivist"

Prisoner arrested for charges against MOS (resisting arrest, assault 2nd, assault on PO, aggravated assault on PO) **more than once within 5 years**. Requires detective squad enhancement.

## Firearm Prohibitions Summary

| SHALL NOT |
|-----------|
| Discharge to endanger innocent persons |
| Discharge in defense of property |
| Discharge at fleeing felon with no imminent threat |
| Fire warning shots |
| Discharge at/from moving vehicle (unless deadly force by other means) |
| Discharge at animal (unless imminent injury, no retreat) |
| Cock a firearm (double action only) |

## Critical Timeframes

| Item | Timeframe |
|------|-----------|
| PD324-140 (Firearms Discharge Report) | Within **48 hours** |
| Temporary assignment after discharge causing death/injury | Minimum **3 consecutive tours** |
| Post-incident member welfare follow-up | **24-48 hours** |
| Report to NYS Commission of Correction (SCOC M187) | Fax within **6 hours**; mail within **10 days** |
| CEW replacement cartridge | Within **72 hours** |
| CEW unintentional discharge retraining | Within **5 business days** |
| O.C. pepper spray effects | Do not dissipate for **15-30 minutes** |
| Vehicle checkpoint maximum duration | **2 hours** at one location |
| CEW standard cycle | **5 seconds** |
| CEW exposure risk threshold | Beyond **15 seconds** |
| Alcohol — prudent abstinence before tour | **4 hours** |
| Undercover alcohol limit | **2 beverages** per tour |

## Approval Authorities

| Action | Authority Required |
|--------|-------------------|
| Tear gas use | **Patrol Borough Commander** (or designee) |
| Distraction device use | **Chief of Department** (or designee) |
| Return to permanent command after firearms discharge | **First Deputy Commissioner** |
| Non-standard vehicle checkpoint | **Deputy Commissioner, Legal Matters** (written) |
| Cancel ESU response at EDP | **Sergeant or above** |
| Canine use (non-authorized tactical) | **Borough Commander** |
| Canine at non-peaceful demonstration | **Chief of Patrol** |

## Key Forms

| Form | Used For |
|------|---------|
| **T.R.I. INCIDENT REPORT** | One per force incident — regardless of number of MOS/subjects |
| **T.R.I. INTERACTION REPORT** | Each involved MOS completes for every reportable force/injury |
| **PD324-140** (UMOS Report of Firearms Discharge) | All firearms discharges — within 48 hours |
| **SCOC M187** (Report of Inmate Death) | Death in custody — NYS Commission of Correction |
| **PD469-150** (Supervisor's Fitness for Duty Report) | Alcohol testing documentation |
| **VEHICLE CHECKPOINT FORM** | Document checkpoint operations |
| **Special Weapons Record** | Log for issuance/qualification/maintenance of special weapons |

## NYPD Duty to Intervene

All MOS **must** intervene to stop excessive force by MOS or other law enforcement. Failure to intervene or report = **serious misconduct** → criminal/civil liability + Department discipline up to **dismissal**.

---

# Section 221 — Review Questions

## Multiple Choice

**1. Which of the following is a firearm prohibition under P.G. 221-01?**

- A) Discharging a firearm at a fleeing felon who presents an imminent threat
- B) Firing warning shots
- C) Discharging a firearm to protect from imminent physical injury
- D) Discharging a firearm in self-defense

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) Firing warning shots**

Warning shots are prohibited. Members SHALL NOT fire warning shots under any circumstances. (P.G. 221-01)

</details>

---

**2. A CEW discharge in cartridge mode is classified as what level force incident?**

- A) Level 1
- B) Level 2
- C) Level 3
- D) Level 4

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**A) Level 1**

CEW discharge in cartridge mode is a Level 1 force incident. CEW in drive stun mode is Level 2. (P.G. 221-03)

</details>

---

**3. Who investigates Level 3 force incidents?**

- A) Immediate Supervisor
- B) Commanding Officer/Executive Officer
- C) Internal Affairs Bureau
- D) Force Investigation Division

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) Internal Affairs Bureau**

Level 3 force incidents (force readily capable of causing death/serious injury, chokehold, excessive force with serious injury) are investigated by the Internal Affairs Bureau. (P.G. 221-03)

</details>

---

**4. The immediate supervisor in a force incident must be:**

- A) Any available supervisor from the same command
- B) A supervisor not involved in the incident, at least one rank higher than all involved members
- C) The desk officer of the precinct of occurrence
- D) The duty captain in all cases

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) A supervisor not involved in the incident, at least one rank higher than all involved members**

The immediate supervisor must be available, NOT involved in the incident, from the same command, and at least one rank higher than all involved members. (P.G. 221-03)

</details>

---

**5. After a firearms discharge causing death or injury, the involved UMOS is temporarily assigned for a minimum of:**

- A) One scheduled tour
- B) Three consecutive scheduled tours
- C) Five consecutive scheduled tours
- D) Seven calendar days

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) Three consecutive scheduled tours**

The involved member is temporarily assigned to the patrol borough office for a minimum of three consecutive scheduled tours (exclusive of sick time or RDOs). (P.G. 221-04)

</details>

---

**6. O.C. pepper spray should be applied using:**

- A) One continuous burst at two feet
- B) Two, one-second bursts at a minimum distance of three feet
- C) Three short bursts at five feet
- D) One long burst at arm's length

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) Two, one-second bursts at a minimum distance of three feet**

O.C. pepper spray is held upright, aimed at the subject's eyes, using two one-second bursts at a minimum distance of three feet. (P.G. 221-07)

</details>

---

**7. What is the recommended minimum "zone of safety" distance from an EDP?**

- A) 10 feet
- B) 15 feet
- C) 20 feet
- D) 25 feet

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) 20 feet**

A minimum distance of 20 feet is recommended for the zone of safety. The distance should be greater than the effective range of the weapon (other than a firearm). (P.G. 221-13)

</details>

---

**8. The UMOS Report of Firearms Discharge (PD324-140) must be completed within:**

- A) 24 hours
- B) 48 hours
- C) 72 hours
- D) End of tour

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) 48 hours**

The PD324-140 must be completed within 48 hours of the incident. (P.G. 221-04)

</details>

---

**9. Who has the authority to approve the use of tear gas?**

- A) Precinct commanding officer
- B) Patrol borough commander or designee
- C) Chief of Department
- D) Emergency Service Unit supervisor

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) Patrol borough commander or designee**

The ultimate decision concerning use of tear gas rests with the patrol borough commander or designee. Technical decisions are made by the ranking ESU member. (P.G. 221-09)

</details>

---

**10. Members of the service have a duty to intervene to stop excessive force. Failure to intervene may result in:**

- A) A verbal reprimand
- B) Command discipline only
- C) Criminal and civil liability and Department discipline up to dismissal
- D) Transfer to another command

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) Criminal and civil liability and Department discipline up to dismissal**

Failure to intervene in or report excessive force is serious misconduct that may result in criminal and civil liability and Department discipline up to and including dismissal. (P.G. 221-02)

</details>

---

**11. A "Resisting Recidivist" is defined as a prisoner arrested for charges against MOS:**

- A) Three or more times in ten years
- B) More than once within five years
- C) Twice within one year
- D) Any repeat offense regardless of timeframe

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) More than once within five years**

A Resisting Recidivist is a prisoner arrested for resisting arrest, assault on PO, or similar charges more than once within five years (i.e., the second time in five years). The detective squad must enhance the arrest. (P.G. 221-06)

</details>

---

**12. The use of distraction devices requires approval from:**

- A) Patrol borough commander
- B) Duty inspector
- C) Chief of Department or designee
- D) Chief of Patrol

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) Chief of Department or designee**

Distraction devices require direct knowledge and specific approval of the Chief of Department (or designee), absent exigent circumstances. (P.G. 221-11)

</details>

---

**13. Who may apply a spit hood to an EDP?**

- A) Any uniformed member of the service
- B) The patrol supervisor
- C) Only Emergency Service Unit personnel
- D) EMS personnel only

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) Only Emergency Service Unit personnel**

Only ESU personnel may apply a spit hood. Only ESU personnel or a medical professional with minimum EMT certification can remove it. (P.G. 221-13)

</details>

---

**14. CEWs should be carried in a holster on which side?**

- A) Shooting side, same as firearm
- B) Support side, opposite the firearm
- C) Either side at the member's preference
- D) Ankle holster

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) Support side, opposite the firearm**

The CEW holster must be attached to the duty belt on the support side, opposite the firearm. Cross-drawing is prohibited. (P.G. 221-08)

</details>

---

**15. An EDP must NEVER be transported to:**

- A) The nearest hospital
- B) A police facility
- C) A psychiatric ward
- D) An ambulance

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) A police facility**

Under no circumstances will an EDP be transported to a police facility. EDPs are removed to a hospital via ambulance (or RMP if ambulance unavailable, at supervisor's direction). (P.G. 221-13)

</details>

---

**16. At the scene of a firearms discharge, the PBT alcohol test threshold is:**

- A) .04
- B) .05
- C) .08
- D) .10

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) .08**

If the PBT reading is less than .08 and no indicia of intoxication, no further testing is required. At .08 or greater, an Intoxilyzer test at the IDTU facility is required. (P.G. 221-23)

</details>

---

**17. Which of the following is NOT an authorized use of O.C. pepper spray?**

- A) Gaining control of a person actively resisting arrest
- B) Preventing an individual from injuring themselves
- C) Controlling a subject who is passively resisting
- D) Controlling a dangerous animal

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) Controlling a subject who is passively resisting**

O.C. pepper spray, CEW, and impact weapons should NOT be used on persons who are passively resisting. (P.G. 221-07)

</details>

---

**18. Vehicle checkpoints should NOT exceed what duration at one location?**

- A) 1 hour
- B) 2 hours
- C) 3 hours
- D) 4 hours

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) 2 hours**

A vehicle checkpoint should not exceed two hours at one location, regardless of conditions. (P.G. 221-16)

</details>

---

**19. Department canine teams shall NOT be used for:**

- A) Tracking missing persons
- B) Crowd control or deterrent at peaceful demonstrations
- C) Searching buildings for break-in suspects
- D) Performing cadaver searches

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) Crowd control or deterrent at peaceful demonstrations**

Canine teams will not be used for crowd control or as a deterrent at peaceful demonstrations. Use at non-peaceful demonstrations requires prior approval of the Chief of Patrol. (P.G. 221-18)

</details>

---

**20. When a UMOS discharges a firearm outside of New York City, the Operations Unit must be notified within:**

- A) 1 hour
- B) 6 hours
- C) 12 hours
- D) 24 hours

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) 6 hours**

If a firearm is discharged outside of NYC, the member must notify local police authorities immediately and the Operations Unit within 6 hours of discharge. (P.G. 221-04)

</details>

---

**21. A CEW standard cycle is how many seconds?**

- A) 3 seconds
- B) 5 seconds
- C) 10 seconds
- D) 15 seconds

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**B) 5 seconds**

Members should use a CEW for one standard cycle of five seconds while constantly assessing. Exposure beyond 15 seconds may increase the risk of death or serious injury. (P.G. 221-08)

</details>

---

**22. The response of Emergency Service Unit to an EDP scene may only be canceled by:**

- A) Any member of the service
- B) The desk officer
- C) A uniformed supervisor in the rank of sergeant or above
- D) The precinct commanding officer only

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) A uniformed supervisor in the rank of sergeant or above**

The response of ESU may only be canceled by a uniformed supervisor in the rank of sergeant or above. (P.G. 221-13)

</details>

---

**23. Members SHALL NOT discharge firearms at or from a moving vehicle unless:**

- A) The vehicle is being used in a pursuit
- B) The vehicle is stolen
- C) Deadly physical force is being used by means other than a moving vehicle
- D) The vehicle is approaching a checkpoint

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) Deadly physical force is being used by means other than a moving vehicle**

Members SHALL NOT discharge firearms at or from a moving vehicle unless deadly physical force is being used against MOS or another person by means OTHER than a moving vehicle. (P.G. 221-01)

</details>

---

**24. How many T.R.I. INCIDENT REPORTS are prepared per force incident?**

- A) One per involved member
- B) One per subject
- C) Only ONE per incident, regardless of number of MOS or subjects
- D) One per force level

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) Only ONE per incident, regardless of number of MOS or subjects**

Only one T.R.I. INCIDENT REPORT will be prepared for an incident, regardless of the number of MOS involved or number of subjects/civilians/bystanders involved. (P.G. 221-03)

</details>

---

**25. For Level 3 and Level 4 force incidents, the immediate supervisor should handle body-worn camera footage by:**

- A) Reviewing all videos and documenting findings
- B) Uploading footage to FORMS T.R.I. application
- C) Safeguarding cameras and NOT viewing — await IAB/FID direction
- D) Forwarding footage to the commanding officer

<details><summary>Answer</summary>

**C) Safeguarding cameras and NOT viewing — await IAB/FID direction**

Body-worn camera footage concerning Level 3 and Level 4 force incidents will NOT be viewed by the immediate supervisor. Immediate supervisors will safeguard the cameras and await direction from IAB or FID. (P.G. 221-03)

</details>

---

# Section 303 — Duties & Responsibilities

## Source
- **Administrative Guide Section:** 303
- **Source PDF:** Public Admin Guide1.pdf
- **Priority:** High

## Overview

Section 303 of the Administrative Guide defines the duties and responsibilities of borough-level command staff. These five procedures establish the chain of command from Borough Commander down through the executive officers, adjutant, and operations commander. For the sergeant exam, the key takeaways are the reporting relationships, the distinction between operational and administrative oversight, and the specific timeframes for inspections/briefings.

## Procedures

| Procedure | Title | Study File | Status |
|-----------|-------|-----------|--------|
| A.G. 303-01 | Borough Commander | borough-command | completed |
| A.G. 303-02 | Borough Executive Officer, Operations | borough-command | completed |
| A.G. 303-03 | Borough Executive Officer, Administration | borough-command | completed |
| A.G. 303-04 | Borough Adjutant | borough-command | completed |
| A.G. 303-05 | Borough Operations Commander | borough-command | completed |
| A.G. 303-06 | Borough Administrator | extended | completed |
| A.G. 303-07 | Borough Special Projects Coordinator | extended | completed |
| A.G. 303-08 | Borough Counterterrorism Coordinator | extended | completed |
| A.G. 303-09 | Patrol Borough Traffic Safety Coordinator | extended | completed |
| A.G. 303-10 | Borough Auxiliary Police Coordinator | extended | completed |
| A.G. 303-14 | Crime Analysis Sergeant | extended | completed |
| A.G. 303-15 | Crime Prevention Officer | extended | completed |
| A.G. 303-17 | Digital Communications Officer | extended | completed |
| A.G. 303-20 | Community Ambassador | extended | completed |

## Priority Topics for Sergeant Exam

1. **Borough Commander (303-01)** — Semi-annual briefings, annual CPP inspections, quarterly meetings
2. **Operations vs. Administration split** — Operations exec handles crime/QOL/traffic; Administration exec handles discipline/personnel/scheduling/QA
3. **Borough Adjutant (303-04)** — Early Intervention Committee, Chronic Absent monitoring, CCRB conferral reviews, self-inspection program
4. **Reporting relationships** — Who reports to whom in the borough hierarchy
5. **Borough Operations Commander (303-05)** — 9 crime categories, youth coordination officer selection


---

# Section 303 — Borough Command Duties & Responsibilities

## A.G. 303-01: Borough Commander

**Effective:** 03/17/25 | **Last Revision:** R.O. 12 | **Page:** 1 of 1

### Duties

1. Be responsible for and ensure efficiency of all police operations within borough.
2. Develop borough specific plans and implement strategies, programs and procedures to:
   - a. Reduce crime and fear of crime
   - b. Improve quality of life
   - c. Ensure traffic safety and optimize traffic flow
3. Utilize borough executive staff to administer, assign, control, coordinate, discipline, evaluate, guide, motivate, supervise and ensure the mission of the command.
4. Develop and maintain strong relationships with clergy, business, community and educational leaders.
5. Visit Internal Affairs Bureau, Community Affairs Bureau, Quality Assurance Section, Office of Equal Employment Opportunity and Personnel Orders Division for a briefing upon assignment to command.
   - a. Confer with the above commands semi-annually thereafter.
6. Inspect each subordinate command's "Confidential Performance Profile," annually.
7. Ensure administrative and operational responsibilities of subordinate executives are performed properly and efficiently.
8. Mentor and provide guidance to subordinate command executive staff.
9. Convene a quarterly meeting (or as needed) with members of the borough's executive staff and members of the Department's line organizations, to discuss command level management issues and to resolve challenges faced by members on patrol.

> **NOTE:** Borough commanders of bureaus performing enforcement functions (e.g., Detective, Transit, Housing, etc.) will ensure that executive staff from their respective borough attend a briefing with Internal Affairs Bureau, Community Affairs Bureau, Quality Assurance Section, and Personnel Orders Division upon assignment to command and semi-annually thereafter.

> **Memory Aid — B.C. SCHEDULE:** **S**emi-annual (S-S) = **S**emi-annual **S**taff briefings (IAB, CAB, QAS, OEEO, Personnel Orders) = **S**taff. **A**nnual = **A**nnual **A**udit (Confidential Performance Profile). **Q**uarterly = **Q**uarterly **Q**uality meetings (executive staff + line organizations). Pattern: **S-A-Q**.

> **Exam Alert — Semi-Annual vs. Annual vs. Quarterly:** The three timeframes for Borough Commander duties are frequently confused. Semi-annual = staff briefings. Annual = CPP inspection. Quarterly = executive staff meetings. A question may ask "How often must a Borough Commander brief with IAB?" — the answer is semi-annual, not quarterly.

> **Exam Alert — "All Police Operations":** The Borough Commander is responsible for *all* police operations within the borough — not just patrol, not just crime, but ALL operations. This is broader than the Borough Executive Officers who divide responsibilities between Operations and Administration.

### Sergeant Exam Focus
- **Semi-annual** briefings with IAB, CAB, QAS, OEEO, Personnel Orders
- **Annual** inspection of Confidential Performance Profile
- **Quarterly** meetings with executive staff and line organizations
- Borough Commander is responsible for **all** police operations within borough

---

**Role Comparison — Borough Command Structure:**

| Role | Reports To | Primary Focus | Key Responsibilities |
|------|-----------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Borough Commander** | None (top of borough) | All police operations within borough | Strategic plans, community relationships, mentor executive staff, inspect CPP annually |
| **Borough Executive Officer, Operations** | Borough Commander | Operational efficiency | Crime control, QOL, traffic, graffiti, liaison with Counterterrorism Coordinator |
| **Borough Executive Officer, Administration** | Borough Commander | Administrative oversight | Discipline, personnel, scheduling, QA, property, budget |
| **Borough Adjutant** | Exec Officer, Administration | Risk management | Early Intervention, auditing, sick monitoring, CCRB reports, self-inspection |
| **Borough Operations Commander** | Exec Officer, Operations | Crime reduction strategies | 9 crime categories, special events, youth coordination officers |

---

## A.G. 303-02: Borough Executive Officer, Operations

**Effective:** 05/27/21 | **Last Revision:** I.O. 27 | **Page:** 1 of 1

### Duties

1. Supervise the following:
   - a. Borough Operations Commander
   - b. Borough Special Projects Coordinator
   - c. Borough specialty units
2. Provide for borough strategic and tactical efficiency through the equitable distribution of borough operational resources.
3. Provide operational oversight and support at the borough command level, including housing developments covered by the Patrol Services Bureau, for the following:
   - a. Planning and execution of crime control strategies
   - b. Development of initiatives for quality of life improvement and maintenance
   - c. Traffic management and safety
   - d. Graffiti enforcement and removal
4. Liaison with the Borough Counterterrorism Coordinator to ensure the operational readiness of:
   - a. Emergency preparedness
   - b. Mobilizations

### Sergeant Exam Focus
- Supervises Borough Operations Commander, Special Projects Coordinator, and specialty units
- Responsible for **operational** oversight (crime, QOL, traffic, graffiti)
- Liaison with Borough Counterterrorism Coordinator for emergency preparedness

> **Exam Alert — Operations vs. Administration Split:** A frequent exam question presents a scenario and asks whether it falls under Operations or Administration. Remember: **Operations = crime, QOL, traffic, graffiti** (the visible, field-facing work). **Administration = discipline, personnel, scheduling, budget, property** (the internal, paper-facing work).

> **Exam Alert — Crime Reduction Strategy Covers 9 Categories:** The Borough Operations Commander develops strategies for: (1) Sex crimes, (2) Robberies, (3) Assaults, (4) Burglaries, (5) Larcenies/thefts, (6) Vehicle theft, (7) Domestic violence, (8) Firearms violence/weapons, (9) Other crimes as they emerge. Know this number — it appears on exams.

> **See Also:** A.G. 303-03 — Borough Executive Officer, Administration (discipline, personnel, evaluations)

---

## A.G. 303-03: Borough Executive Officer, Administration

**Effective:** 06/26/23 | **Last Revision:** R.O. 33 | **Page:** 1 of 1

### Duties

1. Supervise the following:
   - a. Borough Administrator
   - b. Borough Adjutant
2. Provide operational and administrative oversight at the borough command level for the following:
   - a. Community affairs
   - b. Communications (written)
   - c. Discipline
   - d. Facilities/logistics/supply management
   - e. Personnel issues:
     - (1) Budget/overtime monitoring
     - (2) Evaluation process (entire borough)
     - (3) Training (crossover with Counterterrorism and operational)
     - (4) Resource use/needs/distribution
     - (5) Sick monitoring
   - f. Property held in Department custody
   - g. Scheduling:
     - (1) Executive scheduling
     - (2) Court/agency appearances verification and follow up
   - h. Quality assurance:
     - (1) Inspections
     - (2) Audit review
     - (3) Data integrity
     - (4) Data quality

> **Exam Alert — Operations vs. Administration Split:** A frequent exam question presents a scenario and asks whether it falls under Operations or Administration. Remember: **Operations = crime, QOL, traffic, graffiti** (the visible, field-facing work). **Administration = discipline, personnel, scheduling, budget, property** (the internal, paper-facing work).

> **Exam Alert — Crime Reduction Strategy Covers 9 Categories:** The Borough Operations Commander develops strategies for: (1) Sex crimes, (2) Robberies, (3) Assaults, (4) Burglaries, (5) Larcenies/thefts, (6) Vehicle theft, (7) Domestic violence, (8) Firearms violence/weapons, (9) Other crimes as they emerge. Know this number — it appears on exams.

> **See Also:** A.G. 303-03 — Borough Executive Officer, Administration (discipline, personnel, evaluations)

---

### Sergeant Exam Focus
- Supervises Borough Administrator and Borough Adjutant
- Responsible for **administrative** oversight (discipline, personnel, scheduling, QA)
- Distinction: **Operations** exec handles crime/QOL/traffic; **Administration** exec handles discipline/personnel/scheduling

---

## A.G. 303-04: Borough Adjutant

**Effective:** 05/03/24 | **Last Revision:** R.O. 24 | **Page:** 1 of 1

### Duties

1. Report to Borough Executive Officer, Administration.
2. Oversee all risk management and mitigation functions within borough of assignment.
   - a. Coordinate with Professional Standards Division and implement strategies to improve performance and remediate deficiencies.
   - b. Utilize information and data analytics to identify deficiencies and indicators of negative performance and take remedial action, as necessary.
3. Participate in Early Intervention Committee.
   - a. Oversee implementation of intervention strategies and performance monitoring programs.
4. Audit data integrity functions to ensure compliance.
5. Oversee and ensure equity of the disciplinary system throughout borough command.
6. Manage the self-inspection and auditing program.
7. Ensure property in custody of the Department is properly accounted for and maintained.
8. Manage overtime to ensure efficient use of resources.
9. Monitor budget and overtime allowances within borough.
10. Monitor sick records of assigned personnel with attention to members designated Chronic Absent.
11. Review firearms discharges by uniformed members of the service.
12. Conduct staff Performance Evaluations.
13. Review reports submitted by commanding officers within borough regarding conferrals with uniformed members of the service prior to Civilian Complaint Review Board (CCRB) appearances for accuracy and completeness.

> **Exam Alert — Borough Adjutant Reports to Administration, Not Operations:** The Borough Adjutant reports to the Borough Executive Officer, **Administration** — not Operations. This is a common test trap. The chain is: Borough Commander → Exec Officer Administration → Borough Adjutant. Ops chain is separate: Borough Commander → Exec Officer Operations → Borough Operations Commander.

> **Exam Alert — Adjutant's Multiple Roles:** The Borough Adjutant wears many hats — risk management, Early Intervention, auditing, disciplinary equity, self-inspection, overtime management, sick monitoring, firearms discharge review, CCRB conferral review. Know that all of these flow through the Adjutant and feed into the Administration executive's oversight function.

### Sergeant Exam Focus
- Reports to Borough Executive Officer, **Administration**
- Key role in **Early Intervention Committee** and performance monitoring
- Monitors **Chronic Absent** members' sick records
- Reviews **CCRB conferral reports** for accuracy
- Manages **self-inspection and auditing** program
- Ensures **equity of disciplinary system** throughout borough

---

## A.G. 303-05: Borough Operations Commander
